\*blikan- s.v. 'to shine' - OE blican s.v. 'to shine, glitter', OFri. blika s.v. 'to be visible', OS b/ikan s.v. 'to shine', Du. blijken s.v. 'to appear', MHG blichen s.v. 'to shine' ⇒ \*bhleig-e- (NEUR) - OCS bliskati Sfl, bliscati Sfl 'to sparkle, shine', Ru. blistat' 'to shine', SCr. bn'skati, blistati (se) 'id.' < \*blig-ske-; Lith. blyskJti, blizgJti 'to shine' < \*blig-sk-eh₁-. Also cf. ON blfkja w.v. 'to shine' < \*blikjan-, \*blaika- and \*blanka-.

\*blinda- adj. 'blind' - Go. blinds adj. 'id.', ON blindr adj. 'id.', OE blind adj. 'id.', OFri. blind adj. 'id.', OS blind adj. 'id.', Du. blind adj. 'id.', OHG blint adj. 'id.', G blind adj. 'id.' (DRV). Derived from \*blandan-.

\*blipa- adj. 'mild, kind' - Go. bleips\* adj. 'merciful', ON blfor adj. 'gentle, friendly; pleasant', OE bffoe adj. 'joyful, glad', E blithe, OS blithi adj. 'joyful, happy', Du. blij adj. 'id.', OHG blidi adj. 'id.' (GM?). No certain etymology: possibly related to \*bliwa- 1 if the original meaning was 'bright'. Also cf. OE blips, E bliss, OE blidsea f. 'joy' < \*blipiso-.

\*bliwa- 1 n. 'color, hue' - OE bleo n. 'color, hue, complexion', ME ble 'color, hue; appearance, guise', OFri. bli n.(?) 'complexion', OS bli n. 'color', MDu. blie n. 'complexion' ⇒ \*bh/oi-uo- (NEUR) - Lith. b/aivytis (blaivaus) 'to become bright, clear up' < \*bh/oi-u-eie-. Unrelated to \*bliwa- 2 'lead'.

\*bliwa- 2 n. 'lead' - ON b!y n. 'id.', Far. b!yggj n. 'id.', OS bli n. 'id.', OHG blio n. 'id.', G Blei n. 'id.' ⇒ \*m//uo- (NIE). A non-IE Wanderwort reminiscent of Myc. mo-ri-wo-do-, Gr. μ6A.u(3oo<;, μ6A.L(3o<;, (36A.Lμo<; m. 'lead' and Lat. p/umbum 'id.'. The Greek forms, with their interchange of initial μ and (3, are highly irregular and must be analyzed as borrowings from a Pre-IE source, e.g. \*mo/ii(m)bd-. It therefore seems likely that PGm. \*b/iwa-, too, goes back to a non-IE proto-form \*mliuo- with initial m.
\*bloan- s.v. 'to bloom, flourish' - OE blowan s.v. 'id.', OFri. b/oia s.v. 'id.', OS b/oian s.v. 'id.', Du. b/oeien w.v. 'id.', OHG bluoen s.v. 'id.', G blilhen w.v. 'id.' ⇒

\*bhfeh₁-e- (WEUR) - Lat. flos,floris m. 'flower' < \*bh/ehros-; Olr. bl<ith, MW blawd m. 'flower' < \*bh/(e)h₃-tu-. A verb only attested in Germanic. The laryngeal of the underlying root has been identified as \*h₁ in view of Olr. blath and PGm. \*b/oman- (Schrijver 1991: 131). lndeed, PGm. \*bloman- is likely to contain an e-grade in view of the original mn-suffix. OE blci!d m.(?) 'flower, blossom, fruit' has contrarily been adduced to reconstruct a formation \*bledu- < \*bh/ehi-tri- with h₁, but it is more likely that this form represents a variant of the ti-stem bled m./f. 'id.' (for which see \*blodi-). It therefore seems safe to reconstruct the Germanic strong verb as \*bhfeh₁-e- rather than an o-graded \*bh/6h₁-e-.

\*bloda- n. 'blood' - Go. blop n. 'id.', ON b/60 n. 'id.', Elfd. bluoo n. 'id.', OE blocl n. 'id.', E blood, OFri. blod n. 'id.', MDu. bloet n. 'blood; blushing', Du. bloed n. 'id.', OHG bluot n. 'id.', G Blut n. 'id.' (DRV). A neuter collective created to either \*blean- 'to blow' or \*bloan- 'to flower'. The latter connection is based on the idea that the color of the blood served as the Benennungsmotiv (cf. MDu. b/oet n. 'blushing'), the former on the assumption of a semantic primitive 'to gush' (cf. EWAhd: II, 212). Alternatively, it is possible to start from a meaning 'life' derived from 'breath', cf. OHG bliit m. 'blowing', OE blci!d m. 'breath, spirit, life' < \*b/eda-.

\*blodi- f. 'bloom' - OE bled f. 'shoot, branch, flower, fruit', OHG bluot f. 'blossom, blossoming', G Bliite f. 'id.' ⇒ \*bh/eh:rtf- (DRV). A ti-stem derived from the root \*bh/eh₃-, for which see \*b/6an-.

\*bloman- m. 'flower' - Go. bloma m. 'id.', ON b/6mi m. 'id.', Far. b/6mi m., b/6ma f. 'flower, bloom', Elfd. bljomme m. 'id.', OS blomo m. 'id.', Du. bloem c. 'id.', OHG b/uomo m., bluoma f. 'id.', MHG bluome m./f. 'id.', G Blume f. 'id.' ⇒ \*bh/eh₁-mon- (DRV). Apparently an mn-stem derived from the strong verb \*b/6an- (q.v.). It is possible, too, to derive \*b/6man- from \*blomman- < \*b/6zman-, which would be a Verner variant of the lngvaeonic formation OE blos(t)ma m. 'blossom', MLG blosem, blossem m. 'id.', MDu. bloesem m. 'id.', Du. bloesem c. 'blossom' < \*blosman-.

\*blotan- s.v. 'to sacrifice' - Go. blotan 'to serve, worship', ON biota s.v. 'to worship; to sacrifice', OE bl6tan s.v. 'to sacrifice', OHG blozan s./w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*bh/6h₂d-e-(?) (NEUR?) - Lat.flamen m. 'priest' < \*bh/eh₂(d/g)-men-. The traditional connection with flamen m. 'priest' (cf. Pokorny IEW: 154) is possible, but uncertain because it is impossible to see whether the root of the Lat. word was \*flii-, \*fliid- or \*flag-. In view of the potentially archaic semantics of the loanword Fi. /uote 'enchantment', it is possible to alternatively connect e.g. Lith. b/6deti, Latv. bliidet 'to babble, ramble' <

\*bOan- - \*biian- s.v. 'to live, dwell' - Go. bauan s./w.v. 'id.', ON bUa s.v. 'to live; to prepare, fix', Far. bUgva s./w.v. 'id.', OE biian, biigan s.v. 'to live, dwell', OS biian w.v. 'id.', Du. bouwen w.v. 'to build', OHG bii(w)an, bii(w]en s./w.v. 'to live, dwell; to build', G bauen w.v. 'to build' ⇒ \*bheh₂u- (IE) - Skt bhavati 'to become, happen, come about', OAv. bauuaiti 3sg.pres. 'to become' < \*bheuh₂-e-; Gr. cpi>oμm 'to grow, arise, spring up, become', Lith. buti, Latv. but 'to be', OCS byti, Ru. byt' (bUdu), SCr. bl'ti 'id.' < \*bhuh₂-e-; Lat fui 'to become' < \*bhuh₂-; Olr. bi id 'is wont to be', OW bez, bit 'to be' < \*bhh₂u-ie- (cf. McCone 1991: 122; Zair 2009); Alb. bij 'to sprout, grow out'(?). The etymological cluster belonging to this verb is difficult to analyze formally (cf. Seebold 1970: 127), but in all likelihood, the difference between \*biian- and \*bOan- is simply to be explained as resulting from an ablauting paradigm, e.g. a root present 3sg. \*bheh₂u-ti, pl. \*bhh₂u-enti. The full-grade root \*bheh₂u- should through \*bOw- regularly have given \*bO- by the loss of u after \*o in open syllables (cf. e.g. \*no- 'ship' < \*neh₂u-), while \*bii- developed from \*bhh₂u- by laryngeal metathesis (cf. \*biira-). See also \*bewwaand \*buwwen-.

\*bogu- m. 'shoulder, upper arm' - ON b6gr m. 'shoulder', Elfd. buog m. 'id.', OE bag m. 'shoulder, arm, branch', E bow, Du. boeg c. 'bow', OHG buog m. 'shoulder, hip, bow', G Bug m. 'id.' ⇒ \*bheh₂9h·u- (IE) - Identical to ToA poke, ToB poko 'arm', Skt bahu- m. 'arm, forearm, forefoot of an animal', YAv. b<izu- m. 'arm', NP biizii 'id.', Oss. bazyg / bazug 'id.', Gr. niixu<;. Dor., Aeol. nfixu<; m. 'forearm, arm; cubit'. A u-stem related to \*bOsma- 'bosom' and \*bagma- 'beam; tree' (q.v.). Also cf. ON bregsl, Ice!. bægsl, Far. beksl n. '(front) flipper of a whale' < \*bOg-isla-.

\*bOk- f. 'book' - ON b6k, pl. brekr f. 'id.', Far. b6k, pl. bekr f. 'id.', Elfd. buok, pl. byoker f. 'id.', OE Mc, pl. bee f. 'id.', E book, OFri. bOk f. 'id.' (DRY). Also cf. Go. boka f. 'letter', OHG buoh m./n./f. 'book, script, scripture, letter', Buch n. 'book', OS bok n. 'book, tablet', Du. boek n. 'book', OFri. bOk f./n. 'id.' < \*boka/o-. Related to \*bokOJo- 'beech' (q.v.); it is generally assumed that the first scriptures consisted of wooden tablets (cf. OS bOk), cf. Tacitus (Germania 10): Virgam frugiferae arboris decisam in surculos amputant, eosque notis quibusdam discretos super candidam vestem temere ac fortuite spargunt.
\*bokO)o- f. 'beech' - ON b6k f. 'id.', Far. b6k f. 'id.', OE Mc, bece f. 'id.', E beech, OS boka f. 'id.', Du. beuk c. 'id.', OHG buohha f. 'id.', G Buche f. 'id.' ⇒

\*bhehfgl-eh₂- (EUR) - Gr. <pl]y6<;, Dor. <pay6<; f. 'oak', Lat. fii9us f. 'beech', Gaul. \*bii9os 'id.' (in French toponyms) < \*bhehfgLo-. An old IE tree name. Most Germanic forms straightforwardly point to PGm. \*bOk-. Ice!. beyki n. 'beech', a collective formation, has been interpreted as continuing \*baukja-, apparently with a -u- in its root, but it is more likely that beyki [peiklx] is an irregular continuant of ON \*breki (cf. breki-sk69r 'beech forest'), a form directly continued by Ice!. breki (cf. Magnusson 1989: 53). Du. beuk does not go back to a PGm. form with \*u either, but was borrowed from the eastern dialects that were affected by umlaut of PGm. \*o to o. Any direct connection to Ru. boz 'elder', allegedly continuing PIE \*bhugh-o-, must therefore be rejected, not least in view of the mismatch between Pre-Gm. \*(gJ and Pre-Slav. \*gh.

\*bOni- f. 'request, prayer' - ON bren f. 'id.', Far. ben f. 'id.', Elfd. byon f. 'id.', OE bOn f. 'id.' ⇒ \*bhehi-ni- (EUR/DRV) - Arm. ban 'word, speech; matter, thing' < \*bhehi-ni-. A Germanic-Armenian isogloss (Olsen 1999: 79) derived from the root of \*bannan- (q.v.) with a ni-suffix.

\*bonjan- w.v. 'to decorate' - OE bonian w.v. 'to ornament', MDu. boenen w.v. 'to polish', Du. boenen w.v. 'id.', MHG buenen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*bheh₂- (IE) - Skt. bhdti 'to shine, be bright', YAv. fra-uuiiiti 'id.', Oss. ivajyn 'to become pale' < \*bheh₂-; Gr. <paivw 'to show, make visible, bring to light' < \*bhh₂-n-ie-. The verb looks like a factitive of an adjective \*bona- < \*bhoh₂-no-, which is possibly attested as MDu. boen adj. 'fit, good, pretty', and has an exact parallel in Olr. Mn adj. 'white'. Derived from the PIE root \*bhehr 'to shine'. Related to Far. bfna, Nw. bina w.v. 'to stare' < \*bhh₂-i-n-eh₁-?

\*bosma- m. 'bosom' - OE bosm m. 'id.', E bosom, OFri. bosem m. 'id.', MDu. boesem, bosem m. 'id.', Du. boezem c. 'id.', OHG buosum m. 'bosom, lap', G Busen m. 'id.' (DRV). A collective(?) derived from \*b69u- 'shoulder, upper arm'.

\*boto- f. 'improvement' - Go. bota f. 'advantage, benefit', ON b6t f. 'improvement; compensation; patch', Far. bot f. 'id.', Elfd. buot f. 'cure, remedy; fine', OE bot f. 'help; remedy', OFri. bOte f. 'recovery; compensation, fine', OS bota f. 'healing; penitence', Du. boete c. 'penitence; fine', OHG buoz f. 'id.', G Bufle f. 'id.' ⇒ \*bh0Hd-eh₂- (DRV). An o-stem derived from the root \*bat-, for which see \*batiz 'better'. Also cf. Go. botjan 'to help', ON breta 'to repair, compensate', OE betan, OFri. beta, OS bOtian, OHG buozen, G buflen w.v. 'to improve, compensate' < \*botjan-.

\*bragna- m. 'brain' - OE brregen n. 'id.', OFri. brein n. 'id.', MLG bragen, bregen n. 'id.', MDu. bragen, brein n. 'id.', Du. brein n. 'id.' ⇒ \*mr6gh-no- (IE) - Gr. ȬPEXμ<'><; m. 'front part of the head' < \*mregh-mo-; Skt. mastrhan- m. 'brain', YAv. mastarayan- m. 'id.', MP mastarg, masturg 'brains' < \*most-(m)rgh-e/on- (Lubotsky 2008a). An IE word preserved only in the lngvaeonic area. The development of \*bragna- to Du. brein, -ei- usually being a contraction of -eg(i)- rather than -ay-, could point to a Frisianism. Frisian influence may similarly account for the e of MLG bregen, although it has been claimed on the basis of this form that PGm. had a stem variant \*bragina- (Liihr 1988: 332). Both \*bragnaand \*bragina- are explicable from an IE ablauting consonant stem, e.g. a static neuter nom. \*mregh-mn, gen. \*mr6gh-mn-s, Joe. \*mrogh-men-i. The e-grade would then have been preserved by Gr. ȬPEXμ6<;. For the loss of the m in Germanic, cf. \*budman- - \*buttaz 'bottom'.

\*braida- adj. 'broad' - Go. braips adj. 'id.', ON breic'Jr adj. 'id.', Far. breic'Jur adj. 'id.', Elfd. briec'J adj. 'id.', OE brad adj. 'id.', E broad, OFri. bred adj. 'id.', OS bred adj. 'id.', Du. breed adj. 'id.', OHG breit adj. 'id.', G breit adj. 'id.' ⇒ \* bhroidh-o-(?) (GM). A Germanic word without any clear cognates in other IE languages. Perhaps to Lith. bei'ti (beriu) 'to strew, distribute' < \*bher-e- (cf. Heidermanns 1993: 137): if so, \*braida- would have to be a to-participle to an (unattested) i-present \*bhr-oi-. Rather to a disappeared strong verb \*bridan-? Cf. OHG breta f. 'palm of the hand' < \*brido-.

\*brakka- adj. 'briny, brackish' - MLG brack adj. 'id.', Du. brak adj. 'id.' (whence E brack, brackish) (GM). An adjective related to \*broka- 2 'swamp'. No further cognates. Olr. bren, MW braen adj. 'putrid, foul' < PCelt. \*bragno- does not belong here, but to the PIE root \*bhrh₁9- 'to smell' (see under \*brakkan-). The often compared Gr. Ȭpcxy6<; 'swamp' is a Hesychian gloss that in combination with the irregular variant Ȭpaxo<; m. 'shallow water' cannot possibly be derived from a PIE proto-form \*mrg-o-. This leaves us with an exclusively Germanic word.

\*brakka(n)- m. 'sleuth dog' - MDu. bracke m. 'id.', Du. brak c. 'id.', OHG bracko m. 'id.', G Bracke m. 'id.' (Lw?) - Lat. fragro, -are 'to smell strongly' (derived from an adj. \*bhrh₁g-ro-?); Mir. bren adj. 'putrid, foul', MW braen, MoB brein adj. 'putrid, corrupt' < \*bhrh₁9-no-. No existing etymology: the word may be connected with PCelt. \*brokko-, cf. Olr. brace 'badger', an animal with a strong sense of smell. In view of the animal's strong odor, the word has been linked to MHG brrehen w.v. 'to smell', which when reconstructed as PGm. \*brekjan-, can be compared to Lat. fragro, -are 'to smell strongly' (derived from an adj. \*bhrh₁9-ro-?) and

\*brakkon-(?) f. 'pants' - Elfd. brakka f. 'id.', EDa. brakker pl. 'lower leg covers', OE braccas m.pl. 'breeches' ( < \*brakka-(?)) (GM?). Usually assumed to be a loanword from Lat. bracca, which developed from Lat. briica 'breeches', an originally Gaulish word connected with PGm. \*brak-. The attestation in (East) Nordic is peculiar, however, and seems to imply a Germanic origin.
\*brannjan- w.v. 'to make burn' - Go. brannjan w.v. 'id.', ON brenna w.v. 'id.', OE brernan w.v. 'id.', OHG brennen w.v. 'id.', G brennen w.v. 'id.' (DRV). The causative to \*brinnan- (q.v.).

\*branta- adj. 'steep' - ON brattr adj. 'steep; sudden', OE brant, bront adj. 'high, deep, steep, difficult' ⇒ \*bhrond-o-(?) (WEUR?). No certain etymology: perhaps to W brynn m. 'heap, mound' if from \*bhrendo-. The alternative is to link the cluster to Olr. do-eprinn 'to gush, pour out' < \*bhre-n-d- and Mir. bruinnid 'to spring, flow, rush' < \*bhrond-eie, which would require a semantic shift from 'skewed, slanting' to 'pour' in Celtic. Also cf. ON bretta w.v. 'to turn upwards', OSw. brrenta w.v. 'to bend back, bend upwards' < \*brantjan-.

\*brauda- n. 'bread' - ON brauo n. 'id.', OE bread n. 'id.', E bread, OFri. briid n. 'id.', OS brad n. 'id.', Du. brood n. 'id.', OHG brat n. 'id.', G Brat n. 'id.' ⇒ \*bhrouhrt6- (DRY). A to-stem derived from \*brewwan- 'to brew' (q.v.).

\*brazda- n. 'edge, brim' - Ice!. bradd n. 'edge', Nw. bradd m. 'shore, side', Elfd. bradd m. 'edge', OE breard m. 'brim, margin', OHG brart 'edge', MHG brart m. 'edge, board' ⇒ \*bhrozdh-o- (WEUR). An o-grade formation related to \*brezda- and \*burzda- (q.v.). It is cognate with Olr. brat m. 'goad, spike' < \*bhrozdh-o-, which was probably not borrowed from ON broddr < \*bruzda- (Greene 1972: 70). A secondary formation is represented by \*barzda- 'edge; beard' which in view of the position of the ablaut slot must have been created on the basis of the zero grade \*burzda- (q.v.).
\*brean- s.v. 'to fume, smell' - MHG brrehen w.v. 'to smell' ⇒ \*bhrehre- (GM).

\*brekan- s.v. 'to break' - Go. brikan s.v. 'id.', OE brecan s.v. 'id.', E to break, OFri. breka s.v. 'id.', OS brekan s.v. 'id.', Du. breken s.v. 'id.', OHG brehhan s.v. 'id.', G brechen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*bhreg-e- (WEUR). A strong verb closely related to the iterative \*bruk(k)on-, which may be etymologically identical to Lat. frango 'to break' < \*bhrg-neh₂-. There must also have been an a-grade intensive verb \*brakan- in view of OE bracan, pret. brae. It probably served as the basis for \*brok- 'breeches' ( q.v.).

\*breman- - \*brimman- s.v. 'to drone, hum' - OHG breman s.v. 'id.', MHG bremen, brimmen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*bhrem-e- (IE) - MP bl'm- 'to cry, weep', Lat. fremo, -ere 'to drone, roar', W brefu 'to roar' < \*bhrem-e-. A strong verb derived from a Proto-Indo-European onomatopoetic root \*bhrem-. MHG bremen preserves the original form of the verb, while brimman appears to have been influenced by the iterative \*brummon- (q.v.). See further \*bremisa/on-.

\*bremisa/on- m./f. 'gadfly' - OE breosa, briosa m., brimse f. 'id.', ME brese, brimse 'gadfly; locust', OS brimissa f. 'gadfly', MLG bromese f. 'id.', MDu. breemse, bremse f. 'gadfly; locust; beetle', Du. brems c. 'gadfly' (DRY). Derived from the more primary \*breman-, cf. OS, OHG bremo m. 'id.', an n-stem created to the strong verb \*breman- - \*brimman- (q.v.). OE breosa / briosa, which previously has been derived from \*breusan-, probably developed from \*bremisan-, just as OE f>lestre adj. 'darkness' developed from \*f>emestrja- (see under \*f>emestra-). This development (see Gqsiorowski 2012) implies a form \*briUs- with loss of the m before s after the syncope of the medial vowel. Also cf. ODu. bremmia, MDu. brem f. 'gadfly' and MLG bromete f. 'id.' < \*bremuto(n)- (with the same suffixation as e.g. \*hurnuta/o'hornet').

\*brestan- s.v. 'to break, burst' - ON bresta s.v. 'id.', Far. bresta s.v. 'to break, collapse, strike', OE berstan s.v. 'to burst', E to burst, OFri. bersta s.v. 'to break; to disappear', OS brestan s.v. 'to burst, break', Du. barsten w.v. 'id.', OHG brestan s.v. 'to burst, tear; to lack', G bersten s.v. 'to crack' ⇒ \*bhrest-e(WEUR?) - To Olr. brissid 'to break; to defeat'? A strong verb to an obscure Pre-Gm. root \*bhrest-. Also cf. the inchoative Elfd. brussn w.v. 'to break' < \*brustnan- and the derived u-stem \*brustu-.

\*breusta- n. 'breast, chest' - ON brj6st n. 'id.', OE breost n. 'id.', E breast, OFri. briast n. 'id.', OS briost n. 'id.' ⇒ \*bhreus-sth₂-o- (EUR) - Olr. bnl.asach adj. 'big-bellied' < PCelt \*brous-t-<iko- < \*bhreus-t-; Ru. brjuxo n. 'belly, paunch' < \*bhreus-o-; Olr. bnl., gen. bronn f., MW bru m. 'womb, belly', MW bron f. < \*bhrus-on-; Olr. bruinne m. 'breast, bosom, chest' < \*bhrus-n-io-. In (unclear) ablaut relation with the root noun \*brust- (q.v.). Given the largely complementary dialectal distribution with the latter word, it is likely that both formations split off from a single PGm. paradigm. It is unclear, however, how this paradigm should be reconstructed.

\*breutan- s.v. 'to break (open), bud' - ON brj6ta s.v. 'to break, break open; to destroy', Far. br6ta s.v. 'to break; to infringe; to offend', Elfd. briuota s.v. 'to break', OE breotan s.v. 'id.', MHG briezen s.v. 'to break open, to bud, swell up' (DRY?). A strong verb possibly back-formed to the iterative \*brut(t)on- (q.v.). Closely related to OE breatan s.v. 'to break, kill', continuing an o-grade iterative-intensive verb \*brautan-. For a similar parallelism of a class 2 with a class 7 strong verb, cf. \*beutan- - \*biitan- vs. \*bautan- and \*brekan- vs. \*brakan-.

\*brewo- f. 'eyebrow' - ON br(!, bra f. 'eyelid', lcel. bra f. 'eyelid; eyelash', Far. bra f. 'eyelash', OS br<iwa f. 'eyelid', MDu. brauwe f. 'eyebrow; edge', Du. wenk-brauw c. 'eyebrow', OHG briiwa f. 'id.', G Augen-braue f. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₃bhreuH-o/eh₂- (IE). Also cf. OHG wint-briiwa, OS wint-briiwa, MDu. wint-brauwe 'eyebrow; eyelid', Du. wimper 'eyelash' < \*winda-brewo- (for the first element, cf. Gr. fov-Oo<; m. 'young, downy hair' < \*ui-uondh-o-) and the closely related OE br;Ew, breaw m. 'eyelid', OFri. iich-bre n. 'eyebrow; eyelid' < \*brewa-. In PIE, the word may have been in paradigmatic relation with the zero-grade formation \*brii- 'bridge; eyebrow' (q.v.), e.g. nom. \*h₃bhreuH-s, gen. \*h₃bhruH-6s (Beekes 1995: 190).

\*brewwan- s.v. 'to brew' - ON brugginn ptc. 'id.', Far. bryggja w.v. 'id.', OSw. bryggia s.v. 'id.', Elfd. bryddja w.v. 'id.', OE breowan s.v. 'id.', E to brew, OFri. briouwa, brouwa s.v. 'id.', OS gi-breuwan s.v. 'id.', Du. brouwen s./w.v. 'id.', MHG briuwen, briiwen s.v. 'id.', G brauen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*bhreuh₁-e- (EUR) - Gr. Hsch. an-EcppucrEv aor. 'brewed' < \*bhruh₁-s-; Lat. de-frutum n. 'must' < \*-bhruh₁-to-. A strong verb to the PIE root \*bhreuh₁- 'to boil, brew' (cf. LIV2: 96). The weak verb ON, lcel., Elfd. brugga appears to continue a secondary iterative \*bruwwon-. See also \*brauda-, \*brunnan- and \*beura-.

\*brezda· n. 'side, edge; board' - Far. breddi m. 'edge, side', Nw. bredd m. 'id.', OSw. brredder m. 'id.', OHG bret n. 'board', G Brett n. 'id.' ⇒ \*bhrezdh-o(WEUR). A full-grade formation to \*burzda- ( q.v.). The loss of the \*z in OHG bret is irregular, but may be due to dissimilation in the plural OHG \*brertira < \*brezd-izo. ME bred 'board, tablet' can be a loanword from Old Norse \*breddr as presupposed by the OSw. and Far. forms. Du. berd 'board' does not belong here, but represents a dialectal form of bard < \*burzda-.

\*brinda(n)· m. 'elk' - Elfd. brinde m. 'id.', Nw. bringe m. 'male elk' (GM). Unrelated to Messap. ,p£vt5ov 'deer' < \*bhrendh-(?) as given by Hesychius or to OPru. braydis, Latv. briedis 'elk' < \*braidi- (pace Pokorny IEW: 168-9). In view of Elfd. brunda w.v. 'to rut' and the Nw. Sunnmi;lre context e/gen hev alt teke ti/ a brunda 'the elk has started rutting' < \*brundojan-, it is evident that the word started out as a late North Germanic deverbal formation that has nothing to do with the extra-Germanic look-alikes mentioned above. It is rather cognate with Nw. brund m. 'rut; male elk' < \*brunda-, which was derived from \*breman- - \*brimman - 'to drone, roar' (q.v.), cf. MHG brunft, G Brunst f. 'rut' < \*brumpi-.

\*bringan· s.v. 'to bring' - Go. briggan w.v. 'id.', OE bringan w.v. 'id.', E to bring, OFri. bringa w.v. 'id.', OS bringan w.v. 'id.', Du. brengen w.v. 'id.', OHG bringan w.v. 'id.', G bringen w.v. 'id.' (IE?). A verb of obscure origin. Perhaps it can be analyzed as a conflation of the PIE roots \*bher- 'to carry' and \*h₂enli- 'to arrive', arisen from a suppletive paradigm, cf. Gr. φέρω, aor. T]vEyKa < \*h₁ne-h₁nli-, cf. OCS nesti, Ru. nest[ (nesu), SCr. nesti 'to carry' < \*h₁nel<-, cf. MW he-bryngaf'to bring' < \*-bhrenk< \*bhr- + \*h₁;2nli- (Matasovic 2009: 76). Alternatively, it is possible to analyze the perfective \*ga-bringan (cf. OE ge-bringan, OS gi-bringan) as a re· modeling of \*kom-pro-h₁enli-, cf. Olr. ro-ic, W rynghu 'to meet' < \*pro-h₁(e]nli·. The initial \*b < PIE \*p would then have to be explained by Verner's law.

\*brinka· m. 'edge' - ON brekka f. 'slope' ( < \*brinkon-), ME brinke, brenke 'seashore; rim, edge; margin, end, boundary', E brink, MLG brink m. 'hill, edge, shore', MDu. brine m. 'edge, grass verge; stretch of grass; market', Du. brink c. 'village square, green' ⇒ \*bhrong-o· (IE) - ToA prank, ToB prenke 'island' < \*bhrong-o-. A Germanic-Tocharian isogloss.

\*brinnan· s.v. 'to burn' - Go. brinnan s.v. 'id.', ON brinna, brenna s.v. 'id.', Far. brenna s.v. 'id.', Elfd. brinna, pres. brio s.v. 'id.', OE beornan s.v. 'id.', E to burn, OFri. berna w.v. 'id.', OS brinnan s.v. 'id.', OHG brinnan s.v. 'id.' ⇒

\*bhre-n-y.h₁-e- (IE) - Olr. do-bruinn 'to flow, trickle' < \*bhru-n-hi-ie-; Lat. ferveo, -ere 'to be hot; to boil' < \* bheryhi-e-. An n-present to the root \*bhreuh₁- 'to boil, brew'. See also \*brewwan-, \*brunnan- and \*beura-.

\*broan- s.v. 'to singe' - MDu. broeyen w.v. 'id.', Du. broeien w.v. 'to be brewing', MHG briiejen w.v. 'to singe', G briihen w.v. 'to boil, brew' ⇒ \*bhr6hi-e- (GM). An a-grade intensive verb related to \*brean- (q.v.). Also cf. OE brod f. 'brood', MHG bruot f. 'heat; incubation' < \*brodi-.

\*brok- f. 'loin-cloth, breeches; rump(?)' - ON brok f. 'leg of a pair of breeches', pl. brrekr 'breeches', Far. br6k f. 'id.', pl. brekur 'pants', Elfd. bruok, pl. bryoker f. 'shorts; diapers', OE broc f. 'behind, breech', pl. 'breeches', E breech(es), OFri. brek f. 'pants', Du. broek c. 'id.', OHG bruoh f. 'id.' (GM?) - To Lat. suffrago, -inis n. 'joint in the hind leg of a quadruped; sucker shoot' < \*-bhrg-en-? Etymology disputed. Within Germanic, the word can have been derived from an a-grade intensive to \*brekan-, viz. OE bracan s.v. 'to break, bruise' < \*brakan-, but only if the original meaning was 'behind' (i.e. "area where the legs split"). This meaning does allow for the well-known etymological connection with Lat. sujfrago, but the exact derivation of this (late) Latin word is problematic (cf. De Vaan 2008: 597-8). The position of Gaul. \*braca, implied by Latin bracae, braccae (for which see \*brakkon-) is unclear: it may have been borrowed from Germanic in view of the root-final \*k, but borrowing in the opposite direction has, too, been suggested (Griepentrog 1995: 89). The inflection of the Germanic word as a root noun could be a confirmation of that, as old loanwords were absorbed by this category more frequently (cf. \*arwit- 'pea').

\*broka- 1 n. 'spot, speckle(?)' - Nw. brok m. 'young (speckled) salmon', Sw. dial. brok 'spot; spotted animal' ⇒ \*bhroh₁9-o- (IE) - Skt. bhrtljati 'to shine, to beam, to sparkle', YAv. brazaiti 'to shine' < \*bhrehi,9-e-; Lith. brdksti (brdksta) 'to dawn' < \*bhreh₁g-ske-; OCS pro-brezgo, Ru. dial. brezg, Sin. br˫sk m. 'dawn' < \*bhreh₁g-sk-o-. A Nordic word related to \*berhta-. Also cf. OSw. brokoter (Sw. brokig), Da. broget adj. 'variegated' < \*brok-uhta-.
\*broka- 2 m. 'swamp' - OE broc m. 'id.', E brook, MLG brok n. 'wet pasture', MDu. broec m. 'marsh', Du. broek n. 'id.', OHG bruoh n. 'swamp', MHG bruoch n. 'id.' (DRV).

\*broper- m. 'brother' - Go. bropar m. 'id.', ON br6oir, pl. brrear m. 'id.', Far. br6oir, pl. breaur m. 'id.', Elfd. bruoaer, pl. bryoarer m. 'id.', OE brooor m. 'id.', E brother, OFri. brother m. 'id.', OS brothar m. 'id.', Du. broer c. 'id.', OHG bruodar m. 'id.', G Bruder m. 'id.' ⇒ \*bhreh₂-ter- (IE) - ToA pracar, ToB procer 'brother', Skt bhrdtar-, Av. bratar- m. 'id.', Arm. efbayr 'id.', Gr. Att. q>ptrn1p, Dor. q>paTJ'ip m. 'member of a brotherhood', Phryg. ,pan:pE dat.sg. 'brother', Latfrater m. 'id.', Olr. brathair, W brawd m. 'id.', OPru. briiti, brote, Lith. br6lis, Latv. briilis 'id.', OCS bratn, bratb m. 'id.'. The Indo-European word for 'brother'.

\*brii- f. 'bridge' - ON bru f. 'id.', Far. brugv f. 'bridge; quay; heap, pile; eyebrow', Nw. bru f. 'bridge', dial. 'edge; eyebrow', OE bru f. 'eyebrow', E brow ⇒ \*h₃bhruH- (IE) - ToB piirwci-ne du. 'eyebrow', Skt bhni- f. '(eye)brow', YAv. bruuatbiiqm datdu. 'id.', MP brug 'id.', Oss. <£rfyg / ærfug 'id.', Gr. ocppu<; f. 'id.'; Lith. bruvls f. 'id.'; OCS bnvb f. 'id.'. A zero-grade root noun, perhaps originally the same word as \*brewo(q.v.). See also \*bru[w)i- \*brujjo-.

\*briidi· f. 'bride' - Go. brups f. 'id.', ON bruar f. 'id.', Far. bruour f. 'id.', Elfd. brauoe f. 'id.', OE bryd f. 'id.', E bride, OFri. breid f. 'id.', OS briid f. 'id.', Du. bruid c. 'id.', OHG brut f. 'id.', G Braut f. 'id.' (GM). No certain etymology. The reconstruction as \*mruH-tf- "who is spoken for" (Hirt 1897: 234) is based on the erroneous derivation of Skt braviti, bruvanti 'to say' from PIE \*mr[e)uH- rather than \*ml[e)uH-, cf. Ru. m6/vit'. Cz. mluviti 'to say'. I am tempted to assume a correlation with ON bruoa 'bundle of flax', Icel. bruoa f. 'doll, puppet' < \*brudon- and Nw. brugde f. 'bundle, tuft' < \*bruwwVþon-, both from a Pre-Gm. root \*bhruH-. The ti-suffix can then be compared to the one of \*magapi- 'girl' ( q.v.).

\*bruki- m./f. 'breach' - OE bryce f. 'id.', E breach, OFri. breke m./f. 'id.', MDu. broke f. 'id.', Du. breuk c. 'id.', OHG bruh m. 'id.', G Bruch m. 'id.' (DRV). An i-stem derived from \*brekan- (q.v.).

\*bruk(k)on- w.v. 'to break, crumble' - Nw. broka w.v. 'to break, bite, tear', MDu. brocken, broken w.v. 'to bend, break', MHG er-brochen w.v. 'to crush, squash' (WEUR) - Lat frango, -ere 'to break' < \*bhrg-neh₂- ( < \*frag-n- with Thurneysen's law).
\*brummon- w.v. 'to drone, hum' - Du. brommen w.v. 'id.', MHG brummen w.v. 'id.', G brummen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*bhrm-neh₂- (IE). The iterative to \*breman- \*brimman- (q.v.).

\*brunjon- f. 'breastplate' - Go. brunjo f. 'id.', ON brynja f. 'coat of mail', OE byrne f. 'id.', OS brunnia f. 'id.', OHG brunja, brunna f. 'id.', G Briinne f. 'id.' (LW). Suspected to be a loanword from Celtic, perhaps from a pre-form of Oir. bruinne m. 'breast' < \*bhrus-n-io-, for which see \*breusta-.

\*brunna(n)- m. 'well, spring' - Go. brunna m. 'id.', ON brunnr m. 'id.', OE brunna, burna m. 'id.', OS brunno m. 'id.', Du. bron c. 'id.', OHG brunno m. 'id.', G Brunnen m. 'id.' ⇒ \*bhreh₁u-r/n- (IE) - Gr. cppfop, -croc; n. 'well', Arm. afbewr, afbiwr, gen. afber 'fountain, spring' < \*bhreh₁u-r, \*bhrh₁u-n(t)-6s. An n-stem continuing a PIE heteroclitic nom. \*bhreh₁u-r-, gen. \*bhrh₁u-en-s / \*bhrh₁u-n-6s. The second genitive is postulated on the basis of the PGm. root \*brun-, which developed from \*bhruhi-n-6s by Dybo's law of pretonic shortening. The geminated root \*brunn- may have arisen by the generalization of the obi. stem \*brun- and the subsequent addition of a secondary n-suffix. See also \*brewwan-.

\*bruska- m. 'shrub' - Nw. brusk m. 'shrub, undergrowth' (NEUR). E brush < ME brusche was adopted from OFr. brosse, broce, broche, which in turn must be a loanword from WGm. \*bruska-. Thus, the PGm. origin of the word seems firmly established. Outside Germanic, the only known potential cognate is Lith. bruzgai m.pl. 'underbrush' (Torp 1909: 282), but the exact derivation of this word is uncertain: from \*bhrusgo-?

\*brust- f. 'breast, chest' - Go. brusts f. 'id.', OFri. brust, burst n. 'id.', OS brust f. 'id.', MLG borst f. 'id.', Du. borst c. 'id.', OHG brust f. 'id.', G Brust f. 'id.' ⇒ \*bhrus-sth₂-o- (EUR). A root noun closely related to \*breusta- (q.v.) with the same meaning. In the older literature, a link with MHG briustern 'to swell up' is usually assumed, but this is probably a frequentative to a strong verb \*breusan- (cf. OE briesan w.v. 'to break' < \*brausjan-) through a meaning 'to break open, to bud'.
\*brustu- m. 'fissure' - OHG brust m. 'burst, rip', MHG brust m. 'id.' (DRV).

\*brut(t)on- w.v. 'to bud' - MHG brozzen w.v. 'id.', G Als. brossen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*bhrd-neh₂- (WEUR) - To Lat [rems, -ondis f. 'foliage, leaves' < \*bhrod-ni(with Thurneysen's law). The iterative to \*breutan- 'to break, bud' (q.v.), originally conjugated as 3sg. \*bruttopi, 3pl. \*brutunanpi. The Pre-Gm. root seems to have been \*bhrud-, but in view of the potential Latin cognate, the u is likely to have arisen secondarily in an iterative derived from a strong verb \*bretan- ( < \*bhred-e-). The resulting \*brut(t)on- may in turn have given rise to \*breutanby back-formation.

\*bru(w)i- - \*brujjo- f. 'bridge' - ON bryggja f. 'pier, quay; bridge', Far. bryggja f. 'wharf, quay; bridge of a ship', Elfd. bryddja f. 'dock', OE brycg, bricg f. 'bridge', E bridge, OFri. bregge f. 'bridge; arch', OS bruggia f. 'bridge', Du. brug c. 'id.', OHG brucka f. 'id.', G Briicke f. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₃bhruH-ihi- (IE). An old ih₂-stem related to the root noun \*bru- (q.v.). The original paradigm \*h₃bhruH-fh₂, gen. \*h₃bhruH-ieh₂-s regularly developed into PGm. \*bru(w)f, gen. \*brujjoz under the influence of Dybo's law (pretonic shortening) and Holtzmann's law (pretonic gemination of \*-j- and \*-!,'·). The genitive root was generalized in the NGm. paradigm, thus giving rise to ON bryggja < \*brujjon-. In WGm., the \*w that arose in the hiatus of the nom. \*bruf became velarized (cf. OHG jugund 'youth' < \*juwunpi-). The resulting root \*brug- then spread to the genitive, thus giving rise to the required stem \*brugjo- (Kroonen 2011b: 158-159).

\*bruzda- m. 'spike' - ON broddr m. 'id.', Far. broddur m. 'thorn, prickle; spike', Elfd. brudd m. 'shoot, sprout', OE brord m. 'point; grass shoot', OHG brort m. 'spear; edge' (WEUR). A zero-grade variant of \*brazda- ( q.v.).

\*buda- n. 'offer' - ON boa n. 'id.', OE bod n. 'command; message', OFri. bod n. 'command; announcement', Du. bod n. 'bid', OHG bot n. 'authoritative pronouncement' (ORV). Derived from the strong verb \*beudan- (q.v.). Also cf. OE ge-bod, OS gi-bod, Du. ge-bod, G Ge-bot n. 'command, commandment' from the perfective \*ga-beudan-.

\*budan- m. 'messenger' - ON boiJi m. 'id.', OE boda m. 'id.', OFri. boda m. 'id.', OS bodo m. 'id.', Du. bode c. 'id.', OHG boto m. 'id.', G Bote m. 'id.' (DRV). Derived from the strong verb \*beudan- (q.v.).

\*budman- - \*buttman- m. 'bottom' - ON botn m. 'id.', Far. botnur m. 'id.', Elfd. buottn m. 'id.', OE botm m. 'id.', E bottom, OS bodom m. 'id.', Du. bodem c. 'id.', OHG bodam m. 'id.', G Boden m. 'id.' ⇒ \*bhudh-men- (IE) - Gr. 1IDBμJ1v m. 'bottom, depth, root' < \*bhudh-men-; Skt. budhnci- m. 'bottom', Lat.[undus m. 'id.', Mir. bond 'foot sole' < \*bhudh-n6-. An old hysterokinetic mn-stem nom. \*bhudh-men, gen. \*bhudh-mn-6s. In the genitive, the m was dissimilated at the Proto-Indo-European stage, which resulted in Skt. budhna-, Lat. [undus (with Thurneysen's law), and PGm. \*buttaz (Kluge 1884; Kroonen 2006). The resulting paradigm \*budme, \*buttaz gave rise to multiple stem variants, i.e. OS bodom < \*budma-, OE botm < \*buttma- and ON botn < \*buttna-. The PIE root \*bhudh- itself seems to have been metathesized from \*dhubh-, for which cf. \*deupa- 'deep'.

\*bugan- m. 'bow' - Go. Crim. boga 'id.', ON bogi m. 'id.', Elfd. bugi m. 'id.', OE boga m. 'id.', E bow, OFri. boga m. 'id.', OS bogo m. 'id.', Du. boog c. 'id.', OHG bogo m. 'id.', G Bogen m. 'id.' (DRV). An n-stem derived from \*beugan- - \*bugan- (q.v.).

\*bugjan- w.v. 'to buy' - Go. bugjan w.v. 'id.', ON byggja w.v. 'to get married; to lend out, let out', OE bycgan w.v. 'to buy', E to buy, OS buggian w.v. 'to buy, pay' ⇒ \*bhugh-ie- (GM). No clear etymology (cf. Lehmann 1986: 84). In spite of the formal similarities, probably unrelated to \*beugan- 'to bend'.

\*bug6n· w.v. 'to brag(?)' - OE bogian, boian w.v. 'to boast', OFri. biigia w.v. 'id.', MDu. bogen w.v. 'id.', Du. bogen op w.v. 'id.' (GM). No etymology: perhaps related to Nw. bauka w.v. 'to plow through, rush forth, roar', Da. bege w.v. 'to roar' < \*baukon- ( < Pre-Gm. \*baukk-?). The connection with Olr. bocaid 'to move, shake, brandish', refl. 'to boast' < \*bukka· is uncertain, the origin of this verb being obscure as well.

\*bukka(n)- m. 'billy-goat' - ON bokkr, bukkr m. 'id.', bokki m. 'fellow', Far. bukkur m. 'billy-goat', Elfd. bukk m. 'id.', OE bucc m. 'roebuck', bucca m. 'billy-goat', E buck, MDu. boc m. 'id.', Du. bok c. 'id.', OHG bock m. 'id.', G Bock m. 'id.' ⇒ \*bhug-on- (IE) -Av. buza- m. 'goat, buck', P buz 'id.' < \*bhuy-o-. Most of the evidence points to a thematic form \*bukka-, but in view of the gemination of the final consonant it seems likely that the word was originally inflected as an n-stem, viz. \*buko, gen. \*bukkaz < \*bhug-on, \*bhug-n-6s. Olr. boc, W bwch m. 'billy-goat' must in view of the geminate have been borrowed from Germanic.

\*bukk/gon- w.v. 'to bend' - Far. boga, buga, bukka w.v. 'to bend, curve', Nw. boga w.v. 'to bend, swing', bukka w.v. 'to nod', OFri. bukkia w.v. 'to bend down', MHG bocken w.v. 'to alight' ⇒ \*bhugh-neh₂- (IE). The iterative to \*beugan- - \*biigan- (q.v.). Also cf. Swi. Visp. bikku w.v. 'to bend, turn' < \*bugjan-.

\*bulgjan- - \*bulkjan- w.v. 'to bellow' - OE bylgian w.v. 'id.', E to bellow, MDu. bu/gen w.v. 'to cough, rattle', Du. bu/ken w.v. 'to bellow' ⇒ \*bh/Cglh-ie(GM). Related to OE bealcan w.v. 'to utter; to belch', MDu. balken w.v. 'to bellow', Du. balken w.v. 'to bray, hee-haw' < \*balkon- and MDu. be/ken w.v. 'to bellow', E to belch < \*balkjan-. The g of OE bylgian and MDu. bu/gen must be old, which implies that the k of the other forms arose under the influence of a related iterative \*bulkon- < \*bh/Cglh-neh₂-, cf. MDu. bolken w.v. 'to bellow'. Further related to the cluster of \*balgi- in the sense of 'belly'?

\*bulgjon- f. 'wave; bag' - ON bylgja f. 'wave', Far. bylgja f. 'id.', E billow, MLG bulge f. 'wave; hide', MHG bulge f. 'wave; leather bag' (ORV). A zero-grade formation (quasi PIE \*bh/gh-ieh₂-) to \*belgan- 'to swell' and \*balgi- 'skin bag'. The word was taken over by the Romans, cf. Lat bulga 'leather knapsack', and it seems likely that the continuant OFr. boulge, bouge was the source of ME bulge 'leather bag, wallet; hump', E bulge. As the meaning 'protuberance' is reminiscent of \*belgan-, however, it is possible that bulge at least partly continues a native word, i.e. OE \*bylge. E billow is usually taken to be a Norse loanword. Further cf. MDu. bu/gen w.v. 'to swell up' < \*bulgjan- and the derived bulge m. 'blister, tumor; entrails of a dead animal' < \*bulgjan-.

\*bullan- 'ball' - ON bolli m. 'cup', Far. bolli m. 'cup, bowl; head; ball', OE bolla m. 'bowl', OFri. bolla m. 'type of (round?) bread', MDu. bolle m. 'sphere, round object', Du. bol c. 'id.', OHG bolla f. 'bud; bowl' (EUR). Ablauting with \*ballan- (q.v.).

\*bul(l)an- m. 'bull' - ON boli m. 'id.', OE bu/a m. 'id.', E bull, MLG bulle m. 'id.', MDu. bul(le) m. 'id.', Du. bu/ c. 'id.' ⇒ \*bh/-en- (EUR? /ORV?) - Gr. cpaAA.6c; m. 'penis' < \*bh/-no-; Latfollis m. 'bag, sack; ball, testicle' < \*bho/-ni-; Oir. ball m. 'member, penis', W balleg 'sack, purse' < \*bh/-no-. An n-stem alternating between \*bu/an- and \*bullan-, thus pointing to a paradigm with consonant gradation, nom. \*bu/6, gen. \*bullaz. Analogically to other hysterokinetic male animal names, e.g. \*uhsan- 'ox' and \*urzan'capercaillie', the Pre-Germanic paradigm may be reconstructed as \*bh/-Ĭn, \*bhl-n-6s, assuming that the vocalized I was generalized throughout the paradigm. The formation seems to have been derived from a PIE word for

\*bullon- w.v. 'to roar, howl' - Icel. bulla w.v. 'to bubble; to talk nonsense', Far. bu//a w.v. 'to gush, bubble; to chatter, babble', MDu. bullen w.v. 'to bubble up', OHG bullon w.v. 'to howl, bark, roar', MHG bullen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*bhlhrneh₂- (EUR) - Lat.jleo,jlere 'to cry, weep', Latv. blet (bleju) 'to bleat'; RuCS blejati, Ru. b/ejat' 'to bleat, dial. to cry' < \*bhleh₁-ie-. An iterative synchronically belonging to \*bel/an- (q.v.). This verb may be . secondary, however, replacing a more primitive \*blejan-.

\*bulstra(n)- m. 'bolster, pillow' - ON bolstr m. 'id.', OE bolster n. 'id.', Du. bolster 'husk', OHG bolstar m. 'cushion', G Polster m. 'id.' ⇒ \*bho/gh-stro(DRV?). A formation created to the same root as found in \*balgi- ( q.v.).

\*bulton- w.v. 'to roll, turn over' - Far. b6lta w.v. 'to turn over, overturn, tumble', G bolzen w.v. 'to kick around (a ball)' ⇒ \*bhld-neh₂- (NEUR) - Formally identical to Lith. bildinti 'to rumble' < \*bhld-neh₂-; further cf. Lith. Misti (beldiiu) 'to knock, poke' < \*bhe/d-ie- and Lith. b<ildyti (balda) 'to rumble' < \*bhold-eie-. An old iterative formation closely related to the o-grade variant G balzen w.v. 'to croon; to perform a courtship display' < \*balton- < \*bhold-neh₂·, which is semantically close to Nw. bolt m. 'male cat or bird; bragger', Bolze m. 'male cat' < \*bulta(n)-. There is evidence for a meaning 'to kick over, make roll', cf. ON bolti m. 'ball', Far. boltur, b6ltur m. 'id.' < \*bulta(n)-, but this may have developed out of an older 'to hit, bump', cf. OE bolt m. 'bolt, arrow', MLG bolte(n) m. 'id.', Du. bout c. 'peg', OHG bolz m. 'bullet, bolt, peg', G Bolz(en) m. 'cross-bow bolt' < \*bulta(n)- and MLG ane-belte, MDu. ane-belt, aen-belt, Du. aambeeld n. 'anvil' < \*ana-baltja-.
\*bunda- m. 'binding' - OS gi-bund n. 'bundle', Du. bond c. 'league', MHG bunt m./n. 'fetter; bundle', G Bund m. 'league; bundle' (DRV). A zero-grade neuter derived from \*bindan- ( q.v.).

\*biira- n. 'cabin, hut' - ON bUr n. 'women's apartment; pantry; storehouse', OE bur m. 'hut, chamber', E bower, OS bur n. 'chamber, dwelling', OHG bur m. 'dwelling; cage', G Bauer m. '(bird)cage' ⇒ \*bhuh₂-ro- (IE). Related to \*bOan- - \*buan- (q.v.).

\*burdi- f. 'birth' - Go. ga-baurps f. 'id.', ON burðr m. 'birth; foetus', OE (ge-)byrd f. 'birth', E birth, OFri. berde f. 'birth; foetus', OS gi-burd f. 'birth, descent', Du. ge-boorte c. 'birth', OHG gi-burt f. 'id.', G Ge-burt f. 'id.' ⇒ \*bhr-tf-

\*burg· f. 'fortified place, town' - Go. baurgs f. 'id.', ON borg f. 'town; citadel; small hill', Far. borg f. 'castle; town', OE burg f. 'city', E borough, OFri. burch, burich m. 'castle; city', OS burg f. 'id.', OHG burg f. 'id.', MHG burc f. 'id.' ⇒ \*bhr(gJh_ (IE). A root noun related to either \*berga- 'mountain' < \*bhergh-o- or \*bergan'to keep safe' < \*bhergh-e-; the formal as well as the semantic properties of the word allow for both of these etymologies.

\*burgjan- w.v. 'to hide(?)' - ON byrgja w.v. 'to close, shut', OE byrian w.v. 'to bury', E to bury ⇒ \*bhrgh-ie- (EUR). A zero-grade ie-present related to \*bergan- (q.v.). Also cf. OE byrga, OS burgio, OHG burigo, G Biirge m. 'surety' < \*burgjan-.
\*buri· m. 'son' - Go. baur m. 'id.', ON poet. burr m. 'id.', OE byre m. 'son, child' (ORV). An i-stem to the strong verb \*beran- 1 (q.v.).

label_gloss: w.v. 'to raise; to come to pass'
gloss: to raise; to come to pass
opening: \*burjan· w.v. 'to raise; to come to pass' - ON byrja w.v. 'to start', OE ge-byrian w.v. 'id.', OFri. bera w.v. 'to raise, collect', OS gi-burian w.v. 'to happen', Du. beuren w.v. 'to collect money', ge-beuren w.v. 'to happen', OHG burren w.v. 'to raise', gi-burren w.v. 'to happen', MHG biirn w.v. 'id.', G ge-bilhren w.v. 'to collect charges' ⇒ \*bhr-ie- (IE). A zero-grade ie-present related to \*beran- 1.
\*burkon· w.v. 'to roar' - Nw. borka w.v. 'to boast', OE borcian w.v. 'to bark' ⇒ \*bhrg-neh₂- (NEUR?). The iterative to \*berkan- (q.v.).

\*burojan- w.v. 'to bore' - ON bora w.v. 'id.', OE borian w.v. 'id.', E to bore, OS boron w.v. 'id.', Du. boren w.v. 'id.', OHG boron w.v. 'id.', G bohren w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*bhr(H)-eh₂-ie- (WEUR) - Identical to Lat. fora, -are 'to bore through, pierce'. A denominal verb derived from \*buro-, cf. OHG bora f. 'bore', or from \*bura- as in OE, MLG bor n. 'id.'. The suffixation of OE byris f.(?) 'graving-iron, file' < \*buriso- is comparable to that of OE lynis m. 'axletree' < \*lunisa- (for which see \*luniz-). Related to \*barjan-?

\*burzda- n. 'board' - Go.fotu-baurd n. 'footstool', ON bora n. 'board, plank, table', Far. bora n. 'id.', OE bord n. 'board, table', OFri. bord m./n.? 'id.', Du. bord n. 'plate, plank' ⇒ \*bhrzdh-o- (WEUR). Related to \*brezda- and \*brazda- (q.v.). There is no trace of the PGm. \*z because it was assimilated by the preceding r in Gothic as well as in NWGm. (after the rhotacism). It was preserved, however, in the secondary zero-grade form \*bruzda-, cf. ON broddr m. 'tip, edge, shoot', OHG brort m. 'point, margin'. Apparently, the original zero grade was remodeled on the basis of the closely related full-grade forms in this formation (cf. Kroonen 2011a: 149ff).

\*butt/don- w.v. 'to shove' - Nw. butta w.v. 'to hit, shove', OFri. del-boddia w.v. 'to knock down', MDu. botten w.v. 'to bump, hit', G Als. butzen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*bhudh-neh₂- (GM). The iterative to \*bautan- (q.v.).

\*buwwen- w.v. 'to dwell; to form, build' - ON byggva, byggja w.v. 'id.', Far. byggja w.v. 'id.', Elfd. byddja w.v. 'id.', OE bogian, bug(i)an w.v. 'to inhabit', OFri. bogian w.v. 'to dwell' ⇒ \*bhuh₂-ehi- (IE). The NGm. forms point to a jan-verb closely related to \*b6an- - \*biian(q.v.). The formation cannot be derived from \*bewwjan- (pace Pokorny IEW: 146-50; Magnusson 1989: 69), as this would have given Elfd. \*\*begga. As a result, the verb can safely be reconstructed as \*buwwjan- or - in view of the WGm. forms - perhaps rather as a stative \*buwwen- < \*bhh₂u-eh₁-. Note that the formation may have gone through an intermediate stage \*biiwĬ-. which by 1) Dybo's law and 2) Holtzmann's law would have regularly produced \*buwwen-.

\*daban- s.v. 'to fit' - Go. ga-daban s.v. 'to happen, be suitable, be appertinent to' ⇒ \*dh6bh-e- (NEUR) - OCS dobro adj. 'good' < \*dhobh-ro-; OCS doba f. 'opportunity' < \*dhobh-eh₂-; Lith. dabnus adj. 'gracious' < \*dhobh-nu-; hardly to Lat. Jaber ' craftsman' in view of the a-vocalism. An o-grade present to a Germanic-Balto-Slavic root \*dhebh- 'to fit'. The appurtenance of Hitt. tape5ni 'in the act' (Puhvel 2008: 64) is less certain.

\*daga- m. 'day' - Go. dags m. 'id.', ON dagr m. 'id.', Far. dagur m. 'id.', Elfd. dag m. 'id.', OE dreg m. 'id.', E day, OFri. dei m. 'id.', OS dag m. 'id.', Du. dag c. 'id.', OHG tac m. 'id.', G Tag m. 'day' ⇒ \*dhogh-o- (IE).

\*daiga- m. 'dough' - Go. daigs m. 'id.', ON deigr m. 'id.', Far. deiggj n. 'id.', Elfd. dieg m. 'id.', OE dah m. 'id.', E dough, MDu. deech n. 'id.', Du. deeg n. 'id.', OHG teig m. 'id.', G Teig m. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhoigh-o- (DRV) - ORu. deia f. 'dough trough' < \*dhoigh-ieh₂-. Derived from the strong verb \*dlgan- (q.v.). Also cf. ON deigr, Far. poet. deigur, MHG teic, MDu. deech adj. 'soft, weak' < \*daiga-.

\*daila- n. 'share, part' - OE d<il n. 'id.', E dole, OFri. de/ m./n. 'id.', Du. dee/ n. 'id.', OHG tei/ m./n. 'id.', G Tei/ m. 'id.' ⇒ \*dh(h₁)oi-lo-(?) (NEUR) - Rues delo m. 'part', Ru. dial. de/ 'division, section', SCr. d'!jel m. 'part, mountain'. A Germanic/Balto-Slavic isogloss. Theoretically, \*daila- can be derived from \*dhhi-oi-lo-, i.e. a derivative of the root \*dheh₁- 'to put', cf. Hitt. dai tianzi 'to put' < \*dhhi-6i-ei, \*dhhi-i-enti, but the formation has no direct parallels in any other IE language. Olr. da/ and OW daul 'share, part' appear to have been adopted from OE da/. Within Germanic, further cf. Go. dai/s f. 'part, share', OE dǣl m. 'id.', E deal < \*daili- and the factitive Go. dailjan, ON deila, OE dǣlan, E to deal, OFri. de/a, OS delian, Du. de/en 'to share' < \*dailjan-.

\*dajjan- w.v. 'to suckle' - Go. daddjan w.v. 'id.', OSw. dreggia w.v. 'id.', OHG taen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhh₁-oi-eie- (IE) - OCS doiti (dojp) 'to breast-feed, nurse', Ru. doft' (doju) 'to milk', SCr. dojiti 'to breast-feed, suckle, give milk' < \*dhhroi-eie-. Both the strong verb \*dfan- and the causative \*dajjan- point to a PIE i-present \*dhh₁-ei-ti, \*dhh₁-i-enti. The causative \*dhhi-oi-eie- developed into PGm. \*dajjan- with a geminate -jj-. This geminate may have arisen as the result of Holtzmann's law (pretonic Verschärfung), but it is conceivable, too, that it arose out of an intermediate form \*dajijan- by loss of the i in the second syllable (cf. Jasanoff 1978a: 85; Rasmussen 1991: 1, 381). OHG taan 'to suckle' may continue the derivationally more primitive \*dean- < \*dhehre-.

\*dala- m. 'valley' - Go. dal(s) m./n. 'id.', ON dalr m. 'id.', Far. dalur m. 'id.', Elfd. dal m. 'id.', OE dre/ n. 'id.', E dale, OS dal n. 'id.', Du. dal n. 'id.', OHG ta/ n. 'id.', G Tai n. 'id.' ⇒ \*dho/h₂-o- (EUR) - Ru. obs. do/ m. 'valley; ditch', SCr. do m. 'valley, dale' < \*dho/h₂-o-; MW do/ f. 'meadow, dale' < \*dho/h₂-eh₂-; Gr. aa>.aμri 'den, lair' < \*dh/h₂-em-eh₂-. A European o-stem that in view of the possible connection with Gr. 6<1A6:μri can be derived from a root \*dhe/h₂-. See also \*d6/j6- and \*du/a-.

\*dampa- m. 'vapor' - ME damp 'fire damp', MDu. damp m. 'vapor, fog', Du. damp c. 'id.', MHG tam pf, dam pf m. 'id.', G Dampf m. 'id.' (DRV). Derived from \*dimban- - \*dimpan- (q.v.). Nw. damb n. 'dust' was created from the same verb, but retained the original consonantism. Also cf. E damp adj. 'humid' < \*dampa-.

\*dangjan- w.v. 'to beat' - ON dengja w.v. 'to beat, to hammer', Far. deingja w.v. 'to beat, strike; to fling, throw; to hang about', Elfd. daindja w.v. 'to hit, bear', OE dencgan w.v. 'to knock', MHG tengen w.v. 'to hit, knock, hammer' ⇒ \*dhongwh.eie- (GM). An iterative-intensive causative to \*dingwan- (q.v.). The delabialization of the velar was triggered by the combination of a preceding round vowel and a following j-suffix (cf. \*sagja- 1 'man, hero' < \*so'f<wH-i6-). Also cf. OHG tanga/ m. 'hammer' < \*dangala-.

\*dank/gon- w.v. 'to beat' - Ice!. danga w.v. 'to beat, hit', Nw. dakka w.v. 'to slap', Sw. dial. danka w.v. 'to hit' ⇒ \*dhongwh.neh₂- (GM). The iterative to \*dingwan- ( q.v.). There are additional traces of a zero-grade variant \*dung/kon-, cf. Ice!., Far., Nw. dunka 'to beat, thump, knock': the n should have been assimilated in the West Norse forms continuing \*dankon- and \*dunkon-, but may have been restored under the influence of the ungeminated formations \*dingwan- and \*dangjan· (q.v.).

\*dapp/bon- 1 w.v. 'to become heavy, sink, deteriorate' - Nw. dabba w.v. 'to fade, decrease, deteriorate (esp. of sight)', dapa w.v. 'to fade; become downcast' ⇒ \*dhobh-nehr (NEUR) - RuCS debelyj adj. 'fat', Ru. debelyj adj. 'plump, corpulent', SCr. debeo adj. 'fat' < \*dhebh./o-; OCS doblb adj. 'strong', Ru. d6blij adj. 'valiant', Latv. dab]s adj. 'strong, lush' < \*dhobh-/.io-. An iterative to a Germanic-Balto-Slavic root \*dhebh- 'heavy'. Also cf. Nw. davre w.v. 'to decrease, to diminish' and perhaps also OHG debben w.v. 'to , depress' (whence OHG tabf f. 'numbness'). The iterative served as the derivational base of \*dapra- (q.v.).

\*dapp/bon- 2 w.v. 'to beat' - Nw. dabba w.v. 'to beat, kick', ME dabben w.v. 'to strike', E to dab, G Pal. tapfen, tappen w.v. 'to grope' ⇒ \*dhh₂bh·neh₂- (NEUR) - Lith. d6bti (d6biu) 'to beat, kill', Latv. dabt 'to strike, beat' < \*dheh₂bh-ie-. An iterative 3sg. \*dappopi, 3pl. \*dabunanpi < \*dhh₂bh-neh₂-ti, \*dhh₂bh-nh₂-enti to the Germanic-Baltic root \*dheh₂bh- (cf. Pokorny IEW: 233). With secondary full-grade and geminate, cf. MHG tiipe, Swi. (App.) t:nppa, Visp. daappo m. 'paw, hand' < \*debban- (Kroonen 2011a: 327). Unrelated to \*tabjan- (q.v.).

\*dapra- adj. 'heavy' - ON dapr adj. 'downcast, sad', Icel. dapur adj. 'sad', sjon-dapur 'blind', Nw. daper adj. 'shy, weak, with young', MLG dapper adj. 'heavy', MDu. dapper 'stout, strong', Du. dapper 'brave', G tapfer adj. 'brave, stout' (ORV) - RuCS debelyj adj. 'fat', Ru. debelyj adj. 'plump, corpulent', SCr. debeo adj. 'fat' < \*dhebh-lo-; OCS doblb adj. 'strong', Ru. d6blij adj. 'valiant', Latv. dabjs adj. 'strong, lush' < \*dhobh-/-io-. This adjective is usually directly compared to the aforementioned forms in Slavic, but the underlying root \*dhebh- can only be reconciled with PGm. \*dapra- by assuming that it was derived from \*dapon-, an analogical variant belonging to the iterative \*dapp/bon- 'to become heavy' (q.v.) < \*dhobh-neh₂-. The original meaning 'heavy' split into both 'depressed' and 'bold'. In Nordic, the root \*dap- further became associated with eyesight deterioration, cf. Icel. depra f. 'weakening eyesight' < \*daprjon- and Icel. depl n. 'drooping of the eyes' < \*daplja-. See also \*dobnan-.

\*darbja- adj. 'hostile' - OFri. derve adj. 'fierce, severe', OS derbi adj. 'powerful, hostile, bad', MLG derve adj. 'bad' (DRV). A ja-stem adjective derived from \*derban- 'to perish' or the pertaining causative \*darbjan- (q.v.).
\*darbjan- w.v. 'to destroy' - MLG derven w.v. 'to shrink, wither, spoil', MHG ver-derben w.v. 'to ruin, kill' ⇒ \*dhorHbh-eie- (NEUR). The causative of\*derban- (q.v.).

\*darjan- w.v. 'to hurt' - OE derian w.v. 'id.', OFri. dera w.v. 'id.', OS derian w.v. 'id.', Du. deren w.v. 'id.', OHG terjen, terren w.v. 'id.', MHG teren, tern w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhorh₃-eie- (NEUR) - Lith. durti (duriu) 'to stab, prick, ache', Latv. duft 'to stab, prick' \*dhrh₃- (with \*h₃ in view of the vocalization \*-r- > \*-ur-). A Germanic-Baltic root Also cf. OE daru f. 'damage, injury', OHG tara f. 'injury' < \*daro- < \*dhorh₃-eh₂-.

\*darran- m. 'hinge' - Nw. darre m. 'hinge; vertebra' ⇒ \*dhor-on- (IE) - Gr. 8mp6<; m. 'pivot of a door, axle of a chariot' < \*dhr-io-. An n-stem \*daro, \*darraz < \*dh6r-on, \*dhr-n-6s. Also cf. ON dþrr m. 'spear' < \*darru-, which continues the original ace.pl. \*darruns < \*dhor-n-Jjs, and with a zero grade of the root - Ice!. dorra f. 'piece of wood, stick' < \*durron-. Together, all these forms point to a hysterokinetic n-stem dh6r-en, gen. \*dhr-n-6s, ace.pl. \*dhor-n-Jjs. This formation is likely to be related to Gr. 8mp6<; < \*dhr-io- (the alternative reconstruction \*dh'!:lr-io- is based on the uncertain connection with l'lupa f. 'door' < \*dhur-h₂-).
\*dauba- adj. 'deaf; sluggish; numb' - Go. daubs adj. 'id.', ON daufr adj. 'id.', Far. deyvur adj. 'id.', Elfd. dov adj. 'id.', OE deaf adj. 'id.', E deaf. OFri. daf adj.

\*dauda- adj. 'dead' - Go. daups adj. 'id.', ON dauor adj. 'id.', OE dead adj. 'id.', E dead, OFri. dath adj. 'id.', OS dod adj. 'id.', Du. dood adj. 'id.', OHG tot adj. 'id.', G tot adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhou{H)-t6- (DRV). The original past participle of \*daujan- 'to die' ( q.v.).

\*daugjan- w.v. 'to endure' - OE ge-dfegan w.v. 'id.', OS a-dogian w.v. 'to withstand' ⇒ \*dhough-eie- (DRV). Given the meaning, the verb probably represents an intensive formation rather than a causative. Related to \*dugan- (q.v.).

\*daujan- s.v. 'to die' - ON deyja s.v. 'id.', Far. doy99ja w.v. 'id.', Elfd. diia s.v. 'id.', OE dfegan s.v. 'id.', E to die, OS doian w.v. 'id.', OHG touwen, tewen w.v. 'id.', MHG touwen, touwen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhouh₂;3-ie- (IE) - Hitt. tubbuszi 'to end' < \*dhuh₂;3-s-; Latjinus n. 'funeral' < \*dhuh₂;3-n-es-(?). An originally strong verb \*daujan-, pret. 3sg. \*doe (ON d6, Elfd. duo), 3pl. \*dounp (ON d6u), ptc. \*dawe/ana- (ON dainn) < \*dhou(H)-ie-. LJV2: 123 reconstructs a causative, but this formation cannot be reconciled with the strong conjugation in Nordic. I therefore start from an o-grade intensive verb with a presentic ie-suffix. Within Germanic, it is cognate with Go. diwans\* adj. 'mortal' < \*dewana-, possibly an old participle to an unattested strong verb \*dewan-, and further to \*dauda- 'dead' and \*daupu- 'death' (q.v.). On the Proto-Indo-European level, the formation has been compared to the root \*dheu- 'to run, rush', cf. Skt. dhclvate, Gr. Ƞfo.> 'to run' and \*dhueh₂- 'to blow, smoke, be misty', see \*du(w Jen- (cf. Seebold 1970: 148; LIV2: I.e.), but I find it preferable to assume a link with Hitt. tubbuszi 'to end' < \*dhuh₂;3-s-. Apparently, Hittite preserved the basic meaning of the word, while in Germanic, it euphemistically became associated with dying.

\*dauma- m. 'vapor' - Far. deymur m. 'strong smell', MDu. doom m. 'mist, haze', OHG tuom m. 'vapor, haze, fume', MHG tuom m. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhouh₂-mo(IE) - Close to Skt. dhiima- m. 'smoke, vapor', Lat jimus m. 'smoke', Lith. dilmai m.pl. 'id.', OCS dymo m. 'id.' < \*dhuh₂-mo-. An o-grade mo-stem to the root \*dhuh₂- 'to smoke'. See also \*dauna- and \*dawwa/o-.
\*dauna- m. 'smell' - ON daunn m. 'id.', OSw. d¢n m./n. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhouh₂-no(1E).

\*daupjan- w.v. 'to dip' - Go. daupjan w.v. 'immerse, baptize', ON deypa w.v. 'to dip; to baptize', Far. doypa w.v. 'to baptize', OFri. depa w.v. 'id.', OS dopian w.v. 'id.', Du. dopen w.v. 'id.', OHG toufen w.v. 'id.', G taufen w.v. 'id.' (DRV). A causative created either to the strong verb \*diiban- (q.v.) or - more probably - directly to the iterative \*dupp/b6n- (q.v.) < \*dhubh-neh₂-. See also \*deupa-.

\*daupu- m. 'death' - Go. daupus m. 'id.', ON dauor, gen. -ar m. 'id.', OE deap m. 'id.', E death, OFri. dath m. 'id.', OS doa m. 'id.', Du. dood c. 'id.', OHG wt m. 'id.', G Tod m. 'id.' ⇒ \*dh6u(H)-tu- (DRV). A tu-stem created to \*daujan- (q.v.).

\*dauzan- m. 'fool' - MLG dore m. 'id.', MHG tore m. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhous-on- (EUR) - For the ablaut, cf. Lith. daiisios f.pl. 'air' < \*dhous-ieh₂-. Related to \*dwesa- ( q.v.), with Schwebeab/aut as in Baltic.

\*dawen· w.v. 'to marvel' - ON da w.v. 'to admire' ⇒ \*dhˬ2u-ehi- (EUR) - Gr. flauμa n. 'wonder, astonishment' < \*dheh₂u-mn- (but cf. the irregular alternants -Owμa and -Owuμa). A Germanic-Greek isogloss. Also cf. ON da n. 'trance, senseless state' < \*dawa- and Far. d<ini m. 'wonder'.

\*dawwa/6· m./f. 'dew' - ON dpgg f. 'id.', Far. degg f. 'id.', Elfd. dagg f. 'id.', OE deaw m. 'id.', E dew, OFri. daw n. 'id.', ODu. dou m. 'id.', Du. dauw c. 'id.', OHG tou m./n. 'id.', G Tau m. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhouh₂-o/eh₂- (IE) - Lith. duja f. 'drizzle, mist' < \*dhuh₂-ieh₂-. An o-stem derived from the PIE root \*dhuh₂- 'to smoke, haze', for which see \*dauma- and \*du(w)en-.

\*dazen- w.v. 'to be numbed(?)' - OE darian w.v. 'to lie motionless, be appalled', ME daren w.v. 'to stay in one place, remain quiet; to lurk; to be motionless, inactive; to hesitate', MDu. be-daren w.v. 'to control oneself, Du. be-daren w.v. 'to settle, calm down', Flem. ver-daren w.v. 'to be appalled' (GM). A stative verb that can theoretically be related to Skt. dhi.r<iyati 'to hold, support' < \*dhor-eie- and Arm. dad a rem 'to settle (of wind)' < \*dho-dhor(thus Pokorny IEW: 252-5). It is preferable, however, to reconstruct the verb as \*dazen- rather than \*daren-, and derive it from \*daza-, an adjective apparently in Verner alternation with MDu. daes 'foolish', Du. daas 'dizzy,

\*dauda- adj. 'dead' - Go. daups adj. 'id.', ON dauor adj. 'id.', OE dead adj. 'id.', E dead, OFri. diith adj. 'id.', OS dod adj. 'id.', Du. dood adj. 'id.', OHG tot adj. 'id.', G tot adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhou(H)-t6- (ORV). The original past participle of \*daujan- 'to die' ( q.v.).

\*daugjan- w.v. 'to endure' - OE ge-diegan w.v. 'id.', OS ii-dogian w.v. 'to withstand' ⇒ \*dhough-eie- (ORV). Given the meaning, the verb probably represents an intensive formation rather than a causative. Related to \*dug an- ( q.v.).

\*daujan- s.v. 'to die' - ON deyja s.v. 'id.', Far. doyggja w.v. 'id.', Elfd. dii.a s.v. 'id.', OE diegan s.v. 'id.', E to die, OS doian w.v. 'id.', OHG touwen, tewen w.v. 'id.', MHG touwen, touwen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhouh₂;3-ie- (IE) - Hitt. tubbu5zi 'to end' < \*dhuh₂;3-s-; Lat fan us n. 'funeral' < \*dhuh₂;3-n-es-(?). An originally strong verb \*daujan-, pret 3sg. \*doe (ON d6, Elfd. duo), 3pl. \*dounp (ON d6u), ptc. \*dawe/ana- (ON dainn) < \*dhou(H)-ie-. LIVZ: 123 reconstructs a causative, but this formation cannot be reconciled with the strong conjugation in Nordic. I therefore start from an o-grade intensive verb with a presentic ie-suffix. Within Germanic, it is cognate with Go. diwans\* adj. 'mortal' < \*dewana-, possibly an old participle to an unattested strong verb \*dewan-, and further to \*dauda- 'dead' and \*dauþu- 'death' (q.v.). On the Proto-Indo-European level, the formation has been compared to the root \*dheu- 'to run, rush', cf. Skt dhcivate, Gr. 8fo.i 'to run' and \*dhueh₂'to blow, smoke, be misty', see \*du(w)en- (cf. Seebold 1970: 148; LIV2: I.e.), but I find it preferable to assume a link with Hitt tubbuszi 'to end' < \*dhuh₂;3-s-. Apparently, Hittite preserved the basic meaning of the word, while in Germanic, it euphemistically became associated with dying.

\*dauma- m. 'vapor' - Far. deymur m. 'strong smell', MDu. doom m. 'mist, haze', OHG tuom m. 'vapor, haze, fume', MHG tuom m. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhouh₂-mo(IE) - Close to Skt. dhiima- m. 'smoke, vapor', Lat [Umus m. 'smoke', Lith. dumai m.pl. 'id.', OCS dym'b m. 'id.' < \*dhuh₂-mo-. An o-grade mo-stem to the root \*dhuh₂- 'to smoke'. See also \*dauna- and \*dawwa/o-.
\*dauna- m. 'smell' - ON daunn m. 'id.', OSw. d¢n m./n. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhouh₂-no(1E).

\*daupjan- w.v. 'to dip' - Go. daupjan w.v. 'immerse, baptize', ON deypa w.v. 'to dip; to baptize', Far. doypa w.v. 'to baptize', OFri. depa w.v. 'id.', OS dopian w.v. 'id.', Du. dopen w.v. 'id.', OHG toufen w.v. 'id.', G taufen w.v. 'id.' (DRV). A causative created either to the strong verb \*diiban- (q.v.) or - more probably - directly to the iterative \*dupp/bOn- (q.v.) < \*dhubh-neh₂-. See also \*deupa-.

\*dauþu- m. 'death' - Go. daupus m. 'id.', ON dauor, gen. -ar m. 'id.', OE deap m. 'id.', E death, OFri. dii.th m. 'id.', OS d6d m. 'id.', Du. dood c. 'id.', OHG tot m. 'id.', G Tod m. 'id.' ⇒ \*dh6u(H)-tu- (DRV). A tu-stem created to \*daujan- (q.v.).

\*dauzan- m. 'fool' - MLG dore m. 'id.', MHG tore m. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhous-on- (EUR) - For the ablaut, cf. Lith. dailsios f.pl. 'air' < \*dhous-ieh₂-. Related to \*dwesa- ( q.v.), with Schwebeablaut as in Baltic.

\*dawen- w.v. 'to marvel' - ON dci w.v. 'to admire' ⇒ \*dhp2u-ehi- (EUR) - Gr. t9ai:iμa n. 'wonder, astonishment' < \*dheh₂u-mn- (but cf. the irregular alternants t9wμa and t9wuμa). A Germanic-Greek isogloss. Also cf. ON dci n. 'trance, senseless state' < \*dawa- and Far. dcini m. 'wonder'.

\*dawwa/o- m./f. 'dew' - ON dpgg f. 'id.', Far. degg f. 'id.', Elfd. dagg f. 'id.', OE deaw m. 'id.', E dew, OFri. daw n. 'id.', ODu. dou m. 'id.', Du. dauw c. 'id.', OHG tou m./n. 'id.', G Tau m. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhouh₂-o/eh₂- (IE) - Lith. duja f. 'drizzle, mist' < \*dhuh₂-ieh₂-. An o-stem derived from the PIE root \*dhuh₂- 'to smoke, haze', for which see \*dauma- and \*du{w)en-.

\*dazen- w.v. 'to be numbed(?)' - OE darian w.v. 'to lie motionless, be appalled', ME daren w.v. 'to stay in one place, remain quiet; to lurk; to be motionless, inactive; to hesitate', MDu. be-daren w.v. 'to control oneself, Du. be-daren w.v. 'to settle, calm down', Flem. ver-daren w.v. 'to be appalled' (GM). A stative verb that can theoretically be related to Skt. dhii.rciyati 'to hold, support' < \*dhor-eie- and Arm. dadarem 'to settle (of wind)' < \*dho-dhor(thus Pokorny IEW: 252-5). It is preferable, however, to reconstruct the verb as \*dazen- rather than \*daren-, and derive it from \*daza-, an adjective apparently in Verner alternation with MDu. daes 'foolish', Du. daas 'dizzy,

\*dean- s.v. 'to suckle' - OHG taan, taen w.v. 'id.', G Cimb. taien w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*dheh₁-e- (IE). An originally strong verb to the PIE root \*dheh₁-. This root is more frequently found in the extension \*dhhrei-, for which cf. \*dian- and \*dajjan-. For a form without this extension, see \*delo-.

\*dedi- f. 'deed, action' - Go. ga-deps f. 'id.', ON dáð f. 'id.', Far. dáð f. 'id.', Elfd. duo f. 'id.', OE diiJd f. 'id.', E deed, OFri. dede f. 'id.', WFri. died c. 'id.', OS dad f. 'id.', Du. daad c. 'id.', MHG tat f. 'deed, action, thing' ⇒ \*dheh₁-ti- (IE) - OCS blago-detb 'good deed' < \*dhehrti-; Gr. '9E<nϗ 'placement', Skt. -dhiti- 'id.' < \*dhhrti-. A ti-stem derived from the root \*dheh₁-, for which see \*don-.

\*de/ban- s.v. 'to dig, delve' - OE de/fan s.v. 'id.', OS bi-de/ban s.v. 'to bury', Du. delven s.v. 'to mine', OHG bi-telban s.v. 'to bury', MHG telben, delben s.v. 'to dig' ⇒ \*dhefbh-e- (NEUR) - RuCS d'b!bsti, d!'bbsti 'to scoop, chisel', SCr. dupsti 'id.' < \*dh/bh-; OPru. dalptan 'drift punch', OCS dlato n. 'chisel', Ru. dolot6 n. 'id.' < \*dhofbh-to-; Latv. dafba f. 'pole' < \*dhofbh-eh₂-. A European root Also cf. MLG delfn. 'grave', OE ge-delfn. 'ditch' < \*delbaand MDu. delve f. 'ditch' < \*delbo-.

\*deli- adj. 'easily to deal with(?)' - ON dcell adj. 'gentle, easy to deal with', Nw. dial. dee! adj. 'easy-going, good to talk to', Elfd. dii.l adj. 'trustworthy' (GM). A vṛddhi-gerundive without any etymology. The connection with OCS delo n. 'work' < \*dheh₁-lo- and the root \*dhehr 'to put, do' in general (cf. Torp 1909: 198) cannot be maintained. The Germanic material rather points to an unattested strong verb \*de/an- < \*dhe/-e-.

\*deli· - \*delja- m. 'dill' - OE dile, dyle m. 'id.', E dill, OS di/Ii m. 'id.', Du. dille c. 'id.', OHG tilli m. 'id.', MHG tille, tiille m. 'id.' (GM). The material offers evidence for both an i-stem (OE dile) and a ja-stem (OS dilli, OHG till1l Perhaps the forms with rounded vowels (OE dyle, MHG tiille) can be adduced to reconstruct an additional ablauting pair \*duli- Ŝ \*dulja-. If so, the original paradigm probably had ablaut of the root, viz. nom. \*deliz, gen. \*duljaz < \*dhef-i-s, \*dhf-i-6s.

\*delo- f. 'nipple' - OE delu f. 'id.', OHG ti/a f. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhehrl-eh₂- (IE) - Gr. th]Alj f. 'breast' < \*dheh₁-l-eh₂- (cf. Lat fe/6, -are 'to suck' < \*dheh₁-/-eh₂-ie-); Olr. def 'nipple' < \*dheh₁-l-6-. A word that by pretonic shortening (Dybo's law) regularly developed from \*dheh₁-l-eh₂-, the exact same formation underlying Gr. flriATJ. Also cf. Sw. dial. de/ < \*de/a-, which, in turn, is formally identical to Olr. de/ < \*dheh₁-l6-. See also \*dfan- and \*dajjan-.

\*denra- m. 'hollow hand' - OHG tenar m., tenara f. 'id.', MHG tener m. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhen-r- (IE) - YAv. danara n. 'handful', Gr. fltvap, -apoc; 'palm of the hand'. One of the Indo-European words for the 'palm of the hand'. Also here belongs OHG dreno m. 'sole of the foot', Lomb. treno 'lower arm' < \*drenan-. Riecke 2004: 2, 251 calls the origin of this word uncertain, but it may have metathesized from \*dhn-er- at an early stage. The implied paradigm would then be nom. \*dhen-r, gen. \*dhn-er-s, possibly continuing an older heteroclitic \*dhen-r, gen. \*dhn-en-s.

\*derba- adj. 'brave' - ON djarfr adj. 'bold, daring', OE deorf adj. 'valiant', OS derl'Ji adj. 'powerful' ⇒ \*dherbh-o- (DRV). Derived from \*derban- (q.v.). Also cf. the factitive ON, Ice!. dirfa w.v. 'to make bold', Nw. dirva-st w.v. 'to venture, dare' < \*derbjan-, ON dirflJ f. 'boldness' < \*derbipo- and Nw. dirna, dyrna w.v. 'to grow strong, recover from illness' < \*derbnjan-.

\*derban- s.v. 'to perish' - OE deorfan s.v. 'to labor, perish', MLG vor-derven s.v. 'to perish', MDu. be-derven s.v. 'to be damaged; to perish', Du. be-derven w.v. 'to spoil; to go bad', MHG ver-derben, -terben s.v. 'to be killed, to die' ⇒ \*dherHbh-e- (NEUR) - Lith. dirbti (dirbu) 'to work', dif'bt 'to walk fast, to toil' < \*dhrHbh-; Lith. darbas m. 'work', Latv. daf'bs m. 'work, deed' < \*dhorHbh-o-. A strong verb to a European root \*dherHbh-. For the semantics, cf. Gr. Kaμvw 'to work' vs. ol Kaμ6vTE<; 'the dead'. See also \*derba- and \*darbjan-.

\*derka- adj. 'dark' - OE deorc adj. 'id.', E dark ⇒ \*dherg-o- (IE) - ToA tirkiir, ToB tarkiir 'cloud' < \*dhrg-ru-; Lith. darga f. 'bad, rainy weather' < \*dhorg-eh₂-; Lith. dergti (dergiu) 'to sleet, make dirty, soil, slander, defile, spoil'; ?Olr. derg adj. 'red' < \*derg-o-. Also cf. OHG terchinen, MHG terken w.v. 'to obscure, soil, defile' < \*darknjan-, possibly a causative-inchoative 'to make go dark'.
\*deuhtra- n. 'grandchild' - MHG diehter, tiehter n. 'id.', G Bav. Tichter n. 'id.' ⇒ \*dheugh₂-tro- (DRV).

\*deupa- adj. 'deep' - Go. diups adj. 'id.', ON djupr adj. 'id.', Far. djupur adj. 'id.', Elfd. diuop adj. 'id.', OE deop adj. 'id.', E deep, OFri. diiip adj. 'id.', OS diop· adj. 'id.', Du. diep adj. 'id.', OHG tiuf adj. 'id.', G tie/ adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*dheubh-no(DRV) - ToA tpiir, ToB tapre adj. 'high' < \*dhubh-ro-; Olr. domain adj. 'deep', W dwfn adj. 'id.' < \*dhubh-ni-; Lith. dubits adj. 'hollow, deep, spacious' < \*dhubh-u-. An adjective closely related to the verbal complex of \*duban- and \*daupjan- (q.v.). Since this formation is derivationally parallel to e.g. \*seuka'ill' (see \*seukan-) and other full-grade adjectives created to strong verbs, it is possible to postulate a verb \*dupan-, a variant of the aforementioned strong verb \*duban-. Both variants can be explained as back-formations to the iterative \*dupp/bon- (q.v.). Alternatively, \*deupa- can be derived from a pre-Gm. formation \*dheubh-no- (with Kluge's law and shortening of geminates in overlong syllables). It would then be derivationally parallel to e.g.

label_gloss: - \*hwitta- 'white'. Note that the PIE root \*dheubh- occurs in metathesized form in \*budman-
opening: \*hwlta- - \*hwitta- 'white'. Note that the PIE root \*dheubh- occurs in metathesized form in \*budman- - \*buttman- 'bottom' (q.v.).

\*deurja- adj. 'precious, valuable, expensive' - ON dyrr adj. 'id.', Far. dyrur adj. 'id.', OE dfere, deore adj. 'id.', E dear, OFri. diure adj. 'id.', OS diuri adj. 'id.', Du. duur adj. 'id.', OHG tiuri adj. 'id.', G teuer adj. 'id.' (GM). No accepted etymology. The adjective looks like a vṛddhi-gerundive "mournable" to MHG be-tiiren w.v. 'to cost, have difficulties with', G be-dauern w.v. 'to regret, be sorry', ME douren 'to grieve, suffer' < \*duren-. It has been assumed that this verb was derived from an unattested PWGm. adjective \*dura-, a hypothetical loanword from Lat durus adj. 'tough, hard'. It is possible too, however, that the verb is etymologically identical to OHG truren, MHG truren, G trauern w.v. 'to mourn, be sad' < \*druren- through dissimilation of the first r. PGm. \*deurja- may then likewise continue dissimilated \*dreurja- or even \*dreuzja-.

\*deuza- n. '(wild) animal' - Go. dius n. 'id.', ON dyr n. 'id.', Far. djor n. 'id.', Elfd. diuor n. 'id.', OE deor n. 'id.', E deer, OFri. diiir, dier n. 'id.', OS dior n. 'id.', ODu. dier n. 'id.', Du. dier n. 'id.', OHG tior n. 'id.', G Tier n. 'id.' ì \*dheus-6- (EUR).
\*dian- s.v. 'to suck' - OSw. dfa s.v. 'id.', MHG tfen w.v. 'to suck, suckle' ì

\*dhh₁-ei-e- (IE) - Skt. dhciyati 'to suck, drink mother's milk', Oss. da!jyn / da!jun 'to suck' < \*dhh₁-eie-; Olr. denaid, W dynu 'id.' < \*dhh₁i-neh₂-. An i-present to the primary PIE root \*dheh₁- 'to suck, suckle', cf. Gr. &jaHm 'to suck', Latv. det 'id.', for which see \*dean-. In Germanic, the i-suffix is further found in e.g. \*dajjan- and Elfd. dise f. 'nipple' < \*dison- < \*dhh₁i-s-.

\*digan- s.v. 'to knead' - Go. digan s.v. 'id.' ì \*dheigh- (IE) - ToA tseka-, ToB tsik- 'to form', Skt. -degdhi 'to smear, anoint', YAv. pairi.daezaiiqn 3pl.caus.subj. 'to embank, construct', Arm. dizanem 'to pile up' < \*dheigh-; Lat Jingo, -ere 'to form, fashion', Oir. dingid 'to knead, form; suppress' < \*dhi-n-gh-e-. A strong verb that in view of the zero grade of Go. digands\* is likely to continue a PIE root present 3sg. \*dheigh-ti, 3pl. \*dhigh-enti (cf. uv2, s.v. \*dheigh-). See also \*daiga- and \*dika-.

\*digra- adj. 'big' - ON digr adj. 'big, stout; thick; deep', Far. digur adj. 'thick, heavy, stout; rich (of milk)', Elfd. diger adj. 'thick, coarse', MDu. deger adv. 'completely', MHG tigere, tiger adv. 'id.' (DRV). An adjective apparently continuing \*dhiCglh-ro-. It may have been derived from \*dfgan- 'to knead' (q.v.), which would imply a more primary meaning 'well-formed'.

\*dika- n. 'dam; ditch' - OE die m. 'dike, ditch', E dike, ditch, OFri. dik m. 'dike; road', OS dik m. 'pond', Du. dijk c. 'dam', MHG tich m. 'pond, swamp, channel', G Teich m. 'pond, pool' ì \*dheigh-n6- (IE) - Gr. Totxoc; m. 'wall', Arm. dez 'heap' < \*dh6igh-o-; SCr. zfd m. 'stone wall', OPru. seydis 'wall' < \*dheigh- (with metathesis). A formation continuing Pre-Gm. \*dfkka- < \*dheigh-n6- (with Kluge's law and geminate shortening in overlong syllables). Also cf. ON, Jee!., Far. dfki n. 'swamp, puddle', Elfd. daitje n. 'dike' < \*dikja-. Related to \*dlgan- (q.v.).

\*dikan- s.v. 'to try(?)' - MLG diken w.v. 'to atone', MDu. diken s.v.(?) 'to try(?)', MHG tichen s./w.v. 'to get started; to try; to deal with; to undergo; to seep' ì \*dheig-e- (IE?) - To Gr. -lhyavw 'to touch, handle, injure, harm' < \*dhi-n-g-nH-e-? No certain etymology. Within Germanic, the verb appears to be closely related to the iterative \*dik(k)on-, cf. Ice!. dika 'to run', Far. dika 'to strike, hit, come at speed', Nw. dika 'to run, wander around; get moving', Sw. dial.
\*dimban- - \*dimpandikka 'to run'. The meaning of especially the Faroese verb could point to an etymological connection with Gr. 1'hyavUl.

\*dimban- - \*dimpan- s.v. 'to fog' - Nw. demba s.v. 'to smoke' (with unclear e), Elfd. dimba s.v. 'id.', MHG dimpfen s.v. 'to smoke, steam' ì \*dhembh-e- (IE). A strong verb continuing PIE \*dhembh-e-: the variant \*dimpan- is secondary, and was influenced by the iterative \*dumpon- (q.v.). The root \*dhembhprobably has a Schwebeablaut variant in \*nebala- < \*dhnebh-o/-eh₂-. See fur- · ther \*dampa- and \*dumbOn-.

\*dimma- adj. 'dark' - ON dimmr adj. 'id.', OE dimm adj. 'id.', E dim, OFri. dim adj. 'id.' ì \*dhem(H)-n6- / \*dhembh-no- (IE? /WEUR?) - To Mlr. deime f. 'darkness' < \*dhem(H)-ieh₂-? An adjective with two possible etymologies. One option is to connect Mir. deime, and reconstruct \*dhem(H)-n6-. The alternative is to reconstruct the adjective as \*dimbna- < \*dhembh-no- (for which cf. \*dimban- - \*dimpan-), something that seems to be supported by OHG timber adj. 'dark' < \*dhembh-ro-. This adjective can also be derived from \*dhem(H)-ro-, however, as this form would result in PGm. \*timbra- as well by epenthesis. This would imply a link with Mir. deime after all.

\*dingwan- s.v. 'to beat' - OSw. diunga s.v. 'id.', ME dingen s.v. 'id.', MHG tinge/en w.v. 'to knock, hammer' ì \*dhengwh-e- (GM). A strong verb, probably of onomatopoetic origin. See also \*dangjan- and \*dank/gon-.

\*dinkwa- adj. 'dark' - ON d!i1kkr / dpkkr adj. 'id.', Ice!. dfiJkkur adj. 'id.', Far. dfiJkkur adj. 'dull; dim, dark', Nw. dokk, dfiJkk adj. 'dark', OSw. dionker, diunker adj. 'id.', OFri. diunk adj. 'dark' ì \*dhengw·o- (IE) - Hitt. dankui- - dankuyai'black, dark' < \*dh(e/o)ngw·(o)i-. ON dfiJkkr may continue either \*dankwja- or \*dinkwa-. The latter option seems preferable in view of OSw. diunker and OFri. diunk, which both show (independent) u-infection of + before labiovelars. However, PGm. \*dankwja- should probably have given Ice!. \*dekkur (with e < ON fiJ, cf. Ice!. dekkja w.v. 'to make dark') rather than dokkur, a form directly pointing to ON dpkkr < \*dankwa- (Heidermanns 1993: 146). Did both forms occur side by side? See also \*dunk/a- - \*dunkra- with a zero grade of the root.

\*disi- f. 'lady, fairy, goddess' - ON dfs f. 'woman, girl; fairy, nymph; goddess', Far. dis f. 'goddess, nymph', Nw. dis f. 'goddess', OE ides f. 'woman', OS idis f. 'wife, woman', OHG itis f. 'woman' (GM).
\*dobi· adj. 'suited, fit' - Go. ga-dobs adj. 'id.', OE ge-defe adj. 'fit; mild' (DRV). A V[ddhi-adjective to the strong verb \*daban- ( q.v.).

\*dobnan- w.v. 'to become dull, numb' - Go. af-dobnan w.v. 'to become numb, hold one's peace' (DRV). An inchoative verb implying an unattested adjective \*doba-. This adjective appears to contain a vṛddhi-variant of the root also found in e.g. Ice!. dafna w.v. 'to grow weak; to become numb'. See further under \*dapra-.

\*dogera- - \*dogena- n. 'a full day, twenty-four hours' - ON dregr, dregn n. 'id.', Far. -dregur n. 'id.' (cf. javn-dregur 'equinox'), Nw. dFJger, dFJgn n. 'id.', Elfd. dyongen n. 'id.', Da. dFJgn n. 'id.', OE dogor, doger n. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhi!Jgh-r/n(IE) - Skt. ahar, gen. ahnas, loc. ahan, ahani n. 'day', OAv. asnqm gen.pl. 'id.'; YAv. asni loc.sg. 'id.'. The PIE word for 'day' was a heteroclitic noun, possibly to be reconstructed as nom. \*dh6gh-r, gen. \*dhegh-n-s, loc. \*dhgh-en-i. The Germanic forms with r, e.g. ON dregr, OE doger, cannot be derived from an s-stem \*dogaz- or \*dogiz- < \*dhogh-e/os- (Pokorny JEW: 7), as it would be artificial to separate them from the semantically identical forms with n (cf. ON dregn). l therefore prefer to reconstruct PGm. \*doger- and \*dogen- (for the i-mutation in dregn and dregr, cf. ON tyrr 'fire' under \*for - \*fun-), and maintain the link with the Indo-European heteroclitic. In early Indo-lranian, this formation lost its initial dental, possibly in the locative \*dhgh-en-i, where the root had a zero grade. See also \*daga-.

\*dojan- w.v. 'to exhaust' - Go. af-dauips ptc. 'exhausted' ⇒ \*dhou(H)-eie(IE) - OCS daviti 'to suffocate', Ru. davit' (davlju) 'to press (upon), suffocate, crush', SCr. daviti 'to suffocate, strangle' < \*dhou(H)-eie-. The verb is often analyzed as a causative to \*daujan- 'to die', but it seems more likely that it was created to the root \*dhueh₂- 'to smoke' (see \*dauma-) in view of the meaning of OCS daviti etc. Note that the root-final \*u was regularly lost after PGm. \*ii.
\*doka- n. 'canvas, cloth' - OFri. dok, dek m. 'id.', MDu. doec m./n. 'id.', Du. doek c./n. 'id.', OHG tuoh m./n. 'id.', G Tuch n. 'id.' (GM).

\*doljo- f. 'depression' - ON drel f. 'small valley', MDu. doele f. 'ditch', OHG tuolla f. 'pit, hollow' (EUR). A lengthened grade formation related to \*dala- 'valley' ( q.v.). With the same vowel, cf. ON drell m. 'valley-dweller' < \*doli-.

\*doma- m. 'verdict, evaluation' - Go. doms m. 'insight, esteem', ON d6mr m. 'opinion, judgment, sentence', OE dom m. 'id.', E doom, OFri. dom m. 'id.', OS dom m. 'id.', Du. doem m. 'doom', OHG tuom m./n. 'judgement, power, situation' ⇒ \*dh6h₁-mo- (DRV). A mo-stem created to the PIE root \*dhehi-, for which see \*don- and \*dedi-. It gave rise to the factitive Go. domjan, ON dcema, OE deman, OFri. dema, OS ii-domian, OHG tuommen w.v. 'to judge, sentence' < \*domjan-.

\*don- irr.v. 'to do' - OE don irr.v. 'id.', E to do, OFri. dwii, dwiin irr.v. 'id.', WFri. dwaan irr.v. 'id.', OS don irr.v. 'id.', Du. daen irr.v. 'id.', OHG tuan irr.v. 'id.', G tun irr.v. 'id.' ì \*dh6h₁-e- (IE). An athematic verb apparently continuing Pre-Gm. 3sg. \*dh6h₁-ti, 3pl. \*dhhi-enti. The a-grade of this verb is only found in Germanic, and may be parallel to that of e.g. \*gangan- - \*gungan- 'to go' (q.v.). In the other IE languages, we find a reduplicated present with e-grade of the root, cf. Skt. dádhāti 'to put, place, make, produce', Gr. Tl-BriμL 'to put, place', Lith. dJti (dedu) 'to lay, put' etc. < 3sg. \*dhi-dhehi-ti, 3pl. \*dhe-dhh₁-enti. See also \*dediand \*doma-.

\*draban- s.v. 'to hew' - Go. ga-draban s.v. 'to chip, hew out' ⇒ \*dhr6bh-e(NEUR) - OCS drabiti (drobljp) 'to crumble, chop, break' < \*dhrobh-eie-. An a-grade present to a Germanic-Slavic root, the source of the iteratives \*drapp/b6n- and \*drupp/b6n- 1. These iteratives, in turn, gave rise to the secondary strong verb \*drepan-.

\*drabiz- n. 'dregs' - ON draf n. 'draff, husks', Far. drav n. 'draff; thin beer; rain', ODu. draf 'draff, Du. draf c./n. 'id.', OHG trebir n.pl. 'id.', .G Treber n.pl. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhrobh-es- (EUR) - Gr. TpEcpw 'to raise, nourish; to curdle, coagulate,

\*dragan- s.v. 'to draw, pull, carry' - ON draga s.v. 'id.', Far. draga w.v. 'id.', Elfd. draga s.v. 'id.', OE dragon s.v. 'id.', E to draw, OFri. drega s.v. 'id.', OS dragon s.v. 'to carry, bring', Du. dragen s.v. 'id.', OHG tragan s.v. 'id.', G tragen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhr6gh-e- (GM). An o-grade intensive verb. No clear etymology. The usually compared Gr. TP£xw 'to run' is more likely to be related to \*pragjan- 'to run' (q.v.). The alternative *comparanda* Ru. dor6ga f. 'road, journey', SCr. draga f. 'valley' < \*dfhlorg-eh₂- and Ru. dor6iiti 'to hollow out' < \*dfhlorg-eie- have roots ending in a voiced (glottalized?) stop, and therefore cannot be related either. The remaining option, the link with Lat traho, -ere 'to pull, haul', is impossible in view of the initial t. See further \*durgi5- and \*druggon-. •dragjo- f. 'dregs' - ON dregg f. 'dregs, lees, yeast', Far. dregg f. 'scum, dregs, draff, sediment' ⇒ \*dhragh-ieh₂- (EUR) - OLith. drages, OPru. dragios f.pl. 'yeast', Latv. dradii f. 'remains of cooked fat', OCS droidbjf/ f.pl. 'yeast' < \*dhragh-ieh₂-; Alb. dra m. 'sediment, dregs; sweepings, dirt' < \*dhragh-o-(?). A Germanic and Balto-Slavic ieh₂-stem. Lat [races f. 'fragments of olive pulp left after pressing' does not belong here, and may have been back-formed to Lat. frangi5 'to break', for which see \*bruk(k)on-.

\*drahtu- m. 'pull' - ON drattr m. 'pull; hesitation', Far. drattur m. 'pulling', ME draht 'id.', E draught, MDu. dracht f. 'carrying; dress', Du. dracht c. 'id.', MHG tracht f. 'id.', G Tracht f. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhrogh-tu- (DRV). A tu-stem created to \*dragon- (q.v.). In West Germanic, the word was remodeled into a ti-stem.

\*draibo- f. 'driving, drift' - ON dreif f. 'scattering', OE driiff. 'driving; flock, herd', E drove (DRV). An 6-stem derived from \*driban- 'to drive, be adrift' (q.v.). Also cf. ON dreifa w.v. 'to scatter, bedabble', which probably continues a causative \*draibjan- < \*dhroibh-eie-.

\*dranga- m. 'post' - ON drangr m. '(detached) pillar of rock', Far. drangi, -ur m. 'high projecting rock (in the sea)', Nw. drange m. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhrongh-o(IE) - Identical to Lith. drafigas m. 'pole', OCS dr<;>g'b m. 'id.', SCr. drug m.

\*drankjan- w.v. 'to let drink; to drench' - Go. dragkjan w.v. 'id.', ON drekkja w.v. 'id.', Elfd. drettja w.v. 'to drench; to ret', OE drencan w.v. 'to drench, make drunk', E to drench, OFri. drenza, drinza w.v. 'to drown', MDu. drenken w.v. 'to soak; to Jet drink', Du. drenken w.v. 'id.', OHG trenken w.v. 'id.', G triinken w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhro-n-g-eie- (NEUR). The causative to \*drinkan- (q.v.).
