\*drapp/bOn- w.v. 'to hit' - Nw. drabba w.v. 'to give a blow', OSw. drabba w.v. 'to hit, meet', ODa. drabe w.v. 'to kill' ⇒ \*dhrobh-neh₂- (NEUR). An o-grade iterative to \*draban- (q.v.).

\*drauga- m. 'ghost' - ON draugr: m. 'id.', Far. poet. dreygur m. 'corpse; ghost, apparition' ⇒ \*dhrough-o- (IE) - Mir. aur-ddrach 'ghost' < \*dhrough-o-; YAv. draoya- m. 'lie', OP drauga- m. 'lie, treason, felony', MP dro 'lie, deceit' < \*dhrough-o-. Related to \*dreugan- (q.v.).

\*draugja- - \*driigja- - \*drukknu- adj. 'dry' - OE dryge adj. 'id.', E dry, OFri. druch adj. 'id.', WFri. droech adj. 'id.', EFri. druuch adj. 'id.', NFri. druuge adj. 'id.', OS drukno, drokno adj. 'id.', MLG droge adj. 'dry, infertile', MDu. droeghe adj. 'id.', Du. droog adj. 'id.', OHG trockan, truckan adj. 'id.', G trocken adj. 'id.' (IE?) - Perhaps related to to Skt. druhi/a- adj. 'rough' < \*dhrugh-. An adjective whose PGm. reconstruction is problematic because of the co-occurrence of at least four different formations, i.e. 1) \*drogja- (OE dryge); 2) \*druga- (OFri. droch); 3) \*draugja- (MDu. droge); and 4) \*drukkna- (OS drocno, OHG trockan). The correlation between the different formations is unclear. The proto-form \*drukkna- contains a geminated root \*drukk- from \*dhrugh-n-, and in view of the alternating forms ending in \*g, Lilhr 1988: 336-7 posited an n-stem adjective nom. \*drugo, gen. \*drukkaz < \*dhrugh-on, \*dhrugh-n-6s. The ablaut of \*drugja- and \*draugja- still remains unexplained, however. For the long vowel of OE dryge, also cf. OE drugian w.v. 'to be dry' and OE drugoo m. 'drought' < \*drugopa-.

\*drauma- m. 'dream' - ON draumr m. 'id.', Far. dreymur adj. 'id.', OE dream m. 'id.', E dream, OFri. dram m. 'id.', OS dram m. 'id.', Du. droom c. 'id.', OHG troum m. 'id.', G Traum m. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhrough-mo- (DRV).

\*draupjan- w.v. 'to make drip' - ON dreypa w.v. 'id.', OE driepan w.v. 'id.', OHG troufen w.v. 'id.', MHG troufen, troufen w.v. 'id.' (DRV). The causative to the secondary strong verb \*dreupan- \*driipan- ( q.v.).
\*drauzjan- w.v. 'to make fall' - MHG troren w.v. 'to drip, ooze, shed' ⇒ \*dhrouHs-eie- (NEUR). The causative to \*dreusan- (q.v.).

\*dreko- f. 'streak' - ON drcik f. 'id.', Nw. drdk, pl. drreker f. 'id.' (DRV?). A North Germanic 6-stem (the root noun inflection of Nw. drdk is secondary). It is unrelated to Skt dhr&ji-, dhrajf- f. 'draught of air, wind', which continues \*dhrog-i- with \*o rather than \*e. In all likelihood, \*dreko- was derived from \*dragan- 'to draw, pull' ( q.v.) in the same way that OFri. slek m. 'blow' < \*sleka- was created to \*slahan- 'to hit' (q.v.). The underlying form must then be reconstructed with a Pre-Gm. geminate: \*drekko-.

\*drenan- m. 'drone' - OS dreno m. 'id.', Du. Limb. drene c. 'id.', OHG treno m. 'id.', MHG trene, tren m. 'id.', G Swi. Jaun trena m. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhr-en- (EUR) - Gr. uvBpftVl'], n:v-Bpriowv 'wasp', Lac. -Bpwvaú 'drone' < \*dhen-dhr-en- / \*dhr-on-(?); Lith. trii.nas m. 'drone'; Latv. trans m. 'id.' < \*trono-; SCS trutb 'wasp', SCr. triit 'drone' < \*tronto-; Ru. truten' m. 'drone, parasite' < \*tront-nio-. A word with a complicated prehistory. The aforementioned forms point to a proto-form \*drenan-, which can easily have been created on the basis of a hysterokinetic n-stem \*dhr-Ĭn, acc. \*dhr-en-m. The origin of MLG drone, drane, MDu. darne, dorne, Du. dar, E drone < \*drunan- is not so straightforward, however, although they can perhaps be explained as (secondary?) zero-grades. Even more problematic are OS gl. dran and OE gl. dran, dram, which continue either \*dran- (< \*dhr-on-) or \*dren- < \*dhr-en-. Note, however, that none of these forms would result in E drone. Can there furthermore be a link with OE dora m. 'bumble-bee' < \*duran-? There are many remaining problems, not least the initial consonant of the Balto-Slavic forms.

\*drepan- s.v. 'to hit' - ON drepa s.v. 'to stike, beat, knock; to kill, slay', OE drepan s.v. 'to strike', MLG drepen s.v. 'to hit', MDu. drepen s./w.v. 'to hit, strike', OHG treffan s.v. 'to hit', G treffen s.v. 'to hit, touch' (DRV). A back-formation to the iterative \*drupp/bOn- 1, a zero-grade variant of \*drapp/bOn- (q.v.). As both iteratives were derived from the strong verb \*draban- 'to hew' < \*dhr6bh-e-, the strong verb \*drepan- cannot be used to

\*dreuga- adj. 'enduring' - ON drjugr adj. 'substantial, lasting, ample', Far. drugvur adj. 'long-lasting, adequate', Elfd. driuog adj. 'laborious; disparaging', WFri. dreech adj. 'strong, enduring, long-lasting', NFri. F-A drech adj. 'enduring; stocky, fat' ⇒ \*dhreugh-o- (DRV). An adjective derived from the strong verb \*dreugan- 2 (q.v.). Also cf. ON drjgja w.v. 'to commit, perpetrate, carry out; to make to go far' < \*dreugjan-.
\*dreugan- 1 s.v. 'to mislead' - MHG triegen s.v. 'to deceive, betray' ⇒

\*dhreugh-e- (IE) - Skt. druhyati 'to deceit', Av. drufoiti 'to lie' < \*dhrugh-ie-; Skt dr6ha- m. 'insult, betrayal' < \*dhrough-o-. A strong verb with a clear IE etymology. See also \*drauga- and \*drauma-.

\*dreugan- 2 s.v. 'to do a duty(?)' - Go. driugan s.v. 'id.', OE dreogan s.v. 'to do, work; to endure, suffer', ME drlen s.v. 'to perform; to experience; to put up with, endure' ⇒ \*dhreugh-e- (NEUR) - Lith. draii.gas m. 'friend', OCS drug'b m. 'id.' < \*dhrough-o-. A strong verb with a root that in view of the Baltic forms (formally close to OE ge-dreag 'host' < \*ga-drauga-) may have designated an activity associated with someone's military or clan affiliation. See also \*dreuga- and \*druhti-.

\*dreupan- - \*driipan- s.v. 'to droop; to drip' - ON drjupa s.v. 'to drip, drop; to droop with the head', OE dreopan s.v. 'to drip', E to drip, OFri. driapa s.v. 'id.', OS driopan s.v. 'id.', Du. druipen s.v. 'to drip; to ooze', OHG triufan s.v. 'id.', G triefen s.v. 'id.' (DRY). A back-formation to the iterative \*drupp/bOn- 2 < \*dhrbh-neh₂-. There is no reason to postulate a PIE \*dhreub- (pace Pokorny IEW: 274-5) on the basis of the Germanic evidence, as both the radical \*u and the root-final \*p arose as a result of intra-Gm. derivational processes: in reality, the Pre-Gm. root was \*dhrebh-. This means that the often mentioned connection with Olr. drucht m. 'dew', allegedly from \*dhriJ.b-tu- (De Vries 1962: 84; Matasovic 2011: 12) cannot be maintained.

\*dreusan- w.v. 'to fall' - Go. driusan s.v. 'to fall down', Nw. drjosa s.v. 'id.', OE dreosan s.v. 'to rush, fall, perish', OS driosan s.v. 'to fall' ⇒ \*dhreuHs-e(NEUR). A strong verb related to \*drusen- - \*druzen- ( q.v.). See also \*drauzjan-.

\*driban· s.v. 'to drive' - Go. dreiban s.v. 'id.', ON drifa s.v. 'to drift, drive like spray or snow; to crowd, throng, rush; to perform', Far. drfva s.v. 'to chase; to hit, strike; to press, force; to be carried or blow away', Elfd. draiva s.v. 'to drive; to fell; to pour, spray, flush', OE drifan s.v. 'to drive, force, pursue', E to drive, OFri. driva s.v. 'id.', OS drilian s.v. 'id.', Du. drijven s.v. 'to drive; to float', OHG triban s.v. 'to drive', G treiben s.v. 'to drive; to operate' ⇒ \*dhreibh-e- (WEUR) - Ir. drip 'bustle', Gael. drip 'hurry, confusion' < PCelt. \*drippi- < \*dhribh·ni-(?). In view of the semantics of ON drifa 'to drive (of snow)' as well as drifa f. 'fall of snow, snowdrift' < \*dribon- it is attractive to connect Lith. dribti 'to fall', but "die Bedeutungsbriicke ist ... sehr schmal" (Seebold 1970: 163). More importantly, dribti is likely to have a secondary zero-grade in -i- (see \*drupp/bon- 2) in view of Lith. drebti (drebiu) 'to throw, spatter with mud/dirt, to fall in thick flakes (of snow)'. The connection with Ir., Gael. drip is therefore preferable, although the required development of PIE \*-bh·ninto PCelt. \*-pp- remains controversial. See also \*draibO-, \*drib(b}On- and \*drifti-.

\*drib(b)on- w.v. 'to push little by little' - Nw. dribba w.v. 'to shove, push', E obs. to drib 'to lead one little by little into something', to dribble 'to keep (the ball) moving', Du. dribbelen w.v. 'to walk quickly (with small steps)', OHG tribOn w.v. 'to drive, push' ⇒ \*dhribh-neh₂- (WEUR). The iterative to \*driban- (q.v.). For the semantics, cf. EDu. driven s.v. 'to move (to and fro), run'.

\*drifti· f. 'driving' - ON drift f. 'snowdrift', ME drift 'driving of cattle to pasture; drove; running; falling of rain or snow, snowdrift', MDu. drift f. 'fervor, urge; herd; district', EDu. drift c. 'urge, instinct', MHG trift f. 'pasture, herd', G Trift f. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhribh-ti- (DRV). An old ti-stem created to the root of \*driban- ( q.v.).

\*drinkan· s.v. 'to drink' - Go. drigkan s.v. 'id.', ON drekka s.v. 'id.', Far. drekka s.v. 'id.', Elfd. drikka s.v. 'id.', OE drincan s.v. 'id.', E to drink, OFri. drinka s.v. 'id.', OS drinkan s.v. 'id.', Du. drinken s.v. 'id.', OHG trinkan s.v. 'id.', G trinken s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhre-n-g-e- (NEUR) - Lith. drJgti 'to become moist' < \*dhreg- (also cf. drJgnas adj. 'humid, wet' < \*dhreg-no-). The Germanic verb contains a nasalized variant of the root \*dhreg- as found in Baltic. Also cf. the lexicalized ptc. ON drukkinn, Far. drukkin, OE druncan, (M)Du. dronken, OHG trunkan adj. 'inebriated', whence Go. drugkanei, ON drukkni, OHG trunkenif. 'drunkenness'.
\*drita- n. 'shit, dirt' - ON drit n. 'id.', OE drit n. 'dirt', E dirt (DRV).

\*dritan- s.v. 'to shit' - ON drfta s.v. 'to shit (esp. of birds)', Far. drfta s.v. 'id.', Elfd. draita s.v. 'id.', OE ge-dritan s.v. 'id.', MDu. driten s./w.v. 'id.' (NEUR?) - Perhaps to Ru. dial. dristat', SCr. drfskati, drftkati 'to have diarrhea' < \*dhreid-ske-(?). No good etymology. One usually compares the aforementioned Slavic forms, but the reconstruction of the root-final d is uncertain: are we dealing with an internally Germanic Reimbildun9 to \*skitan-? Another possibility is to connect Lith. trfedziu 'to have diarrhea' < \*treidh-ie- and tride f. 'diarrhea' < \*tridh-ieh₂- under the assumption of metathesis: if correct, the Germanic strong verb must have been back-formed to an iterative \*dritton- < \*dhrit-neh₂-, cf. WFri. drittelje 'to have diarrhea'. See also \*drita-.

\*drobu- adj. 'turbid; troubled' - OE drof adj. 'turbid; troubled', OS drobi adj. 'cloudy, sad', Du. droef adj. 'id.', OHG truobi adj. 'turbid, dark, confused', MHG triiebe adj. 'dim, gloomy' (EUR). A u-stem (remodeled into a ja-stem in continental Germanic) related to \*drabiz- ( q.v.). Also cf. Go. drobjan 'to stir up', OE ge-drefan 'to stir up, make turbid; to trouble', OS gi-drobian 'to sadden', OHG truoben 'to dim, blur', G triiben 'id.' < \*drobjan- and Go. drobnan w.v. 'to become agitated'.

\*druga- n. 'deceit' - NFri. droch 'dream', OS gi-dro9 n. 'illusion', MDu. ge-droch n. 'deceit; apparition, ghost', OHG gi-tro9 n. 'id.', MHG ge-troc, troc m. 'id.' (ORV). Derived from \*dreu9an- ( q.v.). Formally and semantically close to Skt. druh- m. 'deceit, malice; demon' < \*dhru9h-.

\*druggon- w.v. 'to pull, drag' - Nw. dru99a w.v. 'to walk bowed down', ME dru99en, dro99en w.v. 'to labor, toil', E dial. to drug 'to pull forcibly' (ORV). The iterative to \*dra9an-, continuing quasi-PIE \*dhr9h-neh₂- with a secondary zero grade. Also cf. Icel. dro9i m. 'idler, lazy person' < \*dru9an- and with a different vowel - Nw. dra99a w.v. 'to trudge along' < \*dra99on-.

\*druhti- f. 'host, retinue' - Go. drauhti-witop 'military service', ON dr6tt f. 'household, people; host of the king's men, bodyguard of a king', OE dryht f. 'people, multitude, army', OFri. drecht f.(?) 'wedding entourage', OS druht-folk 'army', MHG truht f. 'troop, squad, platoon', G truht f. 'troop, squad, platoon' ⇒ \*dhru9h-ti- (ORV). A ti-stem to the strong verb \*dreu9an- 2. Also cf. Go. ga-drauhts m. 'soldier' < \*9a-druhta- and ON dr6ttinn, OE dryhten, OFri. drochten, OS drohtin, OHG truhtin, trohtin 'lord, king, God' < \*druhtana-.

\*drunki· m. 'drink' - ON drykkr m. 'drink, drinking; draught', Far. drykkur m. 'drink', Elfd. drykk m. 'id.', OE drync m. 'drink; draught', E drink, MDu. drone m. 'drinking', Du. dronk c. 'id.', OHG trunk m. 'id.', G Trunk m. 'id.' (DRV). An i-stem derived from \*drinkan- (q.v.).

\*drupp/bon- 1 w.v. 'to hit' - Nw. drubba w.v. 'to give a blow', E dial. drub 'to thresh, beat, bang' ⇒ \*dhrbh-neh₂- (NEUR). A zero-grade iterative to \*draban- (q.v). It gave rise to the strong verb \*drepan- (q.v.).

\*drupp/bOn- 2 w.v. 'to drip, droop; to drop' - Nw. drubba w.v. 'to walk with a stoop, to fall over', MDu. druppen w.v. 'to sag, drip', Du. dial. drubben w.v. 'to hang one's head, be downcast' ⇒ \*dhrbh-neh₂- (NEUR) - Lith. dribti (dririba) 'to drop, fall down, lie down, become flaccid' < \*dhrbh- (with -rirepresenting a secondary zero grade). An old iterative. The pertaining strong verb \*dreupan- - \*driipan- must given the (shortened) geminates and the \*eu - \*ii alternation be a back-formation to this iterative (cf. \*greupan- - \*griipan- vs. \*grupp/bon-). This means that the position of the zero grade in this iterative is likely to be of secondary origin, something that is also implied by the potential link with Lith. dribti. Nw. drabba w.v. 'to overflow' can theoretically be derived from an o-grade iterative \*dhrobh-neh₂-, but its meaning is somewhat dissimilar. Further cf. ON dropi, OE dropa, OS dropo, OHG tropfo, troffo, G Tropfen m. 'drop' < \*drup(p)an-.

\*driisen· w.v. 'to fall asleep' - OE driisian w.v. 'to become slow', E to drowse ҇ \*dhruHs-eh₁-(?) (NEUR) - Ru. dryxnut' 'to sleep' < \*druHs-ne-. A stative close to Ru. dryxnut'. Although the PSI. \*y may have been created secondarily to a PSI. root \*dn,s-, the Germanic verb seems to confirm the existence of a root \*dhruHs- with a laryngeal.

\*duba- adj. 'dull' - Du. dof adj. 'matt', MHG top adj. 'crazy' ⇒ \*dhubh·o(EVR). Also cf. OE dofian w.v. 'to rage', OHG topen, topon w.v. 'to be crazy' < \*duben- and ON dofna w.v. 'to become weak' < \*dubnan-. Related to \*dauba( q.v.).

\*diiban· s.v. 'to dive' - ON dufa w.v. 'id.', Nw. duva w.v. 'to squat', OSw. diiva w.v. 'to squat, bob', OE diifan s.v. 'to dive', MLG be-diiven s.v. 'to become overwhelmed, become covered', MDu. be-duven s.v. 'to dip' (DRV).

\*dubila- m. 'peg, pin, dowel' - MLG dove/ m. 'id.', Du. deuvel c. 'id.', MHG tilbel m. 'id.', G Diibel m. 'id.' (EUR) - Gr. Hsch. TU<pOL 'wedges' < \*dhubh-o-. Also cf. OHG gi-tubili n. 'peg, pin' < \*ga-dubilja-, MLG dovicke m. 'dowel-pin', Du. deuvik c. 'dowel-pin' < \*dubikan- and Nw. dial. dobbe m./f. 'fastening stick in a river', G Tyr. tuppe 'big piece of wood' < \*dubban-.

\*diibon- f. 'dove' - Go. hraiwa-dubo f. 'id.', ON dufa f. 'id.', Far. dugva f. 'id.', Elfd. dauva f. 'id.', E dove, OS duba f. 'id.', Du. duif c. 'id.', OHG tilba f. 'id.', G Taube f. 'id.' (DRV). A bird name probably derived from \*duban- 'to dive' (q.v.). Also cf. MHG tilber, G Tauber, MDu. duver, Du. doffer 'male dove' < \*dubazan-. For the suffix of the latter formation, see \*ganazan- 'gander' and \*katazan- 'tomcat'.

\*dud(i)ran- m. 'egg yolk' - WFri. djerre, djirre c. 'id.', OS dodro m. 'id.', MLG dod(d]er, doder, dodder n.(?) 'id.', MDu. doder, dodre m./f. 'id.', Du. dooier c. 'id.', OHG totoro m. 'id.', G Dotter m. 'id.' (GM?). The West Germanic languages point to \*dudran- (whence also OE dydring m. 'yolk') alongside more restricted \*dudiran-, cf. WFri. djerre, MLG doder, dodder. The latter is the source of Ice!. doddur (cf. Magnusson 1989: 143). No certain etymology. It is unclear whether the word can be connected to Skt. dudhita- adj. 'dark' (Schindler 1967). ToB tute 'yellow' continues \*dhuh₁-to- (Adams 1999: 301) and therefore must be unrelated. See also \*dudra/on-.

\*dudra/on- m./f. 'dodder(?), camelina(?)' - Ice!. h<ir-doora, akur-doora 'id.', Sw. d<idra c. 'id.', ME doder 'flax dodder', E dodder, MLG doder, dodder m. 'id.', Du. dodder m. 'id.', MHG toter m. 'wild rocket, field rocket' (NEUR?). The word appears in two different variants: WGm. \*dudra- meaning 'dodder', a parasitic plant, and Nordic \*dudron- 'camelina, false flax'. The word has no certain etymology, but may belong to Far. doddur m. 'tuft of hair, topknot, forelock', Du. dot c. 'tangle, tuft' < \*duddan-, which have been compared to Latv. duia f. 'tuft' < \*dhudh-ieh₂-(?) and Gr. θύανον m. 'tassels, fringe' (but cf. Beekes 2010: 566-7). The comparison with \*dud(i)ran- 'egg yolk' (q.v.) is unconvincing in spite of the fact that dodder has yellow or orange stems.

\*dugan- pret.-pres. 'to be fit, avail' - Go. daug 3sg.pres. 'id.', ON duga w.v. 'id.', OE dugan pret.-pres. 'id.', OFri. duga pret.-pres. 'id.', OS dugan pret.-pres. 'id.', Du. deugen w.v. 'id.', OHG tugan pret.-pres. 'id.', G taugen w.v.

\*dugunpi- 107 'id.' ⇒ \*dhugh- (IE) - Skt. d6gdhi 'to milk; to give milk', NP doxtan 'id.', Oss. ducyn / docun 'id.' < \*dheugh-; Gr. TUYX<lvw 'to succeed, hit upon; to meet, get together, happen to be' < \*dhu-n-gh-nh₂-; Lith. daD.g, Latv. daiidz adj. 'much, many' < \*dhough-i-. A preterite-present Go. daug, OE deah, dugon, OS dog, dugun, OHG toug, tugun < 3sg. \*dauge, 3pl. \*dugunp < \*dhough-e, \*dhugh-nt. The original conjugation as a perfect (present) is corroborated by Lith. daiig, Latv. daildz. See also \*dugunþi-, \*daugjan- and \*dukkon-.

\*dugunþi- f. 'valor' - OE duguiJ f. 'manhood; glory, advantage; multitude', OFri. duged f. 'virtue', ODu. dugeth f. 'id.', Du. deugd c. 'id.', OHG tugund, -ind f. 'id.', G Tugend f. 'id.' (DRV). A derivation to \*dugan- with the suffix \*-unpi- (for which cf. \*ju(w)unþi-).

\*duhter- f. 'daughter' - Go. dauhtar f. 'id.', ON d6ttir f. 'id.', Far. d6ttir f. 'id.', Elfd. duotter f. 'id.', OE dohtor, dohter f. 'id.', E daughter, OFri. dochter f. 'id.', OS dohtar f. 'id.', Du. dochter c. 'id.', OHG tohter f. 'id.', G Tochter f. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhughi-ter- (IE) - Skt duhitar-, Av. dugadar-, ToA ckiicar, ToB tkiicer, Gr. {luyaTijp, Arm. dustr, Lith. dukte, OCS d'bstb < \*dhugh₂-ter-. The Indo-European word for 'daughter'. Related to \*deuhtra- (q.v.).

\*dii.jan- w.v. 'to tremble' - ON djja w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhuH-ie- (IE) - Identical to Gr. {)Dw 'to dash, rush'; further compare Skt. dhiin6ti 'to shake, agitate; to blow on a fire' (for older \*dhiindti < \*dhuH-neh₂-?) and Skt dhuvati 'to throw down, shake down' < \*dhuH-e-. An apparently old ie-present closely related to Gr. {)Dw. Also cf. ON dy, Far. dyggj n. 'bog, marsh' < \*diija- and OSw. dyi, Elfd. doe m. 'dirt' < \*dujan-.

\*dii.kan- s.v. 'to duck' - ME douken w.v. 'to dive, plunge, immerse', E to duck, MDu. duken s.v. 'to dive; to duck, cower', Du. duiken s.v. 'to dive', OHG in-tiihhan s.v. 'to immerse', G tauchen w.v. 'to dive, plunge' (DRV). A strong verb derived from the iterative \*dukkon- ( q.v.). Also cf. OE duce, E duck < \*dukon-.

\*dukkon- w.v. 'to duck' - EDu. docken w.v. 'to dive, tumble', MHG tocken w.v. 'to immerse' ⇒ \*dhugh-neh₂-(?) (IE?). Also cf. MHG tucken, tiicken, G ducken, MDu. ducken w.v. 'to cower, duck' < \*dukkjan-. The verb has no accepted extra-Gm. etymology, but by reconstructing it as \*dhugh-neh₂-, it is possible to connect it to PIE \*dheugh-. This root is attested with the meaning 'to be fit' in Greek and Germanic (see \*dugan-), but it is not unlikely that it originally denoted 'to fall', and that this developed into 'to happen, coincide, be fit' at a later stage (cf. G Zu-fall 'coincidence'). See also \*dilkan-.

\*dula· adj. 'foolish, crazy' - MHG to/ adj. 'foolish, nonsensical' ⇒ \*dhu/-o(EUR) - Latv. du/s adj. 'furious' < \*dhu/-o-; Olr. dal/, W dal/ adj. 'blind' < \*dhu/-no-. A zero-grade formation related to \*dwelan- 'to err'. Hence also ON dylja w.v. 'to keep uninformed' < \*duljan-.

\*dulga· adj. 'debt' - Go. du/gs m. 'id.' ⇒ \*dh/gh·o- (EUR) - OCS d/'bg'b, Ru. dolg, SCr. dug m. 'debt'; Olr. dligid 'to owe, be entitled to' < \*dh/gh-e-; Olr. dliged n. 'duty' < \*dh/gh-eto-. The word appears to have a European distribution, but the OCS forms may have been borrowed from Germanic. No further etymology; the appurtenance of ON dolg n. 'enemy', OE dolg, OFri. dolg, dulg, OHG to/g n. 'wound' < \*du/ga- is possible, but uncertain.

\*du/0- f. 'cluster or bunch of grapes' - OHG to/a f. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhh₂/-eh₂- (EUR) - Gr. f)filw 'to flourish', Alb. da/ 'to sprout' < \*dhh₂/-n-; Arm. dalar adj. 'green' < \*dhh₂/-ro-; MW deillyau 'to emanate, proceed, come about' < \*dhh₂l-n-; perhaps also Alb. dal 'to flow out, exit; to grow'.
\*dulo- f. 'ditch' - WFri. dole c. 'puddle, pool', OHG to/a f. 'ditch', MHG tole f. 'id.' ⇒ \*dh/h₂-eh₂- (EUR). A zero-grade formation related to \*dala- (q.v.).

\*dulpi- f. 'festival, celebration' - Go. dulps f. 'id.', OHG tu/d f. 'id.', MHG tuft, du/t f. 'id.' (GM). No certain etymology: probably unrelated to Gr. -OaA.lcx f. 'abundance', pl. 'festival', which is derived from f)filw 'to flourish' < \*dh/H-n-. Despite the vague semantics, there may be a link with \*dwelan- 'to stay, dwell' (Grienberger 1900: 60).

\*dumba- adj. 'dumb' - Go. dumbs adj. 'dumb, mute', ON dumbr adj. 'id.', Far. dumbur adj. 'id.', Elfd. dumm adj. 'dumb', OE dumb adj. 'id.', E dumb, OFri. dumb adj. 'id.', OS dumb adj. 'id.', Du. dom adj. 'id.', OHG tumb adj. 'id.', G dumm adj. 'id.' (DRY). Derived from \*dimban- - \*dimpan- (q.v.): the original meaning was 'hazy', cf. Nw. dum adj. 'dumb; soft, mute; unclear'.
\*dumbon- f. 'fog, haze' - ON dumba f. 'dust, cloud of dust, mist', Elfd. dumba f. 'fog, haze' (DRY). A zero-grade formation derived from the strong verb \*dimban- - \*dimpan- (q.v.).

\*dumpon- w.v. 'to suffocate(?)' - MDu. ver-dompen w.v. 'to suffocate' ⇒ \*dhmbh-neh₂- (IE). The iterative to \*dim ban- - \*dimpan- ( q.v.). There is no connection with Lith. ditmpti (ditmpiu) 'to blow with bellows' (quasi PIE \*dhmHp-), which is likely to have arisen secondarily from Lith. ditmti (dumiit) 'id.' < \*dhmH-ie-, cf. dumpies 'bellows' < \*dmH-1-ieh₂-.

\*diina- m. 'down (of a bird)' - ON dunn m. 'id.', Far. dun n. 'id.', OSw. diin n. 'id.', Elfd. daungen m. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhuh₂-no- (IE). A Nordic word that was borrowed into West Germanic, cf. MLG diine, G Daune, ME doune, E down. Elfd. daungen has secondary TJ as in kraungen f. 'crown' < ON kruna (<< Lat. corona). Related to \*dauma- and \*dauna- (q.v.).

\*dunga- m./f. 'dung' - OE dung m. 'id.', E dung, OFri. dung m. 'id.', OS dung m. 'id.', G Dung m. 'id.' (GM). An a-stem related to \*dungon- (q.v.). Also cf. Elfd. dyndja, OFri. denga, EDu. dungen, OHG tungen, G diingen w.v. 'to fertilize' < \*dungjan-, ON dyngja f. 'dunghill; lady's bower' < \*dungjon- and OE dung, ding f. 'cellar, groundhouse', OHG, MHG tune m./f. 'id.' < \*dungi-.

\*dungon- w.v. 'to cover(?), pour over(?)' - Far. dunga w.v. 'to pile up; to snow heavily', Sw. dunga w.v. 'to get soaked', obs. 'to rain, pour down', MLG dungen w.v. 'to irrigate' ⇒ \*dhngh-neh₂- (NEUR) - Lith. defigti (dengiu) 'to cover' < \*dhengh-ie-; Lith. danga f. 'cover', RuCS duga f. 'arch, curve', SCr. duga f. 'rainbow' < \*dhongh-eh₂-; Lith. dangus 'heaven' < \*dhongh-u-. The verb \*dungonprobably continues an iterative \*dunkopi, \*dungunanpi < \*dhngh-neh₂-, cf. Nw. dial. dokk f. 'cover (e.g. of snow)' < \*dunko-. In view of the Balto-Slavic evidence, the original meaning of the root \*dhengh- seems to have been 'to cover' and - in a meteorological sense - 'to be overcast'. See also \*dunga- 'dung'.
\*dunkla- - \*dunkra- adj. 'dark' - OS dunkar adj. 'id.', Du. danker adj. 'id.', OHG tunkal adj. 'id.', G dunkel adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhngw-/o- (IE). A zero-grade adjective related to \*dinkwa- (q.v.).

\*dunsta- n.(?) 'powder, dust(?)' - OE diist n. 'id.', E dust, WFri. dust n./c. 'dandruff; chaff, MLG dust m. 'chaff, husk, dust', MDu. donst, dunst, dust m.? 'fluff, pollen' ⇒ \*dhuns-to- (IE) - ToB tiints- 'to scatter' < \*dhuns-; Skt. dhvaf!lsati 'to fall to dust, perish' < \*dhuens-e-. A to-stem to the PIE root \*dhuens-.
\*dupp/bOn- w.v. 'to dip' - Nw. duppa, dubba w.v. 'to dip; to duck; to nod', Elfd. duppa w.v. 'id.', MDu. doppen w.v. 'to dip' ⇒ \*dhubh-neh₂- (IE).

\*dura- n. 'gate, (single) door' - Go. daur n. 'id.', OE dor n. 'id.', E door, OS dor, dur n. 'id.', ODu. duri f. 'door', Du. deur 'door', OHG tor n. 'id.', G Tor n. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhur-o- (IE). The original neuter singular whose dual \*dhur-ih₁ 'double door' may have served as the basis for the i-stem \*durf- (q.v.). Also cf. Go. auga-dauro n. 'window', OFri. andern n. 'id.' < \*aug(n)a-duron-.

\*durgo- f. 'fishing-line' - ON dorg f. 'trailing-line', Far. dorg, pl. dergur f. 'short fishing-line' ⇒ \*dhrgh-eh₂- (DRV?). Also cf. the factitive ON dyrgja w.v. 'to take with a hand-line' < \*durgjan-. Related to \*dragan-?

\*durf- f.pl. 'door' - ON dyrr f.jn.pl. 'id.', Far. dyr f.pl. 'id.', Elfd. darer f.pl. 'id.', OS duri f. 'id.', MDu. dore f. 'id.', Du. deur c. 'id.', OHG turi f.pl., G Tiir f. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhur-ih₁ (IE) - Gr. -Bupa f. 'door, doorleaf, Arm. dur-k' pl. 'door', Lith. durys, dial. dures m.pl. 'id.', Latv. d(v)uris m.pl. 'id.', OPru. dauris 'gate', OCS dvbrb, Ru. dver', SCr. dviir f. 'door' < \*dhur-; Skt. dvara, dvarau du. 'id.', Alb. dere f. 'id.' < \*dhuor-; ToB twere m. 'id.', Lith. dviiras m. 'estate, village', Lat. fores f.pl. 'door'; Dir. dorus m. 'id.', W dor 'id.', Latv. dvars m. 'gate'; OCS dvon, Ru. dvor, SCr. dvor m. 'court, courtyard' < \*dhuor-o-. Most forms point to a (plural) i-stem, which in view of the neuter gender of ON dyrr is likely to have been based on the old dual \*dhur-ih₁ of \*dura'single door, gate' < \*dhur-o-. OE duru, OFri. dore, OHG tura f. 'door', on the other hand, goes back to \*duro-, which is formally identical to Gr. '9upa.

\*durron- f. 'stick' - Ice!. dorra f. 'piece of wood, stick' ⇒ \*dhr-on- (IE) - Gr. -3mp6c; m. 'pivot of a door, axle of a chariot' < \*dhr-io-. A zero-grade n-stem related to the semantically similar Nw. darre < \*dhor-on, \*dhor-n-6s (see \*darran-). The co-occurrence of these n-stems may point to an originally ablauting paradigm \*dh6r-en, gen. \*dhr-n-6s, ace.pl. \*dhor-n-6s > \*daren, \*durraz, \*darruns. The derivation from Ice!. darra w.v. 'to swing, vibrate' is unlikely, at any rate, since this verb is etymologically identical to daora < \*dadron-.

\*dursti- f. 'courage' - OE dyrst, ge-dyrst f. 'tribulation', OHG gi-turst f. 'courage, audacity', MHG turst, ge-turst f. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhrs-ti- (IE). A ti-stem to the preterite-present \*durzan- ( q.v.).

\*durzan· pret.-pres. 'to dare, be brave' - Go. ga-daursan pret.-pres. 'id.', OE durran s.v. 'id.', OS gi-durran pret.-pres. 'id.', ODu. durran pret.-pres. 'id.', Du. dorst pret. 'id.', OHG turran, gi-turran pret.-pres. 'id.', MHG turren, ge-turren pret.-pres. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhors- - \*dhrs- (IE) - Skt. dadh<ir!ja 3sg.perf. 'is bold' < \*dh6rs-e; Gr. {lapooc;, Aeol. {)£pooc; n. 'courage' < \*dhers-os, gen. \*dhrs-s-6s (whence {lapoew, {lapptw 'to be brave'); Lith. drfsti (drjstU) 'to become bold' < \*dhr-n-s-ske-; Lith. drqsus adj. 'brave' < \*dhro-n-s-u-. A preterite-present PGm. 3sg. \*darse, 3pl. \*durzunp < \*dh6rs-e, \*dhrs-nt. See also \*dursti-.

\*dusen- w.v. 'to slumber' - E to doze, MHG dosen w.v. 'to keep oneself quiet, slumber' ⇒ \*dhus-eh₁-ie- (EUR) - Lat. Jura, -ere 'to be mad, rave' < \*dhus-e-; Lith. dusti (drJ.stU) 'to suffocate', Latv. dust 'to gasp' < \*dhus-ske-; OCS dux'b m. 'breath', Lith. dusas m. 'short breath, asthma' < \*dhus-o-. A stative verb. Its original meaning seems to have been 'to lose one's senses', whence 'to fall asleep', 'to be wild', and 'to go mad'. Apparently, the Indo-Europeans assumed that the spirit (i.e. 'breath') left the body during sleep. See also \*dwesa-, \*dauzan- and \*deuza-. \*duska- 1 m. 'drizzle(?)' ś Nw. dusk n. 'drizzling rain', Sw. dial. dusk m. 'light rain' (NEUR) - OCS d'bidb, Ru. dozd' (doidja), SCr. diizd m. 'rain' < \*dusg-io-. A Germanic-Slavic isogloss. The derivation of PSlav. \*dbzdz'b from \*dus-diu- "bad-sky" (Vaillant 1927) is less evident, as the prefix \*dus- (see \*tuz-) otherwise does not occur in Slavic.

\*duska- 2 adj. 'dark' - OE dox, dux adj. 'sallow', E dusk 'twilight' ::) \*dhus-ko(EUR) - Identical to Lat. fuscus 'dark-colored, dusky'; also cf. Lat. furvus 'id.' < \*dhus-uo- (with the uo-suffix for chromonyms). Also cf. Far. ill-duskutur adj. 'with mixed and bad colors' < \*dusk-uhta-; OHG jir-tuskon w.v. 'to confuse', OE doxian w.v. 'to become dark' <

\*duskojan-, G Tusche f. 'ink' and - with a different suffix - OE dosen adj. 'chestnut', OHG tusin 'brown' < \*dhus-no- (cf. Olr. donn adj. 'dun, brown; light'). The original meaning may have been 'to obscure', which is compatible with the etymological cluster of\*dusen-.

\*du{w)en- w.v. 'to be misty(?), windy(?)' - Nw. dial. duva w.v. 'to blow softly' and Elfd. duva w.v. 'to drizzle' ⇒ \*dhuh₂-eh₁- (IE). An old stative formation related to \*dauma- and \*dawwa/o- (q.v.).

\*dwaljan- w.v. 'to delay, hinder' - ON dve/ja w.v. 'to delay, tarry', Far. dvelja w.v. 'to delay; to hesitate, wait', OE dwelian w.v. 'to lead astray', E to dwell, OS bi-dwellian w.v. 'to delay, hinder', MDu. dwellen w.v. 'to lead

\*dwelan- s.v. 'to err' - OE 9e-dwolen ptc. 'wrong', EFri. dwylje w.v. 'to rave; to roam', OS for-dwelan s.v. 'to neglect, fail', ODu. dwelon s.v. 'to fail, err', OHG 9i-twe/an s.v. 'to be stunned, tarry, fall asleep' ⇒ \*dhue/H-e- (EUR). A strong verb to the root \*dhue/H-, which is otherwise only attested in Baltic and Celtic (see under \*du/a-). Related to \*dwaljan-.

\*dwerga- m. 'dwarf - ON dver9r m. 'dwarf; short pillars which support the beams and rafters in a house', Far. dver9ur m. 'dwarf, OSw. dvrer9her m. 'id.', Elfd. dy6r9 m. 'spider; deathwatch beetle; wrinkle, crease', OE dweor9, dweorh m. 'dwarf, E dwarf, MDu. dwerch m. 'monster, dwarf, giant', Du. dwer9 c. 'dwarf, OHG twerc m. 'id.', G Zwer9 m. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhuer9h·o· (GM). An etymologically debated word. Some have compared Skt dhvaras- n. 'demon' < \*dhuer-o- and Lith. dvii.sas 'spirit' < \*dhuos-o- (cf. MHG 9e-twas 'ghost'), but these linkages are extremely doubtful given the formal dissimilarities. I therefore assume that the word was derived from the strong verb \*dwer9an- attested as MHG zwer9en s.v. 'to squeeze, press'. The meanings 'pillar' and 'staff in Nordic are believed by some to refer to the pagan belief that dwarfs carried the firmament (cf. Simek 2006: 504). Also cf. ON dyr9ja f. 'female dwarf < \*dur9I· / \*dwer9I- and lee!. dur9ur m. 'boor, lout' < \*dur9a-.
\*dwergan- s.v. 'to squeeze, press' - MHG zwer9en s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhuer9h-e(GM). No extra-Gm. cognates, but there may be a link with \*dwer9a- 'dwarf (q.v.).

\*dwesa- adj. 'foolish' - OE dwres adj. 'id.', OFri. dwes adj. 'id.', MLG dwii.s adj. 'id.', MDu. dwaes adj. 'id.', Du. dwaas adj. 'id.', MHG twas adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhues-o(EUR) - Lith. dvii.sas m. 'breath, soul, sickly person' < \*dhuos-o-; Lith. dvasia f. 'breath, spirit, soul' < \*dhuos-ieh₂·. Also cf. MHG 9e-twas, MDu. 9e-dwaes n. 'foolishness, ghost' (with a similar meaning to MHG tuster m. 'ghost' < \*dus-tra-). See also \*dusen-.

\*dwinan- s.v. 'to diminish' - ON dvfna w.v. 'id.', Far. dvfna w.v. 'to yield, give way; to diminish', OE dwinan s.v. 'to dwindle', ME dwinen s.v. 'id.', MDu. dwinen s.v. 'to disappear, dwindle, pine away', Du. ver-dwijnen s.v. 'to disappear' ⇒ \*dh9whei-ne- (IE) - Skt. k$il)dti, k$i1)6ti 'to destroy, corrupt, ruin' < \*dh9whi-neh₂- / -neu-; Gr. q>i9lvw 'to decline, decay, perish; to cause to de-

\*ebanp- - \*ebund- 113 dine, consume, destroy' < \*dhgwhi-nu-e-; Lat. situs, -iis m. 'neglect, disuse, rot' < \*dhgwhi-tu-. An n-present to the root \*dhgwhei-, cf. Nw. dva w.v. 'to abate' < \*dwfan-, cf. Skt. k$6yati 'to destroy' < \*dhgwhei-e-. A slightly different formation is ON dvena, dvina w.v. 'to dwindle, cease, subside' < \*dwinon- < \*dhgwhi-neh₂-, which unlike the factitive Skt. k$ir;ufo appears to be an (intransitive) iterative. The Germanic reflexes of this verbal formation show a remarkable alternation between \*dwlnan-, \*swfnan-, \*pwlnanand perhaps also \*kwfnan-: apparently, the outcome of the initial cluster \*dhgwh_ was unstable in Germanic.

\*ebanp- - \*ebund- m. 'evening' - ON aftann, eftann m. 'id.', Far. aftan m. 'id.', Elfd. otn m. 'early supper' ( < ON \*pftunn?), OE refen m. 'evening', E eve, OFri. ewend, iowen[d}, iouwend, ion(d}, ioun(d) m. 'id.', WFri.jun c. 'id.', EFri. aiwend m. 'id.', OS aband m. 'id.', ODu. avont m. 'id.', Du. avond c. 'id.', OHG aband, abund c. 'id.', G Abend m. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₁eh₁pt-ont- (IE?). An etymologically and derivationally problematic word. The suffix has a clear parallel in Hitt. ispant- c. 'night' (see \*westera-), and indeed, the alternation of \*-anp/da- vs. \*-unp/da- points to an accentually mobile consonant stem. It remains unclear, however, how the paradigm must be reconstructed, especially in view of the difficult root alternation of Nordic \*aft- vs. West Germanic \*eb-. I am inclined to follow Hamp (apud EWAhd: I, 11), and reconstruct it as nom. \*h₁eh₁pt-ont, gen. \*h₁f,11pt-nt-6s. This reconstruction has two advantages. First, the root \*eb- can be explained by t-dissimilation in a secondary genitive \*h₁eh₁pt-nt-6s (cf. \*sebunp/dan- 'seventh' < \*septm-tH6-, Brugmann 1895: 376). Second, the predominantly Nordic root \*aft- can be accounted for as having developed from \*h₁f,11pt-, i.e. with regular vocalization of the laryngeal between stops. The etymology of the word nevertheless remains unclear: the comparison with \*afteri < \*h₂ep-tero- is semantically sound (cf. reften-tfd 'evening' < "after-tide"), but formally impossible in view of the initial laryngeal.

\*ebna- adj. 'even, level' - Go. ibns adj. 'even, level, flat', ON jafn adj. 'even, equal', Far. javnur adj. 'id.', OSw. iamn, iremn adj. 'id.', Elfd. iemmen adj. 'id.', OE efen adj. 'even, equal', E even, Du. effen, even adj. 'level, even', OHG eban adj. 'even, equal, straight', G eben adj. 'level' ⇒ \*em-no- (IE) - Skt. amnas adv. 'just, just now; at once' < \*h₁em-n-e/os-.

\*ebura- m. 'boar' - ON jpfurr m. 'king', OE eofor m. 'boar', MDu. ever m. 'id.', Du. ever-zwijn 'id.', OHG ebur m. 'id.', G Eber m. 'id.' ⇒ \*epr (NIE) - Lat. aper m. 'wild boar; kind of fish', Umb. abrons ace.pl. < \*apro-; Latv. vepris 'castrated boar', OCS veprb, Ru. vepr' (gen. veprja), SCr. vepar m. 'wild boar' < \*uepri- (\*uepro-). The irregular alternation of Pre-Gm. \*epr, Pre-It. \*apr and Pre-Slav. \*ueprcould be an indication that the word was not inherited from PIE, but rather adopted from an unknown (extinct) source by the individual daughter languages after their settlement in Europe. Another word in which non-IE \*a emerges as Gm. \*e is \*þeura- 'bull' (q.v.).

\*edara- m. 'edge; fence' - ON jaoarr m. 'edge', Far. jaoari m. 'edge, corner', Nw. jar(e) m. 'edge, side', OE eodor m. 'hedge, fence, enclosure, dwelling; limit, end, region', OS edar m. 'pale, picket, bar', OHG etar m./n. 'edge, fence', MHG eter m. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₁edh-ro- (IE) - OCS odro m. 'bed', Ru. (arch.) odr m. 'bed, couch', Cz. odr 'pillar, frame, summer-house', SCr. Odar m. 'bed, scaffolding' < \*h₁odh-ro-. ON jpourr has a suffix variant \*-ura-, which may also be present in OE eodor. Also note Far. jaour n. 'layer, stratum' < \*jaora- and the ablauting MLG ader(e) 'pole, beam in a fence' < \*adr-. Perhaps the word originally had a static paradigm, e.g. \*adur, gen. \*edurz < \*h₁6dh-r, \*h₁edh-r-s.

\*edi conj. 'again' - Go. ip conj. 'but, however' ⇒ \*h₁eti (IE) - Gr. en adv. 'still, also, further', Lat. et conj./adv. 'and what is more, too; and', Gaul. eti adv. 'yet, still, equally', NPhr. ETL conj. 'and'.

\*edis- f. '(noble, divine) lady' - OE ides f. 'female, lady', OS idis f. 'wife, woman', OHG itis f. '(divine) woman' (GM). A word without a certain etymology. The connections with \*edara- 'fence' (Eichner/Nedoma 2000: 33) and \*aida- 'pyre' (parallel to G Flamme, Bammesberger 2007) are semantically unconvincing. The link with ON dfs f. 'woman, girl; fairy, nymph; goddess', Far. dfs f. 'goddess, nymph', Nw. dis f.

\*edra- adj. 'quick' - OE ii!dre adv. 'immediately', OFri. eder, edre, idder(e) adv. 'soon, quickly', OS iidro adv. 'early', OHG iitar adj. 'quick' (GM). No certain etymology. The word has been compared to Lith. otrus, Latv. iitrs adj. 'quick', but this may be a loanword from Germanic.

\*egila- m. 'hedgehog, sea urchin' - ON igull m. 'urchin', lee!. fgul-ker n. 'sea urchin', .ODa. jrewre/ m. 'hedgehog', OE igil, fl m. 'hedgehog, porcupine; sea urchin', MDu. egel m. 'hedgehog', Du. egel c. 'id.', OHG fgi/ m. 'id.', G Igel, obs. Eigel 'id.' ⇒ \*h₁egh-i-lo- (IE) - Oss. wyzyn / uzun 'hedgehog' (with unexplained initial u); Arm. ozni 'id.' < \*h₁ogh-i-Hn-io-; Gr. txTvo<; m. 'id.' < \*h₁egh-i-Hno-; Lith. ezys, Latv. ezis m. 'id.', Ru. ez (gen. eza), Ser. jez 'id.' < \*h₁egh-io-. Most of the attested forms point to \*egila-, but ODa. jrewre/ developed from a divergent form, i.e. either ON \*jagall < \*egala- or \*jpgull < \*egu/a(with breaking of initial e). ON igull appears to contain the same suffix \*-ula-, but is due to a late substution of -ill by -ull. Note that the lengthening of ON I to i in Icel. fgull is automatic before g in open syllables.

\*egja- m. 'sea' - ON regir m. 'id.', Far. poet. regir\* m. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂ekʷ-i6- (EUR?). The word, which occurs in Old Norse and in Faroese ballads (a regin b/Q 'to the sea'), is usually derived from \*h₂ekʷ-i6-, i.e. a lengthened grade formation to \*ahwo- 'river' < \*h₂ekʷ-eh₂- (Darms 1978: 29). OE eagor 'sea, flood' has been adduced as well, but in this word the r is a suffix, not an ending. It is therefore more likely to be related to Du. agger 'sea swell' < \*agra- (q.y.).

\*ehwa- m. 'horse' - Go. ailva-tundi f. 'thorn bush', ON poet j6r m. 'stallion', OE eoh m. 'war-horse', OS ehu-skalk m. 'groom' ⇒ \*h₁el<-uo- (IE) - HLuv. asu- c., Lye. esb- 'horse' < \*h₁el<-u-; Skt cisva- m. 'horse, steed', ToAyuk, ToB yakwe m. 'horse', Gr. 'lmi:o<; m.f. 'horse, mare', Lat equus m. 'horse', Olr. ech, OBret eb m. 'id.' (cf. W ebawl 'foal' < \*ekwo-polHo-, for which see \*fulan-); Lat equa, Lith. efra f. 'mare' < \*h₁el<-u-eh₂-. Most Indo-European languages, including Germanic, have a thematic noun reflecting PIE \*h₁el<-uo-, but the Anatolian evidence points to a u-stem, \*h₁el<-u-s, gen. \*h₁1<-u-6s (Kloekhorst 2008: 237). This archaic paradigm can

\*ek(a) pron. T - Go. ik, dat mis, acc. mik pron. 'id.', ON ek, dat mer, acc. mik pron. 'id.', Far. eg, dat. mrer, acc. meg pron. 'id.', OSw. iak, dat. mrer, acc. mik, mek pron. 'id.', Elfd. ig, obi. mig pron. 'id.', OE ic, obi. me, acc. mec pron. 'id.', E /, obi. me, OFri. ik, obi. mi pron. 'id.', OS ik, obi. mi pron. 'id.', Du. ik, obi. mij pron. 'id.', OHG ih, dat mir, acc. mih pron. 'id.', G ich, dat mir, acc. mich pron. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₁eg-H-om (IE) - Identical to Skt. aham 'I', OAv. azam 'id.', YAv. azam 'id.', OP adam 'id.', Arm. es 'id.', OLith. es 'id.', Lith. as 'id.', Latv. es 'id.', OPru. as, es 'id.', OCS az'b, ORu. [j)az'b, Ru.ja, SCr.jiJ., dial.jiiz 'id.' < \*h₂eg-H-om; also cf. Hitt rik 'id.' < \*h₂ug; ToA nuk (f.) 'id.', ToB niis 'id.' ( < \*h₁mene-ge?); Gr. fyw, Dor. fywv 'id.', Lat ego 'id.'< \*h₂eg-on. The IE pronoun of the first person. The Germanic form must go back to \*eka < \*h₁eg-H-om in view of the breaking of -e- to -ja- in East Nordic, cf. OSw. iak, Sw. jag, Da. jeg. It is further conceivable that the emphatic WGm. variants OHG ihha, MHG iche, Du. ikke (cf. Howe 1996: 241) continue \*eko, a form that may correspond to e.g. Gr. fyw and Lat ego < \*h₁eg-0H. See also \*mina-.

\*ela- m. 'eel' - ON all m. 'id.', Far. alur, alli, allur m. 'id.', Elfd. al m. 'id.', OE eel m. 'id.', E eel, OFri. el m. 'id.', MDu. ael m. 'id.', Du. aal c. 'id.', OHG al m. 'id.', G Aal m. 'id.' (GM). An old fish name, perhaps related to \*ala- and \*elo- (q.v.).

\*elgra- adj. 'blunt (of teeth)' - G obs. elger adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₁el(H)k-r6- (NEUR) - Lith. alkti 'to be hungry'; OCS al'bkati 'id.', Ru. alkat' (a/cu) 'to hunger (for), crave (for)' < \*h₁o1Hk- (or \*h₁eh₁olk-, Rasmussen 1999: 199). A ro-stem to the root \*h₂el(H}k-. Also cf. OHG ilki 'fames vel stridor dentium' < \*elgin- and MHG ilgern w.v. 'to become blunt (of teeth)' < \*elgrjan- (DWB: 10, 2060-61). I assume that the original meaning 'having blunt teeth' developed into 'hungry' in both Germanic and Balto-Slavic. Unrelated to \*elhja- 'evil' (q.v.).

\*elha(n)- m. 'elk' - OE eolh, eola m. 'id.', ME elk 'id.', E elk, LG elk m. 'id.', EDu. elgh 'bison', OHG elaho, elho m. 'elk' ⇒ \*h₂el-lfo(n)- (IE). An ablauting variant of \*algi- (q.v.) with the same meaning. The Middle Germanic forms with final k are unexpected, and may point to borrowing from Nordic. Alternatively, they can be accounted for by assuming generalization of the oblique stem of an n-stem \*elho, \*elkaz < \*h₁elK-on, \*h₂elK-n-6s. For the suffix -Ko-, cf. \*selha- 'seal'.

\*elhja- adj. 'evil' - ON illr adj. 'ill, evil, bad, mean', Far. illur adj. 'evil, unfriendly; poor, miserable; angry' ⇒ \*h₁elk-io- (WEUR) - Olr. elc adj. 'mischievous, capricous' < \*h₁elk-o-(?). The cluster -II- can be derived from several sources in Old Norse and has therefore given occasion to many different etymologies. In view of the Finnish loanwords elkiii adj. 'mean, malicious' and ilkeii adj. 'bad, mean, wicked', the derivation of illr from \*elhila- (with \*-ila- as in \*mekila- 'big' and \*lftila- 'small'?) through syncope is least problematic. Alternatively, it is possible to assume that the root illr arose from \*fli-ligr, cf. ON drella adv. 'friendly' < \*dreli-liga, etc. (see \*airi for more examples). The final i of \*fli- in \*fli-ligr just like Fi. elkiii points to an original ja-stem \*elhja-.

\*elo- f. 'awl' - OE re/, ea/ f. 'id.', ME el, a(u)l 'id.', OHG ala f. 'id.', G Ahle f. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₁eh₁l-eh₂- (IE) - Skt. &ra- f. 'awl'. The Sanskrit and Germanic forms are identical, even regarding the accent, as \*h₁eh₁l-eh₂- would have resulted in PGm. \*\*e/0- by pretonic shortening. Cf. \*ala- and \*alasno-.

\*embja- n. 'swarm of bees' - OE imbe m. 'id.', MDu. imme f. 'bee', OHG impi m. 'swarm of bees; bee', MHG imbe, imme m. 'id.' (GM). The neuter OE imbe continues a collective \*imbja-, but OHG impi and MDu. imme in the sense of 'bee' probably represent different formations, i.e. \*imbja- and \*imbjo-. No further etymology: the comparison with Gr. tμrcii;, -ioo<; f. 'gnat' is erroneous because this word was derived from tμrclve:tv 'to drink oneself full' (with blood).

\*emon- 1 f. 'erysipelas' - ON cima, cimu-s6tt f. 'id.', OE oman f.pl. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₁ehi-mon- (IE) - Skt. cimci-, NP xam, Oss. xom, Arm. hum, Gr. wμ6<;, Olr. om, W of adj. 'raw' < \*h₁ohi-m6-. An mn-stem to the root \*h₁eh₁- 'raw, reddish (of skin)', which is further supported by the aforementioned mo-stem adjectives in the other IE languages. Also note OE om m. 'rust', G Ohm m. 'skin infection' < \*ema-, which may be a thematicization of the origin mn-stem in view of the e-grade of the root. Unrelated to \*am[m)on- 'to irritate' (q.v.), which is based on the root \*h₃emh₃-.

\*emon- 2 f. 'larva' - Nw. dme f. 'larva, caterpillar', Elfd. oma f. 'id.', OE emel f. 'caterpillar; weevil', MLG emel, emelte 'corn bug; aphid', Du. emelt c. 'mosquito larva' (GM). An old word for 'larva' or 'caterpillar'. There are some formal difficulties: Nw. dme straightforwardly points to \*emon-, but the West Germanic forms can be derived from both \*emilo- and \*amilo-. The dental suffix of MLG emelte, Du. emelt is also found in the doublet MLG homele - home/te 'bum-

\*ɣnu- prep. 'without' - Go. inu prep. 'id.', ON an, 6n prep. 'id.', Far. 6n prep. 'id.', OS cino prep. 'id.', OHG cinu, cine prep. 'id.', G ohne prep. 'id.' ::) \*h₂enu (IE) - Gr. Civw adv. 'far from, without'. A preposition (adverb, conjunction) that on the basis of the comparison with Gr. Civru can be reconstructed as \*enu < \*h₂inu for NWGm. \*cineu and as \*enu < \*h₂enu for Gothic inu (the latter with pretonic shortening, i.e. Dybo's law): apparently, the adverb was grammaticalized from an originally mobile u-stem, e.g. nom. \*h₂in-u, Joe. \*h₂n-eu (= Gr. Civw). Note that both proto-forms must be reconstructed with a lengthened grade (cf. Nikolaev 2007: 165) in order to explain the non-coloring by the laryngeal (Eichner's law).

\*erba(n)- m. 'wolverine' - lee!. jerfur, obs. jarfi m. 'id.', Nw. jerv, dial. jarv, erv m. 'id.' (GM). A Nordic word, probably continuing a proto-form \*erba(n)- that developed into ON jarfr, Ice!. jarfi, Nw. dial. jarv by a-breaking. The form jerfr is irregular, but may be a contamination of jarfr with an unbroken form \*erfr, cf. Nw. dial. erv. Etymologically, the word has been compared to ON jarpr 'brown' < \*erpa- and to Gr. E:ptq:>oc; 'goat', but it is perhaps better to assume a link with Nw. dial.jarva w.v. 'to gnaw, chew'.

\*erpa- adj. 'light brown' - ON jarpr adj. 'chestnut, reddish-brown', Far. jarpur adj. 'light or yellowish-brown', OE eorp, earp adj. 'dark, dusky, brown, swarthy', OHG erpf adj. 'brown, dark-brown, dark' ::) \*h₁erbh-n6- (IE) - Gr. 6pq:iv6c; adj. 'dark brown' < \*h₁orbh-no-; Ru. dial. rjab6j [adj.] 'speckled' < \*h₁re-m-bho-. Related to the ablauting Gr. 6pq:>v6c; through Kluge's law (Pre-Gm. \*erppa-). There is no direct etymological connection with \*reupon- 'grouse': Slav. \*r҈bo served as the basis for the semantically parallel derivation of RuCS rebb 'partridge', cf. the late derivatives ON jarpi m. 'hazel grouse' and Ice!. irpa f. 'brown mare' < \*erpjon-.

\*erpo- f. 'earth, soil, land' - Go. airfla f. 'id.', ON jpro f. 'id.', Far. jfdro f. 'id.', Elfd.juord f. 'id.', OE eorfl f. 'id.', E earth, OFri. erthe f. 'id.', OS ertha f. 'id.', Du. aarde c. 'id.', OHG erda f. 'id.', G Erde f. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₁er-t-eh₂-(?) (IE?) - Gr. £pa f. 'earth' < \*h₁er-ehr(?). Possibly related to Gr. £pa. Within Germanic, the word is probably cognate with \*erwan- (q.v.).

\*erwan- m. 'sand, soil' - ON jprfi m. 'sand (bank)', OHG ero 'earth' ⇒ \*hier-uon-(?) (IE?) - ToB yare 'gravel, dirt' (if ya re is a misspelling?). A word possibly containing the same root as \*erpo- 'earth'. ODu. \*erende for ereude 'fines terrae' has been analyzed by Van Helten 1902: 261 as a compound of the same word (ODu. \*ero) with \*andja- 'end' (q.v.), but it may alternatively continue \*uz-andja- 'far end'.

\*erzja- adj. 'stray' - Go. airzeis adj. 'deluded, misled, in error', OS irri adj. 'furious', OHG irri adj. 'erring, ignorant', MHG irre adj. 'astray' ⇒ \*hiers-io(IE) - Semantically close to Lat. erriire 'to roam, waver' < \*hiers-eh₂-ie-. A ja-stem derived from a PIE verbal root \*hiers-, which originally may have been identical to \*hiers- 'to flow', cf. Hitt. arszi - ar5anzi 'id.' < \*h₁ers-ti, \*hirs-enti, Skt. <ir$ati 'id.', assuming that the meaning 'to roam, err' developed from 'to be on the run'. It is furthermore possible to compare PIE \*h₁ers- 'to boil, to rage', cf. Hitt. ar5anezi\* 'to be envious' < \*h₁rs-neh₁-, Arm. efam 'to boil, be restless' < \*h₁ers- and Lith. arsits adj. 'vehement' < \*hiors-u-. The adjective served as the basis for OE irsian w.v. 'to deceive' < \*erzison- (for the suffixation, cf. the derivation of firsian 'to remove' from feor 'far' and hlci!nsian 'to make lean' from hlci!ne 'lean').

\*esa- n. 'dead meat; bait' - OE res n. 'id.', Du. aas n. 'id.', OHG iis n. 'id.', G Aas n. 'id.' ⇒ \*hied-so- (IE) - ToByetse m. '(outer) skin' < \*hied-so-. A thematicized s-stem derived from \*etan-. The Germanic word can be reconstructed as both \*hied-to- and \*hied-so-, but the latter option is corroborated by ToB yetse.

\*etan- s.v. 'to eat' - Go. itan s.v. 'id.', ON eta s.v. 'id.', Far. eta s.v. 'id.', Elfd. jiita s.v. 'id.', OE etan s.v. 'id.', E to eat, OS etan s.v. 'id.', Du. eten s.v. 'id.', OHG ezzan s.v. 'id.', G essen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*hied- (IE) - Hitt. ez(za)zi - adanzi, Skt. atti, 3pl. adanti, Gr. f.6w, Lat. edo, esse, Olr. ithid, Lith. Jsti, Latv. est, OPru. istwei, OCS jasti (jamb), Ru. est' (em), SCr. jesti 'to eat' < \*hied-ti, 3pl. \*h₁d-enti. Also cf. Go. fra-itan, OE fretan, E to fret, (M)Du. vreten, OHG frezzan, G fressen s.v. 'to eat (up), gourmandize' < \*fra-etan-. See further \*atjan- and \*esa-.

\*epman- - \*edman- m. 'breath' - OE ci!pm m. 'breathing, breath', OFri. ethma m. 'breath', OS iithum m. 'breath, spirit', Du. adem, coll. asem c. 'breath', OHG iitum m. 'breath, breeze, spirit', MHG atem, G Atem m. 'breath' ⇒ \*hiehit-mon- (IE) - Skt. atman- m. 'soul, self, breath'. The Sanskrit paradigm nom. atma, Joe. atmani - tman(i) points to an ablauting paradigm \*hiehit-mon, Joe. \*h₁h₁t-men-(i). Apparently, the accen-

\*epro- - \*edro- f. 'vein, rivulet' - ON ceor, gen. ceaar f. 'vein' ( < \*ediz, -joz), Nw. ci.r(e) f. 'rivulet; vein', OE ci!dre f. 'river; vein, artery; nerve, sinew; kidney', OFri. eddere, edere f. 'vein', MDu. adere f. 'vein, artery; sinew, fiber', Du. ader c. 'vein', OHG ii.d(a)ra f. 'vein, sinew, muscle', G Ader f. 'sinew', pl. 'bowels' ⇒ \*h₁eh₁t-r- (IE) - Gr. KTOP n. 'heart' < \*h₁eh₁t-r-; unrelated is Olr. inathar m. 'entrails, bowels', MCorn. inederen 'extum' < \*h₁en-utro- (Anders J0rgensen, p.c.). The word, which may contain the same root as \*epman- - \*edman- (q.v.), alternates between \*epro- and \*edro- in Germanic, and this may point to an originally mobile paradigm, e.g. sg. \*h₁eh₁t-r, pl. \*h₁h₁t-6r. Also cf. the collective OHG inn-ii.d(i)ri, MHG in-ceder(e) n. 'entrails, veins', OLFra. inn-ethron dat.pl. 'visceribus' < \*eprja-.

\*eudra- - \*iidra- n. 'udder' - ON jugr n. 'id.', Far. jugur n. 'id.', Elfd. juor n. 'id.', OE uder n. 'id.', E udder, OFri. uder n. 'id.', WFri. jaer n. 'id.', NFri. Hall. jaodar n. 'id.', MLG jeder, jiidder n. 'id.', Du. uier c. 'id.' (dial. jadder 'teat' « OFri. \*jadder), OHG utar(o) m. 'id.', G Euter n. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₁(e)uHdh-r/n- (IE) - Skt. ddhar - ildhan- n. 'udder'< \*h₁uHdh-r/n-; Gr. o(;-8ap, -aTO<; n. 'id.' < \*h₁ouHdh-r/nt-; Lat. uber n. 'breast; udder' < \*h₁e/ouHdh-r-. While the PGm. form \*udra- is backed up by Skt. udhar, the e-grade \*eudra- (or perhaps rather \*eudur in view of the dissimilation of \*-IJ- to \*-gin ON jugr, cf. fj9gur '4' < \*fe91Juru < \*fedur-6) < \*h₁euHdh-r- is uniquely Germanic. Together with Gr. o(;-8ap < \*h₁ouHdh-r-, the material provides evidence for a static paradigm \*h₁6uHdh-r, gen. \*h₁euHdh-n-s, but the origin of the zero-grade root \*h₁uHdh- remains uncertain. The ultimate derivation of the word is not entirely clear, but it is possible that it was created to a root \*h₁euHdh- as evinced by Ru. dial. udit' 'to ripen (of grain); to swell up with liquid' < \*h₁ouHdh-eie- (Vine/Yokoyama 2010). This may be a dhhi-extension to a more primary base \*h₁eu(H)- possibly preserved in Hitt. uya- 'nurse(?)' (Melchert 1986: 111, 114). The heteroclitic r/n-suffix was strongly associated with body parts in Indo-European.

\*eup - \*upp adv. 'up, upwards' - Go. iup adv. 'id', ON upp, Far. upp, Elfd. upp adv. 'id.', OE up adv. 'id.', E up, OFri. up, op adv. 'id.', OS up adv. 'id.', Du. op adv., prep. 'up; on', OHG uf, G auf adv., prep. 'id.' ⇒ \*(h₁e)up-n- (DRV?). An ablauting directional adverb, related to \*uba 'under; above' (q.v.), with a geminate that was shortened in the full-grade form (cf. Luhr 2000: 190). The geminate arose regularly in the locatival form \*uppai < \*up-noi, whose suffix can be compared to the one of e.g. OCS V'bne 'outside' < \*ud-noi (cf. \*ut). It is difficult to determine whether \*eup and \*upp had a suffix \*-e or \*-a

\*ewwadjon- f. 'tit' - ON igoa f. 'small bird', Far. poet igoa f. 'tit(?)', Nw. igde f. 'id.' (NIE). An exclusively Nordic word derived from a more primitive masculine form Nw. egde < \*ewwadan-. Somehow connected to lmandra Saa. avig3ine 'tit(?)' as cited by Qvigstad 1902: 270? Either way, the word could be a loanword from an extinct Pre-Nordic language, for which cf. \*awadf' eiderduck'.

\*fader- m. 'father' - Go. fadar m. 'id.', ON faoir m. 'id.', Far. faoir m. 'id.', Elfd. fader m. 'id.', OE feeder m. 'id.', E father, OFri. feder m. 'id.', OS fadar m. 'id.', Du. vader c. 'id.', OHG fatar m. 'id.', G Vater m. 'id.' ⇒ \*ph₂-ter- (IE) - ToA pacar, ToB pacer, Skt pitcir-, OAv. pta, acc. pataram, YAv. pita, acc. pitaram, OP pita, NP pidar, Oss. fyd / ftdce, Gr. na-oip, gen. na-rp6c;, Lat. pater, -tris, Olr. athir, athair m. 'father'. The ludo-European word for 'father', a hysterokinetic ter-stem, nom. \*ph₂-t҉r, gen. \*ph₂-tr-6s, acc. \*ph₂-ter-m. See also \*fadurwjan-.

\*fadi- m. 'lord' - Go. brup-faps m. 'bridegroom', hunda-faps, pusundi-faps m. 'leader of hundred/thousand' ⇒ \*pot-i- (IE) - ToA pats 'husband', Skt. pati-, Av. paiti- m. 'lord, ruler, husband', Gr. n6mc; m. 'husband', Lith. pats m. 'id.' < \*pot-i-; Lat. potis adj. 'capable, powerful' < \*pot-i-. An old ludo-European word often used as a second member of compounds, e.g. Skt. vis-pa ti- 'lord of the house', Li th. vies-pats 'lord', Lat. hos-pes 'guest, host', Ru. gos-pod' 'Lord, god'. Also cf. Hitt. -pat adv. 'the same, also, even', Av. xvae-paiti- 'himself, Lith. pats 'self, adv. pat 'even'. An unattested PGm. form \*swabadi- ( < \*suo-poti-) has been reconstructed on the basis of PSlav. \*svo-bodb 'free' (Vaillant 1958: §277), but this word is actually derived from an adjective \*suobho-, cf. Latv. at-svabinat 'to set free' < \*suobh-neh₂-, with the Slavic suffix \*-od-.
\*fadojan- w.v. 'to feed' - OHGfaton w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*ph₂t-eh₂-ie- (EUR).

\*fadurwjan- m. 'paternal uncle, father's brother' - OEfeedera m. 'id.', OFri. federia, fedria, feria, fadria m. 'id.', MDu. vedder m. 'father's brother; uncle; brother's son', OHG fatureo,fetiro m. 'father's brother', MHG vetere, veter m. 'father's brother, brother's son, male cousin', G Vetter m. 'male cousin' ⇒ \*ph₂-tr-u-ion- (IE) - Skt. pitrvya-, YAv. trliriia- m. 'father's brother' < \*ph₂tr-u-io-; Lat. patruus m. 'id.' < \*ph₂tr-uo-; Gr. mhpwc; m. 'male relative, father's brother' < \*ph₂tr-ou-. A PIE derivation from \*fader- (q.v.). The Germanic word is identical to the lndo-Iranian formation, but Greek appears to attest to an amphikinetic u-stem \*ph₂-tr-ou, gen. \*ph₂-tr-u-os. For the suffix \*-wjan-, cf. \*suhturwjan-.

\*fagjan- w.v. 'to please' - Go.fulla-fahjan w.v. 'to satisfy' ⇒ \*pok-eie- (IE). The causative to \*fehan-. The same form of the root is found in Go. faheps f. 'joy, gladness', which was derived from an unattested Go. stative \*fahan continuing \*fagen-. Note that the effects of Vemer's law were undone in Gothic.

\*fagra- adj. 'fit (good, beautiful)' - Go. fagrs adj. 'fit, good', ON fagr adj. 'beautiful, handsome', Far. fagur adj. 'id.', OE feeger adj. 'id.', E fair, OS fagar adj. 'id.', OHG /agar adj. 'id.', MHG vager adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*ph₂l{-r6- (IE) - Lat. pax f. 'peace' ( < "settlement") < \*peh₂K- and pactus 'agreed' < \*ph₂K-to-. An adjective created to the root of \*fogjan- 'to fit together' (q.v.). The comparison with \*fehan- 'to rejoice' is probably erroneous, as ro-adjectives usually take the zero-grade of the root. Also cf. the factitive Go. ga-fahrjan w.v. 'to prepare' < \*fagrjan-.

\*fahiz- n. 'sheep' - ON Jeer n. 'id.', OSw. far n. 'id.', Sw. far n. 'id.' ⇒ \*p6K-es(IE) - Lat. pecus, -oris n. 'sheep; cattle'. An old s-stem \*fahaz, gen. \*fahizaz. The Nordic forms can be unified into a proto-form \*fahaz-, assuming that ON Jeer was affected by R-mutation. Related to the root of\*fahsa- and \*fahti- (q.v.).

\*fahsa- n. 'hair of the head' - ON fax n. 'mane', OE feax n. 'hair of the head', OFri. fax n. 'id.', OS fahs n. 'id.', OHG fahs n. 'id.', MHG vahs m.f n. 'id.' ⇒ \*pol<-so- (IE) - Gr. nEKO<; n. 'fleece' < \*pel<-es-; Gr. n6Koc; m. 'wool, fleece' < \*pol<-o-; Arm. asr 'sheepwool, fleece' < \*poK-u-. A thematicized s-stem closely related to \*fahiz- and \*fahti- (q.v.). See also \*Jehu-.

\*fahti- m. 'fleece' - OSw. fret m. 'id.', OE feht 'vellus', OFri. fecht m. 'wool, fleece', MDu. vacht m./f. 'fleece', Du. vacht c. 'fleece, fur' ⇒ \*poJ<-ti- (IE) - Gr. n£Kw 'to comb (oneself), to card, to shear', Lith. pesti (pesu) 'to pluck' < \*peJ<-e-. A ti-stem to an unattested o-grade present \*fahan- < \*p6J<-e-. Related to the PIE verb \*peJ<-e- 'to pull out hair' as preserved by Lithuanian. See also \*Jehu-.

\*faigi- adj. 'bound to die' - ON feigr adj. 'id.', OE f:i!ge adj. 'id.', E fey, OFri. Jach, fai adj. 'id.', OS feg, fegi adj. 'id.', Du. veeg adj. 'id.', OHG feigi adj. 'id.', G feige adj. 'coward', Swi.feig adj. 'slow, lazy' (IE). The form and meaning of the word point to a gerundive "who is to die". Within Germanic, the adjective can be linked to \*faiha- 1 'hostile' (Heidermanns 1993: 184-85), but it is also possible to derive it directly from the PIE root \*peiJ<- 'to carve, paint', for which see \*faiha- 2. The original meaning must then have been 'marked for death'.

\*faiha- 1 adj. 'hostile' - OE fiih, fiig adj. 'hostile, criminal', E foe, OFri. /ii.ch adj. 'guilty, outlawed', MDu. vee, ge-vee adj. 'hostile', OHG gi-feh adj. 'id.', MHG ge-vech adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*p6i<k'lo- (IE) - Skt. pifona- adj. 'evil, treacherous' < \*piJ<-; Olr. oech m. 'enemy' < \*poirJ<l-o-; Lith. pefkti (peikiu) 'to blame' < \*peik-ie- (also cf. Lith. piktas adj. 'angry' < \*pik-to-). An adjective derived from a root \*peiJ<- or \*peik-. Also cf. Go. ga-faihon w.v. 'to defraud' and OS ii-fehian, OHG fehen 'to treat in a hostile way', whence OE f:i!hO, OFri. faithe, feithe, (M)Du. vete, OHG fehida, G Fehde f. 'feud' < \*faihipo-. See also \*faigi-. \*faiha- 2 adj. OE fiih adj. 'colored', ME Jou adj. 'particolored, variegated, spotted, streaked', OS feh adj. 'colorful', OHG feh adj. 'id.', MHG vech adj. 'variegated' ⇒ \*p6iJ<o- (IE) - ToAB pik- 'to write', Skt. pirrisati 'to carve, cut, adorn', YAv. fra-pixsta- ptc. 'adorned' < \*pi-n-J<-; Lat. pingere 'to paint' < \*pil<-ne-; Gr. noLKiAos adj. 'varicolored' < \*poiJ<-ilo-; Lith. piesti 'to paint, write', OPru. peisiii 'they write' < \*peiJ<-; OCS pbsati (pisp) 'to write' < \*p(e)iJ<-ie-; OCS pbstn adj. 'varicolored' < \*piJ<-ro-. An adjective to the root \*peiJ<- 'to paint'. Also cf. the factitive OHG fehen, MHG vehen w.v. 'to colorize, stain' < \*faihjan- and ON fa w.v. 'to draw, paint' < \*faihojan-.

\*faima(n)- m. 'foam' - Nw. feim(e) m./n. 'thin layer of dew, dust, floating fat', MHG veim m. 'foam, dross' (IE) - Skt. phena- m. 'foam' < \*speh₃i-no-; Lat. spuma f. '(sea) foam' < \*speh₃i-m-eh₂-; OCS pena, Ru. pena, SCr. pjena f. 'foam' < \*peh₃i-no-; OPru. spoayno 'foam' < \*peh₃i-neh₂-; Lith. spaine f. 'foam

\*buttaz 'bottom' and \*hr/man- - \*hr/pan- 'hoarfrost'). De Vaan 2008: 583 points to the problem that no actual mn-stem is attested, but Nw.feime m./f. < \*peh₃i-mon- can be adduced as such. Also note Nw. Jim 'layer of dust or dew' < \*fima-, which may continue the original zero-grade \*ph₃i-mon- (with laryngeal metathesis).

\*faita- adj. 'fat' - ON feitr adj. 'id.', Far. feitur adj. 'id.', Elfd. fiet adj. 'id.', MLG vet adj. 'id.', MHG veiz adj. 'id.', G Cimb. voaz adj. 'id.' (IE?). An adjective probably related to \*fajOJa- (q.v.) < \*poih₃-o-. The Germanic root-final t has no extra-Germanic parallel (Heidermanns 1993: 187), but can tentatively be identified as the zero grade of the root \*deh₃- 'to give', thus starting from a meaning 'to fatten up' ( < "to give to eat''). PGm. \*faitagave rise to a factitive \*faitjan- (ON feita, OHG feizzen 'to make fat'), whose ptc. \*faitida- was again lexicalized, cf. OE [ci!tt, E fat, (M)Du. vet, OHG[eizzit, G feist adj. 'fat'. See also \*fitnan-.

\*fajOJa- adj. 'fat(?)' - Du. dial. vei adj. 'fat, lush, fertile' ::) \*poih₂-o- (IE) - Skt payate 'to swell' < \*peiH-e-; Av. poWpa- 'fat' < \*peiH-tuo-. An adjective derived from the PIE root \*peiH- (cf. LIV2 s.v. \*peiH-). Probably related to \*faita- 'fat' (q.v.).

\*/aka- n. 'enclosure, section' - OE /rec n. 'space, interval, distance, portion of time', OFri. fek n. 'part of the house, niche', MDu. vac n. 'compartment, section', Du. vak n. 'space; profession', OHG [ah n. 'wall, compartment', G Fach n. 'compartment, panel; discipline' ::) \*phi9-o- (IE) - Skt pdjas- n. 'side, surface(?)' < \*pehiB-os-; Gr. m']yvuμL 'to attach, join' < \*pehiB-neu-; Lat pango 'to insert firmly, fix' < \*phi9-neh₂- (with n-infixation through Thurneysen's law); Lat pti.gus m. 'district' < \*pehiB-o-. Also cf. MLG vake, MDu. vake, vaec, Du. vaak adv. 'often'.

\*fakk/gon- w.v. 'to become sleepy' - E to fag w.v. 'to tire, weary', obs. 'to hang loose, flap', Scot to faik w.v. 'to fail from weariness; to cease moving' (IE). A West Germanic iterative: the consonant variation of E to fag < \*faggonand Scot. to faik < \*fakon- is suggestive of a paradigm 3sg. \*fakkopi, 3pl. \*fagunanpi < \*po(f<l-neh₂-. In view of E fag 'fag-end, hanging end of a piece of cloth, untwisted end of a rope', it seems likely that the meaning 'to become

\*falan- m. 'board' - OE fa/a m. 'id.' (NEUR) - ORu. pol'b m. 'foundation', Ru. pol m. 'floor'; Ukr. pol m. 'sleeping bench' < \*pol-o- (also cf. Ru. polfca, SCr. polica f. 'shelf and with an ablauting root OCS palica f. 'staff, Ru. palica f. 'club, cudgel', SCr. piilica f. 'stick, staff; Ru. palka f. 'stick, staff < \*pol-). The connection with the Slavic forms is beyond doubt, but the relation between the roots \*pol- and \*pol-(?) is unclear (Derksen 2008: 390, 410). See also \*faluda- and \*felo-.

\*falgo- f. 'fallow land' - ME falge, fa/we 'arable land lying idle; plowing as part of the fallowing process', Efallow, OFri.fallach 'arable land', MDu. valge f. 'id.' ⇒ \*polh₁tk'l-eh₂- (EUR) - Gaul. olca 'arable land' ( = Fr. ouche 'id.'); perhaps also Lith. plė̃sti (-siu, -siau), Latv. plēst 'to tear, pluck; to plow land for the first time' < \*pleh₁ḱ-ie-. Also cf. MHG valgen, Du. dial. valgen, WFri.felgje 'to plow' < \*falgojan- and OEfealh f. 'harrow' < \*fa/go-.

\*falkan- m. 'falcon' - ON falki m. 'id.', Far. falkur m. 'id.', OSw. falke,falker m. 'id.', MDu. valce m. 'id.', Du. valk c. 'id.', OHG fa/co m. 'id.', G Falke m. 'id.' (GM?). A West Germanic word: the Norpic forms are irregular, and thus point to borrowing from West Germanic. No certain etymology. The derivation from \*falwa- 'pale, fallow' is possible, but uncertain in spite of the potential parallel G dial. Falke m. 'fallow horse'. The latter word has -lk- from -lch-, and belongs to G Pal.fa/ch adj. 'pale' < \*fa/ha- < \*p61K-O-, cf. Lith. pal5as adj. 'pale (of cows)'. Slightly more attractive is the link with OPru. pele 'harrier'. The Germanic word must then have a k-suffix like e.g. \*alkon-, \*balika/on- and \*habuka-. Latfalco was adopted from Germanic.

\*fallan- s.v. 'to fall' - ON falla s.v. 'id.', Far. fa Ila s.v. 'id.', Elfd. folla s.v. 'id.', OE[allan s.v. 'id.', E to fall, OFri.falla s.v. 'id.', OS[allan s.v. 'id.', Du. vallen s.v. 'id.', OHG[allan s.v. 'id.', Gfallen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂p6-h₃/h₁- (IE) - Identical to Gr. arr6Uuμt, -μat 'to wreck, destroy, lose', mid. 'to go to waste, be ruined, be lost', Lat aboleo, -ere 'to destroy; banish; abolish', Arm. p'lanim 'to fall', Lith. pulti (puolu), Latv. pult 'to fall', OPru. ail-pallai 'finds'.

\*falljan- w.v. 'to fell' - ON fella w.v. 'id.', Far. fella w.v. 'id.', Elfd. fella s.v. 'id.', OE fiellan w.v. 'id.', E to fell, OFri. fella w.v. 'id.', OS fellian w.v. 'id.', Du. vellen w.v. 'id.', OHGfellan w.v. 'id.', Gfiillen w.v. 'id.' (DRV). The causative to \*fallan- (q.v.).

\*falon- f. 'plain' - OSw. fa la f. 'id.', Sw. fa la 'id.' ⇒ \*po/h₂-eh₂- (IE) - OCS polje, Ru. pole, SCr. polje n. 'field' < \*polh₂-io-. Derived from the PIE root \*pelh₂- 'flat', for which see \*fulmo-.

\*falþan- s.v. 'to fold, ply' - Go. falþan s.v. 'id.', ON falda s.v. 'to hood oneself, Far. falda w.v. 'to put on clothes', Elfd. folda w.v. 'to hem, seam', OE fealdan s.v. 'to fold', E to fold, WFri. faldzje w.v. 'id.', MDu. volden w.v. 'id.', Du. vouwen s./w.v. 'id.', OHG faldan, faltan s.v. 'id.', G fa/ten w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*p61-t-e- (IE) - Cf. Gr. c5mA6oi;, c5mA.oui; adj. 'twofold', Lat. duplus adj. 'double, twofold' < \*dui-plo-; Arm. haf 'time' < \*pol-o-(?). · An o-grade intensive verb. The Pre-Gm. root-final \*t appears to be suffixal. Also cf. MDu. volde, Du. vouw, MHG valte, G Falte f. 'fold' < \*fa/do- and Go. ain-falþs, ON einjaldr, OE ii.n-feald, OFri., OS en-fald, OHG einjald adj. 'simple' < \*-falda-.

\*faluda- m. 'pen(?)' - Da. fold m. 'enclosure, pasture', OE fald, falod, falud m. 'fold, stable', E fold, OS faled m. 'id.', MDu. vaelde, vaelt m. 'enclosure, yard; dunghill', Du. mest-vaalt c. 'dunghill' (GM). A word with an original meaning 'enclosure', possibly related to 'board' and esp. OE/a/a 'tabula' < \*fa/an- (q.v.). The word contains the \*-uda- suffix that also occurs in e.g. OS rakud m. 'house', OE rceced n. 'house, hall' < \*rakuda-. Theoretically, it could be the zero grade of the PIE root \*uedh- 'to bind, tie' (for which see \*wedan-), but this is uncertain.

\*falwa- adj. 'pale' - ON fplr adj. 'id.', OE fealo adj. 'id.', E fallow, OS falu adj. 'fallow, yellow', Du. vaal adj. 'faded, drab', OHG falo adj. 'fallow, yellow', G falb adj. 'fallow, dun' ⇒ \*pol-uo- (IE) - Lat. pullus adj. 'drab-colored, sombre', Lith. palvas adj. 'light yellow, straw-colored', OCS plaVb adj. 'white', Ru. pol6vyj, polov6j adj. 'pale yellow, sandy', SCr. plav adj. 'blond, blue' <

\*fanan- m. 'cloth' - Go. Jana m. 'id.', OE Jana m. 'flag, banner', E vane, OFri. Jona m. 'id.', OS fa no m. 'id.', Du. vaan c. 'id.', OHG fa no m. 'id.', G Fahne m. 'id.' ⇒ \*peh₂-on- (EUR) - Gr. mlVTI f. 'the thread of the woof; woof, Lat. pannus m. 'piece of cloth, rag' < \*pehi-no/eh₂-. A European word. If \*fanan- is related to Gr. mlVT) and Lat. pannus ( < \*panus by the littera-rule), as is assumed by Pokorny IEW: 788, it seems logical to derive the Germanic form from \*ph₂-no- or \*peh₂-n6- (with Dybo's law). From this perspective, it is tempting to assume that the Gm. n-stem (like the Latin and Greek forms?) was remodeled from \*p(e)h₂-on, gen. \*ph₂-n-6s. Less likely is the connection with OCS o-pona f. 'curtain' < \*ponhi-eh₂-, which was derived from the root \*penhi- 'to spin'.

\*fandon- w.v. 'to investigate' - OE fandian w.v. 'to test, investigate, visit', OFri.fand(el)ia w.v. 'to visit a sick person', WFri. (early) /dn/e w.v. 'to collect fish traps', fandelje w.v. 'to collect, fetch', MDu. vanden w.v. 'to test, investigate, visit', OHG fandon w.v. 'to investigate', MHG vanden w.v. 'to visit', G fahnden w.v. 'to search, hunt for' ⇒ \*pont-neh₂- (DRV?). An o-grade iterative to \*finþan-. See also \*fundon-.

\*fane prep. 'from' - OFri. fan prep. 'id.', OS fan, Jana prep. 'id.', Du. van prep. 'id.', OHG Jana, Jona prep. 'id.', G van prep. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂po-neh₁ (DRV) - Lith. pa- pref., OCS po, Ru. po, SCr. po prep. 'after, on, by, at', Alb. pa prep. 'without'. A directional derived from the element \*fa, continuing the original allative case \*h₂p-6 of a PIE root noun \*h₂ep-, with the ablative suffix \*-ne < \*-neh₁. See further under the closely related \*aba.

\*fangi- m. 'catch' - Go. ga-fahs m. 'id.', ON fengr m. 'id.', OE Jeng m. 'grasp, span, hug', OFri.feng,fang,fong m. 'catch, capture', OHGfang m. 'id.', G Fang m. 'id.' (DRV). An i-stem derived from \*fanhan- (q.v.).

\*fangjan- - \*fankjan- w.v. 'to ignite' - MDu. ont-fengen, -fenken w.v. 'id.', MHG vengen, enpfengen, venken w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*ponk-eie- (GM). A causative to the strong verb \*finkan-, which replaces expected \*finhan< \*penk-e-: the alternation of \*fangjan- MDu. ont-fengen, MHG vengen) and \*fankjan- (MDu. ont-fenken, MHG venken) indicates that both \*finkan- and \*fankjan- adopted their root-final consonant from the iterative \*funkon- < \*pnk-neh₂- (q.v.).

\*fanhan- s.v. 'to catch' - Go. fahan s.v. 'id.', ON fa s.v. 'id.', Far. faa s.v. 'id.', Elfd . .fi1 s.v. 'id.', OE/on s.v. 'id.', OFri.fa s.v. 'id.', OSfahan s.v. 'id.', Du. vangen s.v. 'id.', OHGfahan s.v. 'id.', Gfangen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*p6nl<-e- (NEUR). A univerbation of the adverb \*h₂po and the root \*h₁enl<- 'to obtain' (or to PIE \*h₂enll- 'to reach', Scheungraber 2012). A similar univerbation is \*bringan- 'to bring' (q.v.) < \*pro-h₁enll-e-. Also cf. \*fangi-.

\*fanja- n. 'fen, swamp' - Go. Jani n. 'mud', ON fen n. 'fen, bog', OE fen m./n. 'marsh; mud', E fen, OFri. Jenne m. 'pasture', OS Jeni n. 'fen', MDu. veen, vene· n. 'bog', Du. veen n. 'id.', ven n. 'small lake', OHGfenni n. 'swamp', MHG venne n. 'id.', G Fenn n. 'id.' ⇒ \*pon-io- (EUR) - OPru. pannean acc. 'ditch' < \*pan-io-; Gaul. anam acc.f. 'paludem' < \*pen-eh₂- (with Joseph's rule -eRa- > -aRa-?), Mir. an, en 'water' < \*pen- (cf. enach n. 'swamp' < \*pen-akos-?). A collective noun potentially identical to OPru. pannean. The derivation of the Irish forms, esp. the variation of Mir. en and an, is problematic, but it is possible that radical e was mutated into a by Joseph's rule. Related to OE fyne 'mold'?
\*fanþjan- m. 'foot soldier(?)' - OE feoa m. 'foot soldier; troop', OHG fendo m. 'foot soldier, pedestrian', MHG vende m. 'pedestrian, boy' (DRV). Derived from \*finþan- with the agentive jan-suffix.

\*faran- s.v. 'to go' - Go.faran s.v. 'id.', ONfara s.v. 'id.', Elfd.fara s.v. 'id.', OE faran s.v. 'id.', E to fare, OFri. Jara s.v. 'id.', Du. varen w.v. 'to sail', OHG faran s.v. 'to go, travel, pass', G fahren s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*p6r-e- (IE) - Skt. pf-parti 'to bring across' < \*pi-par-; OAv. (fra-)fra lsg.subj.aor. 'to cross' < \*pre-pr-e-; Skt. parayati 'to make cross', YAv. ni-paraiieiTJti 3pl.pres. 'to lead to', OCS pariti 'to fly' < \*por-eie-; Gr. m:tpw 'to pierce, to penetrate' < \*per-ie-. An a-grade "intensive-iterative" verb. The ablaut of the preterite, i.e. \*for-, arose secondarily on the basis of verbs with roots of the structure CHC. See also \*fardi-, \*farjon-, \*farma-, \*Jori·, \*forjan- and \*furdu-.

\*fardi- f. 'journey' - ONferlJ f. 'id.', Far.ferlJ f. 'id.', Elfd.ferd f. 'id.', OEfierd f. 'expedition', OFri. ferd f. 'journey, voyage', OS Jard f. 'id.', Du. vaart f. 'canal', OHG fart f. 'trip, voyage', G Fart f. 'id.' ⇒ \*por-t[- (DRV). A ti-stem to the strong verb \*faran- (q.v.).

\*fargjan- w.v. 'to push (down)' - Icel. fergja w.v. 'to press, put pressure on', Far.fergja w.v. 'to press, squeeze, push', Nw.fergja w.v. 'to press/weigh down' ⇒ \*h₂po-h₂ork-eie- (IE) - Close to Lat. porcere 'to prevent' <
\*h₂po·h₂ork-eie-; also cf. Hitt. barzi - barkanzi 'to hold, have' < \*h₂erk-ti, \*h₂rk-enti; Gr. ap1c£w 'to avert, defend; to suffice', Lat. arceo 'to contain; to

label_gloss: 129 keep out' < \*h₂erk-eie-; Ann. arge/ 'hindrance' (on the /-suffix, see Martirosyan 2009: 133). The connection with Skt. pp:uikti
opening: \*farha- 129 keep out' < \*h₂erk-eie-; Ann. arge/ 'hindrance' (on the /-suffix, see Martirosyan 2009: 133). The connection with Skt. pp:uikti - prncati 'to fill, give abundantly' < 3sg. \*pr-n-ek-ti, 3pl. \*pr-n-k-enti and Skt. piparkti 'id.' < \*pi-pe/ork- and Mir. ercaid 'to fill' < \*perk-eh₂-ie- is not impossible, as the original PIE meaning may have been 'to stuff, whence 1) 'to fill' and 2) 'to weigh down'. However, it seems preferable to assume a direct link with Lat porcere 'to prevent', which continues the root \*h₂e/ork- prefixed with the adverb \*h₂p-6. The fact that this univerbation is found in both Latin and Germanic probably means that it continues an old PIE syntagm. Also cf. ON, Far., Nw. farga w.v. 'to press', which in combination with Nw. dial. farka w.v. 'to press someone into something' points to an iterative 3sg. \*fark6/Ji, 3pl. \*fargunanpi < Pre-Gm. \*pork-neh₂-ti, \*pork-nh₂-enti.

\*farha- m. 'farrow, pig' - OEfearh m. 'id.', Efarrow, OHGfarah,farh n. 'id.', MHG varch m. 'id.' ⇒ \*porl<-o- (IE) - Identical to Khot. pa'sa, Kurd. purs 'pig', Lat porcus m. 'id.', Olr. poet. ore m. 'id.', Lith. paf5as m. 'castrated boar' < \*porl<-o-. One of the Indo-European words for 'pig(let)'. In view of the possible connection with Gr. rr6pKo<; m. 'fishing-net' and Arm. ors 'hunt; catch; game' < \*porl<-o-, the word may have originally referred to the wild boar rather than the domesticated animal. Also cf. the diminutives MDu. verkijn, varkijn, Du. varken n. 'pig' < \*farha-kina- and OHGfarhili(n), MHG varchelfn, G Ferkel n. 'id.' < \*farha-lfna-.

\*farjon- f. 'ferry' - ON ferja f. 'id.', MLG vere f./n. 'id.', MDu. vere f./n. 'id.', Du. veer n. 'id.', MHG vere f. 'id.', G Fiihre f. 'id.' (ORV). Derived from Go. farjan, OS ferian, MHG vern 'to travel by boat', whence also OHG ferjo, MHG, MDu. vere m. 'ferryman'. The jan-verb is again based on ON far n. '(means of) passage, ship', OE Jeer n. 'journey; ship', OHG var n. 'place to cross a river' < \*Jara- (cf. Gr. rr6poi; m. 'passage (over a body of water), ford', MP, NP btir 'shore, bank'). Also cf. ON ferja 'to ferry', OE ferian 'to convey' < \*farjojan-.

\*farma- m. 'moving(?)' - ON farmr m. 'freight, cargo', Far. farmur m. 'id.', Nw. farm m. 'cargo; flock (of cattle)', OE fearm m. 'id.', OS farm m. 'onslaught', OHG farm m. 'fast ship', MHG varm m. 'id.' ⇒ \*por-mo- (ORV). A mo-stem based on the strong verb \*fa ran- ( q.v.).

\*farna- m. 'fern' - OE /earn, fern m. 'id.', E fern, OS farn m./n. 'id.', MDu. varen m. 'id.', Du. varen c. 'id.', OHG farn, farm m./n. 'id.', G Fam m. 'id.' ⇒ \*ptorH-no- (IE) - Skt paf\la- n. 'feather, leaf, Av. parana- n. 'feather, wing' <

\*farwa- adj. 'colorful' - OHG faro adj. 'id.', MHG vare adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*porli-u6- (IE) - Skt. pffoi- adj. 'spotted, varicolor' < \*prli-ni-; Gr. nEpKVoc; adj. 'dark-spotted' < \*perli-no-; Mir. ere adj. 'spotted, red', MW erch adj. 'spotted" < \*perli-o-. An adjective derived from the PIE root \*perli- 'spotted' with the suffix -uo-, which is common in chromonyms. Also cf. OHG farawa, G Farbe, MLG varwe, verwe f. 'color', MDu. varuwe, verwe f. 'color, paint', Du. verf c. 'paint' < \*farw6- and OFri.ferwen adj. 'colorful' < \*farwina-.

\*farza(n)- m. 'young bull' - ON farri m. 'bull', OE fearr m. 'bull, ox', MDu. var(r)e m. 'young bull', Du. var c. 'id.', OHG farro m. 'bull, ox', G Farre m. 'young bull' ⇒ \*pors-6(n)- (EUR). An n-stem possibly created to the adj. E farrow, WFri. fear, Du. vaar adj. 'without calf < \*far(z)wa-. The underlying root \*pors- can be an extension of \*por- 'to be with young', cf. Lat. pari6 'to give birth to, bear' < \*pr-ie-, Lith. perhi 'to brood, sit on eggs' < \*per-eh₁- and - within Germanic - \*fora- 'piglet'. Also cf. the feminine MHG verse, G Fiirse, MLG verse, MDu. vaerse, ve(e)rse f., Du. vaars c. 'heifer' < \*farsj6- < \*p6rs-ieh₂-.

\*fasa/iin- m./f. 'fringe; fiber' - OE /res n. 'fringe' ( < \*fasa-), MDu. vase f. 'fiber; fringe', OHG Jaso m., fasa f. 'fiber, fringe, thread', MHG vase m./f. 'id.', G Faser m. 'fiber' (DRV?). An etymologically difficult word. It has been compared to Ru. pcismo n. 'string' < \*poHs-mo-(?), thus arriving at a Pre-Gm. formation \*p!Js-on-, but within Germanic, \*fasa/6n- can hardly be separated from \*fes6n- ( < \*fis6n-), which developed from the PIE root \*tpis-, cf. Gr. nT[cmw 'to pound or grind corn in a mortar'. I therefore assume that \*fasa/6n- was created on the basis of an unattested tudciti-verb \*fisan- < \*tpis-e- that was transferred to the class 4/5 strong verbs after a-mutation had taken place. For a similar derivational history, cf. \*hrepan-, \*stekan- and \*swepan- which, too, seem to be originally zero-grade presents. In view of the secondary iteratives \*hrutt6n(see \*hrudjan-) and \*stuk(k}6n- belonging to these verbs, it is furthermore likely that the postulated \*fesan- gave rise to a formation \*fus(s}6n-, as implied by G fus(s )en 'to fray' (cf. G Rhnl. fuse/ 'fuzz, frills; drizzle'), E to fuzz 'to ravel out; to cover with particles; to blur', to which also MLG vossen 'with loose fur' is related. Also cf. ON f9sull m. 'thread' < \*fasula-.

\*fastu- adj. 'firm, solid' - ON fastr adj. 'id.', Far.fastur adj. 'id.', Elfd. fast adj. 'id.', OE fæst adj. 'id.', E fast, OFri.fest adj. 'id.', OS fast,Jesti adj. 'id.', Du. vast adj. 'id.', OHG festi adj. 'id.', G fest adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*ph₂sth₂-o- (IE) - Arm. hast adj. 'firm, steady, standing still, tough' < \*pHst-i-. A u-stem (remodeled into a ja-stem in WGm.) directly related to Arm. hast. The combination of these forms straightforwardly points to a PIE root \*pl,;lst-; the reconstruction \*post- is impossible, because this would have given Arm. \*\*host (note that the connection with Skt. pastya- 'homestead' < \*h₂po-sth₂-io-(?) therefore must be abandoned). I derive this root from \*phiO-sth₂-, i.e. a compound of \*pehiB- 'to become firm' (cf. Gr. rn'JyvuμL 'to fasten, fix, make solid') and \*steh₂- 'to stand'. Apparently, the g was lost in the heavy cluster \*ph₂gsth₂-. Within Germanic, also cf. Go. fastan, ON fasta, OE fastian, OFri. festia, OHG fasten w.v. 'to fast' < \*fasten- and the derived Go.fastubni, OS fastunnia f. 'fasting' < \*fast-umnjo-.

\*fata- n. 'vat, vessel' - ON fat n. 'id.', OE fæt n. 'id.', E vat, OS fat n. 'id.', Du. vat n. 'id.', OHG faz n. 'id.', MHG vaz n. 'id.', G Fafi n. 'id.' ⇒ \*podo- (NIE) - Lith. puodas 'pot'. The Lithuanian word is identical to PGm. \*fata-. These forms are reminiscent of ON pottr, OE, OFri. pot 'pot', which, however, have been explained as loanwords from Fr. pot ( < VLat. pottus < Lat. potus 'drinking cup'). Fi. pa ta, Hung. fazek 'pot' < PFU \*pata has been analyzed as a loanword from PIE (Parpola/Carpelan 2007: 122), but it is highly unlikely, in view of the limitation to Baltic and Germanic, that the word was inherited from the parent language.

\*fatta- adj. 'bent' - ON fattr adj. 'bowed backward' ⇒ \*pt-no-(?) (IE?) - To Lat. pandus 'bent' < \*pt-n6-? The Germanic word has been compared to Lat. pandus, which developed from \*pt-n6- through a form \*pat-no- with schwa secundum that underwent Thurneysen's law (\*-tn- > \*-nd-). However, the comparison can only be maintained by assuming a similar schwa secundum in Germanic, for which the evidence is extremely limited (see \*hesan- - \*hazan- 'hare').

\*fat(t)on- w.v. 'to fetch' - ON fa ta w.v. 'to step', Ice!. fatta w.v. 'to catch on, take in', Far. fatta (in: lukki fatti teg! 'may you prosper') w.v. 'to catch; grasp', OE fatian w.v. 'to fetch', OFri. fatia w.v. 'to load', MLG vaten w.v. 'to grasp, pack, load, prepare, dress', MDu. vaten, vatten w.v. 'to seize, assume, understand', Du. vatten, dial. vaten w.v. 'to seize, understand', OHG fazzon w.v. 'to load, pack, prepare', G /assen w.v. 'to catch, grasp' ⇒ \*pod-neh₂(NEUR) - OCS po-pasti 'to fetch' < \*p6d-e-. An iterative created to a lost o-grade iterative-intensive verb \*fatan- < \*p6d-e-, as retrieved from OCS po-pasti.

\*faþma- m. 'fathom' - ON faðmr m. 'id.', Far. favnur m. 'id.', Elfd. farm m. 'id.', OE fæþm m. 'fathom; bosom', E fathom, OFri. fethm m. 'id.', OS fathmos m.pl. 'two stretched arms', Du. vadem c. 'fathom', OHGfadam,fadum m. 'id.', G Faden m. 'thread' ⇒ \*pot(h₂)-mo- (WEUR) - Gael. aitheamh f. 'thread, fathom (of rope)', OW etem, W edau 'thread' < \*pet(h₂)-i-m-eh₂-. A Germanic-Celtic formation (cf. Hamp 2007) usually derived from the PIE root \*peth₂- 'to spread', cf. Gr. πετάννυμι. Further cf. MDu. vedemen w.v. 'to measure with outspread arms, fathom' < \*faþmjan- and OE f«!amian w.v. 'to fathom, to embrace', OHGfadamon w.v. 'to spin, to sew' < \*faþmōjan-.

\*faþō- f. 'fence' - Go. faþa f. 'hedge, fence, wall', MHG vade f. 'fence' ⇒ \*ph₂-teh₂-(?) (IE?). No accepted etymology. Perhaps from \*p!J.2-t-eh₂- to PIE \*peh₂-t- 'to protect, herd, graze' (see \*fOdjan-), or to Hitt pattar - pattan- n. 'basket' if the underlying root \*potH- originally designated 'wickerwork'. The variant MHG vatte seems to point to a geminated proto-form \*faþþōn-.

\*faujan- 1 w.v. 'to let rot' - ON feyja w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*pouH-eie- (IE) - Skt. pilyati 'to become foul/putrid; to stink' < \*puH-ie-; Lith. pilti (puvu) 'to rot' < \*puH-. The causative to a PIE present \*puH-ie- 'to rot'. See also \*fUan- and \*fula-.
