\*faujan- 2 w.v. 'to sift' - OHG fowen,fewen w.v. 'id.', MHG vewen w.v. 'id.' ⇒
\*pouh₂-eie- (IE) - Skt. puncfo 'to clean' < \*pu-n-eh₂-; Lat. purus 'clean', Olr. ur, w ir 'green, fresh' < \*puh₂-r-o- etc. An old causative to the PIE root \*peuh₂-.

\*fawa- adj. 'few' - Go. Jaus\* adj. 'id.', ON far adj. 'id.', Far. faur adj. 'id.', Elfd. /tier adj. 'id.', OE/ea adj. 'id.', E/ew, OS fa adj. 'id.', OHGfo adj. 'id.' ɢ \*ph₂u-o(EUR) - Gr. naupoc; adj. 'small, little, few', Lat. parvus adj. 'small' (with metathesis) < \*peh₂u-ro-; Lat. paucus adj. 'few, small in number' < \*peh₂u-llo-. The OHG formfoh has been adduced to reconstruct the Germanic form as \*fauha-, so as to equate it with Lat. paucus, but the h can be epenthetic in this form. This leaves \*fawa- as the only possible proto-form. It must be derived from a PIE zero-grade form \*p!J.2u-o-, with (regular) vocalization of the laryngeal, as the full-grade \*peh₂u-o- would have given \*\*Joa-.

\*fean- s.v. 'to blame' - Go. faian\* s.v. 'id.' ɢ \*pehre- (IE) - Gr. πῆμα n. 'harm' < \*peh₂-men- (also cf. am'Jμwv adj. 'unharmed' < \*n-peh₂-mon-). A strong verb related to \*fi(j)en- (q.v.). The Gothic form (attested only once as 3pl.pres.mid. faianda) is to be interpreted as fean, with t lowered from e in hiatus position; the digraph ai does not allow us to assume a j-suffix (cf. e.g. Seebold 1970: 188).

\*fedwar- num. 'four' - Go. fidwor num. 'id.', ON fj6rir, acc. fj6ra, f. fj6rar, n. fjpgur, gen. fjpgurra, dat. jj6rum num. 'id.', Far. JYra num. 'id.', Elfd. fiuorer, acc. fiuora, f.fiuorer, n.fiuorq, dat.fiuorum num. 'id.', OEfeower num. 'id.', E four, OFri. fiuwer, flower, fiawer num. 'id.', OS fiuwar, fior, n. fieri, dat. fiwarun num. 'id.', Du. vier num. 'id.', OHG fior num. 'id.', G vier num. 'id.' ⇒ \*kwet-uor- (IE) - ToA stwar, ToB stwer, Skt. catvaras, acc. caturas, n. catvari, YAv. ca8þaro, acc. caturq, Arm. c'or-k' < \*kwet-u(o)r-; Gr. -rfoaap£s, Dor. -rE-cop£s, Olr. ceth(a)ir, OW petguar, MW pedwar, Lith. keturi, acc. keturis, OCS cetyre, f./n. cetyri, Ru. cetjre, SCr. cetiri num. 'id.' < \*kwet-ur-; Lat. quattuor num. 'id.' < \*kwt-u6r(-h₂). The IE word for 'four' (with \*/- instead of \*hw- due to influence from \*ftmfe 'five'). The reconstruction of the Germanic paradigm is not entirely straightforward 1) because of the isolated position of Go. fidwor < \*fedwor, appa,rently continuing an old collective formation \*kwet-u6r(-h₂), and 2) because of the difficulties posed by the NWGm. forms. ON fj6rir, -ar, fjpgur continue \*fedur-aiz, \*-oz, \*-o through syncopated \*fjpareR, \*fjparaR, \*fjpauru: \*a was lost before r (causing lengthening of the preceding diphthong \*jp) in the masculine and feminine forms, while in the neuter, it changed into \*g due to the labial environment (as in ON jugr 'udder' < \*eudur-). In WGm., the material points to \*fewar-, but it is unclear how this form could have developed from \*fedwar- (PIE < \*kwet-uor-) in a regular way (for an attempt, cf. Stiles 1984). The first dental may have been dissimilated in the ordinal \*fewurp/dan- < quasi-PIE \*kwet-ur-tHo-, just as \*sebunp/dan- 'seventh' developed from \*septm-tHo-. Given the exclusively WGm. distribution of \*fewar-, it seems pointless, at any rate, to reconstruct a Pre-Gm. stem \*pekw6r- (cf. Ross/Berns 1992: 583). For the retention of the dental, cf. Go. fidur-falp

\*fehan· s.v. 'to rejoice' - OE gefeon, -feohan s.v. 'to be glad, rejoice, exult', OHG gi-fehan s.v. 'to rejoice' ⇒ \*pel<-e- (IE). A not too well attested strong verb. Its past participle \*fagena- became lexicalized as ON feginn, Elfd. faingen, OE fcegen adj. 'happy', and served as the basis for Go. faginon, ON fagna, Elfd. faingen, OHG faginon, OE fcegenian 'to gladden' < \*fagenojan-. See also \*fehi- and \*fagjan-.
\*fehi· adj. 'satisfactory(?)' - Go. ga-fehaba adv. 'properly' (DRV). A V[ddhi-gerundive to the strong verb \*fehan-.

\*fehtan- s.v. 'to fight' - OE feohtan s.v. 'id.', E to fight, OFri. fiuchta s.v. 'id.', Du. vechten s.v. 'id.', OHGfehtan s.v. 'id.', Gfechten s.v. 'id.' (GM). A verb assumed by many to be akin to Gr. rrEKTW 'to comb, card, shear', Lat pecto 'to pluck' < \*pe/{-t-e- (for which see \*fahti-), but the connection depends on the conjectural semantic shift from 'to pluck, pull hair' > 'to fight' (cf. LIV2: 467).

\*fehu- n. 'cattle' - Go. faihu n. 'movable goods, possessions, cattle', ON fe, gen. fjár n. 'cattle, property, money', Far. fre, gen. flggjar n. 'cattle', OE feoh, fioh n. 'cattle, property, money', Efee, OSfehu n. 'cattle; property', Du. vee n. 'cattle', OHG fihu n. 'cattle, animal, money', G Vieh n. 'cattle' ⇒ \*peK-u- (IE) - Skt. p<ifo-, pasri- m./n. 'cattle', Av. pasu- m. 'id.', Lat. pecu n. 'flock, herd' (-u < obi. \*-ou-, cf. genu 'knee' < \*gen-ou-), OPru. pecku, Lith. pekus m. 'cattle' < \*pel<-u-. An ancient PIE u-stem derived from the root \*pe/{- 'to pluck wool', for which see \*fahti- and \*fakk/gon-. The derivation of \*pel<-u- from this root is a clear semantic indication that the first livestock to be kept by the Indo-Europeans consisted of sheep, not cows.

\*fel(e)sa- m. 'mountain' - ON fell, fjall n. 'id.', Far. fjall n. 'id.', OS felis,filis, ft/is m. 'rock, stone', OHGfelis m. 'rock, cliff, MHG ve/s(e) m. 'id.', G Fe/sen m. 'id.' ⇒ \*pe/o/-es-o- (EUR) - Olr. ail f. 'boulder, rock grave-stone' < \*pl-s-i-; Gr. Hsch. rrE>J .. cx 'stone' < \*pe/-s-eh₂-. ON fell, fjall po ins to \*fel(V)sa- (with assimilation of the s and breaking of -e- to -ja-), but the WGm. attestations can go back to both \*felesa- or \*Jalisa-. The latter form is supported by OFr. falise, Fr. fa/aise, fa/oise, a Franconian loanword. The Swiss dialects that differentiate between high e from OHG \*iiz and low e from OHG e, cf. Jaun and Appenzellfe/sa, are ambiguous in this particular case, however, because the vowel may have arisen due to secondary raising of low to high e before \*i in the next syllable (Karsten 1903: 259). On the morphological level, the word looks like a thematicization of an old s-stem. The expected paradigm \*pe/-os, gen. \*pl-es-(o]s fails to account for the o-grade, however. Perhaps, we are dealing with an originally static s-stem, viz. nom. \*p6/-s, gen. \*pel-s-s.

\*felgo- f. 'rim ofa wheel' - OEfea/g f. 'id.', Efelly, MDu. velge, vellige f. 'felly; patella', Du. velg c. 'felly', OHG felga f. 'felly, hoop', G Felge f. 'felly' ⇒ \*pe/gh-eh₂- (NEUR) - Ru. p6loz m. 'runner, skid', Slov. pliiz m. 'plow sole; strip' < \*po/gh·o-. An etymologically ambiguous word possibly related to the aforementioned Slavic forms. Another possibility is to connect \*felhan- in the sense of 'to go'. The link with e.g. Skt. parfr<i- n. 'flank, side', NP pah/U 'side, rib', Oss.

\*felhan- s.v. 'to hide' - Go. fi/han s.v. 'id.', ON feta s.v. 'to hide, conceal; to give in trust', OE felan, feolan, felgan s.v. 'to stick, adhere; to proceed, go, reach; to undergo', OS bi-felhan s.v. 'to entrust, bestow, command; to bury', Du. velen w.v. 'to suffer', OHG felhan, fel(a)han s.v. 'to trust, hand over' ⇒ \*pelk-e- (GM). Given the disparate semantics of the attestations it is difficult to get to an extra-Germanic etymology. The connection with OPru. pelkis 'cloak' is uncertain, at any rate, as the meaning 'to conceal' as well as 'to entrust, bestow, command' may have developed from 'to stow'. The meanings 'to go' and 'to suffer' as found in Old English and Dutch still remain problematic, and may point to an etymologically dissimilar strong verb related to \*fulgen- 'to follow' (q.v.).

\*fella- n. 'membrane, skin, hide' - Go. pruts-fill n. 'leprosy', ON fell, /jail n. 'skin', OSw. fire/ n. 'id.', Sw. fjiill n. 'id.', OE fell n. 'id.', E fell, OS fell n. 'skin, membrane', Du. vet n. 'id.', OHG fel n. 'skin, pelt', G Fell n. 'id.' ⇒ \*pe/-n6- (EUR) - Gr. m::Uopaq>o<; adj. 'sewing skins together' < \*pet-no-; Lat. pel/is f. 'skin' < \*pel-ni-; OCS pe/ena f. 'band for swathing children', Ru. pelend f. 'shroud, nappy', SCr. pe/ena f. 'bandage, shroud' < \*pel-en-eh₂-. All the other IE languages point to a suffix with a nasal, and it is therefore legitimate to similarly derive the Gm. form from \*pel-n6-. It is possible that this stem developed from the genitive of the mn-stem \*felman- 'membrane' in which the m was dissimilated, i.e. \*pe/-mn-6s, but not necessarily so.

\*felma(n)- m. 'membrane' - OE reger-felma m. 'skin of an egg', E film, MDu. velm, vilm m. 'film' ⇒ \*pel-mon- (EUR) - Gr. nO .. μa n. 'sole of the foot' < \*pet-men-. A considerably old mn-stem to the root of \*fella- 'skin' (q.v.). In Anglo-Frisian, the word seems to have been supplanted by the diminutive: OE ft/men n. 'skin, membrane, husk', OFri. fi/mene f. 'skin (on the knee?)' < \*felm-fna/6-.
\*felo- f. 'board' - ON fjp/ f. 'id.', Far. fjRJ/ f. 'id.', Elfd. fyol f. 'id.' ⇒ \*pe/-eh₂- (NEUR). An e-grade formation closely related to \*fa/an- (q.v.).

\*fetpa- m. 'field' - OSw. fiiilder m. 'isolated field', OE fe/d, feald m. 'field', E field, OFri.feld n. 'id.', OSfeld m. 'id.', Du. veld n. 'id.', OHGfeld n. 'id.', G Feld n. 'id.' ⇒ \*pe/th₂-0· (IE). Derived from the PIE root \*pelth₂· 'flat', for which see \*fuldo-.
\*felu- - \*fulu-

\*felu- - \*fulu- adj. 'much, many' - Go. filu adj. 'id.', ON fjpl- pref. 'id.', Far. fjflJI- pref. 'id.', OE Jela, Jeala, Jeola adj. 'id.', OFri. Jelo, Jele, Jule, Joie adj. 'id.', OS filu,filo adj. 'id.', MDu. veel adj. 'id.', OHG filu,filo adj. 'id.', G vie/ adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*pelh₁-u- (IE) - Skt. puni-, OAv. pouru-, YAv. pouru- adj. 'much, abundant', OP paruv adj. 'much, too much, very', NP firih comp. 'more', Oss. fyr /fur adj. 'much' < \*plh₁-u-; Gr. noA.uc; adj. 'much, many, often' < \*polh₁-u-; Olr. ii adj. 'many' < \*pelh₁-u-. A u-stem to the root \*p/ehi- 'to fill', for which cf. \*Julia- 1 'full' < \*plhi-n6-. In Proto-Germanic the adjective may originally only have occurred in the neuter form \*Jelu, taking the following noun in the genitive. In PIE, it had an ablauting paradigm, e.g. common gender \*pelh₁-us, gen. \*plh₁-u-6s vs. neuter \*p6lhi-u, gen. \*plh₁-eu-s. This paradigm explains the ablaut difference between e.g. Gr. noft.U and Go. filu. Notably, Old Frisian Jule, fole and Du. dial. veul together with Ilr. \*prHu- appear to preserve the zero-grade root (Bremmer 2005). See also \*jlaizan-.

\*felwo- f. 'willow' - OHG Jelwa,felawa f. 'id.', G Fe/be f. 'id.' ⇒ \*tpel-u-eh₂-(?) (EUR) - Gr. 1tTEAfo f. 'elm tree' ( = Lat. tilia f. 'lime tree'?) < \*tpel-eu-eh₂-, Arm. t'eli 'id.' < \*tpel-ieh₂-(?). A European word with a remarkable reminiscence in Oss. Jcerv / Jarwe 'alder' (Hiibschmann 1887: 65). The different formations may all go back to an /-stem, e.g. \*tp-el, gen. \*tp-l-6s, acc. \*tp-el-m. The appurtenance of Ru. topol' m. 'poplar', which theoretically can be adduced to reconstruct an ablauting proto-form < \*top-I-, remains uncertain, as this word is likely to have been derived from Ru. top' 'swamp' (Falileyev /Owen 1999).

\*fergunja- n. 'mountain' - Go. Jairguni n. 'mountain range', ON fjprgyn f. 'Mother Earth', OEfirgen n. 'mountain', OHG Firgunnea f. 'Ore Mountains' ⇒ \*perkw-n-ieh₂- (IE). An etymologically debated word. The original meaning may have been 'mountainous forest' (cf. Celt. Lat. Hercynia Silva), and in that case, the word can be compared to \*Jerhwa- 1 'oak' and \*furhwiin- 'fir'. Within Germanic, however, the word can also be analyzed as the feminine equivalent of \*ferga- 'god', cf. ON fjprg n.pl. 'gods' with the feminine unjo-suffix. If so, the proto-form is to be reconstructed as \*perJc<wl-n-ieh₂-, which precludes the direct linkage with Lith. perkunas, Latv. pҊrkuons m. 'thunder' < \*perkw-u-Hn-o- (also cf. Ru. perun 'thunderbolt'). For the latter, cf. Ice!. fjarg-veour n. 'storm'.

\*ferhwa- 1 m./n. 'oak(?)' - ON poet. fjprr m. 'tree', OHG fereh-eih f. 'id.', G Ferch n. 'id.' ⇒ \*perJ<w-o- (WEUR) - Lat. quercus f. 'oak-tree' < \*perJ<w-u-; Olr. ceirt 'apple-tree', W perth 'bush' < \*perkʷ-t-?

\*ferhwa- 2 n. 'life, essence' - Go. fairluus m. 'world', ON f]pr n. 'life, pith', OE feorh m./n. 'life; man', OFri. ferech n.(?) 'life', OS ferah, ferh n. 'life, spirit', OHG ferah,ferh n. 'id.', MHG verch n. 'id.' ⇒ \*perkw-o- (EUR). Possibly related to \*ferhwa- 1 'oak', as the oak-tree symbolized vitality in Germanic mythology.

\*ferrai adv. 'far' - Go.fairra adv. 'id.', ONj]arri adv. 'id.', OE/eorr adv. 'id.', Efar, OS ferro adv. 'id.', Du. ver adv. 'id.', OHG/erro adv. 'id.', MHG verre adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*per-n-oi- (IE). A locative adverb originally ending in \*-noi. Cf. \*ferrjan-.

\*ferrjan- w.v. 'to be far; to remove' - ON firra w.v. 'to lose sight of; to deprive of, OFri.firia w.v. 'to be far away', OHGfirren w.v. 'to remove; to be far away', MHG virren w.v. 'id.' (DRV). The related OE firsian w.v. 'to go beyond, remove' derives from \*ferrisojan- with the same suffix as in e.g. irsian w.v. 'to be angry' (see \*erzja-).

\*fersno- f. 'heel' - Go. fairzna f. 'id.', OE ftersn f. 'id.' ( < \*fersni-), MDu. versene, verse f. 'id.', OHG fersana f. 'id.', G Fersne f. 'id.' ⇒ \*tpers-neh₂- (IE) - Hitt. par5na- c. 'heel(?)' (cf. par5nae-zi 'to squat down, crouch') < \*tper-sno-; YAv. pii5nii- f. 'heel', Gr. rri'.:pvri f. 'id.', Lat. perna f. '(upper) leg, thigh' < \*tper-sneh₂-; Skt pārṣṇi- f. 'id.' < \*tper-sni-. The IE word for 'heel'. The intra-Germanic difference between Go. fairzna, OHG fersana < \*tper-sneh₂- and OE fiersn < \*tper-sni- is neatly mirrored by Gr. rrtpvf], Lat. perna and Skt. pdr$ɣi-. The root \*tper- may have developed from \*pd-sperH- "to kick with the foot" (Lubotsky 2006), for which see \*Jot- and \*spurnan-.

\*fertan- - \*fretan- s.v. 'to fart' - ON freta s.v. 'id.', Far.freta w.v. 'id.', OSw. ficerta w.v. 'id.', Sw. j]iirta w.v. 'id.', Da.fjcerte w.v. 'id.', OE feortan s.v. 'id.', E to fart, MDu. verten, vorten s.v. 'id.', OHG ferzan s.v. 'id.', MHG verzen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*pred-e- (IE) - Skt. párdate 'to fart', YAv. paraoan 3pl.pres. 'id.', Gr. ni'.:pow, ntpooμm 'id.', Alb. pjerdh 'id.' < \*perd-e-; Ru. perdet', SCr. pfdjeti 'id.' < \*prd-eh₁-; Lith. persti (perdiiu, perdiiau) 'id.' < \*perd-ie-. An onomatopoetic formation from PIE times.

\*ferudi adv. 'last year' - ON f fjpro 'id.', Far. f fjrJr adj. 'id.', Nw. i fjor adj. 'id.', Elfd. ifiuord adj. 'id.', MHG vert adv. 'id.' ⇒ \*per-ut-i (IE) - Identical to Skt. p<irut, Gr. ntpuu1, Olr. inn-uraid, Arm. heru adv. 'id.' < \*per-ut(-i): a petrified

\*feson- f. 'chaff, fiber' - OS fesa f. 'pod'. MDu. vese f. 'fiber, husk; fringe', Du. vezel c. 'fiber', OHG fesa f. 'id.', MHG vese f. 'id.', G Swi. Rhntl. feasa f. 'id.' (IE) - Skt. pináṣṭi, piṃṣánti 'to crush', Lat pinsere 'id.' < \*tpi-n-(e]s-; Gr. πτίσσω 'to pound or grind corn in a mortar' < \*tpis-ie-; Gr. πτισάνη 'hulled barley' < \*tpis-n[e]h₂-; Lith. piestas, Ru. pest m. 'pestle' < \*tpois-to-; Lith. paisjti 'to peel barley' < \*tpois-eie-; Ru. psen6 n. 'millet', SCr. psena f. 'clover' < \*tpis-en-o/eh₂-. OHG fesa and MHG vese must have had real e rather than umlauted a in view of Swi. Rhntl. feasa (with ea < OHG e). This means that, if the connection with PIE \*tpis- is correct (esp. note Ice!. fis n. 'fluff, chaff), a-mutation must have lowered \*i to \*e. The collective OHG ga-vissa - ga-bissa f. 'chaff < quasi-PIE \*kom-tpis-io- shows a remarkable Verner alternation similar to the doublet OHG mezzisahs - mezzirahs 'knife' (for which see \*sahsa-). See also \*fasa/on- 'fringe, fiber'.

\*fetan- s.v. 'to go; to fall' - ON feta s.v. 'to step', Far. feta w.v. 'to stride, pace, step out', OE fetan s.v. 'to fall'. OHG fezzan, gijezzan s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*pM-e(IE) - Skt. padyati 'to move, to go; to fall' < \*ped-ie-; OCS padati, Ru. padat' (padaju), SCr. padati 'to fall' < \*pod-eh₂-ie-. An IE verbal formation related to \*Jot- 'foot'. See also the pertaining iterative \*futon-.

\*fetura/o- m./f. 'fetter' - ON fjpturr m. 'id.', Far. /jldtur n. 'id.', OSw. ji<l!tur m. 'id.', OE feter. fetor f. 'id.', E fetter, OS feteros m.pl. 'id.', Du. veter c. 'shoelace', OHG vezzera f. 'fetter', MHG vezzer f. 'fetter; manacle' ⇒ \*ped-ur-o/eh₂- (IE). Derived from the PIE word for 'foot' (see \*Jot-), cf. Gr. n:E:Sri f. 'fetter', Lat pedica f. 'id.'.

\*fepan- m. 'penis(?)' - MDu. vede m. 'id.' ⇒ \*pet-on- (WEUR?) - Lat penis m. 'tail, penis' < \*pet-sn-i-. The Latin word is usually derived from \*pes-ni- in view of Skt. pasas-, Gr. n:fo<; n. 'penis' < \*pes-os-, but \*pet-sni- is possible, too (cf. De Vaan 2008: 458). As a result, MDu. vede (for which cf. Gysseling 1987: 53) receives a possible etymology. See also \*fu/:>i- 'vagina'.
\*fepro- f. 'feather' - ON fjpar f. 'id.', Far. fjeaur f. 'feather; fin', Elfd. fjaaer f. 'feather', OE feoer f. 'id.', E feather, OS fethera f. 'id.', Du. veer c. 'id.', OHG

label_gloss: 139 fedara, federa f. 'id.', G Feder f. 'id.' ⇒ \*peth₂-r/n- (IE)
opening: \*feuhtjon- 139 fedara, federa f. 'id.', G Feder f. 'id.' ⇒ \*peth₂-r/n- (IE) - Hitt. pattar pattan-, pettar - pettan- n. 'feather, wing' < \*peth₂-r, \*pth₂-en-s; Skt patra- n. 'wing, feather' < \*peth₂-ro-; Gr. m£pov n. 'wing' < \*pt(h₂)-er-o-; Lat penna f. 'feather' < \*peth₂-n-ehr; OW atar pl. 'bird', MW adar 'id.' < \*pthrr-, Olr. en 'bird', W edn 'id.' < \*pethrno-. A heteroclitic derived from the PIE root \*peth₂- 'to fly', cf. Skt patati 'to fly; hit', Gr. nfroμm 'to fly', Lat peto, -ere 'to reach; to rush', MW eh-ed- 'to fly' and the intensive Gr. no-raoμm 'to fly around' < \*poth₂-eie-. Also cf. the collective ja-stem ON fiori, OE fioere, Du. ge-vederte, MHG ge-videre, G Ge-fteder n. 'plumage' < \*gajeprja-.
\*feuhtjon- f. 'spruce' - OHG ftuhta f. 'id.', MHG viehte f. 'id.', G Fichte f. 'id.' ¸

\*peul<-t-ih₂- (IE) - Waigali puc, piic 'pine' < \*peul<-i-; Gr. nEuKTJ f. 'spruce' < \*peul<-eh₂-; OPru. peuse 'id.' < \*peul<-ieh₂-; Lith. pusis f. 'id.' < \*pul<-i-. A word with a largely European distribution. In view of Waigali put, piic, however, the word must also have existed in Iranian (Morgenstierne 1945: 229). From there, it probably spread into Armenian, where it surfaces as the second member of the compound t'ef-aws 'holm oak, evergreen oak; pine' (Olsen 2012). For the first element, see Arm. t'efi 'elm' under \*felwo-.

\*feukan- - \*fikan- s.v. 'to blow' - ON Jjuka s.v. 'to be blown away; to fly off, Far. fika s.v. 'to drift; to dash, dart; to be blown away; to be rumored', EDu. ftjcken.fuycken 'to thrust', G fauchen w.v. 'to hiss, snarl' (ORV). In view of the root-final k and the alternation between \*eu and \*ii, it seems likely that the strong verb was back-formed to the iterative \*fuk(k)on- (q.v.). Also cf. MDu.fuke f. 'fish trap', Du.fuik c. 'id.' < \*fikon-.

\*fewurþ/dan- num. 'fourth' - ON fj6roi num. 'id.', Far. fj6roi num. 'id.', Elfd. ftuord num. 'id.', OE feoroa, feoweroa num. 'id.', E fourth, OFri. fiord a, ftiirda, fterda num. 'id.', OS fiortho num. 'id.', Du. vierde num. 'id.', OHG ftordo num. 'id.', G vierte num. 'id.' ⇒ \*kwet-ur-to- (IE) - Skt tur{ya-, turya-, Av. tiiiriianum. 'fourth' < \*kwt-ur-iHo-; Lat quiirtus < \*kwtur-tHo- (for expected \*quortus); OW petuerid num. 'id.' < \*kwet-ur-iHo-; ToA start, ToB starte, Gr. -rfrapToc;, Hom. also -rfrparnc;, Skt. caturthci-, Lith. ketviftas, Latv. cetuf'tais, OCS cetvrbt'b, Ru. cetvertyj, SCr. cetvfti num. 'id.' < \*kwet-ur-tHo-; Arm. c'or-ir num. 'id.' < \*kwet-or-. The ordinal to \*fedwar- 'four' (q.v.). In PIE, the root had the zero grade, but since \*kwtur-t6- in PGm. would have given \*\*hurda-, it was bound to become replaced by an analogical full-grade form. This \*kweturtHo- lost its first t due to dissimilation, and thus gave PGm. \*fewurp/dan- (with initial / after \*fimftan- 'fifth' (q.v.).

\*fifaldra/o- m./f. 'butterfly' - Far. jirvaldur m. 'id.', OSw. jicedhal m. 'id.', Sw. fjaril, dial. fjaralder c. 'id.', Elfd. fyorolder m. 'id.', OE fifea/de f. 'id.', OS fifoldara f. 'id.', MDu. vivalter, viveltere m./f. 'id.', OHG fivaltra f. 'id.', G Feifalter m./f. 'id.' ⇒ \*p(e)i-pol- (EUR) - Lat. papili6, -6nis m. 'butterfly'; OPru. pepelis 'bird'; OPru. penpalo, Lith. pfepala, Latv. pafpala, Ru. perepel 'quail'. A reduplicated formation, probably to be reconstructed as \*/f-faldra/6- or \*fe{aldra/6- (cf. OSw. jicedhal m. 'id.' < ON \*fjaoraldr < \*fjafraldr < \*fefaldra-). Also cf. the neuter ON ftfrildi, lcel. jiorildi, Nw. dial. jivrelde n. 'butterfly' < \*jifraldjan- ( < \*fifaldrjan-). The formation has some parallels in Latin and Balto-Slavic, but the material is severely distorted and cannot be reconciled into one single proto-form.

\*filj)and- m. 'enemy' - Go. fijands m. 'id.', ON Jjandi m. 'id.', Far. ftggindi m. 'id.', OE feond m. 'id.', E fiend, OFri. jiand,fil nd, fin(d) m. 'id.', ODu. fiunt m. 'id.', Du. vijand c. 'id.', OHG fiant m. 'id.', G Feind m. 'id.' ⇒ \*phri-ont- (DRV). A lexicalized participle derived from \*jilj)en- (q.v.).

\*fllj)en- w.v. 'to hate' - Go. fijan, jian w.v. 'id.', ON Jja w.v. 'id.', Far. ftggja w.v. 'id.', OE feon w.v. 'id.', OHG fien w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*phri-ehr (IE) - Skt. pfyati 'to blame'; Skt. pfyu- 'reviler' < \*ph₁-i-u-. A stative verb of clear Indo-European origin. The root \*phrei- arose as an i-present to \*pehr, which in Germanic can be retrieved from \*fean- (q.v.). See also \*jilj)and-.

\*fimfe num. 'five' - Go.fimfnum. 'id.', ON fimm num. 'id.', OEfifnum. 'id.', E five, OFri.fifnum. 'id.', OSfifnum. 'id.', Du. vijfnum. 'id.', OHGjim/num. 'id.', G finf num. 'id.' ⇒ \*penkʷe (IE) - Hitt. panza-, ToA pan, ToB pis, Skt. pafica, YAv. pal)ca, MP pan], Oss. fondz, Arm. hing, Alb. pese / pese, Gr. n£vrE, Lat. qufnque, Olr. c6ic, MW pymp, W pimp, Bret. pemp num. 'five'. Germanic has \*fimfe for expected \*finhwe. This is due to a sporadic change of PIE \*kw to Pre-Gm. \*p after resonants in words starting with a labial, cf. \*wulfa- 'wolf < \*ufkw-o-.

\*fimftan- num. 'fifth' - Go. fimfta num. 'id.', ON jimti, femti num. 'id.', Far. fimti num. 'id.', Elfd. femt num. 'id.', OE fifta num. 'id.', E fifth, OFri. fifta num. 'id.', OS fifto num. 'id.', Du. vijfde num. 'id.', OHG jimto, finfto num. 'Id.', G fiinfte num. 'id.' ⇒ \*penkʷ-t6- (IE) - Skt. pakthci-, YAv. puxoa (with -u- from Plr. \*5ustHa- 'sixth') num. 'fifth' < \*pnkw-tHo-; ToA pant, ToB pirikte, Lat. qufntus, Gr. n£μmoc;, Lith. pefiktas, Latv. piekts, OPru. piencts, OCS pF;tb, Ru. pjatyj, SCr. petf, Alb. peste num. 'id.' < \*penJ<w-tHo-; Gaul. ponpetos, Olr. c6iced, OW pimphet num. 'id.' < \*penkwe-tHo-.

\*fingra· m. 'finger' - Go. figgrs m. 'id.', ON fingr m. 'id.', OE finger m. 'id.', E finger, OFri. finger m. 'id.', OS fingar m. 'id.', Du. vinger c. 'id.', OHG fingar m. 'id.', G Finger m. 'id.' ⇒ \*penk.W-r6- (IE). A ro-stem usually assumed to be derived from the numeral \*fimJe 'five' < PIE \*penk.W-e. The labialization of the \*kw was lost before the operation of Verner's law.

\*finkan· s.v. 'to sparkle, burn' - MDu. vinken s./w.v. 'to sparkle; to smol· der', G obs.finkeln w.v. 'to burn, hurt' (DRY). As the alternation of the pertaining causative \*Jangjan- - \*Jankjanshows, the root-final \*k of this strong verb must be secondary, continuing a (shortened) geminate that spread from the iterative \*funkon- (q.v.). Also cf. the deverbal nouns MHG vine m. 'spark' < \*finka- and MHG vanke m. 'id.' < \*Jankan-.

\*finkja(n)· m. 'finch' - OE fine m. 'id.', E finch, MDu. vink(e) m./f. 'id.', Du. vink c. 'id.', OHG finko m. 'id.', G Fink m. 'id.' ⇒ \*ping-o- (EUR?) - Gr. Hsch. rr(yya 'young bird' < \*ping-eh₂-(?). An exclusively Germanic word. Formally close to \*spinka(n)- ( q.v.). \*fin(n)on- f. OSw. fina f. 'fin', Sw. Jena c. 'fin; chaff, husk', MDu. vinne f. 'fin; wing; prickle; awn' ⇒ \*pin-(m)on- (WEUR?) - Olr. ind, inn n. 'end, extremity, tip, point, edge, surface' < \*pinno-(?). The alternation between ON fina < \*finon- and WGm. \*finno points to an n-stem \*Jina, gen. \*finnaz < \*pin-on, \*pin-n-6s. The variant MDu., EDu. vimme 'fin; prickle, awn' can furthermore be adduced to reconstruct a more primary mn-stem \*fimmo, \*finnaz < \*pfn-mon, \*pin-mn-6s (with loss of m in the cases with zero grade of the suffix), but this vimme may also have arisen secondarily by assimilation of the root-final nasal to the initial J (cf. Du. priem < \*preuna-). No generally accepted etymology. The link with Olr. ind 'extremity' is attractive, not least in view of the semantics of Sw. Jena 'chaff, hus\c' and MDu. vine 'bran(?)', which could have developed from 'awn'. Problematic, on the other hand, is the connection with Lat pinna 'feather, wing, fin, parapet', which does not continue alleged \*pinn-ehr, but represents a dialect form of penna 'pen' < \*pet-neh₂- (De Vaan 2008: 458). Unlikely, too, are the connections with e.g. Skt. sphya- m. 'stick' and Lat. spfna 'spine, thorn', which unlike the Germanic forms have an initial s.

\*finþan- s.v. 'to find; to feel' - Go. finþan s.v. 'id.', ON finna s.v. 'id.', OE findan s.v. 'id.', E to find, OFri. finda s.v. 'id.', OS fidan, findan s.v. 'id.', ODu. findan s.v. 'id.', Du. vinden s.v. 'id.', OHG findan s.v. 'id.', G finden s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*pent-e- (IE) - Skt. pánthās, gen. path6s m. 'road, path, course'. YAv. pantā, OAv. abl. paθō m. 'road, path'. MP pand 'path', Oss. frendreg 'id.', Gr. rra-roc:; m. 'road'. rr6v-roc:; m. 'sea', Arm. hun, gen. hni 'ford', Lat pons m. 'bridge, path'. OCS pptb m. 'road', OPru. pintis 'id.' < PIE \*p6nt-eh₁-s, gen. \*pnt-h₁-6s. See also \*fanþjan-, \*fandon-, \*fundon- and \*paþa-.

\*fisan- s.v. 'to blow' - ON ffsa s.v. 'id.', Icel. ffsa w.v. 'to blow (on a fire); to fart'. Far.jfsa w.v. 'to blow, draw; to get to fly, stir up; to hiss; to snort'. Elfd. faisa s.v. 'to fart', MHG vfsen s.v. 'to fart' ⇒ \*peis-e-(?) (WEUR) - Lat. splro, -are 'to breathe' < \*speis-eh₂-ie-; W fftln 'breath' < \*spoi-n-eh₂-. An originally onomatopoetic verb related to \*fison- (q.v.). The semantic spectrum ranges between 'to blow', 'to hiss'. 'to whisper' and 'to fart'. If Lat. spfrare is related, the PIE form of the verb may have been \*psei-s-e-; normally, the cluster \*ps- would develop into Gm. \*sp- (cf. \*stelan- 'to sneak; to steal' < \*tsel-e-), but in this case, the s may have been lost due to dissimilation.

\*fiska- m. 'fish' - Go. fisks m. 'id.', ON fiskr m. 'id.', Far. fiskur m. 'id.', Elfd. fisk m. 'id.', OE fisc m. 'id.', E fish, OFri. fisk m. 'id.', OS fisk m. 'id.', Du. vis c. 'id.', OHG fisc m. 'id.', G Fisch m. 'id.' ⇒ \*pisk-o- (WEUR) - Lat. piscis m. 'fish' < \*pisk-i-; Olr. [asc m. 'id.' < \*peisk-o-. A West Indo-European word. The connection with Skt. piccha- 'calf of the leg' < \*pili-sk-eh₂- (Sadovsky 1973: 85-6), perhaps justified by the widespread folk association of the calf of the leg with the belly of a fish (Mallory/ Adams 2006: 146) is highly uncertain. Slightly more attractive, then, is the comparison with Skt. piccha- n. 'feather'. Cz. pisk 'young feather'. The alleged cognate Ru. piskar' 'gudgeon', on the other hand, was derived from a verbal root, cf. OCS pi ska ti 'to pipe', Ru. piscat' 'to squeak' (cf. Hamp 1973b ), and therefore cannot be related. Also cf. Go. fiskon, ON fiska, OE fiscian, fixian, OFri. fiskia, Du. vissen, OHG fiskon, G fischen w.v. 'to fish' < \*fiskojan-, which is formally close to Lat. piscor, -ari < \*pisk-eh₂-ie-.
\*fison- w.v. 'to blow' - Nw. fisa w.v. 'to blow; to fart; to whisper', MDu. vesen w.v. 'to whisper' (ORV). The iterative pertaining to the strong verb \*jisan- ( q.v.).

\*fitnan- w.v. 'to become fat' - ON fitna w.v. 'id.', Far.fitna w.v. 'id.' (ORV). An inchoative verb derived from \*faita- (q.v.). With the same ablaut, cf. ON fita f., Far. fiti f. 'fat' < \*fiton-, \*-fn-, whence ON, Far. fita w.v. 'to make fat' < \*fitojan-.

\*Jlahan· s.v. 'to flay, strip' - ON fla s.v. 'to flay; to strip (of clothes)', Elfd. fla w.v. 'id.', OE f/ean s.v. 'to flay', MDu. vlaen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*pl6k-e- (EUR) - Gr. n>.aſ, -aK6<; f. 'plane, plain; flat stone, board, table' < \*pJk-. An a-grade intensive verb related to \*flaho- and \*f/oho- ( q.v.).

\*jlahO· f. 'strip' - ON f/a f. 'strip of land, meadow' ⇒ \*pl6k-eh₂- (DRV) - Latv. plaka f. 'low lying place, plain' < \*plok-eh₂-. Together with Gr. nA.aø -aK6<; f. 'flat surface, board, slab', ON fla has been derived from a PIE root noun \*plak- (cf. Orel 2003: 104). However, the Greek alpha and the Gm. \*a have two entirely different origins, i.e. a vocalized \*/ and an \*o respectively. Also note that, in Old Norse, many monosyllabic o-stems acquired an analogical root-noun inflection, cf. t<i, pl. ter f. 'toe' < \*taihwo-. This taken into consideration, I derive the word from \*pl6k-eh₂-, which happens to be close to Latv. plaka. See also \*f/ahan-.

\*Jlahsa· n. 'flax' - OE f/eax n. 'id.', Ef/ax, OFri.f/ax n. 'id.', MLG vlas m. 'id.', Du. vlas n. 'id.', OHG f/ahs m. 'id.', G Flachs m. 'id.' (DRY). A thematicized s-stem usually derived from the PIE root \*plel<- 'to braid' (see \*flehtan-). It is equally attractive to connect the word to \*flakk/gon- 1 'to beat, thresh' (q.v.).

\*Jlaina· adj. 'flat(?)' - Nw. flein adj. 'bald, naked; flat' ⇒ \*ploi-no- (NEUR?) - Lith. plikas adj. 'bald', Latv. pliks adj. 'bare, bald' < \*pli-ko-; CS plesb adj. 'bald patch' < \*ploi-s-io-. A marginally attested adjective that is often connected to the aforementioned Balta-Slavic forms. The meaning 'bald' is probably secondary, however, as Nw. f/ein also means 'flat'. The straightforward connection with Elfd. f/ina w.v. 'to smile' < \*f/inon-, on the other hand, does seem to point to a primary meaning 'bare' (whence 'to bare the teeth'). Related to OSw.jlen n. 'tumor (of the skin)' and Nw.flein m. 'growth, wound'?

\*Jlaizan- comp. 'more' - Go.flaiza comp. 'id.', ONf/eiri comp. 'id.', Far.fleiri comp. 'id.', Elfd. f/ierer comp. 'id.' ⇒ \*pl6h₁-is-on- (IE) - Gr. nA.dwv comp. 'more' < \*pleh₂-is-on-; Lat plii.s, -ris comp. 'id.' < \*ploh₁-is-. The comparative of \*felu- \*fulu- (q.v.). The development of \*f/aizanfrom \*pl6h₁-is-on- proves that Verner's law preceded the shortening of the long diphthongs (Osthoff's law), as \*pl6ison- would have given \*\*flaisan-. For a similar scenario, cf. \*maizan- 'more' < \*meh₂-is-on- and \*winda- 'wind' < \*h₂uehi-ent-o-.

\*Jlaka- adj. 'flat' - MDu. vlac adj. 'id.', Du. vlak adj. 'id.', OHG flah adj. 'id.', G flach adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*pl6k-o- (EUR) - Gr. nA.aſ, -aK6<; f. 'plane, plain; flat stone, board, table' < \*pJk-; Lith. pl<ikanas, Latv. plakans adj. 'flat' < \*plok-ono-.

\*flakk/gon- 1 w.v. 'to beat' - E to flack 'to beat with a flail', G flacken w.v. 'to beat wool' ⇒ \*plh₂k-neh₂- (EUR). An o-grade iterative, variant of the zero-grade E to flog ( < OE \*floggian) < \*fluggon-. Both formations appear to be (secondary) iteratives to \*flokan(q.v.), cf. OCS plakati 'to beat one's breast; to lament' < \*pleh₂k-eh₂-.

\*flakk/gon- 2 w.v. 'to flutter' - Nw.flaga w.v. 'to fluctuate', MEflakken w.v. 'to beat feebly, flutter', MDu. vlacken, vlaken w.v. 'to flame', Du. flakkeren w.v. 'to flutter', OHG flagaron w.v. 'to flutter', G Swi. flacken w.v. 'to flare' ⇒ \*plok-neh₂- (GM). An iterative that in spite of the dictionaries (Franck 1949: 166; Pokorny IEW: 832-3) cannot be equated with \*flakkon- 'to swerve'. It is more likely that the verb represents a (secondary?) a-grade variant of \*flukk/gi5n- 'to fly' (q.v.). Also cf. ON flaga f. 'quick raid', Far. flaga f. 'gust of wind', MHG vlage f. 'id.', Du. vlaag c. 'id.' < \*flagon- and OE flacor adj. 'flickering' < \*flakra-.

\*flakkon- w.v. 'to swerve' - ON flakka w.v. 'to rove about', Far. flakka w.v. 'to wander about, roam' ⇒ \*plofgLneh₂- (EUR) - Gr. rrA.a0w 'to make devious, repel, dissuade from the right path, bewilder', rrA.a0oμm 'to go astray, wander about' < \*p/fgl-ie-. An o-grade iterative. The Greek word is assumed to be without a good etymology by Beekes 2010: 1201, but the connection with PGm. \*flakkon- < \*p/ofgLneh₂- (cf. Torp 1909: 249) is formally and semantically unproblematic.

\*flata- adj. 'flat' - ON flatr adj. 'flat, level', OS flat adj. 'flat, shallow', MDu. vlat (infl. v/adde) adj. 'id.', OHG flaz adj. 'flat' (IE) - Skt. p[thu- adj. 'broad', Gr. nA.aru<; adj. 'flat' < \*plth₂-u-; Lith. platus adj. 'broad, wide' < \*ploth₂-u- (cf. Lith. plasti (p/antu) 'to broaden').

\*Jlatja- n. 'floor' - ON jlet n. 'raised flooring', OE flett n. 'floor of a house, dwelling', Ejlat, OFri.jlet n.? 'house; home', OSflet n. 'hall, house', OHGjlezzi n. 'floor; flat area', MHG vletz(e) n. 'id.' (DRY). Aja-stem derived from \*flata- 'flat' (q.v.).
\*Jlaugjan- w.v. 'to let fly' - Go. us-jlaugjan\* w.v. 'id.', ON jleygja w.v. 'id.', MHG er-vlougen w.v. 'id.' (NEUR). The causative to \*fleugan- (q.v.).

\*Jlauho- f. 'flea' - ON jl6, pl.firer f. 'id.', OE jleah,flea f. 'id.', E flea, WFri. flie c. 'id.', MDu. vloo, vlo(o)y f. 'id.', Du. vlo c. 'id.', OHG jlo{h) m. 'id.', G Floh m. 'id.' ⇒ \*pl6uk-eh₂- (IE) - Skt. plu$i- m. 'flea', Sogd. 'f3S'h 'id.', Arm. lu 'id.' < \*plus-; Alb. plesht m. 'id.' < \*pious-; Gr. t!JUAA.a f. 'id.' < \*psul-; Lat. pulex 'id.' < \*pus/-; Lith. blusa f. 'id.', Rues bl'bxa, Ru. bloxci, Ser. buha f. 'id.' < \*blus-eh₂-. An inherited Indo-European word. Due to the many tabooistic distortions of the word in the different IE languages, it is difficult to reconstruct its original form.

\*Jlauja- n. 'ship' - ON jley n. 'id.', Far. poet. flay 'vessel, craft' ⇒ \*plou-io(IE) - Identical to Gr. nA.oTov n. 'ship'; further cf. ToB plewe m. 'id.', Gr. nA.60<;, nA.ou<; m. 'id.', Ru. plov m. 'ship, barge' < \*plouo-. Derived from the PIE verb \*pleu-e- 'to sail', for which see \*flauma- and \*floan-. The OE gloss floege 'navicula' appears to be a Norse loanword (Pons-Sanz 2000: 94-5).

\*Jlauma- m. 'stream' - ON jlaumr m. 'eddy' ⇒ \*plou-mo- (IE) - Skt. piavate 'to swim, flow', Gr. nA.Ew 'to travel by sea, sail, navigate', Lat. pluit, pluere 'to rain', oes pluti 'to flow, sail' < \*p/eu-e-; oes plaviti 'to let swim' < \*plou-eie-; Olr. 16 'water' < \*plou-o-(?). See also \*flauja- and \*floan-.
\*Jlauti- f. 'cream' - ON flautir f.pl. 'whipped milk', OE jlf{e)te f. 'cream' (DRY). See \*jleutan-.
\*Jlaupi- f. 'cross-beam' - ON jleyor f. 'id.' ⇒ \*plout-ih₂- (EUR) - Lith. p/aiitas, Latv. plduts m. 'shelf, sideboard', Ser. pluto n. 'flotsam' < \*plou-t6-;

\*jlehtan- s.v. 'to braid, plait' - ON fletta w.v. 'id.', OE fleohtan s.v. 'id.', MLG vlechten s.v. 'id.', MDu. v/echten s.v. 'id.', Du. vlechten s.v. 'id.', OHGflehtan s.v. 'id.', G flechten s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*p/eli-t-e- (EUR) - Identical to Lat plecto, -ere 'to plait, twine', OCS plesti, Ru. plestf (pletu), SCr. plesti 'to plait' < \*p/eli-t-e-, which is an extension of the root \*pleli-, cf. Gr. rrAiKw 'to braid, to knit, to wind, to twine' < \*p/eli-e- and Skt prafoa- m. 'plaited basket, turban', YAv. arazato.frafoa- adj. 'having a silver helmet' < \*ploli-no-.

\*fleugan- s.v. 'to fly' - ON fljrlga s.v. 'id.', Far. flugva s.v. 'id.', Elfd. fliuoga s.v. 'id.', OE fleogan s.v. 'id.', E to fly, OFri. fliaga s.v. 'id.', MDu. vliegen s.v. 'id.', Du. vliegen s.v. 'id.', OHG fliugan, fliogan s.v. 'id.', G fliegen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*pMuk-e(NEUR) - Lith. plailkti (plaukiil) 'to swim, float' < \*p/ouk-ie- and plunksna f. 'feather' < \*plu-n-k-sneh₂-. In Germanic, the form \*fleugan- emerges for expected \*fleuhan- < \*p/euk-e-. Pokorny explains this as the result of deliberate disambiguation with \*pleuhan-, but it is more attractive to assume interference with the iterative \*flukk/gon- < \*pluk-neh₂-. Another such interference is evinced by MDu. vlieken 'to fly' (cf. also MDu. vlieke, vleke f. 'feather; arrow' < \*fleukjon-, whence OFr.fleche, Fr.fleche 'arrow'), which in spite of its weak conjugation must continue strong \*fleukan- with a secondary \*k from the iterative. Similarly, MDu. vluycken 'to transport over water', which is close to the meaning of Lith. plailkti, is likely to continue Pre-Gm. \*fliikan- with both the \*ii and the \*k pointing to influence from \*flukk/g6n-. See also \*flaugjan-, \*flugi- and \*Jug/a-.

\*fleugon- f. 'fly' - Nw. fljuge f. 'id.', OE fleoge f. 'id.', E fly, MLG vlege f. 'id.', MDu. vliege f. 'id.', Du. vlieg c. 'id.', MHG fliuga, flioga f. 'id.', G Fliege f. 'id.' (DRV). Derived from \*fleugan-. Also cf. ON fluga f. 'id.' with a zero grade.

\*fleusiz- n. 'fleece' - OE fleas, flies n. 'id.', E fleece, MDu. vlies, vluus n. 'fleece; tuft', Du. vlies n. 'id.', MHG vlies, vlius n. 'id.' ⇒ \*pleus-o- (NEUR) - Lith. plaufaf m.pl. 'bast' < \*pious-a-; Lith. pluskos f.pl., Latv. p/uska f. 'tuft of hair' < \*plus-k-eh₂-. The co-existence of OE fleas, (M)Du., MHG vlies < \*fleusa- and OE flies, MDu. vluus, MHG v/ius n. 'id.' < \*fleusi- points to an old neuter s-stem \*fleusaz, gen. \*fleusizaz < \*p/eus-os, \*p/eus-es-os.
\*jleuta- n. 'stream' - ON flj6t n. 'stream, river', OE fleot n. 'estuary, sea; fleet, ship', E fleet, MLG flet m./n. 'river', MDu. vii et m. 'body of water' (DRV).

\*fleutan- s.v. 'to float, stream' - ON flj6ta s.v. 'id.', OE fleotan s.v. 'to float', OFri. fliata s.v. 'id.', OS fliotan s.v. 'id.', MDu. vlieten s.v. 'id.', Du. vlieten s.v. 'id.', OHG fliozan s.v. 'id.', G flieflen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*p/eud-e- (IE) - Olr. luaidid 'to move' < \*pJeud-e-; Lith. plilsti (p/astu) 'to float, stream, run' < \*plud-ske-. A strong verb to the PIE root \*pleud-. See also \*fleuta-, \*flauti-, \*jluta- and \*flut(t)on-.

\*fleupra- m. 'elder' - WFri. flear 'elder; soggy area', MLG vleder m. 'elder', ODu. fliethar 'id.', MDu. vlieder, vlier m. 'id.', Du. vlier c. 'id.' (GM). A North-Sea Germanic word. In Frisian, it is only attested in the modern dialects, but Du. dial. v/aar, attested in the originally Frisian province of North-Holland, implies an OFri. form \*fliar (cf. Franck 1949: suppl., 183). No certain etymology. The word seems to contain the \*pra-/dra-suffix common in tree-names (cf. \*apaldra-). In view of the WFri. meaning 'soggy place', it is perhaps possible to link the word to the PIE root \*p/eu- 'to float, swim', for which see \*flauma-. Since the elder has a preference for moist soil, this meaning can also be secondary, however.

\*flitan- s.v. 'to strive, fight' - OE flitan s.v. 'to strive, contend, dispute', MDu. vliten s./w.v. 'to be enthusiastic; to strive for', OHG jlfzan s.v. 'to strive, try, fight' ⇒ \*pJeid-e- (WEUR) - MW llwydaw 'to succeed' < \*pleid-. A Germanic-Celtic isogloss (Schumacher 2004: 521-2).

\*floan- s.v. 'to flow' - ON fl6a w.v. 'to flood; be flooded', OE jlowan s.v. 'to flow', E to flow, MLG vloien w.v. 'to flow; flood', MDu. vloeyen w.v. 'to flow; swim; flood', Du. vloeien w.v. 'to flow' ⇒ \*plou-e- (EUR) - Gr. rrA.ww 'to swim' < \*pl6u-e-; OCS p/aviti 'to let swim' < \*plou-eie-. An old strong verb. The alternation of -w- in Anglo-Saxon and -j- in Low German points to a hiatus, viz. \*floan-. This hiatus arose after the regular loss of \*u after \*6 in a formation \*pl6u-e- (cf. Go. sauil 'sun' < \*soel < \*seh₂u-e/). Gr. rrA.ww has been adduced to alternatively derive \*floan- from a root variant \*pleh₃- (cf. rrA.w•oc; adj. 'floating' < \*p/h₃-to-?), but the Germanic evidence clearly points to \*pl6u-e-, cf. \*flauja- 'ship' (= Gr. rrA.oTov) < \*p/ou-io- and \*flauma- < \*p/ou-mo-. See also \*flodu-.

\*flodu- m./f. 'flood' - Go.flodus m. 'id.', 0Njl6o n. 'id.', Far.fl6o f. 'high tide; heavy rain', OE flod m./n. 'id.', E flood, OS flod m./f. 'flood, river', Du. vloed c. 'flood; high tide', OHG fluot f. 'flood', G Flut f. 'river; tide' ⇒ \*ploh₃-tU- (ORV). A tu-stem created to the root of \*floan-. The u-suffix has been replaced by a- and i-stems in many languages, but must nevertheless be reconstructed
\*flOhO- f. 'layer' - ON fl6 f. 'layer', OE floh f. 'chip', OHG fluoh f. 'rock, cliff, MHG vluo f. 'id.', G dial. Fluh f. 'id.' (DRY). Derived from the adjective \*flaha-, for which see \*flaka-.

\*flokan- s.v. 'to slap, mourn, lament' - Go.flokan\* s.v. 'to lament', OEflocan s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*pl6h₂k-e- (EUR) - Gr. rrl.$aaw 'to strike, slap, thrust, hit' < \*plh₂k-ie-; Lat. plango, -ere 'to slap' < \*plh₂k-neh₂-; OCS plakati st: (placp) 'to beat one's breast; lament' < \*pleh₂k-ie-; Lith. pl6kis, p/Okis m. 'flogging, blow with a stick' < \*pleh₂k-i- (and with secondary ablaut: Lith. plakti 'to beat, chastise'?). An o-grade reduplicating verb, formally and semantically close to Gr. rrl.$aaw and OCS plakati. The apparent incongruity of Gm. \*flak- with PIE \*pleh₂k- can be resolved by assuming that the strong verb was influenced by the corresponding iterative \*flakk/gon- (q.v.). It is not necessary to postulate a PIE root variant \*pleh₂g- with root-final \*g. Lat plango may have developed from \*plh₂k-neh₂- with -ng- < \*-ngn- < \*-kn- according to Thurneysen's law, and also Gr. TIATJYli f. 'hit, wound' can have come about by analogy within Greek itself.

\*flora- m. 'floor' - ON fl6rr m. 'floor of a cowshed', OE flor m./f. 'floor; attic', E floor, Du. vloer c. 'id.', OHG fluor m. 'field', G Flur m. 'id.' ⇒ \*p!e/6h₂-ro(EUR) - Identical to Olr. lar m. 'floor'; also cf. OPru. plonis 'threshing floor' < \*p/eh₂-no-; Lith. pl6nas adj. 'thin', Latv. plans adj. 'even, flat, thin' < \*pleh₂-no-; Lat. pliinus adj. 'flat' < \*plh₂-n6-. Within Germanic also cf. MHG flarre f. 'slab' < \*flarron- (EWAhd: III, 422) with \*flarr- < Pre-Gm. \*plorn6-, the genitive stem from an original paradigm \*p/Or-on, gen. \*p/Or-n-6s? This may in turn be a distortion of an old heteroclitic, e.g. nom. \*pl6h₂-r, gen. \*p/eh₂-n-s.

\*flugi- adj. 'able to fly' - E fledge, MDu. vlugghe ad.j. 'being able to fly; fast, quick', OHG flucki adj. 'able to fly', MHG vliicke adj. 'id.' (DRY). A gerundive to \*fleugan- 'to fly' (q.v.). The o-grade is found in ON fleygr adj. 'id.' < \*flaugja-.

\*flukka(n)- m. 'group' - ON flokkr m. 'troop, host; flock', Far. flokkur m. 'multitude, crowd, party; flock (of birds)', OEflocc n. 'flock, band, company', Eflock, MLG vlocke 'group' (DRY). The original meaning of the word was 'group of moving people or animals', cf. Far. flokkur 'flock of birds', E flock (of birds or sheep), and this al-

\*jlukkan- m. 'flake' - MDu. vlocke f. 'flake, tuft, fluff, Du. vlok c. 'id.', OHG flocko m. 'id.', G Flocke m. 'id.' (DRV). The word has been analyzed as a loanword from Lat. floccus m. 'tuft of wool', but there are other options. It may have been derived from an iterative \*flukkon-, cf. Nw.flokka w.v. 'to pull out (e.g. hay)', G Rhnl.flocken w.v. 'to loosen hay', which in turn can be related to Lith. pl<iukas m. '(single) hair, color of an animal, (pl.) hair, fur'. The iterative that is most likely to be related, however, is \*flukk/gon- 'to fly, flutter' (q.v.). Note that its o-grade variant \*flakk/gon- seems to have been the source of the no doubt related E flake.
\*jlukk/gon- w.v. 'to fly, flutter' - MDu. vlogen w.v. 'to fly', G flocken w.v. 'to fly around, flutter' (NEUR). The iterative to \*fleugan- (q.v.). See also \*flakk/gon-.
\*jluta- n. 'floating' - ON flot n. 'floating; grease', OE flot n. 'sailable body of water, sea', Du. vlot n. 'raft' (DRV). A regular zero-grade neuter formation to the strong verb \*fleutan-.

\*flut(t)on- w.v. 'to float, stream' - ON flota w.v. 'id.', OE flotian w.v. 'id.', MDu. vlot(t)en w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*plud-neh₂·(?) (DRv?). The iterative to \*fleutan-. Also cf. the frequentative OEfloterian w.v. 'to be tossed on the waves'.

\*fneusan- - \*fnii.san· s.v. 'to sneeze' - ON hnj6sa s.v. 'id.', Far. nj6sa s.v. 'id.', Nw. njosa, nysa s.v. 'id.', OEfneosan s.v. 'id.', MEfnesen, snesen s.v. 'id.', E to sneeze, MDu. niesen, fniesen s.v. 'id.', Du. niezen, obs. fniezen w.v., MHG pfnii.sen s.v. 'to snort, sneeze' ⇒ \*pneus-e- (IE) - Gr. rrvtw 'to blow, cough, breathe' < \*pneu-e-. Seebold 1970: 269 reconstructs PGm. \*hneusan- on the basis of the Nordic forms, thus isolating them from WGm. \*fneusan-, but it seems more likely that the sequence \*Jn- merged with \*hn- (also cf. MDu. niesen). This is confirmed by Far. fros n. 'sneezing', a (dissimilated) variant of nos n. 'id.' < ON \*hnos < \*hnusa-. As a result, there seem to be no objections against reconstructing the root as \*fneus-, which appears to be related to PIE \*pneu'to breathe' (with the s-extension pointing to an aoristic root, cf. Gr. EITVEUC1E 'breathed, blew'). Ultimately, the root is likely to be onomatopoetic, cf. OHG fnehan s.v. 'to snort, breathe heavily' < \*fnehan-. See also \*fnuz(z)on-.

\*fnuz(z)fm- w.v. 'to sneeze' - Ice!. hnerra w.v. 'id.', MHG pfnurren w.v. 'to swell; to snort; to grumble' ⇒ \*pnus-neh₂- (IE). Possibly the iterative to \*fneusan- - \*fnusan- (q.v.). Also cf. ON hn!<1ri, hneri, Ice!. hnerri, OE fnora m. 'sneezing' < \*fnuz(z)an-. Some derive Ice!. hnerra, obs. hnor(r)a from the noun (Magnusson 1989: 347), however.

\*fodjan- w.v. 'to feed; to rear' - Go. fodjan w.v. 'id.', ON freoa w.v. 'id.', OE Jedan w.v. 'id.', E to feed, OFri. feda w.v. 'id.', OS fOdian w.v. 'id.', Du. voeden w.v. 'id.', OHG fuottan w.v. 'id.', MHG viieten w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*poh₂t-eie- (EUR) - Gr. mnfoμcn 'to eat' < \*ph₂t-eie-; Gr. &:nacrTO<; 'without food' < \*n-ph₂t-to-. A causative probably created to the marginally attested root \*peh₂t-, possibly an extension of \*peh₂- 'to protect; to graze', cf. Lat. piisco, -ere 'to graze' < \*peh₂-ske-. It is less attractive to explain the dental of the Germanic verb as resulting from back-formation from \*fodra- 'fodder', not least in view of \*fadolja)n-.

\*fodra- n. 'fodder' - Go. fodr n. 'scabbard, sheath, casing', ON Joor n. 'fodder, foddering', OE /Odor n. 'f؍dder; sheath', E fodder, MLG vOder n. 'fodder; casing', MDu. voeder n. 'lining, cover; fodder', Du. voer, voeder n. 'fodder', OHG fuotar n. 'id.', MHG vuoter n. 'id.', G Futter n. 'id.' ⇒ \*peh₂-dhro- (IE) - Lat. pabulum n. 'fodder' < \*peh₂-dh/o-. A merger of two instrumental nouns. In the meaning 'fodder', it is related to \*fodjan- 'to feed'. The attestations meaning 'sheath' or 'casing', on the other hand, were derived from the PIE root \*peh₂-, i.e. 'to protect', cf. ToA piis-, ToB piisk- 'to guard'.

\*fogjan- w.v. 'to join together' - OE fegan w.v. 'id.', E to fay, OS fogian w.v. 'id.', Du. voegen w.v. 'id.', OHG fuogan w.v. 'id.', G fiigen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*poh₂li-eie(IE) - Hitt. piiski - piiskanzi 'to stick in, to fasten, to plant; to set up; to impale' < \*peh₂li-ske-; YAv. paiti.auua.pasii! 3sg.subj. 'to chain together' < \*peh₂l<- (cf. Skt. p&5a- m. 'snare, bond, chain, noose', Av. fiabis <lat.pl. 'with fetters' < \*p(e)h₂l<-; Gr. m'Jcrcrw, Att. m'Jnw 'to fix' < \*peh₂k-ie- (with secondary g: m'JyvuμL 'to fix; to congeal'); Lat. pango, -ere 'to attach, fasten' < \*ph₂k-neh₂- (with \*-kn- > \*-ng- according to Thurneysen's law). A causative verb to a further unattested strong verb \*fahan-, whence MHG vuoge f. 'joint', G Fuge, MLG voge, MDu. voege f., Du. voeg c. 'joint' < \*Jago-. See also \*fagra-.

\*foljan- w.v. 'to feel' - OE felan w.v. 'id.', E to feel, OFri. fela w.v. 'id.', OS gi-folian w.v. 'id.', Du. voe/en w.v. 'id.', Gfihlen w.v. 'id.' (GM). An etymologically obscure word. Within Germanic, the appurtenance of ON fa/ma 'to grope' and \*fulmo- 'palm of the hand' (q.v.) is uncertain. Not

\*fora- m. 'piglet' - OE for m. 'pig, hog', OS for n. 'immature pig', MLG vor 'skinny piglet' ⇒ \*por-o- (DRV?) - Gr. rr6pu;, -we; f. 'calf, heifer' < \*por-i-. A word apparently continuing Pre-Gm. \*por-o-, an o-stem derived from the root \*per- 'to give birth', for which see \*farza(n)-.

\*for - \*fun- n. 'fire' - Go.for, gen.funins n. 'id.', ON fYrr,filrr m. 'fire'.funi m. 'flame', Far. fYrur m. 'fire; lighthouse', funi m. 'dense smoke', OE fYr n. 'id.', E fire, OFri. ftur, fior n. 'id.', WFri. fjoer n. 'id.', OS fiur n. 'id.', ODu. fuir n. 'id.', Du. vuur n. 'id.', OHG fiur, fuir n. 'id.', G Feuer n. 'id.' ⇒ \*peh₂-ur, \*ph₂-uen-s (IE) - Hitt. paḫḫur, gen. paḫḫuenas n. 'fire', Gr. 1tUp n. 'id.', Arm. hur 'id.', OPru. panno 'id.'. An old heteroclitic formation, probably to be reconstructed as Pre-Germanic \*peh₂-ur (hardly \*ph₂-uor as per Simms 2009, as this would become \*fawor), gen. \*ph₂-un-6s, loc. \*ph₂-uen-i. The heteroclisy was preserved by Proto-Germanic, which appears to have had a paradigm \*for (with \*-ou- > \*-o-), gen. \*funins (for older \*funaz < \*pun6s with Dybo's law), dat. \*fu(w)eni. The different languages leveled out the r/n-alternations. Gothic, for instance, incorporated the word into the neuter n-stems, while West Germanic generalized the forms with r. The attestations with front mutation, e.g. ON fYrr, OE JYr, OHG fuir, fiur, are based on a dative form \*fu(w)eni. For similar cases of umlaut in PIE heteroclitic nouns, see \*eudra- \*udra- 'udder', \*libro- 'liver' and \*neura/on- 'kidney'. OEfior and OFri. ftur, WFri. fjoer, apparently from \*feuri, continue the same case form, though with metathesis.

\*fori- adj. 'passable; able to go' - ON frerr adj. 'able to go; capable of being passed, safe', OE /ere adj. 'passable; able to go', OHG gi-fuori adj. 'fit, convenient' (DRY). A vṛddhi-gerundive to \*faran- (q.v.). The difference in meaning between 'able to go' and 'passable' depends on the diathetic role, i.e. passive or active.

\*forjan- w.v. 'to lead' - ON frera w.v. 'id.', OE feran w.v. 'to go', OFri. fera w.v. 'id.', OS forian w.v. 'id.', Du. voeren w.v. 'id.', OHG fuorran, fuoren w.v. 'id.', Gfiihren w.v. 'id.' (DRY). The causative to \*faran- (q.v.). The vowel \*o is secondary, and in synchronic Germanic functions as the o-grade of \*a. It is not to be equated with the a of Skt. parayati 'to take across', which continues \*por-eie- by Brugmann's law.

\*fat- m. 'foot' - Go. fotus m. 'id.', ON f6tr, pl. fretr m. 'foot; leg', Far. f6tur, pl. f£1tur m. 'foot', Elfd.feot m. 'foot; leg', OE/ot, pl. fat m. 'foot', E/oot, OFri.fot m. 'id.', OS Jot m. 'id.', Du. voet c. 'id.', OHG feoz m. 'id.', G Fufl m. 'id.' ⇒ \*pod(IE) - Hitt piit- - pat- c. 'foot', Skt. p&t, pad- m. 'id.', YAv. pad- m. 'id.', OP piida- 'id.', Arm. otn 'id.', Gr. Dor. rrwc;, lon.-Att rrouc;, rro66c; m. 'id.' < \*pod-; Lat. pes, pedis m. 'id.', Olr. ed 'space, interval' < \*ped-. The IE word for 'foot', a static root noun nom. \*p6d-s, gen. \*ped-s, \*p6d-m. Germanic generalized the lengthened grade of the root. See also \*fetan- and \*futon-.

\*fōþra- n. 'load; vessel; fodder' - OE fōþor n. 'cartload; fodder', MLG voder n. 'cartload', MDu. voeder n. 'wine vat; lead weight', OHG feodar n. 'fodder; cartload', MHG vuoder n. 'id.', G Fuder m. 'cartload' ⇒ \*peh₂;3-tro- (GM/IE) - Skt. patra- n. 'vessel. pot'. Given the pan-WGm. meaning 'load' or 'weight', it seems likely that the word continues \*forpra-, i.e. an instrumental noun to \*forjan-, by dissimilation of the first r. The outcome may have merged with two other instrumental nouns, viz. 1) \*fopra- from PIE \*peh₃-tro- 'drinking cup, vessel', and 2) \*fOdra- 'fodder' (q.v.) < quasi-PIE \*peh₂(t)-tro-.

\*fraisan- s.v. 'to tempt' - Go. fraisan s.v. 'id.', OE friisian w.v. 'to ask, inquire, tempt' ⇒ \*pr6is-e- (IE?). A strong verb attested only in Gothic. It gave rise to OFri. /res, OS fresa, OHG freisa f. 'danger; scare' < \*fraiso-, whence OE friisian 'to try', OS freson 'to try, risk', Du. vrezen 'to fear', MHG vreisen 'to endanger' < \*fraisojan- and ON freista w.v. 'to tempt' < \*fraistojan-, which implies a noun \*fraisto- < \*prois-teh₂-. An archaic formation is Go. fraistubni f. 'temptation' < \*proist-mn-ieh₂-. Etymology unclear. From \*pro-h₂is- "to ask forth" (Hoffmann 1903: 38)? Cf. \*aisko-.

\*fraiwa- n. 'seed' - Go.fraiw n. 'id.', ON fr«!,frj6 n. 'id.', Elfd.frie n. 'id.' (IE?). A word with a debated etymology. The connection with Lat. prae9nans (implying PGm. \*frai9wa-) is implausible on many different levels (pace Wiedemann 1904: 43-44), and the same is true for the alleged links with PIE \*sper- 'to sow' and Lat. parere 'to bring forth' (Bugge 1899: 457) < \*pr(H)-ie-. Within Germanic, the attestation of ON frjar, frj6r, fr«!r, lee!. frj6r adj. 'fertile; prolific' < \*fraiwa- clearly seems to point to a stem \*/rai(wJmeaning 'fecund'. Both in form and meaning, this element is reminiscent of Freyr 'fertility deity' < \*frauja-. We may therefore consider the possibility that \*fraiwa- was metathesized from \*frawja-, a collective of some kind. The word is then likely to be related to ON fry90 f. 'blossoming, excellence' < \*fruwwipo-. For a similar metathesis, cf. \*hraiwa- 'flesh' < \*krou-io-.

\*fraton- w.v. 'to fart' - ON frata w.v. 'id.', Far. frata w.v. 'id.', Elfd. frata w.v. 'id.', MHG varzen w.v. 'id.', Gfarzen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*pord-neh₂- (IE). An o-grade iterative to the strong verb \*fertan- - \*fretan- ( q.v.).

\*frapjan- s.v. 'to understand' - Go. frapjan s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*pr6t-ie- (NEUR) - Lith. prasti (prantil) 'to become accustomed to, dial. to apprehend', su-prasti 'to understand' < \*pro-n-t-. A strongjan-verb with close cognates in Baltic. Also cf. Go.frapi n. 'mind' < \*frapja-, OHG frad adj. 'nimble, quick; vigorous, strenuous' < \*frapa- and \*frOda- 'wise' (q.v.). The appurtenance of OHG ant-frist 'translation' < \*-fresti-(?) is uncertain (see Liberman 2008: 211).

\*frauja(n)- m. 'lord' - Go.frauja m. 'id.', OEfrea m. 'id.', 0Sfr6,fr6io m. 'id.', MDu. vroon, vrone m. 'lord' (with n generalized from the oblique cases), OHGfr6 m. 'id.', MHG vr6 m. 'id.' (GM). Unclear etymology. The assumed etymological link with Skt pilrva- adj. 'front, first', YAv. pauruua-, paouruua-, pouruua- adj. 'being in front, first, former', OCS. pnv'b adj. 'first' < \*prH-uo- is problematic, since the full-grade form \*proHu-io- would yield PGm. \*\*fr6jan- (with \*-6u- giving \*-6-) rather than \*fraujan-.

\*frawa- adj. 'quick, light' - ON frar, fr6r adj. 'swift, lightfooted', Icel. frar adj. 'id.', Far. fr6ur adj. 'cheerful, gay, happy', OS frii., fro adj. 'happy', OHG frao, fr6 adj. 'id.', G froh adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*prou-o- (IE) - Skt. pravati 'to jump' < \*preu-e-. Also cf. OHG frawida, frewida, G Freude f. 'joy' < \*frawip6- and probably also the ablauting Du. vreugd(e) c. 'id.' < \*fruwip6- (with \*-uwi- > \*-ugi-).

\*fregi- adj. 'renowned' - ON frcegr adj. 'famous', OE ge-frci!ge adj. 'renowned, celebrated', OS gi-friigi adj. 'known, famous' (DRY). A vṛddhi-gerundive to \*frehnan- (q.v.). The original meaning was "being rumored".

\*frego- f. 'question' - Du. vraag c. 'id.', OHGfrii.ga f. 'question, investigation, doctrine', G Frage f. 'question' (IE) - OAv.f(a)rasii- f. 'question', Lat prex f. 'prayer, supplication', W rheg 'gift; curse' < \*preK{-eh₂)-. An 6-stem derived from the preterite root of the strong verb \*frehnan-: there is no direct link between the lengthened grade of \*freg6- and the one found in the root noun Skt. prds- f. 'dispute, questioning, statement or assertion in a debate or lawsuit'. The 6-stem served as the basis for OHG frii.gen - frii.g6n, G fragen, OS friigon, OFri. fregia 'to ask' < \*freg6jan-. Related to \*fursk6- (q.v.).

\*frehnan- s.v. 'to announce, rumor' - Go. fraihnan s.v. 'to ask a question', ON fregna s.v. 'to hear of, be informed of; to ask', OE frignan s.v. 'to ask, inquire', gi-frignan s.v. 'to find out, hear about, learn something through asking', OS fregnan s.v. 'to ask', gijregnan s.v. 'to hear tell of, learn about' ⇒ \*preJ<-ne- (IE) - Skt. prafoa- m. 'question' < \*pre/oJ<-no-; Lith. piFsti (persu) 'to propose (a marriage); to offer'; Lith. prasyti 'to ask, demand', OCS prositi, Ru. prosft' (prosu) 'to ask', SCr. prositi 'to beg, ask for a girl's hand' < \*proJ<-eie-. A strong verb with a nasal suffix that originally only occurred in the present, cf. Go. fraihnan, pret. frah -frehum and ON fregna, pret fra -fragum. In Old Saxon and Old English, the verb generalized the nasal, and switched to the third class: OS (gi-)fregna, pret. fragn, frugnun and OE (ge-)frignan, (ge-)fringan, pret. frregn, frregn - frugnon, frungon. Originally, \*frehnansimply meant 'to ask', while the meaning 'to learn' of the perfective variant \*ga-frehnan- seems to have developed out of "to have inquired". Related to \*frego- and \*fursko-.

\*freka- adj. 'avaricious' - Go. faihu-friks adj. 'id.', ON frekr adj. 'greedy; harsh; rigorous', Far. frekur adj. 'avid, covetous; severe; vigorous; audacious', Elfd. frek adj. 'friendly, nice', OE free adj. 'eager, greedy, bold', MDu. vrec adj. 'greedy', Du. vrek c. 'avaricious person', OHG freh adj. 'eager, avid', MHG vrech adj. 'brave' ⇒ \*preg-o- (EUR) - Pol. pragnqc 'to yearn for', Cz. prahnouti 'to covet' < \*prog-ne-; Slov. praziti 'to stew' < \*prog-eie-; W rhewydd adj. 'lascivious' < \*preg-io-; OBret. rogedou pl. 'orgies' (Lane 1933: 258). An adjective to a European root \*preg- 'to desire'. Ablauting with OEfrrec, MDu. vrac adj. 'avaricious' < \*fraka- < \*prog-o-.

\*freknon- (. 'freckle' - lcel. frekna f. 'id.', Far. fr¢kna f. 'id.', Nw. frekne, frokne f. 'id.', Sw. friikne 'id.', Da. fregne 'id.', E freckle (NEUR) - Close to Pol. prftgaty adj. 'striped' < \*prng-eh₂-. Most of the Nordic forms can be derived from ON \*frekna (whence ME frekne, E obs.frecken as well as freckle), but Far.frf:fkna and Nw.frokne seem to continue a paradigm \*frakna, obl. \*frpknu. It seems that the original plural \*freknur was reanalyzed as a root noun form continuuing ON \*freknr < \*frfJknr after the vocalization of -r to -ur. This then triggered the back-formation of a singular \*frakna and an oblique \*frpknu in Faroese and Norwegian.

\*freusan- s.v. 'to freeze' - ON frj6sa s.v. 'id.', Elfd. friuosa s.v. 'id.', OE freosan s.v. 'id.', E to freeze, MLG vresen s.v. 'id.', MDu. vriesen s.v. 'id.', Du. vriezen s.v. 'id.', OHG friosan s.v. 'id.', G frieren s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*preus-e- (WEUR) -

label_gloss: 155 W rhew m. 'frost, rime' < \*preuso-; ?Lat pruina f. 'frost' ( < \*pruryina < \*prus-u-iH-neh₂, Hamp 1973a?). An old strong verb. The Verner alternation \*freusan-, ptc. \*fruzana- was resolved in most modern Germanic languages, cf. E to freeze
opening: \*freupan- 155 W rhew m. 'frost, rime' < \*preuso-; ?Lat pruina f. 'frost' ( < \*pruryina < \*prus-u-iH-neh₂, Hamp 1973a?). An old strong verb. The Verner alternation \*freusan-, ptc. \*fruzana- was resolved in most modern Germanic languages, cf. E to freeze - frozen vs. G frieren - ge-froren, but retained in Du. vriezen - ge-vroren and Ice!. frj6sa freoinn ( < ON fr'1rinn.frerinn with dissimilation of the second r, cf. ON fr'1r, frer, icel.freo- n. 'frost'). The original past participle \*prus-to- was preserved as \*frusta- (q.v.). Further cf. Go.frius\* n.(?) 'frost' < \*freusa-.

\*freupan- s.v. 'to froth' - OE ri-freooan s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*preutH-e- (IE) - Skt pr6thati 'to snort (of horses)' < \*preutH-e-; Av. fra68ataspa- 'with snorting horses'. A marginally attested strong verb, the source of some nominal formations in Nordic, i.e. ON, Ice!. frauo n. 'froth', Far. froyour m. 'id.' < \*fraudija- < \*proutH- and ON, Far.frooa, Nw.frode f., Da.frdde c. 'id.' < \*frup6n-. The root \*preutH- may be an extension of PIE \*pru-, cf. Skt. vi-pru- 'to spray in all directions' < \*pru-. Another extension to the same root may be \*prus-: Skt pru$r:i6ti, OCS prysnpti 'to sprinkle' (with secondary \*y) < \*prus-ne- and Ice!. freyr m. 'froth' < \*frauza-. A primitive meaning 'to blow' is implied by Nw. dial.froe f. (=/rode) 'gust of wind'.
\*fri(j)a- adj. 'free' - Go. freis adj. 'id.', OE freo, fri adj. 'id.', E free, OFri. fri adj. 'id.', OS fri adj. 'id.', Du. vrij adj. 'id.', OHG fri adj. 'id.', G frei adj. 'id.' ¸

\*priH-o- (IE) - Skt. priya- adj. 'dear' < \*priH-o-. Also cf. Go. frei-hals, ON frjals, OE freols adj. 'free' < \*friOJa-halsa- and the nominalized OHG, OFri.fri-hals m. 'freedom'. The meaning 'free' arose from 'related', a shift that points to a clan-based societal system. Cognate with \*fri0}6n- (q.v.).

\*fri(j)on- w.v. 'to love' - Go. frijon w.v. 'to love, like', ON frja w.v. 'to love', Far. frfggja w.v. 'to propose, court', OE freogan, frigan w.v. 'to love, marry', OS frion w.v. 'id.', Du. vrijen s.v. 'to make love' ⇒ \*priH-eh₂- (IE) - Close to OCS prijati 'to take care of, SCr. prijati 'to please, agree with' < \*priH-eh₂-; further cf. Skt priyate 'to be pleased' < \*priH-ie-. An old PIE verbal formation. See also \*friOJa- and \*friOJ6nd-.

\*frilj)ond- m. 'friend' - Go. frijonds m. 'id.', ON frrendi m. 'friend; relative', Far. frrendi m. 'relative', OE freond m. 'friend, loved one, relative', E friend, 0Fri.friii.nd,fri6nd m. 'id.', ODu.friuntm. 'id.', Du. vriend c. 'id.', OHGfriunt m. 'friend, loved one', G Freund m. 'friend' (DRV). The lexicalized participle of the verb \*fri0}6n- (q.v.).

\*fripu- m. 'friendship, peace' - ON friOr m. 'peace; security; friendship', Far. friaur m. 'peace; tranquility', Elfd. fria m. 'id.', OE freoao m. 'id.', OFri. fretho m. 'id.', OS frithu,frethu m. 'peace; security', Du. vrede c. 'peace', OHG fridu m. 'peace; protection, security', G Friede m. 'peace' (ORV). A tu-stem derived from \*fri(j)a- < \*priH-o-: the short Gm. \*i cannot have regularly developed from the form \*priH-tu-, so it must be assumed that the tu-stem was derived at a relatively late stage, i.e. after the merger of \*-faand \*-ija-, or - if Dybo's law applied, at a stage when the pretonic shortening of\*prfW6- to \*priWo- had already taken place.

\*froda- adj. 'wise, experienced' - Go. froþs adj. 'id.', ON fr6or adj. 'id.', OE frod adj. 'id.', OFri. frod adj. 'id.', OS frOd adj. 'id.', OHG fruot adj. 'id.', MHG vruot adj. 'id.' (NEUR) - Close to Lith. protas m. 'sense', Latv. prats m. 'mind; will' (with secondary PBalt. \*a). An o-grade adjective related to \*fraþjan- (q.v.). Also cf. Go. frodei, ON frreoi, OHGfruoti, MHG vruote f. 'wisdom, sense'.

\*froi adv. 'early' - MLG vro adv. 'early', MDu. vroech adj. 'id.', Du. vroeg adv., OHGfruo adv. 'id.', MHG vruo adv. 'id.', Gfruh adv. 'id.' ⇒ \*pr6H-i (IE). A petrified locative identical to Gr. rrpwi, rrp£i> adv. 'early in the morning' (for which cf. Skt. priitar adv. 'early in the morning' < \*proH-ter). The adverb gave rise to the adj. \*froja-, cf. OHG fruoji, G fruhe, which is formally identical to Gr. rrpwwc;.

\*frug/kkan- m. 'frog' - OEfrogga m. 'id.', MEfrogge,frock,froke 'id.', Efrog ⇒ \*pruk-en- (GM). The formal variation within ME, i.e. frogge < \*fruggan-, frock < \*frukkaand froke < \*frukan- points to an old n-stem \*frugo, gen. \*frukkaz < \*pr(tgh-on, \*prugh-n-6s or perhaps rather \*pruk-բn. \*pruk-n-6s. The original paradigm was split up into 1) \*frugo, \*fruggaz on the one hand, and 2) \*fruko, \*frukkaz on the other. ON frauki m. 'frog' may be related, too, if it continues an ablauting form \*fraukan- from Pre-Gm. \*fraukkan-. The word has alternatively been derived from \*frauokan- (\*-kan- is a diminutive suffix), but the alleged derivational base, i.e. ON frauar, Ice!. frauour, Nw. frau(d), OSw. fredher, Da. fre 'frog' probably developed from \*fraugr (with -g- > \*-a- in labial environment as in fjpgur n. '4' < \*fjpdur < \*feduro). ME frrld, E obs. fraud 'id.' is therefore likely to have been adopted from Old Norse. No certain etymology: the usual comparison with Ru. prygat', prjgnut' 'to leap' is highly doubtful, as the accent implies a root \*pruHgh- or \*prug- rather than \*prugh-. This leaves the intra-Germanic connection with \*fruska( q.v.).

\*fruman- num. 'former, first' - Go. fruma num. 'id.', ON frum- pref. 'first, oldest', OE forma num. 'id.', E former, OFri. forma num. 'id.', OS formo num. 'id.' ⇒ \*prH-mH-on- (IE) - OPru. pirmas, Lith. pirmas num. 'first' < \*prH-mHo-; Gr. rrpaμoc; m. 'foremost' < \*pr-mH-o-. It is likely that the numeral contains the comparative suffix PGm. \*-uman< \*-mH-on-, but the expected outcome of a proto-form \*pr-mH-on- would be \*\*furman-. Possibly, this form was remodeled into \*fruman- on the basis of other ordinals ending in \*-uman-. A similar remodeling took place in Latin, where primus replaced expected \*purmus < \*pr-mH-o- or \*pramus < \*prh₂-mH-o- due to the productivity of the suffix \*-imus (cf. optimus).

\*fruska- m. 'frog' - ON froskr m. 'id.', OE forsc, frox m. 'id.', OHG /rose m. 'id.', G Frosch m. 'id.' (GM). A formation that in all probability continues older \*fru9-ska- with the same root element as in \*fru9/kkan- 'frog'. The derivation from \*frauda'froth' (see \*freupan-) was inspired by ON frauor 'frog', but this form is likely to have developed from \*frau9r in view of ON frauki 'id.' < \*frauk(k)an-.

\*frusta- m./n. 'frost' - ONfrost n. 'id.', OEforst m. 'id.', Efrost, OFri.forst m. 'id.', OS frost m. 'id.', Du. vorst c. 'id.', OHG frost m. 'id.', G Frost m. 'id.' ⇒ \*prus-to- (DRV). Derived from \*freusan- (q.v.).
\*fian- m. 'mold' - ONfai m. 'rottenness', Far.fa9vi m. 'id.' ⇒ \*puH-on- (IE). An n-stem to the PIE root \*puH-, for which see \*faujan- 1.

\*fugla- m. 'bird' - Go. fu9/s m. 'id.', ON /09/, fu9/ m. 'id.', Far. fu9/ur m. 'id.', Elfd. fu9el m. 'id.', OE fu90/ m. 'id.', E fowl, OFri. fu9el m. 'id.', OS fu9al m. 'id.', Du. vo9el c. 'id.', OHGfo9al,fu9al m. 'id.', G Vogel m. 'id.' ⇒ \*pluk-16- (DRV). Some connect Li th. pailkStis 'bird' < \*pouk-sti- (e.g. Magnusson 1989: 214), but the form \*fu9la- is more likely to have developed from \*flu9-la(by dissimilation), and may thus have been derived from \*fleu9an- 'to fly' (q.v.).

\*fuhon- f. 'she-fox' - Go.fauho f. 'id.', ON f6a,faa f. 'id.', MLG vo f. 'id.', OHG voha f. 'id.', MHG vohe f. 'id.' (IE). The feminine to \*fuhsa- 'fox' (q.v.). Another formation with the same meaning is represented by ME Jixen(e}, vixen, E vixen (with southern v- for f), MHG viihsinne, G Fiichsin, but unlike \*fuhon-, this is a secondary derivation from \*fuhsa-.
\*fuhsa- m. 'fox' - ON fox n. 'deceit', Ice!. fox n. 'fox', OE fox m. 'id.', E fox, WFri.foks m. 'id.', EFri.foaks m. 'id.', MLG vos(se) m. 'id.', MDu. vos m., vosse f.

\*fuk(k)on- w.v. 'to blow' - Icel. fokka w.v. 'to mess around, rush', MLG vucken w.v. 'to rush, scud', MDu. vocken w.v. 'to blow', MHG fochen w.v. 'to blow' ⇒ \*pug-neh₂- (EUR?) - Either to Latv. pilga f. 'gust of wind' < \*pug-eh₂- or to Arm. p'ukf'J 'breath, wind, fart' < \*pug-o- (with sound symbolic aspiration?). The different dialects with their vacillation between \*fukk6n- and \*fuk6npoint to a formation \*pug-neh₂-, which could be related to Latv. pilga and Arm. p'ulef'J. The sometimes compared Lith. pilksti (pilksCiu) 'to cough, snarl', on the other hand, is a loanword from LG pusten 'to pant, snarl' (with klusilspring in the sequence -us-). See also \*feukan- - \*ftikan-.

\*ftila- adj. 'filthy, foul' - Go. fuls adj. 'foul, putrid', ON fell adj. 'foul, stinking; mean', OE fill adj. 'foul, nasty', E foul, OFri. fill adj. 'foul; vile', OS fulitha f. 'filth', MLG ftil adj. 'foul, bad', Du. vuil adj. 'dirty', OHG ftil adj. 'rotten, decayed', G faul adj. 'putrid; lazy' ⇒ \*puH-lo- (IE) - Lith. pziliai m.pl. 'pus' < \*puH-1-io- and piaulaim.pl. 'mold' < \*peuH-1-io-. A lo-stem derived from a PIE root \*puH-. See also \*faujan- 1.

\*fulan- m. 'foal' - Go. fula m. 'id.', ON Joli m. 'id.', Elfd. fuoli m. 'id.', OE fola m. 'id.', E Joie, MLG vole m. 'id.', OHG folo m. 'id.', G Fohlen m. 'id.' ⇒ \*p/H-on(IE) - Gr. nwl.o<; m./f. 'foal' < \*p6/H-o-, W ebawl 'id.' < \*h₁ekwo-p6/H-o-; Alb. pe/e 'mare' < \*p6/H-neh₂-. Also cf. ON, Far., OSw. fyl, Sw. fol, Nw. fel(l}, Da. ffi1/ n. 'id.' < \*fulja- < \*p/H-io-, the WGm. diminutive MDu. volen, vu/en, Du. veulen, OHG vuli, vulfn, MHG viile, viilfn, G Fullen n. 'foal' < \*ful-lfn(a)- and the Franconian feminine MHG viilche, MDu. vilke, Du. dial. vulke, vilke f. '(young) mare' < \*fulik6(n)-. In IE, the word is sometimes reconstructed with a \*u in the root, viz. \*p(o)ul- (cf. Orel 2000: 14), but this seems to be precluded by W ebawl. I therefore reconstruct a root noun \*polH-s, gen. \*p/H-6s (Lubotsky 1988: 132). This formation may ultimately have been derived from a verbal root \*pelH- as in Alb. pjell 'to give birth' (cf. Huld 1984: 102).

\*fuldo- f. 'earth, ground, field, the world' - ON fold f. 'id.', Far. poet.fold f. 'id.', OE folde f. 'id.', OS folda f. 'id.' ::) \*plth₂-eh₂- (IE) - Cf. Skt. p[thvf-, p[thivf- f. 'earth, Mother Earth', YAv. zqm parai9Plm, paraape ... zamo 'broad earth' < \*plth₂-u-ih₂-. The Germanic and Indo-lranian forms continue a feminine form of the adj. 'flat'. In Indo-European, it presumably occurred with the word for 'land' in a sacral context. See also \*fetpa-.

\*fulgen· w.v. 'to follow, accompany' - ON fylgja w.v. 'id.', Far. fy/gja w.v. 'id.', Elfd. fya w.v. 'id.', OE fylgan,folgian w.v. 'id.', E to follow, OSfolgon w.v. 'id.', Du. vo/gen w.v. 'id.', OHG fa/gen w.v. 'id.', G folgen w.v. 'id.' ::) \*plk-[eh₁)-ie- (GM). ON fylgja and OE fylg{e)an continue a formation \*fulgjan- that can be reconciled with OE fe[o)lan s.v. 'to proceed, go, reach' < \*felhan- < \*pelk-e-. Extra-Gm. *comparanda* are unreliable: Bret. heul 'to follow' probably continues \*se/gh- rather than \*pe/gh- and the connection with CS p!'bzati, Ru. polzat', SCr. piizati 'to crawl' must be abandoned in view of the accent pointing to \*plg-eh₂- rather than \*plgh-ehi-.

\*fulla- 1 adj. 'full' - Go. fulls adj. 'id.', ON fullr adj. 'id.', Far. fullr adj. 'id.', Elfd. full adj. 'id.', OE full adj. 'id.', E full, OS full adj. 'id.', Du. vol adj. 'id.', OHG fol adj. 'id.', G vol/ adj. 'id.' ::) \*plhi-no- (IE) - Skt. purria-, Av. parana-, Olr. lan 'id.', Lith. pilnas, OCS p!'bn'b adj. 'filled, full' < \*plhi-no-. An adjective continuing the original past participle of the verbal root
\*pelhi- 'to fill'. See also \*felu- - \*fulu-.

\*fulla- 2 n. 'cup' - ON full n. 'goblet', OE full n. 'cup' ::) \*pl-n6- (IE) - Gr. ntla f. 'milk pail; drinking bowl, goblet' < \*pel-ih₂- and Gr. nelll<;, neA.t<; 'basin' < \*pel-i-s, gen. \*pe/-i-6s; OCS po/'b 'scoop' < \*pol-a-. An Indo-European word for a certain vessel. Unrelated to \*fulla- 1.

\*fulmo- f. 'palm of the hand' - ME folm f. 'id.', OS folmos f.pl. 'both hands', OHG fol ma f. 'palm' ::) \*plh₂-m-eh₂- (IE) - Gr. mv.aμT) f. 'palm of the hand' ( = Lat. pa/ma) < \*plh₂-em-eh₂-; Olr. ltim f. 'hand' < \*plh₂-m-ehi-. An old m-stem. Within Germanic, it is possible to connect ON fa/ma w.v. 'to grope' < \*falmojan-, a verb possibly derived from a lost mo-stem \*falmaz < \*polh₂-mo-.

\*fundon- w.v. 'to try to find(?)' - OE fundian w.v. 'to try to find, to strive, intend', OS fundon w.v. 'to strive, proceed' ::) \*pnt-neh₂-(?) (DRV?). The iterative to \*ftnpan- (q.v.). PGm. \*fandon- can be an o-grade variant of the same iterative.

\*funhsti- f. 'fist' - OE JYst f. 'id.', E fist, MDu. vuust f. 'id.', Du. vuist c. 'id.', OHG ftlst f. 'id.', G Faust f. 'id.' ⇒ \*pn/(N-ti- (IE) - Identical to OCS pˣstb f. 'fist'. Probably a derivation from PIE \*penkwe 'five', for which see \*fimfe.

\*funhtu- adj. 'humid, moist' - OE ftlht adj. 'id.', OS ftlht adj. 'id.', MDu. vocht(e), vucht(e) adj. 'id.', Du. vochtig adj. 'id.', OHG ftlht, ftlhti adj. 'id.', G feucht adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*pnk-tu- (IE?) - Skt parika- m. 'mud' < \*pe/onk-o-(?). A u-stem adjective that became remodeled into a ja -stem in the continental WGm. languages. Possibly related to Skt parika- (if this word is of IE origin). The traditional comparison with \*fanja- 'bog' is doubtful: it would imply that both Skt. and Gm. added a k-suffix, which is rather uncertain.

\*funkon- w.v. 'to sparkle' - MDu. vonken w.v. 'to smolder; to sparkle', MHG vunken w.v. 'to shimmer, shine, sparkle', ent-vunken 'to ignite' ⇒ \*pnk-neh₂- (GM). The iterative to the strong verb \*finkan- as well as the derivational base for OHG Junko, G Funke(n), MLG vunke, MDu. vonke, Du. vonk, ME Junke 'spark' < \*funkan-. The etymon has no extra-Gm. cognates, but it is possible that the meaning 'to spark' arose in elliptic use of the verb \*fanhan- 'to catch (fire)'. The received derivation of the n-stem \*funkan- from PIE \*ph₂un- 'fire' (for which see \*for - \*fun-) is erroneous (pace Pokorny JEW: 828).

\*furdu- m. 'ford' - OE ford m. 'id.', OS ford m. 'id.', MDu. vort, voorde m. 'id.' ⇒ \*pr-tu- (IE) - Av. paratu- m. 'crossing', MP puh/, NP pul 'bridge', Lat portus m. 'entrance, port, refuge', OW rit, W rhyd m. 'ford' < \*pr-tu-. Also cf. the full grade ON fjpror m. 'fjord' < \*ferpu- < \*per-tu-.

\*furh· f. 'furrow' - ON for f. 'id.', OE furh f. 'id.', E furrow, OFri. furch f. 'id.', MDu. vore f. 'id.', Du. voor c. 'id.', OHG furh, furuh d. 'id.', G Furche f. 'id.' ⇒ \*pfk- (IE) - Skt. pcirsiina- m. 'rift' < \*pe/or/(-ono-; Lat. porca f. 'ridge between two furrows' < \*p(o)rl<-eh₂-; Mir. etarche, etrige, eitre f. 'id.' < \*h₁enter-prl<-ieh₂-; MW rhych m./ f. 'furrow'? An archaic root noun \*furh-z, dat. \*furh-i (cf. OE ffrh). WFri.furg and Du. dial.furg (in the originally Frisian territories) point to a Verner variant OFri. \*furg (cf. Weijnen 1996: 228), which is in agreement with the reconstruction of a root noun \*p6rk-s, gen. \*prk-es, dat. \*prk-f. Also cf. the ablauting Nw. dial.Jere m. 'ridge between two furrows' < \*ferhan-.

\*furhno· f. 'trout' - OE forn(e) f. 'id.', OS forhna.furhnia f. 'id.', MDu. voorne f. '?', Du. voorn c. 'roach', OHG forhana f. 'trout', MHG forhen, forhel f. 'id.' ⇒ \*pfl<-neh₂- (IE) - Olr. ere m. 'perch, salmon' < \*perl<-o-.

\*furhta- adj. 'fearful' - Go. faurhts adj. 'id.', OS for[a)ht adj. 'id.', OHG fora ht adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*prk-to- (IE) - ToAB priiskii- 'to fear' < \*prk-sk-. A Tocharian-Germanic isogloss; the proposed connection with Arm. erkn, pl. erkun-k' 'labor pains, pang; fear' is formally problematic because the form \*pergw- as reconstructed by Pedersen 1906: 398-9 would give Arm. \*\*herk-. See further \*furhtjan-.

\*furhtjan- w.v. 'to fear' - Go.faurhtjan w.v. 'id.', OEfyrhtan.fryhtan w.v. 'to frighten; tremble with fear', ME frighten w.v. 'to frighten, become frightened', OFri. fruchta w.v. 'id.', OHG furihtan w.v. 'to fear', G ftirchten w.v. 'id.' (DRV). The formation \*furhtjan- unites two different formations: 1) a simple denominative verb 'to fear' created to \*furhto-, and 2) a factitive 'to frighten' derived from \*furhta- (q.v.).
\*furht6· f. 'fright' - OFri. fruchta f. 'id.', OS forhta f. 'id.', OHG forhta f. 'id.', MHG vorhte, vorht f. 'id.' ⇒ \*prk-t-eh₂· (DRV). See \*furhta- and \*furhtjan-.

\*furhwon- f. 'fir' - ON Jura f. 'id.', OE furh f. 'id.', OHG voraha, vorha f. 'id.', MHG vorhe f. 'id.', G Forhe f. 'id.' ⇒ \*pfkw-eh₂- (IE). A zero-grade formation related to \*ferhwa- 1 'oak' (q.v.).

\*furi adv.jprep. 'in front of, for' - Go. faur adv.jprep. 'id.', ON fyr prep. 'id.', OE for, fore prep. 'id.', E for, OS for, fora prep. 'id.', Du. voor prep./adv. 'for; before', OHG furi prep./adv. 'id.', G ftir prep. 'id.' ⇒ \*prh₂·i (IE) - Gr. nap, napu, napa( adv./prep. 'besides, by, from, next to, alongside', Lat. prae adv./prep. 'in front of, before' < \*prh₂(-ei).

\*firjan- w.v. 'to castrate' - OE [a-)fYran w.v. 'id.', Du. vuren, vieren w.v. 'id.', OHG ar-firian w.v. 'id.' (NEUR) - Lith. pjauti (pjauju) 'to cut, slaughter', Latv. p]ailt 'to mow, harvest' < \*peuH-e- (cf. OPru. piuclan 'scythe' < \*peuH-tlo-). A factitive to an unattested adj. \*fira-, cf. OHG ur-fir m. 'eunuch' < \*JUra< \*puH-ro-.

\*fursa- m. 'gush(?)' - ON fors,foss n. 'torrent; waterfall', Far. fossur m. 'id.', OSw. fors m. 'id.', Elfd. fuoss m. 'id.' ⇒ \*pfs-o- (IE) - Hitt. papparszi papparfonzi 'to sprinkle' < \*po-pars-ti, \*-enti; ToA priisii·, ToB piirsii- 'id.' < \*pers-; Skt. pf$ant- adj. 'speckled, spotted'; Lith. puFslas m. 'spatter, spray' <

\*fursko- f. 'inquiry' - OHG forsca f. 'id.', MHG vorsche f. 'id.' ⇒ \*prK-sk-eh₂- (IE/DRV) - Skt prchOti 'to ask', Av. parasaiti 'to ask, want', Lat posco 'to ask, require', Olr. arcaid 'to ask', W archaf'id.' < \*prK-ske-. A feminine \*prl{-sk-eh₂- derived from the well-attested IE sk-present \*prK-ske-. It again served as the basis for the verb \*furskojan-, cf. OHG forscon, G forschen, Du. vorsen 'to inquire, investigate'. See also \*frehnanand \*frego-.
\*furtu- m. 'fart' - MDu. vort m. 'id.', OHG furz m. 'id.', G Furz m. 'id.' ⇒ \*prd-u- (IE). A u-stem to the strong verb \*fertan- (q.v.).
\*futon- w.v. 'to go' - Nw.fota w.v. 'to step; to go' (DRV). The iterative to \*fetan- (q.v.).

\*fuPi- f. 'vagina' - 0 N fuo f. 'id.', Icel. fuo f. 'vagina; holes in a block of wood (for fishing rods)', Nw. fud f. 'vagina; anus, backside', MHG vut f. 'vagina' ⇒ \*put-i- (GM). Usually connected with the root of \*[Ula- 'putrid' (cf. Pokorny IEW: 848-9), but in view of the Ice!. verb fuoa 'to drill a hole in a tree' and feroa-fuoa 'buckle; travel bag', this seems impausible. MHG votze (cf. LG hunds-fott, Du. honds-vod 'worthless guy', lit 'dog vagina') < \*futton- and ON fytta < \*futtjon- point to a geminated root \*futt-, which may have arisen in an n-stem \*fu/Jo, gen. \*futtaz < \*put-on, \*put-n-6s. No further etymology, though close to Lat. pubes f.pl. 'private parts' < \*peudh-i-.

\*gadojan- w.v. 'to fit (together)' - OFri. gadia w.v. 'to engage in', MDu. gaden w.v. 'to fit, please, mate', OHG be-gaton w.v. 'to gather', MHG (ge-)gaten w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*9hodh-eh₂-ie- (DRV) - Ru. godft' (goiu) 'to wait, loiter', SCr. goditi 'to please' < \*ghodh-eie-; OCS god'b m. '(suitable) time, year', Ru. god m. 'year', SCr. god m. 'important holiday, year, right time' < \*ghodh-o-.

\*gadurojan- w.v. 'to gather' - OE gredrian w.v. 'id.', OFri. gaduria w.v. 'id.', MDu. gaderen w.v. 'id.', Du. ver-gaderen 'id.' (ORV). A verb derived from an adverb \*gadur, cf. OE to gredere, OFri. gader, gadur adv. 'together'. It is possible that the derivational base consisted of the root \*gad- (see \*gadojan-) and the locative suffix \*-r as seen in \*hwar 'where' < \*kwo-r and \*par 'there' < \*to-r. For a typologically similar formation, cf. Lith. dabai' 'now' from the root \*dhobh- 'to suit' (see \*daban-) and the same locative suffix.

\*gaidwa- n. 'lack' - Go. gaidw n. 'id.', OE giid n. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghoidh-uo- (NEUR). Although Go. gaidw can be interpreted as a \*-pwa- derivation of a PGm. root \*gai- < PIE \*ghh₂-ei- 'to be open', the link with \*gfda- 'desire' (q.v.) and the related Lith. gefsti and OCS zbdati seems more economical (Torp 1909: 133; Derksen 2008: 565).

\*gaisjan- w.v. 'to terrify' - Go. us-gaisjan\* w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghois-eie- (IE) - YAv. zae5a- adj. 'terrible' < \*ghois-o-. A causative to the root \*gfs-, cf. Go. us-geisnan w.v. 'to be aghast, terrified'. Also cf. ON geiski m. 'panic, fear' < \*gaiskan- as well as \*gaista- 'spirit'.

\*gaista- m. 'spirit, ghost' - OE giist m. 'breath, spirit, soul, ghost', E ghost, OFri. giist, gest m. 'spirit, ghost, demon', OS yest m. 'soul, vitality, spirit, demon', Du. geest m. 'spirit', OHG geist m. 'id.', G Geist m. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghois-d-o- (IE). The word can be derived from \*gaisjan- 'to terrify' (q.v.) < \*ghois-eie- with a to-suffix: \*ghois-to- (cf. EWAhd: IV, 130-1). In view of the clearly denominative OE gci!stan 'id.' < \*gaistjan-, however, it is more probable that Germanic had an adjective identical to Av. zoitda- adj. 'terrible' < \*ghois-do-. The noun \*gaista- would then be parallel to Skt. Mr;Ja- m. 'anger'. Further cf. Lith. teisti (teidtiu) 'to wound' < \*\*gheis-d-ie-.

\*gait- f. 'goat' - Go. gaits f. 'id.', ON geit f. 'id.', Far. geit f. 'id.', Elfd. djiet f. 'id.', OE giit f. 'id.', E goat, OS get f. 'id.', Du. geit c. 'id.', OHG geiz f. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghaid- (WEUR) - Lat haedus m. 'young goat-buck, kid' < \*ghaid-o-. Also cf. the diminutive Go. gaitein n. 'little kid' < \*gaitfna- and OE greten, OHG geizfn adj. 'of goat' < \*gaitina-, which is formally comparable to Lat. haedfnus adj. 'of a kid' < \*ghaid-ein-o-. As to the etymology, the a-vocalism in Latin as well as the fact that the Germanic word is inflected as a root noun which is common in non-IE elements - indicate that the word may have been borrowed from an indigenous European language. It has earlier been suggested that the word was adopted from Semitic \*gadj- 'goat', cf. Arab.
