\*hlahtra- m. (n.?) 'laughter' - ON hlcitr m. 'id.', Far. lcitur m. 'id.', OE hleahtor, hlehter m. 'id.', E laughter, OHG lahtar, hlahtar n. 'id.', MHG lahter n. 'id.' (DRV). A tro-formation derived from \*hlahjan- (q.v.).

\*hlaiba- n. 'bread' - Go. hlaifs m. 'id.', ON hleifr m. 'id.', OE hliif m. 'id.', E loaf. OHG leip m. 'id.', G Laib m. 'id.' (GM). Possibly related to MLG leve-koke, MHG lebe-kuoche, G Leh-kuchen m. 'gingerbread' < \*hliba-kokan-? No further etymology. The comparison with Gr. KA.lоavo<; m. 'oven, furnace' is spurious.

\*hlainjan- w.v. 'to cause to lean' - OE hlrenan w.v. 'id.', E to lean, OHG leinen w.v. 'id.' (DRV). A late causative to \*hlinen- ( q.v.). Also cf. Go. hlain 'hill', ON hlein f. 'projecting rock, slope' < \*hlaina/6-.

\*hlaiwa- n. 'barrow, funeral mound' - Go. hlaiw m. 'tomb, grave', OE hlciw m. 'id.', OS hleo m. 'grave', OHG leo, hleo m. 'id.', MHG le m. 'id.' ⇒ \*l<loi-uo(IE). A wa-stem to the root \*l<lei- 'to be inclined', for which see \*hlinen-.

\*hlak(k)on- w.v. 'to laugh' - ON hlakka w.v. 'to cry, scream (of eagles); to rejoice, exult', OFri. hlakkia, hlakia w.v. 'id.', WFri. laitsje w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*klok-neh₂- (DRV). The iterative 3sg. \*hlakkofJi, 3pl. \*hlagunanpi to \*hlahUJan- (q.v.). OFri. hlakia and WFri. laitsje continue a variant \*hlakon- with a secondary singulate.
\*hlankjan- w.v. 'to twist, turn' - OE ge-hlencan w.v. 'to braid', MHG lenken w.v. 'to bend' ⇒ \*klong-eie- (GM).

\*hlapan- s.v. 'to load' - Go. af-hlapan s.v. 'to burden, load down', ON hlaoa s.v. 'to pile up; build; load', Far. laoa s./w.v. 'id.', OE hladan s.v. 'to heap, pile up, build, load', E to load, OFri. hleda s.v. 'Id.', OS hladan s.v. 'id.', Du. laden w./s.v. 'id.', OHG ladan, hladan s.v. 'id.', G laden s.v. 'id.' (NEUR) - Lith. kl6ti (kloju), Latv. kliit 'to cover' < \*kleh₂-ie-; OCS klasti 'to put', Ru. klast' (kladu), SCr. arch. klllsti 'to put' < \*kleh₂-dhh₁-. The Balta-Slavic forms imply a root \*kleh₂-, which means that the -d- in Germanic must be suffixal. Presumably, a t was added to the root when the original aorist \*klot < \*kleh₂-t was reanalyzed as a perfect (cf. \*st6d- 'stood' < \*steh₂-t). This reanalysis must have taken place after the merger of \*a and \*6, but before the great sound shifts, as is implied by e.g. the nominal derivative ON hlass n. 'cart-load' < \*klat-to- or \*klat-so-.

\*hlauni- f. 'loin, buttock' - ON hlaun n. 'id.', Nw. dial. faun f. 'id.' ⇒ \*l(/ou-ni(IE) - Skt. sr61J.i-, YAv. sraoni- f. 'buttock, hip'; OPru. slaunis 'thigh', Lith. slaunis f. 'thigh, hip', Latv. slaiina, slaune f. 'hip' < \*l(/ouni- (\*-(i)eh₂-); Lat. cliinis m./f. 'buttocks, haunch', W clun f. 'thigh'; with irregular loss of \*u: Gr. tl6vL<; f. 'haunch' < \*/(/on+. An old IE term for 'thigh'. •h/aupan- s.v. 'to jump, leap' - Go. us-hlaupan 'to leap forth, jump up', ON hlaupa s.v. 'to leap, jump; to run', Far. leypa s.v. 'to jump; to fall, tumble; to curdle', Elfd. Iopa s.v. 'to leap, run; to creep', OE h/eapan s.v. 'to leap, jump, dance, run', E to leap, OFri. hlapa s.v. 'to walk, run; to flow', OS hl6pan s.v. 'to run', Du. /open s.v. 'to walk, run', OHG loufan, hloufan s.v. 'id.', G laufen s.v. 'id.' (DRV) - Lith. klupti (klumpu), Latv. klupt 'to stumble' < \*klu-m-p-. A strong verb back-formed to the iterative \*hlupp6n- (q.v.) < \*klup-neh₂-. The root-final p continues a Pre-Gm. geminate, for which cf. \*hluppjan-. OFri. hlepa w.v. 'to call(?), summon(?)' appears to continue the causative \*hlaupjan-.
\*hlauta- m. 'lot' - Go. h/auts m. 'id.', ON hlautr m. 'id.', OS h/ot m. 'id.', OHG /oz, hloz m. 'id.' (DRV). An o-grade formation to \*hleutan- (q.v.).

\*hie/an- s.v. 'to steal' - Go. h/ifan s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*k/ep-e- (EUR) - Identical to Lat c/epo, -ere 'id.'; further cf. Gr. KJ.brw 'to steal' < \*klep-ie-; Olr. cluain f. 'deceit' < \*k/op-ni-; Cz. klopa f. 'lapel, flap' < \*klop-eh₂-; OPru. au-klipts 'concealed' < \*-kip-to- (with secondary zero-grade -i-?). A strong verb derived from a European root \*klep-. Also cf. Go. hliftus m. 'thief < \*klep-tu- with an agentive tu-suffix (for which cf. \*werdu- 'host').

\*hleuman- m. 'hearing' - Go. hliuma m. 'id.', ON hlj6mr m. 'sound, tone', Far. lj6mur m. 'id.' ⇒ \*J{/eu-mon- (IE) - Identical Av. sraoman- n. 'hearing'. A mn-stem created to the PIE root \*J{/eu-, for which see \*h/eupa- and \*h/ilda-. Also cf. OHG liumunt, hliumunt m. 'reputation', Skt. sr6mata n. 'id.' < \*J{/eu-mn-to-.

\*hleutan- s.v. 'to obtain by lot' - ON hlj6ta s.v. 'to get, obtain; must', Far. lj6ta s.v. 'to get as one's share; to be obliged, compelled to', OE h/eotan s.v. 'to cast lots; to obtain by lot', OS hliotan s.v. 'to acquire, receive', OHG /iozan, hliozan s.v. 'to draw lots', MHG liezen s.v. 'to draw lots; to soothsay, perform magic' ⇒ \*k/euHd-e- (NEUR) - Lith. kliiiti (kliilvu) 'to stick', Latv. k]iit 'to become, succeed' < \*kleuH-; Latv. k]iitas m. 'fate' < \*kleuH-to-. A strong verb apparently created to a Germanic and Baltic root \*kleuH-. The root-final dental of the Germanic verb is probably etymologically dis• similar from the dental of Lith. kliudyti (kliudail) 'to stick', -dyti < \*-dhh₁-eiebeing a productive verbal suffix. It is possible to speculate that the Germanic t continues the root \*dh₃- 'to give'. The underlying stem \*k/euH-dh₃would then originally have meant 'to give by lot'.

\*hleupa- n. 'listening' - Go. hliup\* n. 'hearing', ON hlj6o n. 'listening; silence; sound', Far. ljoo n. 'id.', Elfd. liuoo f. 'sound' ⇒ \*J{/eu-to- (IE) - Av. sraota- n. 'listening'. A to-stem derived from the PIE root \*J{/eu- 'to listen'. See also \*hlilda-.

\*hleuza- n. 'cheek' - ON hljr n. 'cheek; bow of a vessel; flat of an axe', Ice!. hljr n. 'cheek; bow of a vessel; flat of an axe', OE h/eor n. 'cheek, face', OS h/ior n. 'cheek', MDu. lier n. 'cheek, cheekbone' ⇒ \*k/eus-6- (IE?). In view of the potential etymological link with \*hlusen- 'to listen', the word is usually taken to have originally denoted the area around the ears. A typological parallel is provided by Olr. cluas f. 'hearing; ear' < \*li/ou-sth₂-eh₂-.

\*hlewa- n. 'shelter (from wind)' - ON hie n. 'shelter, lee', OE hleo(w) n. 'id.', ME le 'id.', OFri. hli m./n.? 'id.', OS hleo n. 'concealment', EDu. lije 'lee side', Du. lij-zijde 'id.' ⇒ \*J(lehru6- (IE). Also cf. the derived adjective ON hlyr 'mild, warm', OE hlfwe, hleowe 'warm, sheltered', E lew 'lukewarm', MDu. lieu, Du. luw 'sheltered' < \*hleuja-. The word is not ablauting with \*hlewa- (q.v.) < \*lilehruo-, as the regular zero grade \*lilhruo- would never have resulted in PGm. \*hlewa-. It is likely, therefore, that the short \*e of \*hlewa- arose from \*e due to Dybo's law of pretonic shortening.

\*hlewa- adj. 'lukewarm' - Icel. hlcir adj. 'melted; warm', MDu. laeu adj. 'lukewarm', Du. lauw adj. 'id.', OHG liio adj. 'id.', MHG Iii adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*k1ehi-uo(IE) - Lat caleo, -ere 'to be warm' < \*J(lh₁-ehr; without laryngeal: W clyd adj. 'warm', Lith. siftas, Latv. silts adj. 'id.' < \*lil-t6-. Closely related to \*hlewa- 'shelter'. Also cf. ON hl;;er adj. 'warm, mild' < \*hleja- < \*J(lehi-io-.

\*hlinen- w.v. 'to lean' - OE hlinian, hleonian w.v. 'id.', OS hlinon w.v. 'id.', Du. leunen w.v. 'id.', OHG linen, hlinen w.v. 'id.', G lehnen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*kli-n-ehr (IE) - YAv. ni-srinaomi lsg.pres. 'to lean' < \*lili-neu-; Gr. tlfvw 'to bend, incline, lean on, sink' < \*J(li-n-ie-; Lat. -clino, cliniire 'to bend' < \*lilei-neh₂-; Lith. slifiti (slinu) 'id.' < \*lili-n-e-. A stative verb created to an old nasal present \*J(li-neh₂- or \*lili-neu-. The root \*lilei- is again likely to continue an i-present to the root \*lie/-, for which see \*ha/pa-. See further \*hlainjan- and \*hlaiwa-.

\*hloan- s.v. 'to low, bellow' - OE hlowan s.v. 'id.', E to low, OS hloinga f. 'bellowing', ODu. luon, luogin 'id.', Du. loeien w.v. 'id.', OHG luoen s.v. 'id.', MHG liiejen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*lil6hi-e- (IE). An originally strong verb that in view of its o-grade and iterative semantics must continue an intensive liloh₁-e- to the root \*lielh₁- (for which see \*hal6n- / \*hulon-). In spite of OLFr. luogin and OHG luoen, there is no evidence for a j-suffix: this glide just like the w of OE hlowan arose as a hiatus filler in the separate Germanic daughter languages.

\*hlogjan- w.v. 'to make laugh' - Go. uf-hlohjan\* w.v. 'id.', ON hlregja wv. 'id.', Icel. hl;;egja w.v. 'id.', Nw. dial. lngja w.v. 'id.' (DRV). The causative to \*hlahOJan- (q.v.). Also cf. Far. /!iJgin, Nw. lgyen (lngjen) adj. 'funny'.
\*hliida- adj. 'loud' - OE hlud adj. 'id.', E loud, OFri. hllid adj. 'id.', WFri. IUd adj. 'id.', OS hllid adj. 'id.', Du. luid adj. 'id.', OHG Wt, hllit adj. 'id.', G laut adj. 'id.' (DRV).

\*hluni- m. 'maple tree' - ON hlynr m. 'id.', 0Sw. /f1n m.(?) 'id.', Sw. /On c. 'id.', OE hlyn 'id.(?)', MLG /onen-ho/t n. 'maple wood' ⇒ \*k/un-i- (EUR) - Lith. klevas, Latv. k]avs m. 'maple tree' < \*kleu-o-; OCS klen'b, Ru. k/en, SCr. klen m. 'id.' < \*klen-o-. A European word. The alternation of Gm. \*k/un-, Bait. \*kleu- and Slav. \*k/en- is difficult to account for, but can perhaps be resolved by reconstructing a proterodynamic uen-stem, nom. \*k/eu-un, gen. \*klu-en-s that was remodeled into \*k/e-un, \*k/-en-s in Slavic. The Germanic form \*h/uni- would, then again, point to a remodeling \*k/eu-un, \*k/u-n-es. For another potential un-stem, see \*be/unon-.

\*hluppjan- w.v. 'to lift' - MHG lupfen, liipfen w.v. 'to lift; to rush' (DRV). The meaning 'to lift' allows for two potential etymologies, i.e. 1) to \*luftu'sky' (whence ON lyfta, MHG liiften w.v. 'to lift' < \*/uftjan-); and 2) to \*h/aupan- 'to jump, leap'. Since the MHG verb also has an attestation with the meaning 'to rush' (see MHW: 1, 1988), the latter possibility is probably more plausible. The jan-verb may then have directly been derived from the iterative \*hluppon-.

\*hluppon- w.v. 'to run' - MDu. loppen w.v. 'to run' ⇒ \*klup-neh₂- (NEUR) - Latv. klupinat 'to keep stumbling' < \*klup-neh₂-. The iterative belonging to the strong verb \*h/aupan-, apparently continuing a paradigm 3sg. \*h/uppopi, 3pl. \*hlubunanpi < \*klup-neh₂-ti, \*klup-nhi-enti (also cf. ME /open w.v. 'to jump, leap', which does not necessarily have to be a loanword from ON hlaupa, but can also continue \*hlupon-). The formation is exactly parallel to Latv. klupinat, the iterative to klupt. Note that the suffix -ina- in this language arose as a conflation of the zero grade -in- < \*-l}h₂- and the full grade -na- < \*-neh₂-.

\*hlusen- w.v. 'to listen' - OHG losen, h/osen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*lilus-eh₁-ie (IE) - Skt. 5r6$ati 'to listen; to obey' < \*li/eus-e-; Arm. lsem 'to hear, listen; to obey' < \*liluli- by assimilation from \*Ii/us- (cf. luf adj. 'silent' < \*lilus-ro-); OCS slu5ati, SCr. s/Ufoti 'to hear' < \*lilous-ie- (with secondary \*y also OCS sly5atl); ToA klyos-, ToB klyaus- 'id.' < \*lileus-; Lith. klausyti (klausau), Latv. k/ausft 'id.' < \*J(/ous-eie- (with depalatalization of \*libefore -/-). The root \*/(/eus- is an extension of \*J(/eu-, for which see e.g. \*h/euman- and \*h/Uda-, and may have arisen from the s-aorist \*lileus-t 'notices, picks up'. It

\*hlusti- f. 'hearing, listening' - ON hlust n. 'ear', Far. lust f. 'opening of the auditory meatus', OE hlyst m./f. 'hearing', OS hlust f. 'ear, hearing, listening' : \*l<lus-ti- (DRV /IE) - Skt. SrU$ti- 'complaisance'. A ti-stem derivation to the root \*l<lus-, see \*hlusen-. Cf. the secondary derivations ON hlusta w.v. 'to listen' < \*hlustojan- and OE hlystan w.v. 'id.' < \*hlustjan-. E listen (= OE North. lysna) probably corresponds to MHG liisenen 'to listen' < \*hlusinon-.

\*hluti- m. 'share' - ON hlutr m. 'lot; share, part', Far. lutur m. 'id.', E lot, OHG luz, hluz m. 'id.', MHG luz m. 'share ofland' (DRV). An i-stem derived from \*hleutan- (q.v.). Also cf. OE, OFri. hlot, OHG /oz, hloz, G Los n. 'lot; share' < \*hluta-.

\*hluza- n. 'listening' - ON h/f!Jr, hler n. 'listening, eavesdropping', Ice!. hler n. 'id.', Far. fer n. 'waiting', Nw. dial. standa a /ere 'to stand and listen; to peak' (cf. Icel. standa a hleri 'to stand eavesdroping') ⇒ \*J<lus-6- (IE) - Close to OCS slux"O 'hearing' < \*l<lous-o-. From Proto-Norse \*hloRa-, which gave ON hltar by R-fronting. Also cf. ON hlera w.v. 'to eavesdrop', Far. /era w.v. 'to stand and wait' and ON hleoi, Ice!. hleoi, obs. hleri m. 'shutter, trapdoor' ( < 'to stand ajar'). The correspondence of ON -o- with Ice!. -r- in the latter word is irregular, but has a parallel in the dissimilation of ON yor obi.du. 'you' < \*iowiR < \*iRwiR, cf. Go. izwis < \*izwiz. Note that the forms with -r- must be primary, as the vocalism of ON hleoi, lee!. hleri points to R-fronting of PN \*hloR-. The dissimilation of z to o is likely to have taken place after the devoicing of hi- to J-, so \*hloRen > \*hff!JRi > \*]f!JRi > \*Jf!Joi > Jeoi.

\*hnaigjan- - \*hnaiwjan- w.v. 'to make bow (down)' - Go. hnaiwjan w.v. 'to make go down; to humble', ON hneigja w.v. 'to make bow, incline; to lean', OE hnregan w.v. 'to humble', OS gi-hnegian w.v. 'to bend down', Du. neigen w.v. 'to incline', OHG neigan, hneiken w.v. 'to bow', G neigen w.v. 'to tend, to incline, to bend' (WEUR). The causative of \*hniwan- - \*hnigan- (q.v.). The Pre-Gm. form \*knoigwh-eie- regularly lost the labialization after \*o and before \*i/j (cf. \*sagja- 1 'man, hero' < \*sokwH-i6-). Go. hnaiwjan has its w from hneiwan.

\*hnaskwu- adj. 'soft' - Go. hnasqus adj. 'soft, delicate', OE hnesce adj. 'nesh, soft, delicate, tender, effeminate', E nesh adj. 'soft, tender', WFri. nesk adj. 'delicate', MDu. nesch, nisch adj. 'soft' (DRV).

\*hnawwu- adj. 'stingy; tight' - ON hn119gr adj. 'stingy', OE hneaw adj. 'stingy; near', MDu. nauwe adj. 'tight; precise', Du. nauw adj. 'id.', MHG nou adj. 'id.', G ge-nau adv. 'precisely' (DRV). • A u-stem to the strong verb \*hnewwan- ( q.v.). Also cf. Sw. njugg adj. 'stingy' < \*hnewwja- with East Norse breaking of -i- to -ju-.

\*hne/an- m. 'fist' - ON hnefi m. 'id.' (» ME neve, E nieve), Far. nevi m. 'id.', Elfd. nevi m. 'hand', MHG neve-mez 'a fistful' ⇒ \*knep-on- (GM). The -v- of MHG neve-mez proves that the word must be reconstruced as \*hnefan- rather than \*hneban-. This precludes the received connection with the predominantly Baltic root \*knebh- e.g. Lith. knibti (knimbU) 'to kink, snap' < \*kn-rp-bh-e- and Latv. knibinat 'to pick, collect, gather' < \*knbh-neh₂- (with secondary zero grade in -i-) as well as the semantically more attractive link with Lith. tnybti, gnybti 'to pinch' (see \*knfpan-). Also cf. Elfd. nava w.v. 'to dig; to fumble; to grab' < \*hnafojan- and perhaps also MHG nef m. 'begging', G Swab. nafen 'to beg'.

\*hnekkan- - \*hnakka(n)- m. 'neck' - ON hnakki m. 'id.', Far. nakki m. 'neck; face of a hammer; top of a rock drill', Elfd. nakke m. 'neck', OE hnecca m. 'id.', E neck, OFri. hnekka m. 'id.', ODu. nee, nae m. 'id.', Du. nek c. 'id.', OHG nack, hnack m. 'id.', G Nacken m. 'id.' ⇒ \*knek-on- (IE) - ToA kiiuk 'neck' < \*knek-on-. The word for 'neck' emerges with at least two different ablauting n-stems, viz. \*hnekkan- in Ingvaeonic (but also cf. MHG ge-nicke, G Ge-nick n. 'neck' < \*ga-hnekkja-), \*hnakkan- in Nordic and High German. In addition, a zero-grade formation \*hnukkan-, apparently the source of It., Sp. nuca, Fr. nuque, is evidenced by EDu. nocke 'nucha, spina dorsi, medulla spinalis'. In view of the close formal and semantic similarities with To. kiiuk, it seems possible to reconstruct a PIE n-stem \*knek-on, gen. \*kr;,zk-n-6s, ace.pl. \*knok-n-ၶs (Kroonen 2011a: 167ff.). The regular outcome of this paradigm, viz. \*hneho, \*hunkkaz, \*hnakkuns was apparently remodeled into \*hnekko, \*hnukkaz, \*hnakkuns. Since both the a- and zero-grade forms also occur with the meaning 'top, summit', cf. Far. nakkur m. 'steep rock', OHG hnack m. 'summit' < \*hnakka- and Far. nokki m. 'top of the yard', MDu. nocke 'tip' < \*hnukkan-, it is possible that G Hunke 'hillock' is a vestige of the original genitive form. It is therefore likely that Olr. cnoc, W cnwch m. 'hill' was borrowed from Germanic rather than the other way around.

\*hneudan- s.v. 'to rivet' - ON hnj6oa s.v. 'to beat; to rivet, clinch', OHG bi-hneotan s.v. 'to fasten, rivet' (GM). A strong verb usually linked to Gr. Hsch. KV\)flo<; 'small thorn'. This connection is semantically uncompelling, however. Within Germanic, there is a clear link with the iterative \*hnutt/don- 1 (q.v.). The weak verbs MLG neden and nieden, nieten 'to rivet' were derived from the deverbal noun MHG niet, MLG ned 'rivet' < \*neuda-. Further cf. Swi. niitte/ 'penis' < \*hnudi/a-.

\*hneupan- s.v. 'to pluck' - Go. dis-hniupan s.v. s.v. 'to break apart, tear', OSw. niupa s.v. 'to pinch', Elfd. ni1Jopa s.v. 'id.', OE ii.-hneopan s.v. 'to pluck off, E to nip (DRV). A secondary strong verb derived from the iterative \*hnupp/bOn- (cf. Luhr 1988: 369-70; Kroonen 2011a: 106). The root-final \*p continues a shortened geminate, and does not presuppose a PIE \*b (pace Lehmann 1986: 91).

\*hnewwan- s.v. 'to hit' - ON hnfi/ggva, hnyggja s.v. 'to strike, cut off, Elfd. negga s.v. 'to butt', OHG niuwan s.v. 'to thrust; to grind' ⇒ \*kneuH-e- (EUR) - Gr. KVUW 'to scratch' < \*knuH-ie-; Latv. kniit 'to itch'. A strong verb \*hnewwan-. The p of ON hnpggva (for expected \*hnyggva) is analogical after hpggva < \*hawwan- (Rasmussen 1989): the original e-vocalism is confirmed by Elfd. negga. The verb was created to a European root \*kneuH-. The secondary link with Skt. k$m:16ti 'to hurt' < \*tfk'ln-neu(Gunnarson 1971; LIV2: 645-6) is formally difficult, as the European forms seem to point to a root with a laryngeal. See also \*hnawwu-.

\*hnipan- s.v. 'to pinch, squeeze' - MLG nipen s.v. 'id.', MDu. nipen s.v. 'the squeeze; to tear off (with nails or teeth); to snap', Du. nijpen w.v. 'to press' (DRV). The verb continues Pre-Gm. \*hnippan- and must in view of the root-final geminate have been derived from the iterative \*hnippon- (q.v.).

\*hnipon- w.v. 'to be downcast' - ON hnipa w.v. 'to be downcast, droop' ⇒ \*knibh-neh₂-(?) (NEUR) - Lith. knibti (knimbU, knibaii) 'to collapse, sink down' < \*kni-m-bh-e-(?). A secondary stem variant of an iterative 3sg. \*hnippopi, 3pl. \*hnibunanpi possibly continuing \*knibh-neh₂-ti, \*knibh-nh₂-enti. Also cf. ON hnfpa, Far. nfpa w.v. 'to be downcast, droop' < \*hnipen-. The appurtenance of Go. ga-nipnan w.v. 'to be sorrowful' hinges on the emendation to \*ga-hnipnan-. The verb has also been connected to OE (ge-)nipan s.v. 'to grow dark' and ge-nip n. 'cloud', however.

\*hnippon- w.v. 'to pinch' - ON hnippa w.v. 'to poke', ME nippen w.v. 'to nip; to pinch; to grab', E to nip ⇒ \*knibh-neh₂- (NEUR?) - Lith. kniebti (kniebiu), Latv. kniebt 'to pinch' \*kneibh-ie-. A marginally attested iterative. The link with Lith. kniebti is attractive, but both in Germanic and Baltic there is an irregular and unresolved variation between \*kneibh- and \*gneibh- (see \*knipan-). Within Germanic, the iterative served as the basis for the strong verb \*hnipan- (cr.v.).

\*hnissa- m. 'smell' - !eel. hniss n. 'smell (from cooking)', Far. nissur m. '(funny) taste, smell', Nw. dial. niss m. 'smell' ⇒ \*knid-so- (EUR) - Gr. Hom. KVLO"Ol] f. 'steam and odor of fat, smell and savor of burnt sacrifice, fat caul' < \*knid-s-eh₂-; Lat. nidor m. 'strong smell, fumes' < \*kneid-os-. The forms in the different IE languages all go back to an s-stem, nom. \*kneid-os, gen. \*knid-s-6s. Also cf. Ice!. hnissa f. 'strong smell' < \*hnisson-.

\*hnit- f. 'nit' - ON gnit f. 'id.', Ice!. gnit, nit f. 'id.', Far. nitrur pl. 'id.', Elfd. gnit f. 'id.', OE hnitu f. 'id.', E nit, MDu. nete f. 'id.', Du. neet c. 'id.', OHG niz, hniz f. 'id.', G Nifi f. 'id.' ⇒ \*IEh₃nid-(?) (EUR) - Gr. Kov[<; f. 'nit; flea; bug', Alb. therf / theni f. 'nit', Arm. anic 'id.' < \*J(h₃nid- (with loss of IE in Arm.?); Olr. sned f., W nedd, nedden sgl. 'id.' < \*snid-eh₂-. A European word with many different irregular cognates. Gr. Kovl<;, Alb. then[ and Arm. anic can perhaps be reconciled into a proto-form \*J(h₃nid-, assuming that the Albanian form went through a stage \*fan id- and that Arm. anic developed from a simplified form \*onid-s. PGm. \*hnit- < \*IEnid-, on the other hand, shows no trace of a laryngeal, and neither does Olr. sned < \*snid-eh₂-. Originally, the word may have been a compound, but due to all kinds of - apparently tabooistic distortions, its reconstruction remains problematic. Also cf. the formally more dissimilar Lat. lens, -endis and Ru. gnfda, SCr. gnjl'da, Lith. glinda, Latv. gnida, gl)ida f. 'nit'.

\*hnitan- s.v. 'to poke, butt' - ON hnita w.v. 'to poke', OE hnitan s.v. 'to bump, stick', OS ofhnitan s.v. 'to tear off, Du. dial. nijten w.v. 'to butt (of animals)' ⇒ \*kneid-e- (EUR) - Gr. KVL,w 'to scratch, pound, chop up, provoke' < \*knid-ie-; Olr. cned f. 'wound' < \*knid-eh₂-. A strong verb to a European root \*(JEJneid-. Also cf. OE hnitol, MDu. netel adj. 'given to butting' < \*hnitala- and the intensive ON hneita w.v. 'to poke, insult' < \*hnaitjan-.

\*hniwan- - \*hnigan- s.v. 'to bow (down)' - Go. hneiwan s.v. 'id.', ON hnfga s.v. 'to bow down, fall gently', Far. niga s.v. 'to bend, stoop, drop; to bow', OE hnigan s.v. 'to bend, bow down, incline', OFri. hnfgan s.v. 'to bow down', OS hnfgan s.v. 'id.', Du. nijgen s.v. 'id.', OHG nfgan, hnfgan s.v. 'id.', MHG nfgen s.v.

\*hnojan- 237 'id.' ⇒ \*kneigwh.e- (WEUR) - Lat. c6nfve6 'to be tightly closed, close (of the eye)' < \*kom-kneigwh.e-(?). An etymologically obscure verb with Lat. c6nfve6 as the only formally possible, but semantically remote cognate. The vacillation between \*hnfwan- and \*hnfgan- is due to influence from the causative \*hnaigjan- _ \*hnaiwjan-, where the labialization was regularly lost (between \*o and \*i/j, cf. \*sagja- 1 'man, hero' < \*sokwH-i6-). In the strong verb, on the other hand, the change \*gwh > \*w is expected, cf. \*snfwan- 'to snow' < \*sneigwh.e-.
\*hnojan- w.v. 'to groove' - OHG n6en w.v. 'id.', MHG niiejen w.v. 'id.' ँ

\*knehrie- (EUR) - Gr. -KVaiw 'to scrape, scratch', Olr. imm-cna, W cnoi 'to bite, chew', Lith. kn6ti (kn6ja) 'to peel, tear' < \*knehrie-. In many cases, OHG verbs in -en ( < \*-jan-) continue older verba pura, which theoretically allows us to reconstruct a strong verb \*hn6an- < \*kn6h₂-e-. The late OHG giniiiter marchstein 'carved landmark', however, confirm that the PGm. verb really was \*hn6jan-, thus agreeing to the formation \*kneh₂-ie- as supported by the other IE languages. Also cf. OHG nuo(w)il m. 'plane' < \*hn6ila-, OS hn6a 'joint, seam', OHG nuo, hnuo f. 'joint, seam' < \*hn66- and G Nut f. 'groove, notch' < \*hn6d6-.

\*hnupp/bOn- w.v. 'to tear off - Elfd. nuppa w.v. 'id.', OE hnoppian w.v. 'id.', G Alem. noppen w.v. 'to jerk, pluck' (NEUR?) - Perhaps to Lith. knabenti 'to pick (with the beak)' < \*knobh-nh₂-; Latv. knabt 'to pick; to peck' < \*knobh-; Gr. KVamw, KVaμ1rw 'to card, wool; to mangle, tear' (hardly < \*kn-m-bh-ie-) is probably a late internally Greek formation associated with -KVaiw 'to scrape, scratch' and KVll<flll f. 'itch'. An old iterative that gave rise to the strong verb \*hneupan- (q.v.). In view of Nw. dial. nap pa w.v. 'to pluck' < \*hnapp6n- (also cf. E nap 'woolly material removed from the surface of cloth'), I assume that the Germanic root \*hnupp/b- has a secondary zero grade. As a consequence, the Pre-Gm. root must be reconstructed as \*knebh- rather than \*kneubCh₃., something that is further supported by the potential cognate ON hnafa w.v. 'to cut off < \*kn6bh-e-. Also cf. OE hnoppa m. 'nap of cloth', MDu. noppe f. 'wool fluff < \*hnuppa/6n-.

\*hnut- f. 'nut' - ON hnot, pl. hnetr, hnetr f. 'id.', Icel. hneta f. 'id.', Far. ngta f. 'id.', OSw. nut, pl. nyter, nrJter f. 'id.', OE hnutu, pl. hnyte f. 'id.', E nut, MDu. note f. 'id.', Du. noot c. 'id.', OHG nuz, pl. nuzzi f. 'id.', G Nuss f. 'id.' ⇒ \*knud-(?) (WEUR) - Lat. nux f. 'nut' < \*knuk-; Oir. cnu f. 'id.', MW cnau 'id.' < \*knuH-. An old root noun \*hnut-z, pl. \*hnutiz. Icel. hneta does not continue an ablauting root \*hnet-, but was back-formed from the plural hnetur, which regularly continues ON hnrJtr by unrounding of the e to e. Etymologically, the word for 'nut' is somewhat problematic, because the cognates in

\*hnutt/don- 1 w.v. 'to beat' - Ice!. hnooa w.v. 'to rivet', G Hess. nussen w.v. 'to beat' (GM). The iterative to \*hneudan- (q.v.). The verb originally had a paradigm 3sg. \*hnuttopi, 3pl. \*hnudunanpi < \*knut-nehrti, \*knut-nh₂-enti. Further notable is the semantic parallelism between G Lorr. nossen w.v. 'to knock on someones head with the nuckles' and Du. okker-noot 'knock on the top of the head with the knuckles' (the origin of the first element is unknown, but seems to have been inspired by okker-noot 'walnut').

\*hnutt/don- 2 w.v. 'to shake' - Elfd. nooa w.v. 'to sprain, wrench', ME nodden w.v. 'to nod', E to nod, MDu. noten w.v. 'to mate (of animals)', OHG hnoton w.v. 'to shake', MHG notten w.v. 'to move to and fro', G Lorr. nottelen w.v. 'to stagger, shudder' (GM). The iterative to OHG bi-hneotan 'to shake out' < \*hneudan-, which may or may not be identical to \*hneudan- 'to rivet'. Ice!. hnj6ta s.v. 'to stumble' < \*hneut(t)an- in turn appears to be a back-formation to the iterative.

\*hobo- f. 'piece of land' - MLG hove 'id.', MDu. hoeve 'id.', Du. hoeve c. 'farm', OHG huoba f. 'piece of land', G Hube f. 'hide, unit of area' ⇒ \*keh₂p-eh₂- (EUR) - Gr. KijrroЎ, Dor. 1ceirroЎ m. 'garden, orchard, plantation, unworked piece of land' < \*keh₂p-o-.

\*hoda- m. 'hat, hood' - OE hOd m. 'hood', E hood, MDu. hoed m. 'hat', Du. hoed c. 'id.', OHG huot m. 'hood, bonnet, hat', G Hut m. 'hat' ⇒ \*koHt-6- (wEuR?). The formation appears to continue \*koHt-6- and is closely related to \*hattu- 'hat' (q.v.) < \*kHt-nu-. It may have served as the basis for OHG huoten, OE hedan w.v. 'to guard' < \*hodjan-, although the verb could theoretically also have developed from a regular causative \*koHt-eie-. OE hædre adv. 'anxiously', implying an adjective \*hadra- < \*kHt-r6-(?), has been adduced as well, and would imply a primary meaning 'to be cautious'. For an alternative explanation, see \*hattu-.

\*ho/a- m. 'hoof - ON h6fr m. 'id.', Far. h6vur m. 'id.', Elfd. uov f. 'id.', OE hof m. 'id.', E hoof, OFri. hofm. 'id.', OS hofm. 'id.', MDu. hoefm. 'id.', Du. hoef c. 'id.', OHG huof m. 'id.', G Huf m. 'id.' ⇒ \*lfoHp-o- (IE) - Skt. fapha- m. 'hoof, YAv. safa- m. 'hoof, horse-shoe(?)', Oss. sceftæg 'hoof < \*lfopH-o-. The difference between Gm. \*lfoHp-o- and Inda-Iranian \*JfopH-o- implies that laryngeal metathesis occurred in one of the two forms. Since the same

\*hohan- m. 'plow' - Go. hoha m. 'id.' ⇒ \*Jfok-h₂- (IE) - Skt. sákhā- f. 'branch, twig', NP sax 'twig' (with s- for expected s-), Arm. c<ax 'branch', c<ak' 'thorny plant; harrow', Lith. §aka f. 'branch', Ru. soxa '(wooden) plough', SCr. soha f. '(wooden) plow', Alb. thak(e) m./n. 'awn, beard, pin, peg. tassel, fringe'. The word in IE denoted a forked branch that was deployed as a primitive plow. The word has an interesting formal history. Skt. sákhā-, with its combination of a long vowel and an aspirated k, points to a Plr. paradigm \*ciikii, gen. \*cakhas < \*Jfok-eh₂, gen. \*J<(o)k-h₂-6s. A similar kind of variation is implied by the co-occurrence of Arm. ccakc < \*J<ok- and c<ax < \*J<ok-h₂-: since the normal outcome of PIE J< is s in Armenian, it is possible to think that the cc arose by assimilation in the zero-grade genitive root \*Chkh-. The Germanic word seems to be a different formation, i.e. an n-stem, apparently continuing \*J<ok-on-, but it is possible to assume that the long \*o arose in a form \*J<oh₂k- that was metathesized from \*Jfok-h₂- (for a potential example of the opposite metathesis, however, cf. \*hofa-). Also cf. OHG huohili n. 'aratiuncu/a' < \*hoh(i)lja-.

\*hOkina- n. 'kid, young goat' - OE hecen n. 'id.', MDu. hoekijn n. 'id.' ⇒ \*kogfh₃ (NIE) - OCS koza, Ru. koza, SCr. koza f. 'goat' < \*kogh-eh₂-. A word that cannot be separated from OCS koza, although the irregularity of the sound correspondences makes it impossible to reconcile the different forms into a single PIE form. It is conceivable, for this reason, that the term was borrowed from an extinct European language by Slavic and Germanic independently. Also note that the proto-source \*kogfh₃_ is reminiscent of another phonetically irregular, and thus potentially non-IE word meaning 'goat', i.e. Gr. aၵ< \*h₂dgl- and Skt. aja- < \*h₂eg-o-.

\*hiila- n. 'flattery(?)' - ON ho/ n. 'praise, flattery', OE hOI n. 'vain speech, calumny, slander' ⇒ \*k6Hl-o- (EUR) - Gr. KllAEu.> 'to charm, bewitch' < \*keh₁;2l-eie-; Lat. calvor 'to deceive; be deceived' (derived from an unattested adjective \*ca/us < \*kHl-u-). An o-stem adjective possibly derived from a sparsely attested base \*keh₁lor \*keh₂l-. Also cf. Go. afho/on 'to slander' < \*ho/ojan- and ON hre/a w.v. 'to praise', OE he/an w.v. 'to slander', OHG huolan w.v. 'to deceive' < \*holjan-.

\*hiiniz- n. 'hen, fowl' - OS hon n. 'hen', Du. hoen c. 'id.', OHG huon n. 'id.', G Huhn n. 'id.' (DRV). A vṛddhi-derivative from \*hanan- (q.v.). The WGm. languages point to \*honaz, pl. \*honiziz (quasi f>IE \*keh₂n-os, pl. \*keh₂n-es-es). ON hamsn, Far. hesn, Elfd. yons n.pl. 'liens, poultry' points to a diminutive \*honis-ina-, a formation with stem variant in \*-s that apparently was not affected by Verner's law.

\*hiipa- n. 'small bay(?)' - ON h6p n. 'land-locked bay, small inlet', Far. h6pur top. 'small bay', OE hop n. 'pool' ⇒ \*koh₂p-n6- (EUR). A word that may continue Pre-Gm. \*hoppa- < \*koh₂p-n6- and thus can be related to \*habano- 'harbor' < \*kh₂p-6n-eh₂- and \*haba- 'sea, lake' < \*kh₂p-6-.

\*hiira- 1 m. 'adulterer' - Go. hors m. 'id.', ON h6rr m. 'id.' ⇒ \*keh₂-ro- (IE) - Latv. kars adj. 'lecherous, lustful', Lat. ciirus adj. 'sweet' < \*keh₂-ro-; Skt. kii'to desire, like', YAv. kii- 'to take pleasure, desire' < \*keh₂-. Also cf. \*hara- 2 and \*horon-.

\*hiira- 2 n. 'adultery' - ON h6r n. 'id.', OE hor n. 'id.', OFri. hor n. 'id.', OS in: hor-hus 'brothel', OHG huor n. 'id.', MHG huor n. 'id.' ⇒ \*keh₂-ro- (EUR/IE). The noun seems to have given rise to the verb \*h6r6jan-, cf. ON h6ra, OS horian, OHG huoron, G huren 'to commit adultery', which is formally parallel to Olr. caraid, MW caraf'to love' < \*keh₂-r-eh₂-ie-. Cf. with a different suffix Go. horinon 'id.'.

\*hiiriin- f. 'whore' - ON h6ra f. 'id.', OE hare f. 'id.', E whore, OFri. hor f. 'id.', MLG hare f. 'id.', MDu. hoere f. 'id.', Du. hoer c. 'id.', OHG huora f. 'id.', G Hure f. 'id.' (DRV). Also cf. OHG huorra < \*horjon-. The appurtenance of MLG herge, herje 'whore, concubine', possibly from \*harjon-, is less certain, but it can theoretically have developed from \*keh₂r-ieh₂- by pretonic shortening.

\*hrab/ppan- m. 'raven' - ON hrafn m. 'id.', Far. ravnur m. 'id.', OE hrcefn, hrcem m. 'id.', OS naht-raban, -ram m. 'id.', MDu. rave m. 'id.', Du. raaf c. 'id.', OHG rabo, hrabo, rappo, hraban m. 'id.', G Rabe m. 'id.' ⇒ \*krobh-on-(?) (GM). An n-stem that was thematicized to \*hrabna- in ON hrafn, OE hrcefn and OHG raban (cf. Liihr 1988: 332). The original nominative \*hrabO is contin· ued by OHG rabo, hrabo, G Rabe, MDu. rave, Du. raaf, while OHG rappo and MHG rappe appear to have arisen by contamination of the same case and the genitive \*hrappaz (= MHG rapf) < \*krobh-n-6s. The underlying root \*krobh- is likely to have been onomatopoetic in origin, cf. Sw. korp, Elfd. kuorp.

label_gloss: w.v. 'to make stand, wake up, incite'
gloss: to make stand, wake up, incite
opening: \*hragjan- w.v. 'to make stand, wake up, incite' - MDu. regen w.v. 'id.', MHG regen w.v. 'id.', G regen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*krok-eie- (EUR). The causative of\*hregan- (q.v.).

\*hrag/kkon- w.v. 'to jut out' - OE ofer-hragan w.v. 'to loom over(?)', MDu. ragen w.v. 'id.', OHG hragen w.v. 'id.', MHG ragen, racken w.v. 'id.', G ragen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*krok-neh₂- (EUR). A cluster of forms that continue both the iterative \*hrakk/gon- and the stative \*hragen- pertaining to \*hregan- (q.v.). The meaning of OHG bi-raget 'uneven' may point to an etymological connection with \*rak(k)on- ( q.v.).

\*hraifan- m. 'wrist(?)' - ON hreifi m. 'wrist; the hand and fingers; seal's fin, flipper' ⇒ \*kroip-on- (NEUR) - Lith. kreipti (kreipiu) 'to turn' < \*kreip- and kraipyti 'id.' < \*kroip-eie-. Also cf. ON hrifa s.v. 'to scratch; to grasp' < \*kreip -e-and OE ge-hrifnian w.v. 'to pull, grab'.

\*hraigran- - \*higran- m. 'heron' - ON hegri m. 'id.', Far. hegri m. 'id.', Nw. hegre, dial. higre, heigre m. 'id.', OSw. hcegher m. 'id.', Elfd. ager m. 'id.', Da. hejre 'id.', OE hrcigra m. 'id.', OS hegero m. 'id.', MDu. heger, reiger m. 'id.', Du. reiger m. 'id.', OHG reigar, hreigir, heigar, heigiro m. 'id.', MHG heiger m. 'id.', G Reiher m. 'id.' ⇒ \*kr[o)ikr-on- (GM). The derivation of the word is highly complex. Most Nordic forms point to a proto-form \*higran-, thus contrasting with WGm. \*hraigran-, but Nw. dial. heigre together with MDu. heger goes back to \*haigran-. By assuming dissimilation in the forms with only one r, it is possible to reconstruct two ablauting reduplicated formations \*kri-kr-6n- and \*kroi-kr-6n- (cf. \*bebura- 1 'beaver' and \*aikwerna[n)- 'squirrel'). A completely different solution is to assume that the formal irregularities arose when the word together with MW crehyr 'heron' < \*kraxar- was adopted from a European substrate language (Schrijver 1997).

\*hraini- adj. 'pure, clean' - Go. hrains adj. 'id.', ON hreinn adj. 'id.', Far. reinur adj. 'id.', Elfd. rien adj. 'id.', OS hreni adj. 'id.', Du. rein adj. 'id.', OHG reini, hreini adj. 'id.', G rein adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*kroin-i- (DRY). A (vrddh₁?) adjective to an unattested nasal present \*hrinan-, cf. Lat. cerno 'to sift, distinguish, decide' < \*kri-n-e-; Gr. Kptvw 'to separate, choose, decide' < \*kri-n-ie-. An archaic meaning 'fine' is preserved by the Swi. dialects.
\*hrairja- adj. 'rear' - OE hrere-brii:d adj. 'rear (esp. of eggs)', ME hrere, rere adj. 'id.', E rear ⇒ \*lir-oi-ri- (IE) - Skt. srta- adj. 'cooked' < \*lir-to-; Skt. srdyati 'to cook' < \*kf-oi-e-.

\*hraiwa- n. 'dead body, death' - Go. hraiwa-dubo f. 'turtle-dove, wild dark dove', ON hrce n. 'corpse, remains', OE hrre(w}, hrci(w) n. 'id,/, OFri. hre- 'id.', OS hreu, hreo n. 'id.', Du. reeuw n. 'foam or sweat of a dying person', OHG reo, hreo n. 'id.', MHG re n. 'id.' ⇒ \*krou(h₂)-io- (IE) - Skt. kravi$- n. 'raw, bloody meat', Gr. Kpfo<; n. 'meat, piece of meat' < \*kreuh₂-s-; Lat. cruor, -oris m. 'blood from a wound; bloodshed' < \*kruh₂-os-; Olr. cru n.(?) 'gore, blood', MW creu m./f. 'blood < \*kruh₂-; OCS kry, kn.vb, Ru. krov', SCr. kfv f. 'id.' < \*kruh₂(-i)-; Lith. kraiijas m. 'blood', OPru. krawian 'id.' < \*krou-io-. Related to \*hrawa- 'raw' (q.v.): the word probably developed from \*krawja- (cf. Lith. kraiijas) by irregular metathesis. For a similar development, cf. \*fraiwa- 'seed' < \*frawja-.

\*hrakjan- w.v. 'to torment' - ON hrekja w.v. 'to drive away; to worry, vex; to damage, abuse' ⇒ \*krog-eie- (WEUR?) - Olr. crecht m.(?), MW creith f. 'wound' < \*kreg-t-. A verb that is usually compared to the aforementioned Celtic forms. The often connected OFri. hreka 'to tear' (cf. Magnusson 1989: 370) does not exist: the verb is hrekka\* or hretza\* < \*hrukkjan-.

\*hraman- m. 'frame' - MDu. rame, raem m./f. 'window, frame', Du. raam c./n. 'id.', OHG rama f. 'prop, support', G Rahmen m. 'frame' (NEUR) - Ru. kroma f. 'edge' < \*krom-eh₂- and za-kromiti 'to surround' < \*krom-eie-. In spite of the late attestation of the word, the initial \*h- is ascertained by Fr.frame (cf. E frame), a loanword from OLFra. \*hrama. The word served as the basis for Go. hramjan 'to crucify', ON hremma 'to grab, clutch' (cf. Ice!. hrammur 'claw'), OE hremman 'to curb, hamper', (OFri. hrem-bendar m.pl. 'fetters'), Du. remmen 'to curb, brake' < \*hramjan- and probably also for ON hrefni n. 'board in a ship' < \*hramnja-. Da., Nw. ramme, Nw. dial. rcime 'frame' and late ON ramr, Nw. dial. ram m. 'garret' were adopted from LG.

\*hramusan- / \*hramusjon- m.jf. 'ramsons' - OE hromsa m. 'id.', E ramsons, OS hramusia f. 'id.', MLG ramese, remese 'id.' (>> Nw., Da. rams, Sw. rams-16k), G Swi. (Jaun) ramsa f. 'id.' ⇒ \*/from-us- (EUR) - Gr. Kp6μμuov, Kp6μuov, Hsch. KpEμuov n. 'onion' < \*lfre/om-us-o-; Mir. crem, crim, W era/ coll. 'wild garlic' < \*lfrm-u-; Lith. kermuse, dial. kermusa f. 'tip of a drill, top of a flail, wild garlic' < \*kerm-us-(i}eh₂-; Ru. ceremsa, SCr. crl]emufo, srl]emusa f. 'ramson' < \*fk1erm-(o)us-ieh₂-.

\*hrappa- adj. 'fast' - MDu. rap adj. 'swift, fierce', Du. rap adj. 'fast' ⇒ \*krobh-n6- (WEUR) - Olr. crib adv. 'quickly, swiftly' < \*krbfh₃ __ Also cf. MDu. hem reppen, Du. zich reppen w.v. 'to rush' < \*hrapjan-.

\*hratt/don- w.v. 'to rush, tumble' - ON hrata w.v. 'to stagger, fall, tumble', OE hratian, hradian w.v. 'to rush, hasten', MHG razzen w.v. 'to rush' ⇒ \*krot-neh₂- (EUR) - Lith. kretJti (kretil) 'to tremble (with old age)' < \*kret-eh₂-ie-; Lith. kristi (krintu, kritail), Latv. krist 'to fall, drop, die' < \*kr(-n-)t-; Lith. kratjti (kratail), Latv. kratft 'to shake out' < \*krot-eie-; Olr. crothaim 'to shake' < \*krot-eh₂-ie-. The o-grade variant of the iterative \*hurton- - \*hruton- 'to rush, tumble' apparently continuing 3sg. \*hratto/Ji, 3pl. \*hradunanþi < \*krot-neh₂-. The received connection with Gr. Kpac5aw 'to swing, brandish' < \*krd-eh₂-ie-, Dir. fo-ceird 'to put, throw', MW kerddet 'to walk, go' < \*kerd- is erroneous, the Germanic root variant \*hrad- pointing to \*krot-. Also cf. \*hrafla- - \*hrada'fast'.

\*hrapa- - \*hrada- adj. 'fast' - ON hraor adj. 'id.', OE hrCEd, hrCEo adj. 'id.', E rather comp., OHG rad, hrad adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*kroto- (EUR) - Lith. api-krataf adv. 'fast' < \*-kroto-. An a-stem adjective to the root \*kret-, for which see \*hratt/don-. It gave rise to the factitive OE hreddan 'to save', OFri. hredda 'id.', Du. redden 'id.', OHG rettan 'to drive; save', G retten 'to save' < \*hradjan-, which probably had a primary meaning 'to be quick' (whence 'to come quickly, save').

\*hrauka- m. 'pile, rick' - ON hraukr m. 'pile', Far. reykur m. 'bird's crest', OE hreac m. 'stack, haycock, rick', E rick, Du. rook c. 'id.' (GM). A formation closely related to the cluster of ON hr6 'hillock', Far. r6gv n. 'stack' < \*hruha-, ON hruga f. 'pile' < \*hrilgo-, ON hroki, Far. roki m. 'pile' < \*hrukan-, and EDu. rock 'haystack' < \*hrukka-, which probably all split off from an originally ablauting n-stem \*hrilho, \*hrukkaz (Kroonen 2011a: 268-70). The proto-form of \*hrauka-, Pre-Gm. \*kraukka-, is likely to have been borrowed by Celtic, cf. Dir. cruach, W crug 'rick' < \*kroukko/ii-. See also \*hrugja-.

\*hrauza- n. 'artificial stone heap' - ON hreyrr m. 'id.', Ice!. hreyr m./n. 'megalithic grave, cairn, mound', Nw. r121(y)r f. 'edge, side', OSw. r121r n. 'cairn', Sw. ror 'pile of stones', Da. r121r n. 'cairn, pile of stones' ⇒ \*kreuH-so-? (NEUR) - Lith. krciuti (krciuju) · 'to pile up' < \*krouH-; Lith. kruva f. 'pile' < \*kruH-eh₂-. Possibly related to \*hruson- (q.v.). Also cf. Nw. r121ys, Sw. ros(e), Da. r121s(e) 'pile of stones' < \*hrausl- and ON hreysi n. 'cq.Ve, den' < \*hr;ausja-.

\*hrawa- adj. 'raw' - ON hrcir adj. 'id.', Far. rciur adj. 'moist', Elfd. ra(r) adj. 'raw; moist', OE hra:?w, hreaw adj. 'raw', E raw, Du. rauw adj. 'id.', OHG rao, ro adj. 'id.', G roh adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*krouh₂-o- (IE) - Skt krurci-, Av. xrllra- adj. 'bloody, cruel' < \*kruh₂-r6-; OCS knVbn'b, Ru. kr6vnyj, SCr. kfvni adj. 'bloody, sanguineous', Lith. kruvinas adj. 'bloody' < \*kruh₂-ino-. An adjective related to \*hraiwa- (q.v.).

\*hrefiz- n. 'stomach' - OE hrifn. 'id.', OS in-hrif n. 'innards, guts', MDu. rifn. 'body', OHG ref, href n. 'body; womb' ⇒ \*krep-es- (IE) - Skt. kfp- f. 'appearance, beauty', OAv. kahrp- f. 'figure, appearance, body' < \*krp-; Lat corpus, -oris n. 'body', Mir. crf f. 'id.' < \*krp-o/es-; Gr. npantc; 'diaphragm' cannot be related because 1) PIE \*kw ... p- regularly dissimilates to \*k. .. p- (see \*hwalfaand \*hwerban-); and 2) the root did not have a labiovelar in the first place (cf. Olr. en). An old s-stem with two attested variants: OE hrif < \*hrefiz- < \*krep-es- and OHG href < \*hrefaz- < \*krep-os-. This alternation together with the co-occurrence of Lat corpus < \*krp-os- points to a PIE ablauting s-stem \*krep-os, gen. \*krp-es-(o)s. Also cf. OE mid-hrif, mid-rif (E midriff), OFri. mid-rof n. 'diaphragm', Du. midden-rif n. 'midriff < \*medja-hrefiz-.

\*hrejlinga- m. 'shoe' - ON hriflingrm. 'id.', OE hrifeling m. 'id.' (GM). Derived from the verbal form underlying MHG revelen w.v. 'to sew, mend' < \*hrejlon-. There is probably no etymological link with Gr. KPTJTIL<;, -i:cSoc; f. 'man's high boot, half-boot', SCr. krplja f. 'snow-shoe', OPru. kurpe, Lith. kurpe, Latv. kui'pe, f. '(wooden) shoe' < \*krh₁p-ieh₂- and W crydd m. 'id.' < \*karafyo- < \*kerh₁p-io- (by Joseph's rule).

\*hregan- s.v. 'to jut out' - MHG regen s.v. 'to jut out, stand upright' ⇒ \*krek-e- (GM). A strong verb related to \*hrag/kkon- and \*hrehi- (q.v.). The root \*hreginstead of expected \*hreh- may be due to influence from the causative \*hragjan- or the iterative \*hrag/kkon-. No further etymology: unrelated to Gr. Kp6crcrm f.pl. 'stepped copings of parapets', Lith. kriike f. 'rod, staff < \*krok-iehi- and Ru. kr6kva f. 'bar, club, rafters' < \*krok-ueh₂-.
\*hrehi- adj. 'protruding' - MHG ra:the adj. 'id.' (DRY). A V[ddhi-gerundive to the strong verb \*hregan- (q.v.).

\*hret6- f. 'honeycomb' - ODu. riita f. 'id.', MDu. rate, raet f. 'id.', Du. honing-raat 'id.', MHG riize f. 'honeycomb, stake, stake' ⇒ \*kreh₁d-eh₂- (NEUR) - OCS krada f. 'bonfire, stake', Ukr. k6roda 'pile of logs, wood-stack' < \*kor(h₁)d-eh₂-. The Low Franconian evidence clearly points to a proto-form \*hreto- (the initial \*h- is not attested, but follows from MLat frata 'honeycomb'). MHG riiz, G Ro.fl n. 'id.' is in support of a parallel neuter formation \*hreta-. The MDu. variants rete and rote do not point to ablauting formations \*hretonand \*hruton- (pace Torp 1909: 107), on the other hand, but reflect a dialectal change of ii to 6 and the pertaining umlaut products a and o that arose in the plural, cf. Saarbr. ros, pl. res 'honey comb'. See further \*hrota- 1.

\*hrepan- s.v. 'to sift' - OHG redan s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*krit-e- (IE) - Lat cerno 'to sift, distinguish, decide' < \*kri-n-e-; Gr. Kptvw 'to separate, choose, decide' < \*kri-n-ie-; W crynaff'to shake, tremble' < \*kri-neh₂-; Lith. krija f. 'edge of a sieve' < \*kri-(i)eh₂-. A zero-grade present \*hriþan- that shifted to the class S strong verbs after the a-mutation to \*hrepan- (cf. \*stekan-). In view of the dental extension, the verb may have been created to the 3sg. aorist form \*kri-t of the root \*krei- 'to sieve'. See also \*hridra- and \*hripan- - \*hrittan-.

\*hrepra-(?) m. 'bat' - Ice!. leour-blaka f. 'id.', Far. /eour-b/gka 'id.', Elfd. /eoer m. 'id.', OE hreaoe-miis, hrere-mus f. 'id.', E obs. rear-mouse 'id.', Du. vleer-muis c. 'id.', OHG f/edar-miis m. 'id.', G Fleder-maus f. 'id.' (GM). The variants OHG fledar-miis < \*fleflra-, OE hreaoe-miis, hrere-mus < \*hrepra-(?) and Icelandic leor-blaka 'bat', Elfd. leoer < \*leflra- probably all derive from a difficult to reconstruct proto-form \*preþra-, \*þlepra- or \*hrepra- that was distorted by assimilation and dissimilation in several different ways. OHG f/edar- could be the regular outcome of \*þlepra- (cf. G fliehen < \*p/euhan-) but synchronically belongs to fledaron w.v. 'to flutter'. The derivation of OE hreaoe- < \*hraupa- and hrere- < \*hrera- is not transparent, but the initial cluster at any rate seems to have been \*hr-. In Old Norse, the form /eor-b/aka synchronically means 'leather-flapper' (cf. blaka va:tngjunum 'to flap the wings'), but in view of the WGm. evidence, it seems likely that this formation partly results from folk etymology (cf. also ON veor-b/aka, Nw. dial. ver-bloke "sky-flapper"). Perhaps, it is possible to connect all forms to a lost noun \*hrepra- 'skin, membrane', cf. \*hreprja- (q.v.). This would imply a PGm. compound \*hrepra-miis-. The element 'mouse', however, may have been added at many different stages, cf. typologically E

\*hreuba- adj. 'scabby' - ON hrjufr adj. 'rough, scurvy', OE hreof adj. 'rough, rugged, scabby, leprous', OHG riob adj. 'leprous' ⇒ \*kreup-6- (NEUR) - Cz. krupy adj. 'raw, crude' < \*kroup-o-; Latv. krailpa f. 'crust' < \*kreup-eh₂-; Latv. krupt 'to shrivel' < \*krup-; Latv. kraiipet 'to dry up (of wounds)' < \*kreup-eh₁-ie-. Also cf. ON hrufa f. 'rough surface, crust' < \*hruf/bon- < \*krup-eh₂- and OHG (h)ruf, MHG ruff. 'scab, leprosy' < \*hrufi- < \*krup-i-.

\*hreuda- n. 'reed' - OE hreod n. 'id.', E reed, OS hriod n. 'id.', Du. riet n. 'id.' (with Auslautverhiirtung lexified because of its occurrence as singulare tantum), OHG hriot n. 'id.', G Riet n. 'id.' (GM). No clear etymology. Often analyzed as 'the trembling plant', cf. MHG riitten 'to shake' < \*hrudjan-, but this interpretation is uncertain. See also \*rauza-.

\*hreutan- - \*hrutan- s.v. 'to snore' - ON hrj6ta s.v. 'to snore', Far. rj6ta, r6ta s.v. 'id.', Elfd. riuota s.v. 'to bellow, low', OE hriitan s.v. 'to make a noise, to snore', E dial. to rout 'to cry out, roar, bellow; to low, moo loudly', OFri. hriita\* 'to rattle (in the throat)', OS hriitan s.v.(?) 'to snore', OHG riizzan s.v. 'id.' (DRV). In view of the eu/ii-alternation and the (shortened) root-final geminate, this strong verb is likely to have been derived from the iterative \*hrut(t)on(q.v.). lt in turn gave rise to ON hrutr m. 'ram' < \*hriita- (for the semantics, cf. Elfd. riuota, E to rout 'to low, moo'). It follows that the alleged link of the latter word with \*heruta- 'deer' must be abandoned.

\*hrewan- s.v. 'to be sad, make sad' - Ice!. hrja w.v. 'to afflict, vex', OE hreowan s.v. 'to make sorry, grieve', E to rue, OS hreuwan s.v. 'to mourn, be painful', Du. rouwen w.v. 'to mourn', OHG riuwan s.v. 'to make/be sad', MHG riuwen, riwen s.v. 'to sadden, to be sad', G reuen w.v. 'to regret' ⇒ \*kreu(H)-e(GM). Ice!. hrja, though a weak verb, corresponds rather neatly to the WGm. forms. It thus points to a non-geminated proto-form \*hrewan- rather than \*hrewwan-, as the latter form would have produced a strong verb ON \*\*hryggva, Ice!. \*\*hryggja. The attested ON hryggva, hryggja 'to distress,

\*hridra- n. 'sieve' - OE hrfdder, hriddel n. 'id.', E riddle, OHG ritera f. 'id.', MHG riter(e) f. 'id.', G Reiter f. 'id.' (< \*hridr6-) ⇒ \*krei-dhro- (IE) - Lat cribrum n. 'sieve', Olr. criathar m. 'id.', OW cruitr 'winnowing-shovel' < \*krei-dhro-j\*-tro-. Derivation with the instrumental suffix \*-tro- or \*-dhro- from the root \*krei- 'to sift', for which see \*hrepan-.

\*hriman- - \*hripan- m. 'hoar frost, rime' - ON hrfm n. 'id.', Far. rfm n. 'id.', OE hrfm m. 'id.', E rime, OS hripo m. 'id.', Du. rijp, rijm c. 'id.', OHG rifo, hrifo m. 'id.', G Reifm. 'id.' ⇒ \*kreip-mon- (GM). The PGm. variants \*hriman- and \*hrfpan- both go back to a paradigm nom. \*hrfm6, gen. \*hrfpaz from \*kreip-mon, \*krip-(m)n-6s (Kroonen 2006). The m was lost in the weak cases, thus creating the right conditions for the operation of Kluge's law. In the nominative, however, the m remained and absorbed the preceding labial. SaaN riippmo possibly preserves a trace of the labial stop, however, the strong grade of -mm- normally being -bm-.

\*hrinan- s.v. 'to touch, affect' - ON hrina w.v. 'to take effect', OE hrfnan s.v. 'to touch, reach, strike', OS hrinan s.v. 'to touch, lay one's hand on', OHG rinan, hrfnan s.v. 'to touch, get hold of, MHG be-rinen s.v. 'to touch' (GM). No certain extra-Germanic parallels. The connection with Latv. kriet (kreju) 'to skim cream off milk' is unsatisfactory, since the original Germanic meaning was 'to touch indirectly, affect' or 'to leave a trace'.

\*hringa- m. 'ring, circle' - ON hringr m. 'id.', Far. ringur m. 'id.', Elfd. ringg m. 'id.', OE hring m. 'id.', E ring, OFri. hring m. 'id.', OS hring m. 'id.', Du. ring c. 'id.', OHG ring, hring m. 'id.', MHG rinc m. 'ring', G Ring m. 'id.' ⇒ \*kringh-o(NEUR/NIE) - OCS krpg'b, Ser. krug m. 'circle' < \*krongh-o-. An exclusively Slavic and Germanic root that is in violation with the IE constraint against the co-occurrence of plain stops and voiced aspirates. This makes it likely that its origin is to be found in the post-IE phase. Further cf. \*hrungo- and - with irregular onset - \*kringa-. Gr. KpiKoc;, KipKoc; m. 'ring' is unrelated.

\*hrinþiz- - \*hrunþiz- n. 'cow, bovine' - OE hrfaer, hryaer n. 'id.', OFri. hrfther n. 'id.', Du. rund 'id.', OHG rint, hrint n. 'id.', G Rind n. 'id.' ⇒ \*J<r-ent-es(IE). The ablaut displayed by the dialects is suggestive of an ablauting s-stem \*hrinpaz, gen. \*hrundizaz continuing PIE \*/{rent-os, gen. \*/{rf}.t-es-s. The root

\*hrisa- n. 'brushwood' - ON hrfs n. 'id.', Far. rfs n. 'id.', Elfd. rais n. 'brushwood; wood', OE hris 'branch, brush', MDu. rijs n. 'branch', OHG ris, hris n. 'twig, branch; shrubs', G Reis n. 'scion, sprig' ⇒ \*kreis-o- (EUR) - Lat crinis f. 'hair' < \*kris-ni-; OPru. craysi 'stalk', crays 'hay' < \*kroisݭ. A neuter a-stem to a European root \*kreis-. Also cf. Elfd. umbel-riesn f. 'hop shoot' < \*raisno(n)- < \*krois-neh₂-.

\*hrispon- f. 'curl' - MLG rispe f. 'truss', MHG rispe f. 'id.' ⇒ \*krisp-eh₂- (WEUR) - Lat. crispus adj. 'curly, curled (of hair)' < \*krisp-o- and with metathesis W crych adj. 'curly, wrinkled' < \*kripso-. Also cf. OHG hrisp-ahi n. 'shrubbery' < \*hrisp-ahja- and MHG rispen, rispeln w.v. 'to ripple, curl'.

\*hripan- - \*hrittan- m. 'fever, shivering' - OS hrido m. 'id.', MDu. ridde, rede m. 'id.', EDu. rijde, ridde 'id.', OHG rido, rit[t)o m. 'id.', MHG rite, ritte m. 'id.' ⇒ \*kreit-on- (DRY) - Olr. crith, W cryd 'fever' < \*kri-tiju-. An ablauting n-stem to the tudciti-verb \*hripan- (see \*hrepan-), which originally meant 'to shake', then 'to sieve' (see \*hridra-). The ablaut of \*hripo, gen. \*hrittaz, dat. \*hrideni was preserved until in the OHG period, cf. Notker nom. rfdo, dat. rften (Schaffner 2001: 551). OHG ritto, MHG ritte and MDu. ridde continue a stem \*hrippan- that arose by contamination of the nominative \*hripo and the genitive \*hrittaz (cf. Swab. ritze-rot adj. 'crimson').

\*hrofa- n. 'roof - ON hr6fn. 'shed under which ships are built', OE hrofm. 'id.', E roof, OFri. hrof m.(?) 'id.', MLG rof m./n. 'cover, lid, roof of the rear deck of a ship, shelter', MDu. roe/ m./n. 'lean-to', Du. roe/ c. 'id.' ⇒ \*lfrop-o(EUR) - OCS stropo m. 'roof < \*/(rop-o; Olr. cr6 m. 'enclosure, shed, sty', MW creu m. 'shed' < \*J(rop-o-.

\*hroka(n)- m. 'rook, jackdaw' - ON hr6kr, hraukr m. 'rook', Ice!. hraukur m. 'id.', Far. r6kur m. 'jackdaw', OE hroc m. 'rook, raven, jackdaw', E rook, OS hrok m. 'id.' (also hroka f. 'id.' < \*hrokon-), Du. roek c. 'id.', OHG ruoh, ruoho m. 'crow', MHG ruoch, ruoche m. 'id.' (GM). An originally onomatopoetic word with a root comparable to Gr. KpW0W 'to squawk' and Lith. kr6gti (kr6giu) 'to hawk' < \*kro[H)g-ie-. Typologically comparable is also OCS kruko 'raven' < \*krouk-o-. Note that the irregular alternation of ON hr6kr with hraukr cannot be adduced to derive the word with Go. hrukjan 'to cry'.

\*hroma- m. 'fame' - OS hrom m. 'id.', EDu. roem c. 'id.', OHG ruom, hruom m. 'id.', G Ruhm m. 'id.' ⇒ \*kroh₂;3-mo- (DRV). A mo-stem to the root \*kerh₂;r - \*kreh₂;3-. See further under \*hurjan-, \*hropi- and \*hropra-.

\*hriipan- s.v. 'to call, shout' - OE hropan s.v. 'id.', OFri. (h)ropa s.v. 'id.', OS hropan s.v. 'id.', Du. roepen s.v. 'id.', OHG ruofan, hruofan s.v. 'id.', MHG ruofen s.v. 'id.', G rufen s./w.v. 'id.' (GM). No extra-Gm. cognates. The connection with the root \*hro- 'to report, rumor' (cf. \*hropi- 'fame') is problematic, because the origin of the suffix \*-p- is unknown (\*b did not exist in IE). Perhaps, the verb arose due to association with the semantically similar OE wopan, OHG wuoffan < \*wopan-, cf. Go. wopjan, ON cepa < \*wopjan-. Also cf. Go. hrops m., ON hr6p n., OHG ruof m. 'shout, call' < \*hropa- and the derived }an-verb Go. hropjan, OE hrcepa, OHG ruofen w.v. 'to shout'.

\*hrota- 1 n. 'roof - Go. hrot n. 'id.', ON hr6t n. 'id.' ⇒ \*kroh₁d-o- (NEUR) - OCS krada f. 'bonfire, stake', Ukr. k6roda 'pile of logs, wood-stack' < \*kor(h₁)d-eh₂-. A masculine a-stem related to \*hreto- (q.v.). Also cf. OS hrost m./n. 'rafters', OE hrost m. 'wooden framework', E roost < \*hrosta- < \*kroh₁d-sth₂-o-.

\*hrota- 2 m. 'soot' - OS hrot m. 'id.', MDu. roet n. 'grease, soot', Du. roet n. 'soot', OHG ruoz m. 'id.', G Ruf, m. 'id.' (GM). No certain etymology. The received comparison with OE hrum m. 'soot' < \*hruma- would imply a formation \*kroHu-d-o- (with PGm. -ou- > -o-), but the suffixation remains unclear.

\*hropi- m. 'glory' - Go. in: hropeigs adj. 'glorious', OE hreo m. 'id.' ⇒ \*kreh₂;3-ti- (DRV) - Skt. k"irtf- f. 'fame' < \*krh₂13-tf-. A ti-stem to the PIE root \*kerh₂13-, for which see \*hroma- and \*hurjan-.

\*hropra- m. 'praise' - ON hr6or m. 'id.', OE hrooor m. 'solace, benefit, pleasure' ⇒ \*kreh₂;3-tro- (DRY) - Skt. carkcirti, YAv. carakara- 'to praise' < \*ker-korh₂;3-. An instrumental noun derived from the PIE root \*kreh₂;3-. See further \*hurjan-, \*hropi- and \*hroma-.
\*hroza- adj. 'motile' - OE hror adj. 'stirring, active, vigorous', OS hror adj. 'lively', MDu. roer adj. 'mobile' ⇒ \*l<roH-s-6- (IE). An adjective related to \*hrozjan- (q.v.).

\*hrozjan- w.v. 'to touch, move, stire' - ON hrcera w.v. 'id.', Far .• rf/Jra w.v., MHG ruoren, riieren w.v. 'to move' ⇒ \*kroHs-eie- (IE) - Av.frii-xriiryhaiia- 'to be shocked' < \*kroHs-eie-. An old causative formatibn. Related to \*hroza- (q.v).

\*hrudjan- w.v. 'to shake' - MHG riitten w.v. 'to shake', G riitteln w.v. 'id.' Ǹǹ· . A }an-verb created to a secondary iterative \*hrutton- pertaining to \*hrepan- 'to shake' (q.v.). This strong verb again developed from \*hripan- < \*krit-e- by a-mutation, which apparently operated at the PGm. stage. The deverbal formations E rudder and ruddle 'sieve' < \*hrudra-, \*hrudla- are derivationally parallel to OE hridder, hridde/, E riddle < \*hridra- (q.v.).

\*hrugan- m. 'fishroe' - ON hrogn n. 'id.', Far. rogn n. 'id.', OSw. rughn, romn n.(?) 'id.', Sw. rom c. 'id.', Elfd. rungen m. 'id.', MDu. roge 'id.', EDu. roghe 'id.', OHG rogo, rogan m. 'id.', MHG rage, rogen m. 'id.', G Rogen m. 'id.' ⇒

\*kr(h₁)k-on- (NEUR) - Lith. kurkulai m.pl. 'frogspawn', Latv. kurku/is m. 'id.' < \*kr(H)k-u/o-; Ru. krjak (= PSI. \*kn;kb) 'id.', Slov. krݮk 'id.' < \*krek-o-; SCr. krtik m. 'id.' < \*kor(h₁)k-o-(?). An n-stem thematicized as \*hrugna- in Nordic. On the basis of the Balto-Slavic forms, it is theoretically possible to reconstruct a root alternation \*krek- / \*kreh₁k-, \*kr(h₁)k-. The Germanic stem \*hrugan- should then be analogical for regular \*hurgan- < \*kr(h₁)k-6n-. In this scenario, it is further possible to compare Ice!. hrai < \*hrehan- (or \*hrahan-) 'swarm of water-fleas', a formation potentially corresponding to PSlav. \*krek-. It has alternatively been suggested, however, that the differences between the Baltic, Germanic and Slavic forms arose due to repeated borrowing from a disappeared non-IE language (Polome 1986: 661). Within Germanic, further cf. Du. reugel 'herring roe' < \*hrugila- and Icel. hrygna, Nw. rygne f. 'female fish' < \*hrugnjon-.

\*hrugja- m. 'ridge; back' - ON hryggr m. 'back', Far. ryggur m. 'id.', Elfd. ryg m. 'id.', OE hrycg m. 'back, spine, ridge', E ridge, OFri. hreg m. 'back', WFri. rech, rich c. 'id.', OS hruggi m. 'id.', ODu. ruggi m. 'id.', Du. rug c. 'id.', OHG rucki, hrucki m. 'id.', G Riicken m. 'id.' (GM). No certain extra-Gm. cognates. The best comparandum so far is Ir. cruach f. 'heap, hill', W crug 'id.' < \*kroukk-eh₂-, but this is probably a loanword from PGm. \*hrauka- (q.v.). Hardly to Olr. croicenn m. 'skin', MW croen m. 'peel, hide, rind' < \*krok(k)- (cf. Matasovic 2009: 226). Perhaps to Lat. crux, crucis f. 'cross' < \*kruk- (De Vaan 2008: 147)?
\*hrukka- m. 'upper garment, robe' - ON rokkr m. 'id.', OE roe m. 'id.', OS hrok m. 'id.', Du. rok c. 'skirt', OHG rock m. 'id.', G Rock m. 'skirt; coat' (GM).

\*hrungo- f. 'rung' - Go. hrugga f. 'staff, cane', OE hrung f. 'staff, rod, beam', E rung, MLG runge f. 'stanchion', MHG runge f. 'stake', G Runge f. 'id.' (NEUR?) - Pol. kn;iel m. 'distaff < \*krongh-(e/i)li-. A zero-grade formation related to \*hringa- 'ring' (q.v.). The formal and semantic similarities with Pol. kn;iel may be deceptive in view of kqdzie/ 'id.' < \*kpdelb and other irreconcilable variants (see Trubacev 1966: 96ff.).
\*hruson- f. 'crust' - OE hruse f. 'earth, ground', OHG rosa f. 'crust, ice' ::}
\*krus-eh₂- (NEUR) - Lith. krusa f. 'icefloe' < \*krus-eh₂-. Also cf. Nw. dial. rosna w.v. 'to come off (of skin)' < \*hrusnan-.

\*hrut(t)on- w.v. 'to snore, roar' - Elfd. rota w.v. 'to babble', G rotzen w.v. 'to blow the nose' ::} \*krut-neh₂- (GM). An iterative 3sg. \*hruttopi, 3pl. \*hrudunanpi < \*krut-neh₂-ti, \*krut-nh₂-enti, the derivational base of OE hrot m. 'slime', OHG roz, hroz, G Rotz m. 'snot' < \*hrut(t)a- as well as the strong verb \*hreutan- - \*hriitan(q.v.). The ungeminated consonantism is still found in e.g. ON hrooi m. 'snot' < \*hrupan- and lee!. hryoja w.v. 'to hawk, cough' < \*hruþjan-, cf. Far. ryour m. 'coughed up mucus'. With a single \*t, cf. MDu. rote/en, reutelen, G dial. rosseln w.v. 'to rattle'. No further etymology: the connection with Gr. Hsch. Kpouμcu 'snot' ( < \*krou-meh₂-?) is doubtful.

\*hruþjan- m. 'male dog' - OE hryooa m. 'id.', MLG rude m. 'id.', Du. reu c. 'id.', OHG rudo m. 'id.', G Riide m. 'id.' ::} \*krut-ion- (GM). Also cf. OE hroo-hund 'big dog, molossus' < \*hrupa-. Clearly connected to what Kilian gives as EDu. roden 'catulire (to be in heat, to mate)', which technically can be united with the iterative \*hrutton- 'to roar' and the pertaining strong verb \*hreutan- - \*hriitan- 'to snore, rout', cf. E to rut.

\*hudi- f. 'skin' - ON hLJ{j f. 'id.', Far. hLJ{j f. 'id.', Elfd. QUO f. 'id.', OE hyd f. 'id.', OFri. hed(e) f. 'id.', OS hiid f. 'id.', ODu. hiit f. 'id.', Du. huid c. 'id.', OHG hiit f. 'id.', G Haut f. 'id.' ::} \*kuH-tf- (EUR) - Identical to Lat cutis f. 'skin'; further cf. OPru. keuto 'skin', Lith. kiautas m. 'shell, rind, peel' < \*keuH-to/eh₂-; Mir. coda/ f. 'hide, skin' < \*kuH-dhfeh₂- and Lith. kiauklas m. 'shell' < \*keuH-t/o-.

\*hufa- n. 'hillock' - ON ho[ n. 'temple', Far. hov n. 'id.', OE ho[ n. 'house, hall', OFri. ho[ n. 'court, yard', OS hofm. 'id.', Du. hofn. 'id.', OHG ho[ m. 'id.', G Hof m. 'id.' ٻ \*kHup-o- (IE). Related to \*hufra-, \*haupa- and \*hupan-: the original meaning was 'hillock', cf. OS hubil m. 'hill', Du. heuvel c. 'id.', OHG hubil, MHG hiibel, G Hugel m. 'id.' < \*hubila-.

\*hufra- m. 'bump, hillock' - OE hofer m. 'id.', MLG hover m. 'id.', OHG hofar, hovar m. 'id.' ⇒ \*kHup-ro- (IE) - Identical to Latv. kuprs m. 'hump, hunch'; also cf. Lith. kupra f. 'hump, hunch, back' < \*kHup-r-eh₂-. A ro-stem related to \*hufa- (q.v.).

\*hugi- m. 'understanding, mind' - Go. hugs m. 'id.', ON hugr m. 'mind; mood; desire, wish', Far. hugur m. 'id.', Elfd. ug m. 'id.', OE hyge m. 'mind, heart, soul', OFri. hei m. 'mind', OS hugi m. 'thought, mind', MDu. hoge m./f. 'thought, consideration, memory', Du. heug c. 'desire', OHG hugu m. 'mind, spirit, courage', MHG hiige m. 'mind, spirit, memory' ⇒ \*kwJ<-[- (IE?). No certain etymology. Often compared to Skt. sucyati 'to glow, shine; to hurt' < \*J<uk-ie-, but the semantics are not compelling. Rather related to the i-present of the root \*kweJ<-, cf. Skt. akhyat 3sg.aor. 'to look', with vocalization of the labialization like in ON kona 'woman' < \*kunon- < \*gwn-? Within Germanic, also cf. Go. hugjan, ON hyggja, OE hycgan, OFri. hugia, OS huggian, OHG huggen w.v. 'to think' < \*hugjan- and the late iterative ON hugga w.v. 'to comfort' < \*huggon-.

\*hiikan- s.v. 'to squat' - ON huka w.v. 'to squat', Far. huka s.v. 'id.', OSw. huka w.v. 'to peddle', WPhal. huken s.v. 'to crouch, cower, sit', MDu. huken w.v. 'to squat', Du. huiken w.v. 'id.', MHG huchen w.v. 'to squat, stoop over' (DRY). A strong verb continuing \*hukkan-, a back-formation to the primary iterative \*huk(k)on- < \*kuk-neh₂- (q.v.). Further cf. \*heuhman- and \*hauha-.
\*huk(k)on- w.v. 'to squat, cower' - ON hoka w.v. 'id.', G hocken w.v. 'id.' ⇒

\*kuk-neh₂- (IE) - Skt. kucati 'to make crooked, bend or curve' < \*kuk-e-; Lith. kaukas m. 'lump' < \*kouk-o-. Also cf. the frequentative ON hokra 'to crawl', Ice!. hokra 'to stoop', Far. hokra 'to sit with bent knees', Nw. dial. hokra 'to stumble'. All forms contin· ue an iterative formation 3sg. \*hukkopi, 3pl. \*hugunanpi from PIE

\*hula- adj. 'hollow' - ON holr adj. 'id.', OE ho/ adj. 'id.', OFri. ho/ adj. 'id.', Du. ho/ adj. 'id.', OHG ho/ adj. 'id.', G hohl adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*l<uH-16- (with pretonic shortening) (IE) - Gr. KoV.o<; adj. 'hollow, hollowed out, spacious, deep', Alb. thelle adj. 'deep' < \*lfouhi-ilo-; Lat cavus 'hollow, excavated, concave, deep (of water)' < \*lfouh₁-o-; Mir. cua 'hollow, cavity', MW keu adj. 'hollow, closed' < \*J(uh₁-o-; OCS suea acc.pl.n. 'vanities', ORu. sui 'empty, vain' < \*/(ouhi-io-. The adj. gave rise to Go. us-hulon 'to hollow out' < \*hulojan-, Go. hulundi f. 'hole' < \*hulundjo- and also to OE holh, E hollow < \*hulha-. Cf. with a verbal \*k-suffix: MLG ho/ken w.v. 'to hollow out'.

\*hulandra- m. 'elder' - OHG holuntar, holantar m. 'id.', G Wasser-holder m. 'id.' ⇒ \*kul-1}-tro- (GM). Also cf. OSw. hylle n. 'elder' (Nw, Sw. hyll, Da. hyld 'id.') < \*hullja- < \*kul-n-io-, a typically Nordic ja-stem collective. Together with \*hulundra- < \*kul-n-tro-, which like many other tree-names was formed with the suffix \*-tro- (cf. ON apaldr m. 'apple tree' < \*apaldra-), the Germanic evidence points to an n-stem \*kul-n-; the n was vocalized in the derivation with \*-tro-, but assimilated by the \*/ in the collective \*kul-n-io-. No extra-Germanic etymology. The often compared Ru. kalfna f. 'guelder rose, snowball tree' is just like Slov. kalfna f. 'puddle' actually derived from PSI. \*kiifo 'marshy area', cf. OCS kafo 'mud', Ru. kal 'excrement', SCr. kiil m. 'mud, puddle', and therefore cannot be related.

\*hulisa- m. 'holly' - MDu. huls 'id.', Du. hulst 'id.', OHG hulis, huls m. 'id.', G Hulse f. 'id.' ( < \*hulisOJon-) ⇒ \*kuli- (WEUR/NIE) - Olr. cui/enn, W celyn m. 'holly' < \*kolino-. E holly appears to be shortened for OE ho/en, holegn, which in view of the lack of umlaut seems to point to \*hulena- < \*kuleno-. This proto-form is formally close to, but ultimately irreconcilable with PCelt. \*kolino-. lt therefore stands to reason to assume that the Celtic and Germanic words were adopted independently from a third, extinct European language. Also note the even more irregular lookalike ON hulfr, Ice!. hulfur, Da. dial. hylver-torn n. 'holly' < \*hulba- (= ME hulvere).

\*hul(l)on- w.v. 'to move, fetch(?)' - Nw. dial. hula 'to bend over and reach forward, to pull oneself, MDu. hollen w.v. 'to run; to go out of control', Du. hollen w.v. 'to run' ⇒ \*(/(JjH-neh₂- (IE) - ToAB kiilii- 'to lead, bring', Lith. kelti, Latv. ceft 'to raise' < \*kelH-; Lat. -cello, -ere 'to raise oneself < \*fl(Jel-nH-e-; OCS s'blati, Ru. slat' (slju), SCr. siati 'to send' < \*l{lH-eh₂-. It is not entirely certain whether Nw. hula belongs here, but Du. hollen alone allows us to reconstruct an iterative 3sg. \*hullōþi, 3pl. \*hulunanþi. This formation would be identical to Latv. cilinat 'to lift repeatedly' < \*k/H-neh₂-.

\*hulma(n)- m. 'small island' - ON holmr, ho/mi m. 'id.', Far. h6lmur m. 'small island; battlefield', Elfd. uome m. 'islet', OE holm m. 'mound, hill, rising ground', OS holm m. 'hill', MDu. holm m. 'islet in a river; mound' ⇒ \*kJH-mon(EUR) - Lat. columen n. 'top, summit' < \*ke/H-men-; Lat. cu/men n. 'summit (of a building), peak' < \*ko/H-men-; Lith. kdlnas m. 'hill' < \*kolH-no-. The Latin evidence is suggestive of an ablauting mn-stem, nom. \*ke/H-mn, gen. \*k/H-men-s, acc. \*ko/H-men-m. This paradigm may be the direct source of ON form ho/mi, Elfd. uome. Lith. kalnas, on the other hand, appears to be a no-stem, but may perhaps have developed from \*ko/H-mno-. Note that the ablaut pattern is similar to that of \*ha/ma- 'stalk, blade of grass' ( q.v.).

\*hulta- n. 'grove' - ON holt n. 'id.', Elfd. ult n. 'id.', OE holt m./n. 'id.', OS holt n. 'wood', Du. hout n. 'id.', OHG holz n. 'id.', G Holz n. 'id.' ⇒ \*kold-o- (EUR) - Gr. l<Aaooc; m. 'branch, twig, sprout' < \*kld-o-; OCS klada, Ru. kol6da, SCr. klilda 'log' < \*kold-eh₂-. A European word possibly continuing a neuter root noun \*k6ld, gen. \*kid-es.

\*hulpa- adj. 'propitious' - Go. hul/Js adj. 'kind, clement', ON hollr adj. 'faithful, loyal; wholesome', Far. hollur adj. 'faithful; good, favorable', OE hold adj. 'clement, loyal', OFri. hold adj. 'loyal', OS hold adj. 'devoted, graceful', MDu. houd adj. 'gracious, loyal', OHG hold adj. 'propitious', G hold adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*l{f-to- (NEUR). A zero-grade formation to \*hal/Ja- ( q.v.).

\*humara- m. 'lobster' - ON humarr m. 'id.', Sw. hummer c. 'id.' ⇒ \*kumar (NIE) - Gr. KapaĢoc;, KaĢoupoc;, Kaμ(μ)apoc; m. 'lobster'. The Germanic word can theoretically be reconciled with Gr. Kaμapoc; by reconstructing a proto-form \*krphi-er-, but the variants KapaĢoc;, KaĢoupoc; and Kaμμapoc; show that such a reconstruction would be beside the point. It seems more likely that we are dealing with a non-IE word, no doubt of Mediterranean origin. Far. hummari and Sw. hummer were probably borrowed from Da. hummer (Stefan Jacobsson, p.c.).

\*humela- m. 'bumblebee' - Elfd. myoc'J-umbel f. 'id.', ME humbul-be 'id.', MLG homele, homelte 'id.', MDu. home/ m. 'id.', Du. hommel c. 'id.', OHG humbal, hummel m. 'id.' ⇒ \*kTJIH-el-o- (NEUR) - Ru. smel', Pol. czmiel, strzmiel, Slov. cmelj, smelj 'bumblebee' < \*cbmelb < \*kTJIH-el-i-; Lith. kamiine 'bee' < \*komH-on-ieh₂-; OPru. camus 'bumblebee' < \*komH-u-. A word probably derived from the verbal root underlying MHG, G hummen, E to hum < \*hummiin-, cf. Lith. kimti (kimstu) 'to become hoarse' < \*kmH-ske-. Of the aforementioned cognates, the Slavic forms are particularly close.

\*humelan· m. 'hops' - ON humli m. 'id.', Far. humli m. 'id.', Elfd. umbel m. 'id.', OE hymele f. 'id.' ( < \*humeljiin-), MDu. hommel m. 'id.' (NEUR). A prehistoric Wanderwort. ORu. xmelb < PSI. \*x-omelb and VLat. humulus (cf. Fr. houblon) are loanwords from Germanic \*humel(i)-, but the origin of the Germanic word is itself obscure. Lookalikes in the Finno-Ugric and Turkish languages point to an Eastern origin, cf. Hung. kom/6, Mansi qumlix, Tat qolmaq, Chuv. xamla, etc.

\*hrina(n)· m. 'top of a stick' - ON hUnn m. 'knob at the top of the mast-head', Ice!. hunn m. 'door knob, door handle; knob on a flagpole; bear cub', Far. huni m. 'wooden staple or handle; lug in the middle of a wooden tub' (cf. hUn-band 'hole high up in the mast through which the halyard passes'), Nw. dial. hun(e) m. 'upper part of the mast; bear cub', OE hiin-pyrel 'hole in the mast-head for the halyard', MDu. hune 'crow's nest' ⇒ \*J(uhi-no-? (IE?). Since the meaning 'bear cub' developed out of more primary 'knob' (cf. \*dranga- and \*knab/ppan-), the direct connection with Skt sfsu- m. 'child, young of animal' < \*J{i-Ku- and Gr. KUEW 'to be or become pregnant' < \*lluh₁-eie- can only be maintained by starting from the semantically more primitive Skt 5vayati 'to swell, become strong' < \*lluhi-eie- etc. Another alleged cognate, viz. W cwn m. 'head; top, summit', was apparently invented by Pughe (Williams 1972: 10).

\*hunanga- m. 'honey' - ON hunang n. 'id.', Far. hunang n., hunangur m. 'id.', OSw. hunagh m. 'id.', Elfd. onungg m. 'id.', OE hunig n. 'id.', E honey, OS honig 'id.', Du. honing c. 'id.', OHG hona(n)g n. 'id.', G Honig m. 'id.' ⇒ \*k1Jh₂-on/{-o- (IE) - Gr. KVijKoc; f. 'safflower, Carthamus tinctorius' < \*knh₂-lfo-. Both Gr. KVijKo<; and PGm. \*hunanga- seem to be derived from a root or an adj. \*knh₂- with a meaning 'brownish yellow, amber'. The n of the suffix was dissimilated in most Germanic languages, but not in ON hunang, OHG honang and Du. honing. The comparison of ToB kro(ri)kse\* 'bee'

\*hunda- 1 n. 'hundred' - Go. hund n. 'id.', OE hund num. 'id.', OS hund m. 'id.', OHG hunt num. 'id.', MHG hunt num. 'id.' ⇒ \*dl<m-t6- (IE) - Skt. §ata-, YAv. sata- n. 'hundred', OP {)ata-, NP sad 'id.', Oss. seedݯ 'id.', ToA kiint, ToB kante 'id.', Gr. EKa-r6v 'id.', Lat centum 'id.', Olr. cet, OW, MW cant 'id.', Lith. simtas, Latv. simts, ocs S'btO, Ru. sto, SCr. sto 'id.'. Also cf. the compound ON hundrao, OE hundred, OFri. hunderd, hundred, OS hundrod, Du. honderd, OHG hunderit, G Hundert n. 'hundred' < \*hund-rada- 'row of hundred' (cf. \*rado-).

\*hunda- 2 m. 'dog' - ON hundr m. 'id.', Far. hundur m. 'id.', OE hunt m. 'id.', E hound, OFri. hund m. 'id.', OS hund m. 'id.', Du. hand c. 'id.', OHG hunt m. 'id.', MHG hunt m. 'dog', G Hund m. 'id.' ⇒ \*/{u-on- (IE) - Hitt kuyan- - kun- c. 'dog-man', ToAB ku m. 'dog', Skt. svan-, YAv. span- m. 'id.', Arm. 5un, gen. san 'id.', Gr. !CUwv, KUv6<; m./f. 'id.', Lat. canes, -is m./f. 'id.', Olr. cu, W ci m. 'id.', Lith. foo m. 'id.', Latv. suns m. 'id.', OPru. sunis 'id.'. The IE word for dog. In the parent languages, its paradigm is usually reconstructed as \*l<u-on, gen. \*l<u-n-6s. The origin of the Germanic dental is uncertain, but it reappears in the full-grade form \*hwinda- 'greyhound' ( q.v.) < \*l<uent-6-. This full grade is isolated, as the other languages show no sign of an \*e in the suffix, but taken at face value, \*hwinda- together with \*hunda- is suggestive of an originally ablauting paradigm e.g. \*l<u-ont, gen. \*l<u-nt-6s, acc. \*J<u-ent-m.

\*hungru- m. 'hunger' - Go. huhrus m. 'id.', ON hungr m./n. 'id.', OE hungor m. 'id.', E hunger, OFri. hunger m. 'id.', OS hungar m. 'id.', Du. hanger c. 'id.', OHG hungar m. 'id.', G Hunger m. 'id.' ⇒ \*kr,ik-ru- (IE) - Close to Gr. (Photios) KEyK£L 'is hungry' < \*kenk-e- and Lith. kefikras 'emaciated' < \*kenk-ro-. An old ru-stem related to the aforementioned cognates in Baltic and Greek. Less straightforward is the link with Lith. kefikti (kenkiu) 'to harm, injure' < \*kenk-ie- and Lith. kanka f. 'torment' < \*konk-eh₂-, which have been compared to ON ha 'to tease' < \*hanhojan- (see \*hawen-, however, for a different explanation). It is conceivable that the original meaning of the root was 'to pain' (cf. Gr. KaK6<; adj. 'bad, awful, worthless' < \*kr,ik-o-), which later developed into 'to feel hunger'. Also cf. Go. huggrjan 'to be hungry'.

\*hunsla- n. 'sacrifice' - Go. hunsl n. 'id.', ON husl n. 'id.', OE hiisel n. 'id.', E house/ ⇒ \*l<unt-slo- (IE) - Av. spanta-, Lith. 5vefitas, OPru. swints, OCS svt;tb, Ru. svjat6j, SCr. svet adj. 'holy, sacred' < \*l<uent-o-.

\*hunton- w.v. 'to chase' - OE huntian w.v. 'to hunt', E to hunt::} \*fk?nt-neh₂- (EUR). The iterative to \*hinpan- (q.v.). It gave rise to the causative OE hentian w.v. 'to prosecute, catch' < \*hantjan-, which apparently inherited the iterative semantics. Also cf. OE hunta m. 'hunter' < \*huntan-.
\*hiipan- m. 'pile, heap' - OHG hiifo m. 'id.', G Haufen m. 'id.' :::} \*kuHp-on(IE). An ablauting variant belonging to \*haupa- (q.v.).

\*hupi- m. 'hip' - Go. hups m. 'id.', OE hype m. 'id.', E hip, MDu. hope, hupe f. 'id.', Du. heup c. 'id.', OHG huf f. 'id.', G Hiifte f. 'id.' (with secondary t) ⇒ \*kub-i- (WEUR) - Lat. cubo, -are 'to lie down, recline; be lying' < \*kub-eh₂-ie-; MW kyscu 'to sleep' < \*kub-ske-. An i-stem to a European root \*keub-. Unrelated to OE on-hupian w.v. 'to retreat', which belongs to the cluster of \*hupp/bon- 1 'to move backwards'. Also note that Gr. Kliٽoc; 'dice' has erroneously been compared in view of the meaning 'hollow above the hip of cattle'; this meaning is clearly secondary.

\*hupp/bon- 1 w.v. 'to move backwards; retreat' - ON hopa w.v. 'to turn back', lee!. hopa w.v. 'to fall back, retreat', Far. hopa w.v. 'to draw back, recede, retreat', Nw. dial. hop(p )a, hobba w.v. 'to retreat, drive backwards (esp. of horses)', Da. dial. hoppe seg w.v. 'to walk backwards, drive backwards' :::} \*kp-neh₂- (IE) - Gr. Kaμrrw 'to bend, bow, curve' < \*kmp-ie- (a conflation of the zero grade \*Kan- and the full grade \*Ktμn-?); Sogd. p-k'np'to turn away, deflect', Parth. n-kmb- 'to bend' < Pllr. \*kamp-; Lith. kumpas, Olr. camm adj. 'curved' < \*kmp-o-; Lith. kampas m. 'corner, hook' < \*komp-o-. An iterative 3sg. \*huppopi, 3pl. \*hubunanpi that in view of the o-grade variant Nw. hapa, habba w.v. 'to retreat, drive backwards (esp. of horses)', Swe. dial. happa w.v. 'to turn back, drive backwards' < \*happopi, \*habunanpi must have a secondary zero grade. The Scandinavian forms with -b- and -bb- prove that the original root cannot have been \*kub- (pace Pokorny IEW: 588-92; De Vries 1962: 248), which precludes the alleged link with Lat. cu(m}bo 'to recline'. It is possible, however, to derive the verb from the PIE root \*kemp- 'to turn, bend', assuming that the m is infixal. See also \*hamfa-.

\*hupp/bOn- 2 w.v. 'to hop, jump' - ON hoppa w.v. 'to hop, jump, skip', Elfd. uppa w.v. 'id.', OE hoppian w.v. 'to hop, leap, dance', E to hop, MDu. happen, hobben w.v. 'to hop', Du. hobbe/en w.v. 'to bounce', MHG hopfen w.v. 'to hop', G hopfen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*kup:neh₂- (NEUR) - OCS k'opeti 'to hop' < \*kup-ehi-. An iterative 3sg. \*huppopi, 3pl. \*hubunanpi < \*kup-neh₂-ti, \*kup-1Jh₂-enti, apparently to a marginally attested root \*kup-. MDu.·hobben with PGm. \*bb at any rate proves that the formation cannot be derived from the PIE root \*kub- (pace Pokorny JEW: 588-92).

\*hurdi- f. 'wickerwork door' - Go. haurds f. '(lattice) door', ON huro f. 'door', Far. huro f. 'id.', OS hurth f. 'hurdle, wickerwork', MDu. horde f. 'wattle', Du. hor c. 'insect screen', OHG hurt, hurd f. 'hurdle, grate, railing', G Hiirde f. 'wattle, hurdle' ⇒ \*krh₂-ti- (WEUR) - Lat. criitis f. 'construction of wickerwork, hurdle' < \*krh₂-ti-; OPru. corto 'fence' < \*korh₂-to-? Also cf. OE hyrdel m. 'frame of intertwined twigs or bars', E hurdle < \*hurdila-.

\*hurha- adj. 'lean' - ON horr adj. 'id.', Far. hor pred. 'skinny' ⇒ \*kf/(-o- (IE) - Skt. krsa- adj. 'lean, thin, emaciated, slim, weak' < \*krl(-o-; Lith. karsJti (karsJju) 'to reach the end of one's life, become frail with age, die' < \*korlt-eh₁(-ie)-. Also cf. ON horr, Ice!. hor m. 'starvation' < \*hurha- and Ice!. hora-st w.v. 'to become skinny'. It is possible to speculate that \*hungru- 'hunger' belongs here, too; the word must then have been dissimilated from a form \*kr·IJ·K-ru- with a nasal infix.

\*hurhwa- n. 'dirt, mucus' - ON horr m. 'mucus', Ice!. hor m. 'id.', OE horh, horg, horu m./n. 'spit', OS horu, horo n. 'mud', ODu. horo n. 'mud', MDu. hore n. 'mud, dirt', OHG horo n. 'dirt, mud, manure' ⇒ \*kork-uo-(?) (WEUR?) - To Mir. corcach 'moor' < \*kork-iiko-? Also cf. OE ge-horwian, -horgian w.v. 'to spit upon, defile' < \*-hurwojanand hyran w.v. 'to spit' < \*hurhwjan-. Possibly related to Mir. corcach. Other connections, e.g. to Gr. Kopu0cx 'mucus', are doubtful.

\*hurja- m./n. 'burning coal(?)' - Go. hauri n. 'coal, burning charcoal, ember', ON hyrr m. 'fire' ⇒ \*krh₃-io- (NEUR) - Lat. carbo m. 'piece of charcoal'(?); Lith. kitrti (kuriit) 'to light, kindle', Latv. kuft 'to light, kindle, heat' < \*krH-ie-; with secondary ablaut: OCS kuriti, Ru. kurit' (kurju), SCr. dial. kuriti 'to smoke, set fire to' < quasi-PIE \*kourh₃-eie-. A ja-stem created to a Germanic and Balto-Slavic root \*kerh₃- (with h₃ because of the vocalization of -r- to PBS!. \*-ur-). See also \*herpa-.

\*hurjan- w.v. 'to repeat?' - OE hyrian, on-hyrian w.v. 'to imitate' ⇒ \*k[h₂;3-ie- (IE). Semantically close to OHG and-haron w.v. 'to imitate' < \*harojan- < \*korh₂;3-eh₂-ie- and ON herma w.v. 'to relate, repeat, report; to imitate, mimic' < \*harmjan-. The latter appears to have been derived from a lost mo-stem \*harma-, which is in Schwebeablaut with \*hroma- (q.v.).

\*hurna- n. 'horn' - Go. haurn n. 'id.', ON horn n. 'id.', Far. horn n. 'horn; corner', Elfd. uonn n. 'horn', OE horn m. 'id.', E horn, OFri. horn m. 'id.', Du. horen c. 'id.', OHG horn n. 'horn; point, end', G Horn n. 'horn' ⇒ \*lfrn-o- (IE) - Skt. sfnga- 'horn' < \*fr-n-go-; Lat. cornrl n. 'id.' < \*llr-nu-; W earn 'hoof < \*kr-no- ( = PGm. \*hurna-); W asgwrn, Bret. askorn 'bone' < \*h₂est-ltor-n-. An old IE word for 'horn', the derivational base of \*heruta- 'deer' <
\*lter-ud- as well as \*hersan- - \*herznan- 'brain' < \*lier-hrs-.

\*hurnuta-/6- m./f. 'hornet' - OS hornut m., horneta f. 'id.', MLG hornte 'id.', MDu. hornete, hornte, hoornte f. 'id.', OHG hornuz, horniz m. 'id.', G Hornisse f. 'id.' ⇒ \*ltrh₂-s-n- (IE/EUR) - Lat crcibro m. 'hornet' < \*ltrh₂-s-r-on-; OLith. sir5uo m. 'id.' < \*lfrh₂s-on- and sirse, sirse f. < \*ltrh₂-s-en-, \*-er-; OPru. sirsilis m. 'id.' < \*ltrh₂s-il-io-; Latv. sirsenis m. 'id.', CS s[t)rbsenb, Ru. sersen' m. 'id.', SCr. Cak. srsen m. 'big wasp, hornet' < \*frh₂-s-en-(i)o-. A formation created to the PIE root \*ltrh₂s- by the addition of the animal suffix \*-ut(a/6)- (cf. \*al but- 'swan' and \*heruta- 'stag'). It gave rise to the feminine OE hyrnet(e/u}, hyrnytte f. 'id.', which through \*hiirniittii developed from \*hurznutjo-. Together with the Baltic and Latin forms, the word goes back to an r/n-stem, possibly \*lfrh₂-s-er, \*ltrh₂-s-n-6s. Du. horzel 'hornet' appears to be an independent formation \*hurs-Io- from \*ltrh₂-s-Io-, but it may also continue \*krh₂-s-ro- with dissimilation of the second r. If so, it would be fully parallel to Lat crcibro.

\*hursa- n. 'horse' - ON hross n. 'id.', Far. ross, poet hors n. 'id.', OE hors n. 'id.', E horse, OFri. hors n. 'id.', OS hros, hors n. 'id.', Du. ros n. 'id.', OHG ros, hros n. 'id.', G Ross n. 'id.' (DRV /LW). The word has been derived from the verb \*hurzon- 'to rush', which is akin to Lat curr6 'to run' (cf. De Vaan 2008: 1 57-8). This seems attractive in view of the fact that Olr. carr 'vehicle, cart', which similarly can be derived from

label_gloss: contains the same root Another possibility is that the word
opening: \*krs-o-, contains the same root Another possibility is that the word - like \*papa- 'path' and ON refr 'fox' (see \*fuhsa-) was borrowed from the Alans during the Migration Period (Brپndal 1928: 5, 15f., 2f.). It is remarkable, at any rate, that Ossetic wyrs/urs, the continuant of Pllr. \*ur5an- 'male animal' is the only Iranian language in which the word has come to mean 'stallion', cf. Skt. vf$an- m. 'man, male animal (bull, stallion)', YAv. arfan- m. 'man, male'.

\*hurton- - \*hruton- w.v. 'to rush, tumble' - Nw. rota w.v. 'to slide down', MHG hurzen w.v. 'to rush' ⇒ \*krt-neh₂- (EUR). The zero-grade variant 9f the iterative \*hratt/don- 'to rush, tumble', continuing Pre-Gm. 3sg. \*hurttopi, 3pl. \*hurdunanpi < \*krt-neh₂-. Nw. hrota < \*hrut6n-, which received an inverted zero grade on the basis of the full-grade forms, gave rise to the secondary strong verb ON, Ice!. hrj6ta 'to fall, fly, be slung', Nw. rjota 'to fall down' < \*hreut(t)an-.

\*hurz6n- w.v. 'to rush' - Far. hurra w.v. 'to take off, roar', Nw. hurre w.v. 'to turn, swirl, rumble', E to hurry, MHG hurren w.v. 'to dash' ⇒ \*krs-nehr (EUR) - Lat. curr6 'to run' < \*kors-e-; Olr. carr, W car 'vehicle' < \*krs-o-; Gr. En:L-Koupo<; m. 'helper'. The semantics of the different forms point to an iterative 3sg. \*hurzn6pi, 3pl. \*hurzunanpi < \*krs-neh₂-. Possibly, this formation contains the same root \*hursa- 'horse' (q.v.).
