\*hrisa- n. 'house' - Go. -hus n. 'id.', ON hus n. 'id.', Far. hus n. 'id.', Elfd. aus n. 'id.', OE hiis n. 'id.', E house, OFri. hiis n. 'id.', OS hiis n. 'id.', Du. huis n. 'id.', OHG hiis n. 'id.', G Haus n. 'id.' ⇒ \*kuH-so-(?) (GM). An etymologically obscure word. The old comparison with Gr. KEu-Bw 'to hide' is erroneous, because the related \*huzda- 'hoard' (q.v.) < \*kudh-toproves that the root was \*keudh- without a laryngeal. In view of the neuter gender of \*hiisa-, it is plausible that the word represents a thematicized s-stem to the root \*kuH- 'to cover' (cf. \*haw6- 'hide'), which would point to an original meaning 'shelter'.

\*hut(t)6n- w.v. 'to run' - Nw. hotta, hutta w.v. 'to quickly move up and down, go fast, run around', MHG hossen, hotzen w.v. 'to run', G dial. hutzen w.v. 'to incite' ⇒ \*kfwlud-nehr (IE) - Skt. c6dati 'to urge, impel, incite' < \*kfwleud-e-; Latv. kudinat 'to fly, urge, goad' < \*kfwlud-neh₂-. The High German forms point to an iterative paradigm 3sg. \*huttopi, 3pl. \*hutunanpi < \*kud-nehrti, \*kud-nh₂-enti. This formation could be fully parallel to Latv. kudinat.

\*huzda- n. 'treasure' - Go. huzd n. 'id.', ON hodd m. 'id.', OE hard n.jm. 'id.', E hoard, OS hard n. 'id.', OHG hart n. 'id.', MHG hart n. 'id.' ⇒ \*kudh-to- (IE?) - Gr. KEU8w 'to hide' < \*keudh-e-. In view of OSw. hydda f. 'shelter, hut' < \*huzdjon-, it seems plausible that the word is related to Gr. Kru-Bw. If so, it can be reconstructed as \*kudh-to-, assuming that PGm. \*-zd- arose by Bartholomae's law.

\*hiizjan- w.v. 'to hire' - OE hyr(i)an w.v. 'id.', E to hire, OFri. hera w.v. 'id.', MLG huren w.v. 'id.', MDu. huren w.v. 'id.', Du. huren 'id.' ⇒ \*kuHs-ie- (IE) - Hitt. kusfan- n. 'pay, salary, fee, hire' < \*kuHs-n-. The verb has been compared to the Hittite neuter deverbative kusfan(Goetze 1954: 189), which seems plausible.

\*hwa- pron. 'who? what?' - Go. lvas, acc. lvana, n. lvata pron. 'id.', ON hverr ( < \*hwaz-ja-), n. hvat pron. 'id.', OE hwii, n. hwcet pron. 'id.', E who, what, OS hwe, n. hwat pron. 'id.', Du. wie, wat pron. 'id.', OHG wer, hwer, n. waz, hwaz pron. 'id.', G wer, n. was pron. 'id.' ⇒ \*kwo- (IE) - Hitt. kui- - kue- - kuyapron. 'who, what', Skt. kf- pron. 'which', Gr. Tic; pron. 'who, which, what', Lat quis, quae, quid pron. 'who, what', Olr. cfa, OW pui, MW pݰ pron. 'who', OCS cbto, Ru. cto, SCr. sto pron. 'what'. The Indo-European interrogative pronoun. The Germanic evidence points to masculine \*hwaz (Go. lvas) vs. \*hwiz (OS hwe, OHG wer) < \*kwe/os, feminine \*hwo < \*kweh₂ and neuter \*hwat < \*kwod. See also \*hwapera'who/which of two'.

\*hwaino- f. 'red-top grass' - ON hvein top. 'swamp(?)', Nw. kvein(e) f. 'red-top grass; hair', Sw. dial. hven 'red-top grass; swamp', Da. hvene c. 'id.' (GM). A formation ablauting with Icel. hvin-gras 'red-top' (cf. the Scand. loanword E whin 'furze, gorze') and Nw. dial. kvine f. 'April' (< "grass month"?) < \*hwin-. The red-top or black bent is a high grass that grows in poorer soils (hence the occasional semantic shift to 'swamp'). In view of the Norwegian adjective kvein 'sharp' < \*hwaina- ( < \*kwoi-no-?), I assume that it was named after its tall stalks. No certain extra-Gm. etymology. The received links with Latv. svinit 'to soil oneself and Lat. caenum, coenum n. 'dirt' must be abandoned in view of the primary meaning 'sharp'.

\*hwaitja- m. 'wheat' - Go. lvaiteis m. 'id.', ON hveiti n. 'id.', Far. hveiti f./n. 'id.', OE hwcete m. 'id.', E wheat, OFri. wet m. 'id.', OS hweti m. 'id.', MDu. weit(e) m./f. 'id.', OHG weizi, hweizi m. 'id.', G Weizen m. 'id.' (DRY). A term usually taken to be derived from \*hwita- - \*hwitta- 'white' (q.v.). The suffixation with \*-ja- is parallel to \*hersja(n)- 'millet' and follows from the fact that the formation started out as a collective. Note that the preservation of the diphthong of MDu. weit is regular before an umlaut factor (cf. e.g. \*aik- 'oak', \*gait- and \*hraini-).

\*hwaja- - \*huja- m. 'whey' - OE hwceg n. 'id.', E whey, OFri. wiii m. 'id.', WFri. waai c./n. 'id.', MDu. wey, hoey f./m. 'id.', EDu. wei, dial. hui c. 'id.' ⇒ \*tkw-oi- (IE) - Skt /cyfra- n. 'milk', NP sir 'id.' < \*tkwiH-ro- (cf. Rasmussen 1990; Rasmussen 1999: 2, 385); Alb. hirre f. 'whey' < \*tkwfH-r-neh₂-(?).

\*hwalfa- n. 'vault' - ON hvalf n. 'concavity', Ice!., Far. hvalv n. 'arch, vault', OE hwealf f. 'arched covering' ⇒ \*kw6/p-o- (EUR) - Gr. K61.rcoc; m. 'bosom; womb; hollow space, pocket' < \*kwo/p-o- (with dissimilation of \*kʷto \*k as in Kaprc6c; m. 'wrist' < \*kwrp-o-). Also cf. OE hwealf adj. 'vaulted' < \*hwalba- < \*kwo/p-6-. The adjective probably gave rise to ON hvelfa, Far. hvg/va, OE be-hwielfan, OS be-hwe/Dian, Du. we/ven, MHG welben, G wolben 'to cause to turn (over), vault' < \*hwalbjan-, but it is possible, too, that this formation represents a causative directly derived from the strong verb \*hwelfan- (q.v.).

\*hwali- m. 'whale' - ON hvalr m. 'id.', Far. hvalur m. 'id.', Elfd. wal m. 'id.', OE hwæl m. 'id.', E whale, MDu. wa/-visch 'id.', Du. wal-vis 'id.', OHG wal, wa/-fisk m. 'id.', G Wal, Wal-fisch m. 'id.' ⇒ \*kwo/-es- (IE?) - OPru. kalis 'wels catfish' < \*kwo/-i-? The word probably contains the PIE root \*kwe/- 'to turn' (see \*hweula'wheel'), cf. Du. tuimelaar 'bottlenose dolphin' (lit. "tumbler"). In view of the closely related formations OHG walira f. 'whale' < \*hwalizo(n)- and G Weis 'wels catfish, sheatfish' < \*hwalisa-, it is conceivable that the stem \*hwa/ideveloped from an old hysterokinetic s-stem \*kwo/-ݱs. gen. \*kwo/-s-6s, acc. \*kwo/-es-m. Schrijver 2001: 423, on the other hand, adduced Lat. squalus 'unidentified sea-fish', OPru. kalis 'catfish' and Fi. kala 'fish' to substantiate a European substrate word. Also note Ice!, hvelja, Far. hvglja f. 'whaleskin' < quasi-PGm. \*hwaljon-.

\*hwanno- f. '(stalk of) angelica' - ON hvpnn f. 'id.', Far. hvonn f. 'id.', Nw. kvann f. 'id.' ⇒ \*/(uondh-neh₂- (EUR) - Lith. svefi.drai m.pl. 'cattails' < \*l<uendh-ro-; Lat. combretum 'kind of rush' < \*l<uendh·r- (for the suffix, cf. corn-etum 'plantation of cornel-cherries'); ?Ir. contran 'water horehound' < \*l<undh-r-? (cf. Heiermeier 1982); Ir. cuinneo9 'angelica' is a loanword from ON hvpnn.

\*hwapjan- w.v. 'to choke' - Go. af-lvapjan w.v. 'id.', MHG ver-wepfen w.v. 'to go bad' ⇒ \*kwh₂p-ie- {EUR) - Close to Gr. Karrv6c; m. 'smoke, steam' < \*kwh₂p-no- (with dissimilation of \*kW ... p- > \*k. .. p-); Lat. vapor m. 'steam' < \*kwh₂p-os-; Latv. kupet 'to smoke, steam' < \*kuh₂p-eh₁-. The Germanic base \*hwap- is clearly related to the European root \*kwh₂por \*kuh₂p- (for the formal difficulties, see Schrijver 1991: 260-263), which implies that its root-final \*p arose in a formation affected by Kluge's law, e.g. in an iterative 3sg. \*hwappopi, 3pl. \*hwabunanpi- < \*kwh₂p-neh₂-. Also cf. Go. /vapnan w.v. 'to be choked, be quenched'. Lith. kviipas m. 'smoke' looks like a Germanic loanword, cf. Sw. kvav n. 'smokiness' < \*kwaba-, for which see \*kwebjan-.

\*hwar adv. 'where' - Go. Ivar adv. 'id.', ON hvar adv. 'id.', Far. hvar adv. 'id.', Elfd. war adv. 'id.', OE hwiir, hwci!r adv. 'id.', E where, OFri. hwiir adv. 'id.', OS hwar adv. 'id.', Du. waar adv. 'id.', OHG wii, wiir, hwiir adv. 'id.', G wo adv. 'id.' ⇒ \*kwo-r (IE) - Lat cur adv. 'why', Skt. kar-hi adv. 'when', Alb. kur afv. 'when' < \*kwor; Lith. kuf adv. 'where' < \*kwu-r. An adverb created to the PIE root \*kwo- by the addition of the locative suffix \*-r (cf. \*par 'there'). The WGm. forms pointing to \*hwcir do not continue PGm. \*hwer, but arose by monosyllabic lengthening of the vowel, like e.g. Lat cur < OLat. quor < PIE \*kwor.

\*hwarbjan- w.v. 'to make turn' - ON hverfa w.v. 'to turn', OE hwierfan w.v. 'to turn, to revolve', OS (gi-)hwerbian w.v. 'to whirl, turn, reverse, change', OHG werben, hwerben w.v. 'to turn' ⇒ \*kWorp-eie-? {IE). The causative to \*hwerban-.

\*hwarma- m. 'eyelid' - ON hvarmr, augna-hvarmr m. 'id.', Far. hvarmur, varmur\* m. 'id.', Nw. augne-kvarm, -kvarv m. 'edge of the eyelids', Elfd. ogen-walm m. 'eyelid' {IE?). The oldest Nordic languages have forms pointing to \*hwarma-. Far. eygna-barmur 'edge of the eyelids, area around the eyes' and Elfd. ogen-walm are probably distortions, especially the latter, which if it really continued ON \*augna-hva/mr should have developed into \*\*ogen-uom. Pos-

\*hwassa- adj. 'sharp' - Go. lvassaba adv. 'id.', ON hvass adj. 'id.', Far. hvassur adj. 'id.', Elfd. wass adj. 'id.', OE hwres adj. 'id.', OS hwass adj. 'rough, shaggy, serrated', OHG was, hwas adj. 'sharp, strict', MHG was adj. 'sharp, pointy' ⇒ \*kwh₁d-to- (DRV). The original participle to \*hwetan- (q.v.).

\*hwata- adj. 'quick' - ON hvatr adj. 'active, brisk, vigorous', OE hwret adj. 'fast, bold', MDu. wat adj. 'sharp', OHG waz, hwaz adj. 'fierce, sharp' ⇒ \*kwh₁d-o- (IE). Adjective to \*hwetan- < \*kweh₁d-e-. Cf. the factitive Go. ga-lvatjan, ON hvetja, OE hwettan, OHG wezzen w.v. 'to incite, sharpen, whet'.

\*hwapera- pron. 'who of two?' - Go. lvapar pron. 'id.', ON hvcirr pron. 'id.', Ice!. hvor pron. 'id.', Far. hvfiJr pron. 'id.', OE hwreoer pron. 'id.', E whether, OFri. hweder, hwether, hwoder, hod er pron. 'id.', OS hwethar pron. 'id.', OHG wedar, hwedar pron. 'id.', MHG weder pron. 'id.' ⇒ \*kwo-t(e/o)r-o- (IE) - Skt. katarci-, Av. katti.ra-, Gr. n6TEpoc;, Lat. uter pron. 'which of two', Lith. katras, dial. kataras, Latv. katrs pron. 'id.', OCS kotoni pron. 'who, someone', Ru. kot6ryj pron., rel. 'which, who', SCr. dial. koteri 'which' < \*kwo-t(e/o)ro-. An Indo-European pronoun derived from \*kwo- 'who, which' (see \*hwa-) with the contrastive suffix \*ter-o-.

\*hwapon- f. 'foam' - Go. Jvapo f. 'foam', Sw. kva f. 'id.' ⇒ \*kuotH-eh₂- (IE) - Skt. kvcithati 'to boil, seethe' < \*kuetH-e- (cf. kvti.thci- m. 'decoct'). A formation derived from the obscure root \*kuotH-, cf. Go. lvapjan w.v. 'to foam (at the mouth)' and OE hwaperian, hwoperian 'to foam'. MDu. hotten w.v. 'to coagulate (of milk)' could technically represent a related iterative \*kutH-neh₂-. The often cited connection with OCS kvas'b 'sourdough', OCS v'b(s)-kyseti 'to become sour' and Latv. kusat 'to boil' is formally difficult.

\*hwehla- - \*hweula- n. 'wheel' - ON hj61 n. 'id.', OE hweohhol, hweol, hweowol, hweogol n. 'id.', E wheel, OFri.fiti.l, titi.l n. 'id.', MDu. wiel n. 'id.', Du. wiel n. 'id.', G Wiel n. 'id.' ⇒ \*kwe-kw/- (IE) - Skt. cakrci-, YAv. caxra- m.jn. 'wheel' < \*kwekw/o-; ToA kukiil, ToB kokale m. 'cart, wagon, chariot', Gr. J<Ul<Aoc; 'wheel' < \*kϣ-kl-o- (with vocalized labialization as in yuvɿ 'woman' < \*gϣn-eh₂- (see \*kweno-) and \*-ukw- > -uk- as in ĢouK6A.oc; 'cowherd' < \*gweh₃-u-kwo/-o-); without reduplication cf. Gr. n6A.oc; m. 'axle', Lat. co/us m. 'distaff < \*kwe/-o- and W pel 'ball', OPru. kelan 'wheel' < \*kwel-o-.

\*aikwernan- - \*fkwernan- 'squirrel'). ON hv<eJJ and Far. poet hva?I n. 'wheel' have been adduced to reconstruct a non-reduplicated form \*hwe/a- (cf. OPru. kelan) < \*kwe/-o-, but they are probably better unified with OE hweohhol < \*hweh/a-.

\*hwekkan- s.v. 'to be startled' - Far. hvr,'1kka s.v. 'to be startled; to diminish; to begin slowly' ⇒ \*kwegh-ne- (EUR) - OCS ceznpti 'to vanish, perish'; is-ceznpti 'to flinch', Lat. conquinfsco, -ere 'to crouch down' < \*kwegh-n-e-. Both the Slavic and the Latin forms point to a thematic nasal present of the root \*kwegh-, cf. the Lat perf. -quex- < \*kwegh-s-. The same nasal present seems to be extant in Far. hvekka < ON \*hvekka (Old Norse only has the preterite hvak) < PGm. \*hwekkan-. Also cf. the causative Far. hvekkja w.v. 'to frighten, make start' < \*hwakkjan-.

\*hwelfan- s.v. 'to vault, revolve' - ON ho/ftnn adj. 'vaulted', MDu. welven s./w. 'to vault; to turn, revolve', Du. welven w.v. 'id.', MHG walb pret 'id.' ⇒ \*kwelp-e- (EUR). A marginally attested strong verb from a European root \*kwe/p-. See also \*hwa/fa-.

\*hwera· m. 'kettle' - ON hverr m. 'id.', OE hwer m. 'id.', OHG wer, hwer m. 'id.' ⇒ \*kwer-o- (IE) - Skt caru- m. 'kettle, pot' < \*kwer-u-; Ir. coire, W pair 'id.' < \*kwor-io-; OCS cren'b m. 'frying pan' < \*kwer-no-; Ru. cara f. 'bowl' < \*kwer-eh₂-. Also cf. ON hverna f. 'pot' < \*hwernan-. The lcel. variant hvarna, which occurs beside hverna, was back-formed from the oblique cases of the secondarily rounded form hvorna (Magnusson 1989: 393). Go. fuairnei f. 'skull' < \*hwernfn- is usually compared as well.

\*hwerban· s.v. 'to turn, move around' - Go. fuairban s.v. 'to go around, circulate', ON hverfa s.v. 'to turn; to disappear', Far. hverva s.v. 'to disappear, vanish', OE hweorfan s.v. 'to turn, change', OFri. hwerva s.v. 'to turn, move around, swap', OS hwerban s.v. 'to (re)turn', Du. werven w.v. 'to recruit', OHG werban, hwerban s.v. 'to return, drive', MHG werben, werven s.v. 'to turn, be concerned with', G werben w.v. 'to recruit' ⇒ \*kwerp-e-(?) (EUR?).

\*hwerhwetjo- f. 'gourd(?)' - OE hwerhwette f. 'cucumber', ME hwerwette, werwette 'cucumber, gourd' ⇒ \*kwerkwet- (NIE) - Lat. cucurbita f. 'gourd'. The English attestations can be reconstructed as PGm. \*hwerhwetjo-, continuing quasi-PIE \*kwerkwet-ieh₂-. The word is reminiscent of, but formally irreconcilable with Lat. cucurbita. I therefore assume that a European word referring to one of the members of the Cucurbitaciae was adopted by Germanic and Italic independently when these languages had settled in Europe. The word no doubt belongs to the layer of agricultural terms that are clearly non-Indo-European, cf. \*arwft- 'pea' and \*murhon- 'carrot'.

\*hwesan- s.v. 'to wheeze' - OE hw:i:!san s.v. 'id.', E to wheeze ⇒ \*ltues-e- (IE) - Skt. svasati 'to hiss, breathe, sigh' (cf. Skt. su?ma- m. 'hissing, roaring'), Lat. queror 'to complain' < \*ltues-; YAv. susi du.f. 'the lungs' < \*ltus-ih₁. Also cf. ON hvresa w.v. 'to hiss', Far. hvresa w.v. 'to wheeze', Elfd. wϢsa w.v. 'to breathe' (with secondary nasal vowel) < \*hwesjan- and MDu. hissen, hussen w.v. 'to whistle, hiss', ME hissen w.v. 'to hiss (of serpents, geese); to whistle', E to hiss < \*husjan-(?) < \*ltus-ie-(?).

\*hwetan- s.v. 'to stab, pierce' - ON hvata w.v. 'id. ' ⇒ \*kweh₁d-e- (IE?). A marginally attested strong verb (ON hvata is weak, but the poet. form hvatinn 'pierced' probably continues the original strong past participle) without any certain extra-Germanic cognates. The link with Skt. c6dati 'to stimulate' < \*keud-e- (for which see \*hut(t)on-) cannot be maintained (pace Pokorny IEW: 636), because the Germanic ablaut points to a root \*kweh₁drather than \*kued- with Schwebeablaut. This is especially clear from \*hwassa- 'sharp' (q.v.), which continues the original past participle \*kwt,i1d-to- with vocalization of a laryngeal. See also \*hwata- and \*hwotjan-.

\*hwijon- w.v. 'to whinny' - Icel. hvfa w.v. 'id.', MHG wihen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*kwi-eh₂-ie- (GM). A verb of onomatopoetic origin. G wiehern 'id.' is a frequentative of MHG wihen. Also cf. OHG weij6n, MHG weihen 'id.' < \*hwaijon- < \*kwoi-eh₂-ie-. Related to \*hwfnan-?

\*hwilo- f. 'while' - Go. Jveila f. 'period, while', ON hvfla f. 'bed', Far. hvfla f. 'rest, repose; bed', Elfd. waila f. 'while', OE hwfl f. 'id.', E while, OFri. hwfl(e} f. 'id.', OS hwfla f. 'id.', MDu. wile f. 'id.', OHG wf/a, hwfla f. 'id.', G Wei/e f. 'id.' ٺ

\*kwih₁-l-ehr (IE) - Av. siiiti- 'joy', OP siyiiti- 'comfort' < \*kwieh₁-ti-; Av. s(ii)iita- adj. 'happy', Lat. quietus adj. 'quiet' < \*kwieh₁-to-; Lat. quies, -etis f. 'sleep, rest, repose' < \*kwieh₁-t- (hence quiescþ 'to rest'); OCS po-citi 'to rest' < \*kwih₁-ie- (cf. OCS po-koi m. 'rest' < \*kwoih₁-o-); Arm. hangc'im 'to rest' < \*sm-kwih₁-. Also cf. Go. lveilan w.v. 'to hesitate', ON hvila w.v. 'to rest, lie down', OHG wilon w.v. 'to stay, reside', G weilen w.v. 'to linger' < \*hwilen-.

\*hwinan- s.v. 'to whistle, whiz' - ON hvfna s.v. 'id.', OE hwinan s.v. 'id.', ME to whine ⇒ \*kwei-n-e- (GM). Also cf. the causative OE a-hwoenian w.v. 'to vex, trouble' < \*-hwainjanand - without the nasal infix - \*hwijon- 'to whinny'. It seems probable that the root originally was onomatopoetic.

\*hwinda- m. 'greyhound' - OFri. hwynd 'id.', MDu. wint m. 'id.', Du. wind-hand 'id.', OHG wint m. 'id.', G Wind-hund 'id.' (IE). The full-grade form of \*hunda- 2 'dog' (Pijnenburg 1981). The old view that the word means 'Wendish hound' is obliterated by the late OFri. form hwynden pl. 'greyhounds' with initial hw- (Bremmer 1993: 31-2).

\*hwita- - \*hwitta- adj. 'white' - Go. ƕeits adj. 'id.', ON hvftr adj. 'id.', Far. hvftur adj. 'id.', Elfd. wait adj. 'id.', OE hwit adj. 'id.', E white, OFri. (h)wft adj. 'id.', OS hwit adj. 'id.', Du. wit adj. 'id.', OHG wiz, hwiz adj. 'id.', G weifl adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*l<u{e)it-n6- (IE) - Skt. śvitná- adj. 'white, whitish' < \*l<uit-no-; Skt. svetaadj. 'white, bright', YAv. spaeta-, MP sped, NP saped adj. 'white' < \*l<uoit-o-; Skt. svitra- adj. 'white, whitish' < \*l<uit-ro-. A formally complicated word. Most dialects have \*hwita-, a full-grade form continuing Pre-Gm. \*hwitta- < \*l<ueit-n6- (with Kluge's law), but OFri. wit and Du. wit with a short vowel point to a zero-grade variant \*hwitta- < \*l<uit-n6-. The resulting ablaut alternation raises the question of whether the adjective originally may have been athematic, e.g. a heteroclitic \*l<ueit-or, gen. \*l<uit-n-6s. For a similar type of adjectival ablaut, see \*stora- \*stura- 'big'.

\*hwosan- s.v. 'to cough' - OE hwosan s.v. 'id.', ME hwosen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*kweh₂s-e- (IE) - Skt. k&s- f. 'cough' < \*kweh₂s-; Lith. k6seti (k6siu) 'to cough' < \*kweh₂s-ehi-; Lith. kosu/ys, Latv. kiisulis, Ru. kase/', SCr. kufalj m. 'cough' < \*kweh₂s-ul-io- (the Slavic -s- points to the pre-existence of a present \*kweh₂s-ie-); Mir. casachtach f. 'the act of coughing'; W pas m. 'whooping cough' < \*kwh₂s-to-; Alb. kol/e f. 'cough' < \*kweh₂s-/-eh₂-. The primary verbal root served as the basis for \*hwostan- 'cough' (q.v.).

\*hwostan- m. 'cough' - ON h6sti m. 'id.', OE hwosta m. 'id.', MDu. hoeste m./f. 'id.', Du. hoest c. 'id.', OHG huosto m. 'id.', MHG huoste m. 'id.', G Husten m. 'id.' (ORV). Derived from \*hwosan- (q.v.) with a dental suffix. A denominal verb is ON h6sta, OE hwosta, OHG huoston, G husten, Du. hoesten < \*hwostojan-. .

\*hwotjan- w.v. 'to bore; to threaten' - Go. lvotjan w.v. 'to reprimand, warn sternly, threaten', ON hreta w.v. 'to threaten', OSw. heta w.v. 'to threaten; to bore a hole in the ground' ⇒ \*kwoh₁d-eie- (IE). The formation \*hwotjan- probably consists of a merger of the causative to \*hwetan- (q.v.) 'to stab' and a secondary jan-formation to Go. lvota, ON h6t f. 'threat' < \*hwoto- < \*kwoh₁d-eh₂-. This noun also gave rise to ON h6ta w.v. 'to threaten' < \*hwotojan-. Cf. \*hwata- and \*hwassa-.

\*i- pron. 'he/she, that one' - Go. is, n. ita pron. 'id.', Du. hij, n. het pron. 'id.' (with secondary h-), OHG er, n. iz pron. 'id.', G er, n. es pron. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₁i- (IE) - Skt. ayam, f. iyam, n. idam pron. 'this here, he', OAv. aiiam, f. YAv. fm, n. OAv. i! pron. 'this, that', Lat. is, f. ea, n. id pron. 'this, that', Oir. e pron. 'he', n. ed 'it', Li th. jis 'he', f. ji 'she', OCS ja 'she', n.je 'it' < \*h₁ei, f. \*h₁ih₂, n. \*h₁id. The PIE demonstrative pronoun, probably originally inflected as m. \*h₁ei, f. \*h₁i-ih₂, n. \*h₁id.

\*ida- m. 'chub, orfe' - Nw. id m. 'id.', Sw. id c. 'id.', Gutn. iiid f. 'id.', G dial. Aitel 'id.' (GM). A fish name usually taken to be derived from the root \*h₂eidh- 'to burn' (see \*aida-) because of its light color. This etymology is impossible, however, as \*id- cannot possibly have developed from this root in a regular way.

\*idala- adj. 'void, idle, futile' - OE idel adj. 'id.', E idle, ODu. idi/ adj. 'id.', Du. ijdel adj. 'id.', OHG ital adj. 'id.', G eitel adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*ihrtlo- (EUR) - Latv. Nfs adj. 'raw, crude, unworked', Ru.ja/yj,jalovyj adj. 'infertile, unused (of land)', SCr. jalov adj. 'infertile' < \*ieh₁-lo-; W ial f. 'brightening' < \*ibi-1-eh₂(Matasovit 2011: 44). The word has been compared to \*aida- 'fire' (q.v.) and Gr. i-Oap6c; 'pure, clear', but the implied semantic shifts are problematic. If the original meaning was 'empty', however, the word can simply be connected to the aforementioned Saito-Slavic and Celtic forms, which point to a proto-meaning "empty land, clearing".

\*idojan- w.v. 'to move about restlessly' - Ice!. ic'Ja w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₁i-t-eh₂-ie(IE) - Identical to Gr. tTaw 'to go here and there', Lat. ito, -are 'to go', Mir. ethaid 'id.'. The Icelandic verb has not been given a straightforward etymology, but probably continues the well-attested IE frequentative in -t- created to the root \*h₁ei- 'to go' (cf. \*aidja-). Also cf. Ice!. io n. 'constant moving, quivering' and Ice!. fa, ia f. 'profession, job' (Far. fain adj. 'eager, industrious'), the latter perhaps continuing an ablauting i-stem \*h₁ei-ti-s, \*h₁it-ei-s (hardly \*h₁eit-ih₂, \*h₁it-ieh₂-, Magnusson 1989: 415).

\*iliP- m.(?) 'sole of the foot' - ON ii f. 'id.', Far. ii f. 'id.', OE ile m. 'sole of the foot; hard skin, callosity', MLG ele, elde, elt n. 'callus', Du. eelt n. 'id.' ⇒ \*ii-it( GM). The different formations all seem to continue an old t-stem, \*iii, gen. \*ilipiz < \*ii-it, \*ii-it-es. Magnusson 1989: 418-9 connects ON tu, Ice!. ili m. 'fishing net weight; wedge', Nw. dial. ile m. 'fishing net weight, anchor; turf wall around a hut' < \*flan-, which point to an original meaning 'foot' or 'anchor'. No further cognates, except, perhaps ON ilki 'sole of the foot' if it derives from \*il-kan- (with the same suffix as in e.g. \*mankan- 'neck of a horse', q.v.). This ilki, however, has also been explained as borrowing from a lost Saami word corresponding to Fi. jii/ka 'track' (Hyllested 2008).

\*iljan- w.v. 'to rush' - OS flian w.v. 'to hurry', MDu. ilen w.v. 'to rush', Du. ijlen w.v. 'to hurry', OHG fllan, ilen w.v. 'to strife, rush', G eilen w.v. 'to rush' ::) \*h₁e(i)-h₁i-l-ie-? (IE?). Possibly an /-derivative of an intensive formation to the root \*h₁ei- 'to go', cf. \*aidja- and \*idojan-.

\*in(i) prep. 'in' - Go. in prep. 'id.', ON f prep. 'id.', Far. f prep. 'id.', Elfd. i prep. 'id.', OE in prep. 'id.', E in, OS in prep. 'id.', Du. in prep. 'id.', OHG in prep. 'id.', G in prep. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₁en(-i) (IE) - ToAB y-,yn-, ToB in-, Arm. i, Gr. £v, EVL, Lat. in, OPru. en, Latv. ie-, Lith. j, OCS Vb(n), Ru. v(o}, SCr. u 'in(to)' < \*h₁en(-i); also cf. Skt. anika-, YAv. ainika- m. 'face' < \*h₁eni-h₃kW-o-. A PIE preposition, originally an adverb. Related to the directional \*innai.

\*inkan- m. 'pain, torment' - ON ekki m. 'sorrow, grief, Far. ekki m. 'pain, suffering; grief; anxiety', OE inca m. 'grudge, grievance, suspicion' ⇒ \*h₁eng-on- (EUR) - OCS jt;dza f. 'disease', SCr.jeza f. 'shudder, horror, (obs.) disease, (dial.) anger' < \*h₁eng-ieh₂-; Lith. engti (engia) 'to press, strangle, torture' < \*h₁eng-. An n-stem created to a Germanic and Balto-Slavic root \*h₁eng-. Also cf. OFri. ink adj. 'angry' < \*enka- and Nw. ekke n. 'harm, damage' < \*inkja-.

\*inkwa(n)- m. 'lump' - Ice!. okkr, okkvi m. 'lump; hillock', Sw. dial. ink 'ulcer', MDu. enke, inke m. 'small wound', EDu. incke 'wound, scar' ⇒ \*engw·on(IE) - Gr. aoi)v, -£voe; f./m. 'gland', Lat. inguen, -inis n. 'swelling on the groin; groin' < \*ngw-en-. Together with the Latin and Greek forms, PGI\}. \*inkwan- points to a proterodynamic n-stem \*engw-n, gen. \*ngw-en-s. In view of the impossible ablaut of this formation, it is possible that it replaced more primary \*negw-n, \*ngw-en-s.

\*innai adv. 'in( side)' - Go. inna adv. 'id.', ON inni adv. 'id.', OE inne adv. 'id.', E in, OS inne adv. 'id.', OHG inni adv. 'id.', MHG inne adv. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₁en-noi (DRY). A locative adverb created to \*in(i) with the suffix \*-noi, for which cf. \*ferrai 'far'.

\*instra- n. 'intestinal fat' - ON fstr n., fstra f. 'paunch-fat', Ice!. fstra f. 'paunch, pot-belly', Far. fstur n. 'id.', Elfd. jster n. 'grease', MLG inster n. 'entrails of slaughtered cattle', MHG inster n. 'entrails' ⇒ \*h₁en-sth₂-ro- (IE) - Lith. [sCios, Latv. iek5as f.pl. 'entrails' < \*h₁n-sth₂-ieh₂-. A noun derived from the preposition \*h₁en 'inside' and the locational suffix \*-sth₂-, for which see \*in(i) and \*inpera-. OPru. instran 'lard' may have been borrowed from MLG inster.

\*inþera- n. 'entrails' - ON iðr, innr, indr n. 'id.', Far. indur n. 'intestines for cooking blood sausage', pl. 'entrails' ⇒ \*h₁en-tero- (IE) - Close to Gr. EVT£pa n.pl. 'intestines, bowels, guts' < \*h₁en-ter-h₂-; also cf. Skt. antara- adj. 'interior, adjacent to; intimate', YAv. ai:1tara- adj. 'interior', Lat. interior adj. 'inner, internal' < \*h₁en-tero-. A noun derived from the comparative adjective \*h₁en-tero- 'internal'. Also cf. ON iorar f.pl. 'bowels' < \*inþerōz. See further \*instra-.

\*irha- m. 'he-goat' - OHG irah, ireh, irh m. 'id.', MHG ireh m. 'id.' ⇒ \*irK.-o(EUR) - Gr. δόρξ, ζόρξ, Hsch. ἴορκες, ὕρκες (pl.) 'kind of deer, roe, gazelle' < \*iorK.-(?), Arm. ors 'hunt, game' < \*iorK.-o-; W iwrch m. 'roebuck', Bret.yorch, iourc'h 'id.'; OCo. yorch 'caprea' < \*iorl<-u-; Lat. hircus, ircus m. 'billy-goat' < \*irK.-(?). Also cf. OHG ir(a)h 'buck leather', MLG er(e)ch, errich n. 'tanned leather'. All forms point to PGm. \*irha-. This form has a close match in the a-grade formation Arm. ors (Martirosyan 2009: 544-5) and W iwrch < \*iork-o- (with raising before -rk-, cf. Schrijver 1995: 61). The variation of Gr. δόρξ, ζόρξ, ἴορκες and Lat. hircus, ircus is unexplained.

\*isa- m./n. 'ice' - ON fss m. 'id.', Far. fsur m. 'id.', Elfd. ais m. 'id.', OE fs n. 'id.', E ice, OHG fs n. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₁eiH-so-(?) (IE?) - To Av. aexa- n. 'cold', Oss. ix / yex 'ice' < \*HeiH-ko-? In view of the neuter gender in West Germanic, it is possible that the word represents a thematicized s-stem, e.g. \*h₁ei(H)-(o)s, gen. \*h₁i(H)-es-(o)s. No certain extra-Gm. cognates. Av. aexa- can only be related if it continues a form \*h₁eiH-ko- that developed into \*HeikHo- by laryngeal metathesis (for which cf. \*hofa-).

\*isarna- - \*izarna- n. 'iron' - Go. eisarn n. 'id.', ON fsarn n. 'id.', OE fsern, isen, fren n. 'iron', OS isarn n. 'iron', MDu. fser, fsen n. 'id.', Du. ijzer n. 'id.', OHG fsarn, fsan n. 'id.', G Eisen n. 'id.' (NIE). A PGm. loanword from PCelt. \*fsarno- 'iron', cf. Olr. farn 'id.'. The latter word was borrowed by Old Norse at a later stage, emerging there as jarn. It is uncertain whether \*fsarno- belongs to the family of \*aiza- 'ore' (q.v.) < \*h₂ei-es-.

\*iwa- m. 'yew' - ON yr m. 'id.', Elfd. ayve n. 'id.' (< \*fwja-), OE fw, eow m. 'id.', E yew, OHG fwa f. 'id.', G Eibe f. 'id.' ( < \*fwo-) ⇒ \*h₁eiHu-o- (EUR) - Gr. oa, OT] f. 'elderberry tree, mountain ash', Lat. iiva 'branch of grapes; raisins' < \*h₁oiHu-ehr; Olr. e6 m.(?) 'stem, shaft, yew-tree', W ywen 'yew-tree' < \*h₁ieHu-o-; Lith. ieva, Latv. ieva f. 'bird-cherry'; ORu. iva, Ru. fva, SCr. /'va < \*h₁iHu-ehr. A European word. Also cf. the collective Sw. ide n. 'yew grove' < \*fwepja-. Elfd. ayve is a collective as well, as is common for tree-names, cf. ON p/r m. 'alder' < \*aluza-vs. Ice!. elri n. 'id.' < \*aluzja-.

\*jakk/gon- w.v. 'to rush, chase' - OS jagon w.v. 'to drive', WPhal. jacken w.v. 'to rush', EDu. jacken 'id.', Du. jagen w.v. 'to rush, hunt'; jakkeren 'to speed', OHG jagon w.v. 'to rush, chase, hunt', G jagen w.v. 'id.', Pal. jacken w.v. 'to rock',jackern w.v. 'to rush, speed' ⇒ \*Hiogh-neh₂-(?) (IE?) - To Skt. sam-fhate 'to strive, desire', OAv. iziia 'I wish', YAv. iziieiti 'to wish' < \*High-eand Gr. ixavaw 'to desire' < \*High-sk-? An iterative verb 3sg. \*jakkopi, 3pl. \*jagunanpi, closely related to the zero-grade variant \*jukkon-. It further seems possible that the intensive MHG jouchen,j6uchen w.v. 'to drive, hunt' < \*jaukjan-, belongs here, although this word is usually compared to YAv. yaozaiieiti 'to incite' < \*iougChl-eie- (cf. LIV2: 315-6). No certain etymology; perhaps related to the root \*High- as

\*jaukja- m. 'draft animal' - ON eykr m. 'draft animal, horse' ⇒ \*ioug-io(DRV /IE) - Identical to Skty6gya- m. 'draft animal'. Derived from the PIE \*iug-o- 'yoke', for which see \*juka-. Also cf. Nw. !ifykje f. 'mare' < \*jaukjon-.
\*jazjan- w.v. 'to make ferment' - Nw. erja w.v. 'to dissolve', OHGjerien w.v. 'to make ferment' ⇒ \*ios-eie- (IE). The causative to \*jesan- ( q.v.).

\*jedan- s.v. 'to weed' - OS iit-jedan s.v. 'to root out', MLG geden w.v. 'to weed', OHGjeten s.v. 'id.', Gjii.ten w.v. 'id.' (GM). Also cf. OHG jetto m. 'weed' < \*jeddan- and OS jeda f. 'weeding hoe, plane(?)' < \*jed6n-. Perhaps also related are OE weod, OS wiod n., OHG wiota f. 'weed' < \*weuda/6-, assuming that these forms continue \*wi-jada- or \*wi-juda-, i.e. the reflex of PIE \*h₁ui- 'away, apart' and a noun \*jada- / \*judaderived from the verb \*jedan- (Torp 1909: 329). The word cannot possibly be reconstructed as a reduplicated noun \*wiuda- < \*ui-udh-o- (pace Franck 1949: 792; Koivulehto 1971: 170-71), the implied root \*uedh- 'to bind' being semantically remote.

\*jehan- s.v. 'to acknowledge' - ON ja w.v. 'to say yes; to promise', OFri.jii, jiin s.v. 'to confess, acknowledge', OS jehan s.v. 'to say, confess', ODu. gian, be-gien s.v. 'to confess', OHG jehan, gehan s.v. 'to confess, witness' ⇒ \*iek-e(WEUR) - Lat. iocus m., also ioca, -arum pl. n. 'joke' < \*iok-es-. A strong verb to a European root \*iek-. ON ja can be derived from \*jehan(through \*ea > \*ed), but like the frequentative ON jatta, OHG jiizzen w.v. 'to say yes' < \*jahaton- or \*jahatjan-, it seems to have been in close association with \*ja 'yes'. See also \*jehti-.

\*jehti- f. 'confession' - Du. biecht c. 'id.', OHG jiht f. 'utterance', bi-jiht, bfht f. 'confession', MHG giht, begiht, biht f. 'utterance, confession', G Beichte f. 'confession, admission' ⇒ \*iek-ti- (DRY) - Ir. icht 'people, tribe', W ieith f. 'language, nation, race', Bret.yezh f. 'language' < \*ieK-ti-. A ti-stem created to the root of \*jehan- (q.v.). Some have also connected OHG giht, gi-giht f. 'paralysis', G Gicht f. 'gout, arthritis', Du. jicht c. 'id.' by assuming an original meaning 'curse'.

\*jeka(n)- m. 'ice' - ON jaki m. 'broken ice, ice-floe', LG is-jack 'icicle', G Swi. jii.ch m. 'hoar-frost' ⇒ \*ieg-o(n)- (IE) - Hitt. eka- n./c. 'cold, frost, ice' < \*ieg-o-; Olr. aig, gen. ega f. 'ice'; W ia m. 'id.' < \*ieg-i-; Lith. yzia f. 'ice-floe' < \*ig-ieh₂-(?). Also cf. ON jpkull m. 'icicle; glacier', OFri \*j(i)ukul (with u-mutation, cf. Lofstedt 1931: 141, fn. 2), WFri. juke/ c. 'icicle, cone', NFri. jug/ m. 'id.' ( = MLGjokele 'id.'); OSw. ikil m. 'id.', Elfd. ais-ikkel m. 'id.', OFri. itsil, OE gicel m. 'ice-floe; icicle' (E icicle < \*ice-yickle) < \*jekila-; OHG ihhilla, OS jikila f. 'icicle' < \*jekiljo-.

\*jera- n. 'year' - Go. jer n. 'id.', ON ar n. 'id.', OFri. jer n. 'id.', OS jiir n. 'id.', Du. jaar n. 'id.', OHG jar n. 'id.', G Jahr n. 'id.' ⇒ \*iehi-ro- (IE) - Luv. iira/i- c. 'time' < \*ieh₁-ro-?; YAv. yiira, gen. yet yii n. 'year' < \*ieh₁-r/n-; Gr. wpa, f. 'season, year, hour, time' < \*ioh₁-reh₂-; Lat. hornus adj. 'grown, produced in this year (of crops and yield)' < \*ghi-iohi-r-no-(?); OCS jero m.,jera f. 'spring' < \*ieh₁-ro/eh₂-; RuCS, ORu. jarऀ m. 'lamb', SCr. jare m. 'kid, young goat' < \*ieh₁r-en-; Lith. Jras, diaI.þras, Latv.Hrs m. 'lamb' < \*iehi-ro-. Most languages show an r-suffix, but the Avestan paradigm preserved an archaic heteroclitic inflection. With the additional evidence for e/o-ablaut in the different dialects, the paradigm is therefore to be reconstructed as static, nom. \*i6hi-r, gen. \*ieh₁-n-s (Schindler 1975: 5).

\*jesan- s.v. 'to ferment' - Sw.jii.sa w.v. 'id.', Elfd.jii.sd s.v. 'id.', OHGjesan s.v. 'id.', MHGjesen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*ies-e- (IE) - ToAyii.s- 'to boil', ToB yii.s- 'to excite sexually; ravish', Skt. yasati 'to boil, become hot', YAv. yaesiial)t- ptc. 'boiling', Gr. 0£w 'to boil, ferment'. A strong verb \*jesan-. There also seems to have been an o-grade variant, cf. Ice!. asa w.v. 'to rush', Far. asa w.v. 'to struggle', Nw. ase s.v. 'to ferment' < \*jasan-, which probably represents an old intensive formation. It again gave rise to the secondary causative ON resa, Ice!. cesa, Far., Nw. esa w.v. 'to incite' < \*josjan-.

\*jestu- m. 'yeast' - ONjpstr m. 'id.', Nw.jest m. 'id.', OSw. icester m. 'id.', Elfd. jii.st c. 'id.', E yeast, MLG gest 'id.', MDu. gest, gist m. 'id.', Du. gist c. 'id.', MHG jest m. 'foam', G Gischt f. 'id.' ⇒ \*ies-tu- (ORV) - Identical to W iiis f. 'seething'. A tu-stem derived from the root of \*jesan- ( q.v.).

\*jewan- m. 'creeping insect(?)' - Nw. dial. jo(e) m. 'maggot' ⇒ \*ieuH-on(IE) - Skt.yukii- f. 'louse' < \*iuH-k-eh₂-. An n-stem related to Nw. y w.v. 'to squirm' < \*jujan- < \*iuH-ie-. The word has not yet been etymologized, but is reconcilable with Skt. yukii-.

\*juka- n. 'yoke' - Go.juk n. 'id.', ON ok n. 'id.', Far. ok n. 'id.', Elfd. uok n. 'id.', OE geoc n. 'id.', E yoke, OS Juk n. 'id.', Du. Juk n. 'id.', OHG joh n. 'id.', G joch n. 'id.' ⇒ \*iug-o- (IE) - Identical to Hitt. juka- n. 'yoke, pair', Sktyuga- n. 'yoke, MP ywg 'id.', Gr. 0uy6v n. 'id.', Lat. iugum n. 'id.', OW iou 'id.', MW iau,yeu 'id.'; also cf. Lith.Jungas, Latv.jugs m. 'id.', OCS igo, Ru. fgo 'yoke', SCr. arch. igo n. 'cross-beam' < \*iu-n-g-o-. Also cf. Go. Jukuzi f. 'yoke' < \*iug-us-ihr and MHG Jiuch n. 'acreage' < \*Jeuka-. See further \*Jaukja-.

\*jukjan- w.v. 'to itch' - OE gyccan w.v. 'id.', E to itch, OS Juk{k)ian w.v. 'id.', Du.Jeuken w.v. 'id.', OHGJucken w.v. 'id.', GJucken w.v. 'id.' (GM?). A WGm. Jan-verb 3sg. \*JukJepi, 3pl. \*JukJanpi. The \*j was lost before front vowels, which after WGm. J-gemination resulted in an allomorphic paradigm 3sg. \*Jukipi, 3pl. \*jukkJanpi. This allomorphy explains the difference between e.g. G Jucken and Du. Jeuken. Also cf. OE gydJa m. 'itch', MDu. jucte, Joocte f. 'id.', OHG jukido, MHG Jukede m. 'scabies' < \*Jukipan-. No certain etymology. It is perhaps conceivable that the verb, which appears to have had an oblique subject (\*mike JukJepi 'I have an itch'), is related to \*Jukkon-, cf. G Als., Pal.Jucken 'to jerk'.

\*jukkon- w.v. 'to hop; to run' - G dial. Jucken w.v. Swab. 'to jump, hop', Pal. 'to jerk, rock', Als. 'to jerk, jump up, turn away', Swab. 'to jump, run; to hop', Cimb. 'to throw; jump', Swi. (Rhntl.) 'to run fast', (Schfh.) JU.kxa w.v. 'to hop' (GM?). Probably related to \*Jakk/gon- ( q.v.) 'to rush' in view of the semantics of e.g. G Pal. Jacken w.v. 'to rock'. In the German dialects, the word has often merged with Jucken 'to itch' < \*jukjan-, but this may originally have been a different verb.

\*jumja- m. 'divine twin(?)' - ON Ymir m. 'primordial giant in Norse mythology' ⇒ \*iT[IH-io- (IE) - Skt. yama- m. 'twin; name of the mythical first man', OAv.yama- m. 'twin', Av.yima- (< \*yama-) m. 'name of a mythical primeval king'; Olr. emon m. 'twin' < \*i(e)mH-o-; Latv. Jumis 'paired couple, double fruit' < \*imH-io-.

\*junga- adj. 'young' - Go. Juggs adj. 'id.', ON ungr adj. 'id.', Far. ungur adj. 'id.', Elfd. ungg adj. 'id.', OE geong adj. 'id.', E young, OFri. Jung adj. 'id.', OS Jung adj. 'id.', Du. jong adj. 'id.', OHG Jung adj. 'id.', G Jung adj. 'id.' ʬ

\*h₂iu-nl<-6- (IE) - Identical to Skt. yuva.M- adj. 'young, youthful', m. 'youth'; Lat. iuvencus m. 'young bull'; Oir. 6ac adj. 'young', W ieuanc 'id.', Bret yaouank 'id.'. This adjective is commonly reconstructed as \*h₂iu-Hn-l<o-, i.e. a formation derived from PIE \*h₂oi-u- 'lifetime, age' (for which see \*aiwa/6- 1) with the

\*jii.z pron. 'you (pl.)' - Go. jus, obl. izwis 'id.', ON er, obl. ylJr pron. 'id.', OE ge, obl. eow, low pron. 'id.', OFri. f, gi, obl. io, iii pron. 'id.', OS gf, obl. eu, iu pron. 'id.', Du. jij, obl. jou pron. 'you (sg.)', OHG ir, dat. iu, acc. iuwih pron. 'id.', G ihr, obl. euch pron. 'id.' ⇒ \*iuH-s (IE) - Skt. yiiyam, Lith. j{is, Latv. jus, OPru. iuse, Alb.ju pron. 'you (pl.)'. The pronoun of the second person plural. In the nominative, \*jiiz is the primary form. It is attested in Gothic, but the NWGm. languages introduced \*jiz on the basis of \*wfz 'we' (q.v.). The original oblique form was \*iwe < PIE \*iu(H)-e, with an ending -e as in PIE \*h₁me 'me'. In WGm., this form was extended by \*-ike (OE eowic, OHG iuwih, G euch) on the basis of \*mike in the accusative case. North and East Germanic point to \*izwiz in both the dative and the accusative case; ON yor arose from \*iRWiR by dissimilation of the first palatal r. This \*izwiz seems to have come about by metathesis of a form \*iw-siz, i.e. the oblique form \*iwe plus a dative suffix abstracted from \*unsiz 'us'. It replaced the original dative \*iwiz that lived on as OFri. io, iii, OHG iu, etc. The suffix \*-iz itself arose under the influence of the datives \*miz and \*piz. See also \*wiz and \*jut.

\*ji'ista- m. 'cheese' - ON ostr m. 'id.', Far. osݲur m. 'id.', OSw. aster m. 'id.', Elfd. uost m. 'id.' ⇒ \*iuHs-to-(?) (IE?) - Skt. y6$-, gen. yii$IJUS n. 'soup, broth, stock', Lat. iiis, iiiris n. 'broth, sauce' < \*iuHs-; Gr. 0DμT) f. 'leaven, beer-yeast' < \*iuHs-meh₂-; Lith. j6se f. 'broth, soup', OPru. juse f. 'soup' < \*iuHs-ieh₂-; CS juxa f. 'broth', Ru. uxa f. 'fish-soup', SCr. dial. juha f. 'soup, broth' < \*iuHs-eh₂-. A Nordic word that is usually connected to the aforementioned non-Germanic forms pointing to PIE \*iuHs-. ON ost points to \*justa- with a short \*u, however. It is possible that this is due to secondary shortening of \*ii, not least in view of the borrowed Fi.juusto 'cheese'. It cannot entirely be excluded either, however, that the long u of the Finnish word is secondary, in which case PGm. \*Justa- becomes analyzable as a petrified past participle to the strong verb \*jesan- 'to ferment' (the u in that case represents a secondary zero grade). The word existed alongside PNWGm. \*kiisja- m. 'cheese', cf. OE cfese, OS kiisi, Du. kaas, OHG kiisi, G Kase, which was borrowed from Lat. ciiseus, ciiseum 'id.'. lcel. kcesir, Nw. kjese, Elfd. kftse m., Da. kcese c. 'rennet', apparently continuing the same form \*kiisja-, is actually a different formation, and was derived from the secondary verb \*ktisjan-

\*jut pron. 'you (du.)' - Go. obi. igqis pron. 'id.', ON it, obi. ykkr pron. 'id.', Far. tit, obi. tykkum, tygum pron. 'id.', Elfd. io pron. 'id.', OE git, obi. inc pron. 'id.', OS git, obi. ink pron. 'id.' ⇒ \*iud (IE) - Lith. judu, f. judvi 'you (du.)' < \*iu-du- (the absence of Winter's law points to late univerbation). The dual pronoun of the second person. The original nominative was probably \*jut in view of Lith.judu. This form is not attested in Gothic, however, and NWGm. introduced \*jit under the influence of \*wit 'we two' (q.v.). The oblique case is to be reconstructed as \*inke. It is entirely parallel to \*unke 'us two', and is likely to have arisen analogically by the substitution of initial u by i on the basis of \*iwe, the oblique form of \*juz (q.v.). Similarly, the dative \*inkwiz, continued by Go. igqis, ON ykkr, adopted the element \*-wiz that was abstracted from the parallel second person plural oblique form \*izwiz.

\*ju(w)unþi- f. 'youth' - Go. junds\* f. 'id.', OE geogoo f. 'id.', E youth, OS juguth f. 'id.', ODu. iugint f. 'id.', MDu. joghet f., Du.jeugd c. 'id.', OHG jugund f. 'id.', G ]ugend f. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂iu-Hg-ti- (IE) - Close to Lat. iuventus, -tiJtis f. 'youth', Olr. 6etiu, 6itiu, gen. -ted 'id.' < \*h₂iu-Hn-tuH-t-. A ti-stem usually assumed to have been created to the PIE word for 'young', i.e. \*h₂iu-Hon, gen. \*h₂iu-Hn-os, for which see \*junga-. The West Germanic material points to a form \*juwunpi- with regular velarization of the \*w (cf. \*su- 'sow' and \*newun '9') between two high vowels (one of them being rounded). The w probably arose in hiatus position of a proto-form \*juunpi-. Go.junds\*, on the other hand, seems simply to have lost the hiatus. Note that the often cited Go.junda\*, supposedly comparable to Lat. iuventa f. 'youth'), is a ghost form. The only attestation is the dat. jundai, which could belong to either an 6-stemjunda\* or an i-stem junds\*. The latter possibility is more likely, however, because of the other Germanic languages pointing to an old ti-stem.

\*kabla/on- m./f. 'piece of wood' - ON kajli m. 'piece cut off, MDu. cavele m.jf. 'stick, lot', Du. kavel c. 'lot, parcel', MHG kabel m./f. 'lot' ⇒ \*gobh-/-on(EUR) - Lith. iiibas m. 'branch, cane' < \*gobh-o-.

\*kagan- m. 'stem of a (small) tree(?)' - ON kagi m. 'shrub, young tree', Nw. dial. kage m. 'small shrub', E dial. cag 'trunk' ⇒ \*gogh-on- (NEUR) - Lith. zagaras m. 'twig', pl. 'shrubs', Latv. iagari pl. 'id.' < \*gogh-oro-. A North European word perhaps related to \*kokon- 'skid' (q.v.). Also cf. OHG kegil m. 'cone, peg, wedge, nail', G Kegel m. 'cone; pin' < \*kagila-.

\*kaiba- adj. 'sharp(?), crooked(?)' - Nw. keiv adj. 'wry, wrong, left', OE ciif adj. 'quick, sharp, prompt', ME cof adj. 'quick' ⇒ \*goibh-o- (DRY). An adjective probably derived from \*kiban- 'to quarrel' (q.v.).

\*kaipa- m. 'to groove, notch' - ON keipr m. 'rowlock', Du. keep c. 'notch' (DRY). An a-stem derived from the iterative \*kippon- 2, or perhaps rather from the pertaining, unattested strong verb \*kipan-.

\*kaizwjan- w.v. 'to turn, direct' - ON keyra w.v. 'to whip, lash; to ride, drive; to fling', Far. koyra w.v. 'to drive; to push; to pour', OE cierran w.v. 'to turn, change', OFri. kera, kerra w.v. 'to turn; to hinder', ODu. keran w.v. 'to turn', Du. keren w.v. 'to turn', OHG keren w.v. 'to turn, direct' ⇒ \*fglois-u-eie(IE) - Hitt k15a(ri) - ki5anta(ri} 'to happen, occur; to become' < \*(gJeis(Eichner 1973: 78). The material offers evidence for three different proto-forms: 1) OHG keren < \*kaizjan-, 2) OE cierran < \*kazjan- and 3) ON keyra < \*kaizwjan-. The Old Norse form is usually derived from \*kaurjan- or \*kauzjan-, as the umlaut of \*au is \*ey, but it is possible, too, that ON ei was rounded by a following \*w. It thus appears that, if keyra is related to OHG keren, which is hardly doubted by anyone, it must have developed from \*kaizwjan-, a causative to an unattested u-present. The reality of this formation is further backed up by Nw. dial. keis m. 'turn, corner' < \*kaisa-, which precludes the reconstruction of a root \*kair- or \*kaur- with \*r. OE cierran < \*kazjanprobably lost the \*i due to dissimilation against \*j in the cluster \*-izj-.

\*kalan- w.v. 'to be cold' - ON kala s.v. impers. 'to freeze', OE ca/an s.v. 'to be or become cool or cold' ⇒ \*g61H-e- (EUR) - Lat. gelus m. 'cold, frost, ice' < \*gel-u-. An o-grade strong verb, whence \*kalda- 'cold' (q.v.), the iterative \*kallon(q.v.) and the i-stem OE cele, ciele, cile, E chill < \*kali-. See further \*kula- 1 and \*kolu-.

\*kalba(n)- m. 'calf (of the leg)' - ON kalfi m. 'calf of the leg', Far. kalvi m. 'small of the leg; middle of the seine net', OSw. kalver m. 'extension of a fishing net', Sw. kalv c. 'calf of the legs; interior of a rope; extension of a fishing net', EDu. kalf'soft part of wood', G dial. Kalb m. 'muscle' (GM?). A word without a certain etymology, but possibly related to \*kulba(n)(q.v.). E calf was adopted from Old Norse.

\*ka/biz- n. 'calf - ON ka/fr m. 'id.', Far. kalvur m. 'id.', Elfd. kav m. 'id.', OE cealf, ca/fur n. 'id.', E calf, OS kalf n. 'id.', Du. ka/f n. 'id.', OHG ka/p, pl. kalbir n. 'id.', G Kalb n. 'id.' (GM). An old s-stem \*ka/baz, pl. \*ka/biz6. Also cf. Go. kalbo, OHG ka/ba f. 'female calf < \*kalbon- and the ablauting OE ci/for-lamb, OHG ki/bur n., ki/burra f., MHG kilbere f. 'female lamb' < \*ke/buza-, \*-j6-. No certain etymology. The connection with Gr. 8tlcpuc; f. 'womb' < \*gwe/bh-u- (cf. Pokorny JEW: 473) is problematic for two reasons. In Greek, first of all, initial labiovelars usually lose their labialization in words with labials (cf. Kanv6c; 'smoke' < \*kwh₂p-no-). This means that the 8 of 8£.A.cpuc; probably continues PIE \*d rather than \*gw. Second, Germanic labiovelars do not lose their labialization in front of \*o. This means that the implied proto-form \*gwo/bh-es- would give PGm. \*\*kwalbiz-, which is not the case.

\*kalda- adj. 'cold' - Go. kalds adj. 'id.', ON kaldr adj. 'id.', Far. kaldur adj. 'id.', Elfd. ko/d adj. 'id.', OE cea/d adj. 'id.', E cold, OS kald adj. 'id.', Du. koud adj. 'id.', OHG kalt adj. 'id.', G kalt adj. 'id.' (DRY). The original past participle of the strong verb \*kalan- (q.v.). The Germanic word is unrelated yet suspiciously similar to OCS xlad'b 'coolness, cool breeze', Ru. x6/od 'cold'. Further cf. ON, Far. kuldi, Nw., Da. kulde 'cold' < \*kuldan-.
\*kallon- w.v. 'to freeze' - G Swi. xa/len w.v. 'to freeze, stiffen' (DRY). The iterative to the strong verb \*kalan- (q.v.).

\*ka/wa- adj. 'bald' - OE ca/o adj. 'id.', E callow, MDu. ca/u adj. 'id.', Du. kaal adj. 'id.', OHG kalo adj. 'id.', G kahl adj. 'id.' u \*go/H-uo- (NEUR) - OCS go/'b, Ru. g6/yj, SCr. go adj. 'naked' < \*go/H-o-; for a comparable formation, cf. OCS glava, Ru. go/ova, SCr. gl<iva f. 'head', Lith. galva, Latv. gaTva, OPru. ga/wo f, 'id.' < \*go/H-ueh₂-. Also cf. OFri. sin-kele f. 'permanent baldness'. A Germanic-Balto-Slavlc isogloss. Unrelated to Skt. kulva- adj. 'bald, thin-haired', YAv. kauruua- adj. 'bald', Lat. calvus adj. 'id.' < \*k/H-uo-.
\*ka/zojan- w.v. 'to call, shout' - ON kalla w.v. 'id.', Far. ka/la w.v. 'id.', Elfd. kolla w.v. 'id.', OE ceallian w.v. 'id.', E to call, Du. dial. kallen w.v. 'to talk',

\*gols-eh₂-ie- (NEUR) - Lith.gaTsas m. 'echo', OCS glaso, Ru.96/os, SCr. g/{is m. 'voice' < \*gols-o-. A denominal verb derived from an unattested noun \*kalza-. The underlying proto-form \*go/so- is mirrored by the Balta-Slavic material.

\*kamba- m. 'comb' - ON kambr m. 'comb; crest', Far. kambur m. 'comb; pointed mountain', Elfd. kamb m. 'comb', OE camb m. 'id.', E comb, OS kamb m. 'id.', Du. kam c. 'id.', OHG kamb m. 'comb, crest', G Kamm m. 'id.' u

\*gombh-o- (IE) - Skt jambha- m. 'tooth', du. 'jaws'; ToA kam, ToB keme\* m. 'tooth', Gr. y6μcpo<; m. 'peg, bolt, nail', Lith. iambas m. 'sharp edge', Latv. zuobs m. 'tooth', OCS zpbo, Ru. zub (gen. zuba), SCr. zub m. 'tooth', Alb. dhemb / dham(b) m. 'id.'. See also \*kimbon- and \*kambjan-.

\*kambjan- w.v. 'to comb' - ON kemba w.v. 'to comb; to card', Far. kemba w.v. 'to comb', Elfd. kemba w.v. 'id.', OE cemban w.v. 'id.', OS kembian w.v. 'id.', OHG kemben w.v. 'id.', G kiimmen w.v. 'id.' (DRV). The factitive to \*kamba- (q.v.).

\*kanhta- adj. 'glad, happy' - ON katr adj. 'id.', Far. katur adj. 'id.', Elfd. kqt adj. 'lively' (GM). This adjective has been reconstructed as \*kawata- (cf. Torp 1909: 33; Magnusson 1989: 450), so as to compare it to e.g. Lat. gaudio, -ere 'to gladden', but Elfd. kqt (cf. ON kceta w.v. 'to gladden', Elfd. kflta 'to exercise' < \*kanhtjan-) with its nasal vowel requires a reconstruction \*kanhta- (cf. Heidermanns 1993: 33). The underlying root \*kanha- 'glad' < \*gonko- is in Verner alternation with Sw. dial. kang n. 'agitation' (also cf. ON kangin-yroi n.pl. 'jeering words') < \*kanga- < \*gonk6- and ME cang adj. 'foolish, lewd' < \*kanga-. The latter may be an Old Norse loanword, however.

\*kanjan- w.v. 'to bring forth' - OE cennan w.v. 'to beget, conceive, create, bring forth', ME kennen w.v. 'id.' u \*gonhi-eie- (DRV). A causative created to the PIE root \*genh₁-. ON kenna w.v. 'to father (a child)' must be an OE loanword, as \*kanjan- would have become \*\*kenja in this language. See also \*kunda-, \*kuninga- - \*kununga-, \*kunja- 1 and
\*kinpa- - \*kinda-.

\*kannjan- w.v. 'to make known' - Go. kannjan w.v. 'to make known, inform of, ON kenna w.v. 'to know, recognize; to assign or attribute', Far. kenna w.v. 'to distinguish, recognize, know', Elfd. kenna w.v. 'to know', OE cennan w.v. 'to choose, to prove, to declare', WFri. kenna 'to know', OS ant-kennian 'to

\*kannon- 1 w.v. 'to get to know' - ON kanna w.v. 'to search, find out', Far. kanna w.v. 'to inspect' (DRY). An inchoative, i.e. medial factitive formation to \*kunnan-, quasi PGm. \*kann-non- < IE \*gonh₃-nh₂- + -neh₂-.

\*kannon- 2 f. 'jug' - ON kanna f. 'id.', Far. kanna f. 'id.', Elfd. kanna f. 'id.', OE canne f. 'can, cup', E can, OFri. kanne f. 'id.', MDu. canne f. 'jug', Du. kan c. 'id.', OHG kanna, kanta f. 'id.', G Kanne f. 'id.' u \*gandh-on-(?) (WEUR?) - Mir. gand 'vessel, can(?)' < \*gandh-(?). A word that - if related to Mir. gand - must have developed from \*kandn(with regular assimilation of d between two nasals). Another possibility is to compare the word to Ice!. kani m. 'bowl; prow', kanni m. 'prow', MLG kane, G Kahn m. 'boat' < \*kan(n)an- and Far. kanna f. 'point of a boat's bow' < \*kanon-. The often adduced OHG kanta, kannata is a loanword from Lat (olla) cannata, and does not point to a proto-form \*kandon- (Luhr 1988: 204). Also cf. ON krena f. 'boat' < \*kenjon-.

\*kanta- m. 'side' - E Scot. cantle 'crown of the head; fleshy part behind the ear in a ram's head', MDu. cant m. 'edge, region', Du. kant c. 'side; edge', G Kanz m. 'mane, upper part of the neck' u \*gond-o- (NEUR) - Lith. zandas m. 'cheek, jaw', Latv. zuods m. 'chin, jaw'. The late attestation of this word has led to the assumption that it was borrowed from Romance, cf. Fr. dial. cant, It., Sp. canto. In view of the semantically similar G Kanz and Scot. cantle, however, it seems plausible that we are dealing with a Germanic word meaning 'side (of the head?)' or 'cheek'. PGm. \*kanta- can then be connected to Lith. zandas < \*gond-o-, whose root looks like a variant of \*gen(H)- as found in \*kin nu- 'cheek, chin'.

\*kap(p)on- w.v. 'to stare' - OE capian w.v. 'to turn', MDu. capen w.v. 'to watch, stare at', OHG kapfen w.v. 'to stare', MHG kafen, kapfen w.v. 'to stare, be in awe' u \*gobh-neh₂- (EUR) - Ru. zab6ta f. 'care, worry' < \*gobh-ot-eh₂-(?). An iterative formation 3sg. \*kappoþi, 3pl. \*kabunanþi (secondary \*kapunanþl) < \*gobh-neh₂-ti, \*gobh-nhi-enti. It probably served as the basis for the stative ON k6pa 'to watch' < \*kopen- and the causative(?) OE kepan 'to watch, keep', WFri. kypje w.v. 'to look' < \*kopjan-. Also cf. OE ge-cop adj. 'fit' < \*ga-kopa- (Heidermanns 1993: 340-1).

\*karo- f. 'worry, care' - Go. kara f. 'care, worry, concern', ON kpr f. 'bed of sickness', OE cearu f. 'suffering, sorrow; care, caution', E care, OS kara f. 'sorrow, lament', OHG kara f. 'sorrow, sadness, repentance' ::} \*9b2r-eh₂- or

\*goh₂r-eh₂- (IE) - MP zryg, zlyk 'sorrow, suffering', Oss. zaryn / zarun 'to sing' < \*geh₂r-; Gr. yfjpuc; f. 'voice, speech' < \*geh₂r-u-; Lat garrio, -ire 'to chatter', Olr. gairid, W garddu 'to call, cry' < \*g(e}h₂r-ie-; Olr. g<iir f. 'cry', W gawr 'crying, battle' < \*geh₂r-i-. PGm. \*karo- can have developed from both \*9b2r-eh₂- with vocalization of the laryngeal and from \*geh₂r-eh₂- or \*goh₂r-eh₂- with pretonic shortening. Also cf. Go. karon, OE cearian, OS karon, OHG karon w.v. 'to worry' < \*karojan- and OE cearig, OS -karag, OHG karag 'sad', G karg, Du. karig 'sparse' < \*karaga-.

\*karska- adj. 'lively' - ON karskr adj. 'brisk, bold', MLG karsch adj. 'vivid', Du. kers-vers adj. 'fresh', MHG karsch adj. 'vivid' u \*hlfJor-sko-(?) (IE?). No certain etymology. Possibly related to Skt j<irate 'to wake up' < \*hi9er-e-; Gr. fydpw 'to awaken, rouse, raise' < \*hi9er-ie-.

\*katazan- m. 'tomcat' - MDu. catere m. 'id.', Du. kater c. 'id.', OHG katere m. 'id.', G Kater m. 'id.' (NIE). A formation that is taken to be derived from \*katton- 'cat' with the masculinizing azan-suffix, for which cf. \*ganazan- 'gander'. The Gottingen dialect variant kiiz, pl. koze (Schambach 1858: 98) would then continue a non-Vernerized form \*katasan- or \*kadasan-. The single t of the implied root \*kat- still remains remarkable, as the derivational base \*katton- has a geminate. Perhaps, this alternation points to an old n-stem with a singulate/geminate alternation, nom. \*kato, gen. \*kattaz. Late OHG katere looks like a variant stem \*kadazan-, but is in spite of Cimb. kattaro usually regarded as a loanword from Low German. The alternative is to conversely assume that Du. kater is a loanword from High German, which would imply a paradigm \*kado, \*kattaz 'cat'.

\*katton- f. 'cat' - Nw. katte f. 'cat', OFri. katte f. 'id.', MDu. catte f. 'cat', Du. kat c. 'cat', OHG kazza f. 'cat', G Katze f. 'cat' (NIE). No Indo-European etymology. The word is usually believed to stem from an Afro-Asiatic language, cf. Arab. qitta, Nub. kadis 'cat', penetrating the European languages through Lat catta. The internally Germanic alternation of \*katton- with Nordic \*kattu-, cf. ON k9ttr, Far. kf:1ttur, kattur (whence \*kattjon- in ON, Far. ketta, Nw. kjette f. 'cat') nevertheless suggests that there must have been a root \*gat- present in Germanic before the great sound shifts, i.e. well before the Roman era. The evidence, at any rate, clearly points to an old n-stem \*kato, gen.sg. \*kattaz, ace.pl. \*kattuns < \*gat-6n, \*gat-n-6s, \*gat-n-os, the latter case being continued by Nordic

\*kaujan- w.v. 'to call, shout' - OE cfegan w.v. 'id.', OHG gi-kewen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*gouH-eie- (IE) - Skt. j6guve 3sg.med. 'to call, announce' < \*geu-guH-e-; Gr. y6oc; 'crying, wailing' < \*gouH-o- (cf. Gr. yoaw 'to cry, lament' < \*gouH-eh₂-ie-); OCS govon 'noise, shout, rumour, murmur', Ru. g6vor 'sound of voices, talk', SCr. govor m. 'speech, dialect' < \*gouH-oro-. . The verb has the form of a causative, but its intransitivity points to an intensive formation. An alternative explanation would be to assume that the causative was created to a deponent verb corresponding to Skt. j6guve. See also \*kewan- 'to cry'.

\*kauma-(?) m.(?) 'folded hands' - Ice!. kaun n.pl. 'id.', Far. keym(i}, kj6m 'id.' (GM?). The forms occur in the contexts Ice!. ao blcisa i kaun, Far. at blcisa f keym, kj6m 'to blow into one's folded hands (to warm them)'. It is likely that we are dealing with a Germanic cognate of Av. gauua- 'hand' < \*geu-o(Magnusson 1989: 451), but the original form of the word is difficult to reconstruct. Icel. kaun and Far. keym can perhaps be unified into an Old Norse proto-form \*kaum(r}, but Far. kj6m at least superficially points to \*kj6mr. Given these difficulties, it seems futile to attempt to project the forms further back into Proto-Germanic. They may, for instance, be more recent formations to ON kjci 'to rub(?)', Icel. kja 'to caress, to rub', Nw. kja 'to rub, toil' < \*kewon- and ON kci w.v. 'to root up, turn' < \*kawon-.
\*kauna- n.(?) 'blister' - ON kaun n. 'sore', Icel. kaun n. 'boil, carbuncle', Nw. kaun m. 'blister' (GM). Also cf. Far. koyna, Nw. ktiJyne f. 'blister' < \*kaunon-. No further etymology.

\*kauno- f. 'jaw' - MDu. coon f. 'jaw (of a fish), gill', Du. koon c. 'cheekbone, jaw, gill' ⇒ \*giouH-neh₂- (DRY) - Lith. iiciuna f. 'jaw, jawbone, gill'; Latv. iailnas f.pl. 'jaw', Bulg. iuna f. 'lip' < \*gieuH-neh₂-. A formation derived from the verb \*kewwan- 'to chew' (q.v.). Other re· lated Germanic formations meaning 'jaw' are difficult to account for due to severe formal incongruences, cf. OSw. kiЦke, Sw. kiike, Nw. kjake < \*kekan· - OE ceace, E cheek < \*kaukon- - OFri. tziiike, WFri. tjeak, EFri. sooke < \*keukon- - MDu. cake, Du. kaak < \*kakon- (see De Vaan 2012). OE cian f.pl. 'gills', MDu. kieuwe f. 'jaw, gill', Du. kieuw c. 'gill', OHG kiuwa, MHG ki(u)we, G Kiiu f. 'jaw' < \*kew(w)on-, on the other hand, can be derived directly from the strong verb.

\*kauton- f. 'knuckle' - OFri. kate f. 'id.', MDu. cote f. 'hoof, fetlock', Du. kootje dim. 'phalanx', G Rhnl. kotche dim. 'dice made of sheep's knuckles' (GM). No certain etymology. The word has been compared to Sw. dial. kut m. 'hunch, hump', which, however, belongs to the unrelated etymological cluster of Sw. dial. kuta (ryggen), Nw. dial. kuuta ryggjn 'to bend one's back', Nw. dial. kut m. 'stuntedness, dwarfism' < \*kuta-, Sw. dial. kot m. 'gnarl' etc. The link with MDu. cote/ f. 'animal dropping', Du. keutel c. 'id.' < \*kuti/6- (cf. Pokorny JEW: 393-8) is not very appealing either.

\*kawo- f. 'jackdaw' - OE ceo f. 'id.', E chough, OS ka f. 'id.', MDu. cauwe f. 'id.', Du. kauw c. 'id.', OHG kaha, ka f. 'id.' (DRY). A West Germanic bird name, possibly related to \*kewon- and \*kaujan- 'to cry'. The word has been compared to Ukr. gava 'crow', but since PSI. g should have changed into h in this language, this form is likely to be a distortion of Slav. \*kava (Danylenko 2006: 184), cf. Cz. kavka 'jackdaw'. It is conceivable, on the other hand, that this word together with Lith. k6vas, k6va 'jackdaw' was borrowed from Germanic, although this would probably imply a WGm. proto-form \*kawo- ( < \*kewo-) rather than \*kawo-. Da. ka, Nw. ka, kaje, Sw. kaja were borrowed from Low German.

\*kaza- n. 'casket, vat' - Go. kas n. 'jar, vase, vessel', ON ker n. 'tub, vessel, goblet', Far. kar n. 'id.', Elfd. kar n. 'id.', OS bi-kar n. 'bee-hive', MDu. caer n. 'creel, bee-hive', Du. vis-kaar n. 'creel', OHG kar n. 'vessel, vat, trough', MHG kar n. 'vessel' (GM). No Indo-European etymology. The word is close to, but phonetically irreconcilable with Olr. coire, MW peir 'cauldron' < PCelt. \*kwaryo- < \*kwr-to-, for which see \*hwera-. The often assumed link with Lat. ger6 'to carry', allegedly continuing a root \*ges-, is erroneous, because this verb continues \*h₂9-es-, i.e. an s-present to ago, -ere < \*h₂eg-e- (for which see \*akan- 1).

\*kebra(n)- m. 'beetle' - OE ceafor, cefer m. 'beetle', E chafer, ODu. kevera f. 'grasshopper', MDu. kever(e) m. 'beetle', Du. kever c. 'id.', OHG kevar(o) m. 'id.', G Kafer m. 'id.' (EUR?). A West Germanic word probably leading back to a proto-form \*kefran- or \*kebran- (with -br- > \*-fr- by Bahder's law). The reconstruction of the vocalism is problematic, however. Swi. (App.) xt:fer (with E < \*e rather than \*ii1 or \*iiz) precludes a formation \*kabiza-, which would have given \*\*xefar in this dialect. A form \*kafra- or \*kabra- must nevertheless be reconstructed on the basis of OE ceafor. Etymologically, the word is often derived from a verbal root \*gebh- as supported by OCS o-zobati 'to spoil', Ru. zobat' 'to eat, peck', SCr. zobati 'to eat' < \*gobh-1 cf. Ru. zob 'bill', Olr. gop m. 'muzzle, snout, beak' < \*gobbo-. The original meaning must then have been 'gnawer'.

\*kegjo- f. 'peg, key' - OE cii!g f. 'key', OFri. kai, kei m. 'id.' ( < \*kega-), WFri. kaai c. 'id.' (GM). An Anglo-Frisian isogloss, perhaps with an original meaning 'peg', cf. MDu. kegghe f. 'wedge' < \*kagjo-. See further \*kagan-.

\*kelka(n)- m. 'jawbone' - ON kjalki m. 'jaw; sledge', Far. kjalki m. 'cheekbone', Nw. kjelke m. 'sledge', dial. 'Adam's apple', Elfd. tjake m. 'sledge', OHG kelah m. 'goiter', MHG kelch m. 'crop, double chin' (IE). A formation related to \*kelon- 'throat' (q.v.). Perhaps, the k-suffix can be explained from a ur/n -stem \*gwefhrur, \*gfwJ/hru-en-s (see \*keluro-), which could regularly have developed into PGm. \*kelkur, \*kulkenaz by the velarization ofpost-sonorantal laryngeals before \*u (cf. \*taikwer-). A potential zero grade is present in Da. kulk 'gullet', dial. 'Adam's apple'. Ice!. kalkur m. 'sledge, bar on a sledge' continues an o-grade formation \*kalka-. Also cf. OHG kelh, MHG kelch m. 'crop, goiter'.

\*kelon- f. 'throat' - OE ceole f. 'throat, jowl', Du. keel c. 'throat', OHG kela, kel f. 'id.', G Kehle f. 'id.' :=} \*gwefh₁-eh₂- (IE) - Close to Lat. gula f. 'throat, gullet' < \*gwfhreh₂-; also cf. Skt. girati, gilati 'to devour' < \*gwJh₁-e-; Arm. ekul 'devoured' < \*h₁e-gwofhr; Gr. MA.mp (gen. -awe;, pl. oeO.arn, oeA.rupa) n. 'decoy, bait' < \*gwe/h₁-u[. The lack oflabialization in Germanic is problematic, but it may have been lost in the zero-grade form \*gul- < \*gwfhr, for which see \*keluro-. The word has a WGm. distribution, but Elfd. grq-tjyolu f. 'Eurasian siskin', lit "firtree-throat"(?), appears to point to the pre-existence of ON \*kjala, obi. \*kjplu (Noreen 1882: 66 fn. 1). See also \*kelkan-.

\*keluro- f. 'throat' - OE ceoler f. 'collar, throat', OHG kelur, kelero m., kelera f. 'id.' :=} \*gwefhrur- (IE). The Germanic word is usually reconstructed as \*keluza-, but the alternative \*kelura- is preferable because it favors the reconstruction of the word as a heteroclitic ur/n-stem, nom. \*gwefh₁-ur, gen. \*gfwlfh₁-en-s. In this paradigm the loss of the labialization would be regular in the genitive, which allows us to connect the word to the PIE root \*gwefhr 'to devour'. See also \*kelon-.

\*kema- n. 'mold' - Ice!. kam n. 'filth', E dial. coom 'soot', EDu. kaem, kaen 'layer of mold (on fluids)', Du. kaam, kaan c. 'id.', MHG kcim, kcin(e) n. 'id.', G Kahm m. 'id.' (GM). A formation that in view of the m/n alternation may continue an mn-stem \*keman-, obi. \*kemn-. It is probably related to Far. kamur adj. 'vague, un· clear', Nw. dial. ktim adj. 'dark' < \*kema-. E keam, keans 'froth' continues a closely related collective OE \*ceme < \*kemja- to the same root. No further

\*kerban- s.v. 'to carve' - OE ceorfan s.v. 'to cut (off); slay', MDu. kerven, caerven s.v. 'to carve; slice; scrap', Du. kerven w.v. 'to carve' ⇒ \*gerbh-e(EUR) - OCS ireb'b, irebij m. 'lot', Ru. dial. zerebej 'cut off piece' < \*gerbh-o(for the semantic shift from 'carve' to 'fate', cf. rezat' ierebja); Gr. ypcl:cpw 'to scratch, carve, write' < \*grbh-e-. A strong verb to a European root \*gerbh-. Olr. cerbaid 'to hack; lacerate' looks like a Germanic loanword.

\*kerla- - \*karla- m. 'man, freeman' - ON karl m. 'man; man of the common people', Far. kallur m. 'man', Elfd. kall m. 'man; husband', OE ceorl m. 'man, low freeman, peasant', E churl, OFri. tzerl, tzirl, kerl m. 'man; servant', MDu. kerle, kerel, caerl m. 'low freeman', Du. kerel c. 'man, guy', OHG karl, karal m. 'husband, spouse', G Kerl m. 'man, fellow' ⇒ \*gerh₂-ol- (IE) - ToB §ray, pl. sriin- m. 'adult man' < \*gerЧz-n- (with nom. -y for -ii?); Skt. jarant- 'old, infirm', Oss. zcerond 'old', Arm. cer(un) 'old; old man', Gr. yepwv, -ovrnc; m. 'old man' < \*gerh₂-ont-. The non-Germanic cognates point to an original n- or nt-stem to the root \*gerh₂- 'to ripen', cf. Skt. jarati 'to age, grow old; to make old, decrepit' < \*gerh₂-e-; OCS Zbreti, Ru. zret' (zreju), SCr. zreti 'to ripen' < \*grh₂-ehi-. The Germanic evidence, on the other hand, is best derived from a thematicized I-stem with root ablaut, e.g. nom. \*gerh₂-ol, gen. \*grh₂-/-6s, acc. \*gorh₂-el-m.

\*kernan- m. 'kernel, core' - ON kjarni m. 'kernel', Far. kjarni m. 'id.', OS kerno m. 'kernel, seed', MDu. kerne, keerne 'kernel, stone', Du. kern c. 'kernel', OHG kerno m. 'kernel, corn', G Kern m. 'kernel' ⇒ \*gerh₂-n-on- (IE). An n-stem related to \*kurna- (q.v.). Also cf. the archaic neuter n-stem Go. kaurno, Nw. dial. korna n. 'id.' < \*kurnon-.

\*kerzan- s.v. 'to creak, to cry (of birds)' - OE ceorran s.v. 'to creak', MDu. kerren, carren w.v. 'to creak, squeak' ⇒ \*gers-e- (EUR) - Lith. gai'sas m. 'sound, rumour, glory' < \*gors-o-; Lat. garrio 'to chatter' < \*grs-ie- (or \*geh₂r-ie with the littera-rule, see \*karo-). A strong verb \*kerzan- for expected \*kersan- < \*gers-e-: the \*z points to influence (back-formation?) from the iterative \*kurzon- (q.v.). Note that the verb was strongly associated with the crying, shrieking and screeching of birds, especially the grouse family, cf. ON, lee!. rjup-ker(r)i, -karri m. 'male ptarmigan', Du. kor-hoen 'black grouse'.

\*kespa- m.(?) 'beam(?)' - Du. kesp c. 'beam lying on top of the foundation' (GM). A Dutch and Low German word that is regarded to be without an etymology (cf. Vercoullie 1925: 161), but which can possibly be related to OE cosp 'fetter' < \*kuspa- ( q.v.).

\*ketwa- n. 'meat' - ON kjpt n. 'id.', Far. kj¢tn. 'id.', Elfd. tjyotn. 'id.' (NEUR?). An exclusively Nordic word that has been compared to Ice!. kvett(i) n. 'piece of meat', Far. kvett n. 'quick cut; lucky strike' < \*kwattja-, which appears to be derived from Far. kvetta w.v. 'to cut, break, bite quickly' < \*kwattjan-. This causative must in turn be related to ME cutten, kitten w.v. 'to cut' < \*kuttjan- and MHG kotze m. 'spud' < \*kuttan- (also cf. G Pal. Kiltz 'intestines' < \*kuttja-), which were created to an iterative \*kut(t)on- (< \*gudfhl-neh₂-), cf. Elfd. kuotci w.v. 'to whittle' < \*kuton-. It remains uncertain, however, whether \*ketwa- could have developed from \*kwetwa- by dissimilation of the first \*w. No certain etymology. Possibly related to Lith. gude f. 'whetstone' < \*gudh-ieh₂-. Hardly to Hitt. kudur- n. 'leg, shank' (Poetto 1986; contra: Hamp 1990).

\*keusan- s.v. 'to trial. select' - Go. kiusan s.v. 'to put to a test, prove by trial', ON kj6sa s.v. 'to choose, select, elect', Far. kj6sa s.v. 'id.', OE ceosan s.v. 'to choose, to elect', OS kiosan, keosan, keosan s.v. 'to choose, recognize', Du. kiezen s.v. 'to choose', OHG kiosan s.v. 'to choose, examine, judge', MHG kiesen s.v. 'to choose' u \*geus-e- (IE) - Hitt. kukufai 'to taste' < \*gu-g(e)us-; Skt. j6$ate 'to enjoy, like, be pleased', Gr. yEfoμm 'to taste' < \*geus-e-; Olr. do-goa 'to choose' < PCelt. \*tu-gus-o-. The oldest meaning, 'to test', is preserved by Gothic. The NWGm. languages, on the other hand, point to a secondary meaning 'to prefer', for which cf. \*kusti- and \*kustu-.

\*kewan- s.v.(?) 'to cry' - Icel. kj<i w.v. 'to cry (of birds)' :=} \*geuH-e-(?) (IE) - Skt.j6guve 3sg.med. 'to call, announce' < \*geu-guH-e-. This verb is compared to the causative \*kaujan- by Magnusson 1989: 465, and although kj<i is weak in Icelandic, it may indeed continue a primary strong verb \*kewan- < \*geuH-e-. Also cf. \*kii.ma-.

\*kewwan- s.v. 'to chew' - ON tyggva, tyggja s.v. 'to chew', Far. tyggja w.v. 'id.', Elfd. tegga s.v. 'id.', OE ceowan s.v. 'id.', E to chew, MDu. couwen, cuwen w.v. 'id.', Du. kauwen w.v. 'id.', OHG kiuwan s.v. 'id.', G kauen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*gieuH-e- (IE) - ToA swa-, ToB Siwa- 'to eat' < \*giuH-; MP jw- 'to chew; to devour', NP javldan / jav- 'to chew' < \*gieuH-; CS ibvati, Ru. ievat' (iuju) 'to chew' < \*giuH-eh₂-.

\*kiban- s.v. 'to quarrel' - ON kifa w.v. 'id.', MDu. kiven s./w.v. 'id.', MHG klben s.v. 'to quarrel, fight' ⇒ \*geibh-e- (GM). MDu. kipen w.v. 'to strive, struggle' could have its p from the iterative \*kippopi, \*kibunanpi (see \*kipp/bon-). This is not the same iterative as \*kippon- 2.

\*kila- m. 'bulb(?)' - MHG kif m. 'onion', G Kiel m. 'bulb' (DRY) - Close to Arm. cil, cif 'sprout, bud, piece of wood' < \*giH-1-. Probably a derivation from the verb \*klnan- (q.v.). The alternative is to derive the formation from \*giH-16- with pretonic shortening.
\*kiman- m. 'germ, sprout' - OS klmo m. 'id.', Du. kiem c. 'id.', OHG klmo m. 'id.', G Keim m. 'id.' ⇒ \*giH-mon- (DRY). An mn-stem derived from \*kman- (q.v.).

\*kimbon- f. 'crest, ridge' - OE cimb-stiin m. 'pedestal, socket', E chimbe, chime 'edge or rim of a cask or drum', MDu. kimme f. 'edge, chime', Du. kim c. 'horizon', dial. 'edge of a cask, chime' ⇒ \*gembh-on- (IE). An n-stem related to \*kamba- (q.v.).

\*kinan- s.v. 'to burst open, germinate' - Go. us-keinan s./w.v. 'to sprout up, bud forth, grow from', OE clnan s.v. 'to gape, break into chinks', OHG klnan s.v. 'to sprout, blossom, germinate' ⇒ \*gei-n-eH- (EUR) - Lith. iydJti, ziedet 'to flower, bloom, blossom' < \*geiH-dh(-)ehi-. An archaic nasal present. Originally, the nasal only occurred in the present, but not in the preterite or the past participle, cf. Go. us-kijanata 'germinated'. Note that OE clnan preserved the primary meaning 'to break open', cf. Sw. dial. kina w.v. 'to yawn; to faint', Nw. dial. kjena w.v. 'to starve, dwindle' < \*gi-neh₂- and MHG kl/, G Keil m. 'wedge' < \*kid/a- < \*geiH-dh/o-. This is a remarkable archaism in view of the fact that the meaning 'to bud' also occurs in Baltic and Armenian (for which cf. \*ki/a-). See further also \*klman- and \*klpa-.

\*kindi- f. 'kind' - ON kind f. 'kind, race' ⇒ \*genh₁-tf- (IE) - Av. fra-zair.iti'offspring, posterity', Gr. yEVE<JLC) 'birth', Lat. gens, -ntis f. 'race, nation, people' < \*genhi-ti-. A ti-stem to the IE root \*genhi- 'to be born'. This root represents a Schwebeablaut variant of \*gneh₁-, for which cf. \*knodi-. See also \*kanjan-.

\*kinnu- f. 'cheek' - Go. kinnus f. 'cheek, jawbone', ON kinn f. 'cheek; slope', OE cinn f. 'chin', OS kinni, kin, kin n. 'jaw, chin', Du. kin c. 'chin', OHG kinni n. 'chin, jaw-bone' ⇒ \*gen[H)-u (IE) - ToA §anwef!I du.f. 'jaws', Skt. hcinu- f. 'jaw', YAv. zanauua du. 'both jaws' (< \*gen-eu-h₁e), Arm. cnawt 'jaw, chin' (with unclear t), Gr. ytvuc;, -uoc; f. 'jaw, edge of an axe', Lat. gena f. 'cheek', Olr. gin m. 'mouth'; W gen (pl. pl. geneu) 'cheek' < \*gen-u-. An amphidynamic u-stem \*gen[H}-u-s, gen. \*gn[H)-u-6s. The stem variant \*gen[H)IJ- regularly developed into PGm. \*kinn-, which means that the u-ending must have been restored on the basis of the expected nom. \*kenus < \*gen[H)-u-s. In West Germanic, the word was reshaped into jo- and fa-stems, while ON kinn, pl. kinnr points to remodeling into a root noun.

\*kinþa- - \*kinda- n. 'child' - Nw. kind n. 'id.', OFri. kind n. 'id.', OS kind n. 'id.', Du. kind n. 'id.', OHG kind n. 'id.', G Kind n. 'id.' ⇒ \*genh₁-to- (IE) - W geneth, Gaul. geneta, genata, gnata f. 'girl' < \*genhret-eh₂-. A formation derived from the PIE root \*genhr 'to be born'. In view of the combination of a full-grade root with a to-suffix, the word can perhaps be analyzed as a thematicized t-stem, e.g. \*genhi-et, acc. \*genhret-m. If so, W geneth 'girl' < \*genh₁-et-eh₂- would be especially close. See also \*kanjan- and \*kin di-.

\*kipp/b6n- w.v. 'to quarrel, fight' - OFri. kebbia w.v. 'id.', WFri. kibje w.v. 'id.', MLG kibben w.v. 'id.', Du. kibbelen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*gibh-neh₂- (GM). The iterative 3sg. \*kippopi, 3pl. \*kibunanpi to \*kiban- (q.v.). Lith. kibinti 'to tease' was borrowed from Low German.

\*kippon- 1 w.v. 'to stumble' - Icel. kippa w.v. 'to feel tipsy; to fall into place', Far. kippa w.v. 'to go under water (of stones)', Elfd. tjippa-s w.v. 'to jump', E chip 'to trip up, cause to stumble', MLG kippen w.v. 'to stumble, stagger, knock over', Du. dial. kippen w.v. 'to tip' ⇒ \*gibh-neh₂- (NEUR) - Lith. geibti 'to weaken, fall over' < \*geibh·e-; Lith. geibus adj. 'clumsy, awkward' < \*geibh-u-. An iterative formation to an unattested strong verb \*kiban- or \*kipan-, for which cf. \*kaipa-. Unrelated to \*kippon- 2.

\*kippon- 2 w.v. 'to chop{?)' - ON kippa w.v. 'to pull, snatch', Icel. kippa w.v. 'to jerk', Far. kippa w.v. 'to cut off, OE cippian w.v. 'to chop, cut', E chip 'to cut away', MDu. kippen w.v. 'to catch' (GM). No extra-Gm. cognates. Perhaps connected to MDu. kippen, E to chip 'to hatch', cf. Du. kip 'chicken'.

\*kisila- m. 'gravel' - OE ciosol, ceosol, cisel m. 'pebble', MDu. kesel 'id.', Du. kiezel c. 'gravel; pebble', OHG kisel m. 'pebble' :=} \*gis-o-(?) (NIE) - Lith. zizdras, dial. iigidras m. 'gravel', iiezdra, ziegidra f. 'id.', OPru. sixdo 'sand' < \*g(e)is-ro/eh₂-. A diminutive to MHG kis m./n. 'pebble, scrap', G Kies m. 'gravel, grit' < \*kisa-, which seems to be in ablaut relationship to LG keisel, kesel < \*kaisila-. In view of the limitation to Germanic and Baltic, the word is unlikely to be of Indo-European origin. Note, for instance, the following non-IE *comparanda*: Udm. (S) gjj, gjj 'grain of sand', Komi keia 'gravel', Khanty xrs 'fine sand' and Georg. kvisa 'sand'.

\*kit(t)on- w.v. 'to tickle' - ON kit/a w.v. 'id.', Nw. kita, kit/a, kisla w.v. 'id.', Elfd. tjissla w.v. 'id.', OE citelian w.v. 'id.', E tickle (with metathesis), ODu. kitilon w.v. 'id.', Du. kietelen w.v. 'id.', OHG kizzilon, kuzzilon w.v. 'id.', MHG kitzen, kitzeln w.v. 'id.', G kitzeln w.v. 'id.' :=} \*gid-neh₂- (IE?) - Perhaps to Arm. ket 'point, dot' < \*goid-o- (whence kcanem 'to bite, sting' < \*goid-ie-, with c < \*-dj-?). In most Germanic languages, a frequentative /-suffix was added to the iterative 3sg. \*kittopi, 3pl. \*kitunanpi, but MHG kitzen and Nw. dial. kita preserve this primary formation. Etymologically, it is clearly linked with the Armenian forms. The implied base \*geid- is in violation of the PIE constraint on roots with two voiced (glottalized?) stops, but this may be due to its iconic meaning.

\*kiþa- m. 'sprout, bud' - OE cfo m. 'young shoot, sprig', OS kith m. 'germ, sprout', OHG kfdi n. 'shoot', MHG kfde n. 'id.' ( < \*kfpja-) :=} \*g(e)iH-tf- (DRY). A ti-stem to the PIE root \*geiH-, for which see \*kfnan-.

\*kizna- m. 'pine tree' - OE cen m. 'pine tree, spruce', MLG ken 'pine cone, pinewood', OHG kien m./n. 'pine tree, pinewood torch', G Kien m. 'resinous pinewood' :=} \*gis-n6- (NIE/WEUR) - Olr. crand-gfus 'pine tree', Ir. giu(i)s, giusach 'bog deal' < \*gis-usto-(?); Ir. giumhas 'resin', Gael. giuthas 'fir'. Usually connected to Olr. gfus 'spruce' through a proto-Germanic reconstruction \*kizna- (with \*-iz- resulting in \*ez in West Germanic). Given the distribution of the word, it is not likely to have been inherited from PIE. Perhaps, there can be a link with Fi. kuusi 'fir, spruce', SaaN guossa 'spruce',

\*klago- f. 'complaint, lament' - OS klaga f. 'id.', MDu. clage, claech f. 'id.', OHG klaga f. 'id.', G Klage f. 'id.' ⇒ \*g(wJfogh-eh₂- (IE?) - Skt.grhe 3sg.med. 'to complain' < \*g(wJlgh-; YAv. garazaite 'to complain, lament', Oss. q;erzyn I g;erzun 'to groan' < \*g(wJlgh-e-; YAv. garaza- f. 'complaint' < \*g(wJlgh-ehi-. A West Germanic o-stem, whence OHG klagon, G klagen, OS klagon, Du. klagen, OFri. klagia w.v. 'to complain, lament' < \*klagojan-. The Germanic word has been compared to the aforementioned Indo-Iranian forms, but the implied Schwebeablaut may indicate that the words are actually unrelated. The appurtenance of Mir. gl<im f. 'satire; outcry', quasi PIE \*gfagh-smeh₂- (Pokorny JEW: 350-1), is even more doubtful.

\*klainja- adj. 'fine' - OE clϋne adj. 'clean', E clean, MDu. cleine adj. 'small, fine, neat', Du. klein adj. 'small', OHG kleini adj. 'small, delicate, fine, pure, clean', G klein adj. 'small' (DRY). An adjective that was derived from the same root as found in ON, Far. klfna w.v. 'to smear' < \*klinjan- and OHG klenan s.v. 'id.' < \*klinan- (cf. Pokorny JEW: 366-7), for which see \*klajja- 'clay'. Its original meaning must then have been 'shining', whence 'pretty' and 'delicate' (cf. Heidermanns 1993: 332). Still problematic is the variant LG klin, Swi. xiii, which appears to continue an ablauting form \*klin- (Mitzka 1934). Heidermanns (I.e.) assumed a verbal origin for this ablaut, but the doublet \*klainja- : \*klin(j)a- is fully parallel to e.g. MDu. droghe, Du. droog 'dry' < \*draugja-: OE drjge 'id.', E dry < \*driigja-, and thus implies nominal or derivationally dependent ablaut.

\*klaipa- n. 'cloth' - OE cloo n. 'id.', E cloth, OFri. klath, kleth n. 'id.', WFri. klaad n. 'id.', Du. kleed n. 'id.', OHG kleid n. 'id.', G Kleid n. 'id.' (GM). A formation that is often compared to the cluster of OE clfoan s.v. 'to adhere, stick' < \*klipan-. The meaning of this verb, however, cannot easily be reconciled with \*klaipa-. Certainly related, on the other hand, is lcel. klfo n. 'piece of woven fabric' < \*klipa- (cf. Magnusson 1989: 45). ON, Far. klc:eCJi, Elfd. kliioe n. 'cloth; garment' was borrowed from West Germanic.

\*klaiwiz- n. 'clover' - OE cltifre, clϋfre f. 'id.', E clover, OFri. klti(?) m.(?) 'id.', WFri. klaver c. 'id.', EFri. kleeuwer m. 'id.', OS k/e 'id.', MDu. clavere f. 'id.', Du. klaver c. 'id.', OHG kle(o) m./n. 'id.', G Klee m. 'id.' (GM). An exclusively Germanic word displaying many formal difficulties (see Liberman 2008: 26-31). The alternation of forms with and without r can possibly be accounted for by reconstructing an s-stem (cf. Van den Berg 1954), e.g. \*klaiwaz (OHG kleo), pl. \*klaiwiz6- (cf. OE clϋ.fre < \*klaiwizon- vs.

\*klajja- m. 'clay' - OE clceg m. 'id.', E clay, OFri. klai m.(?) 'id.', WFri. klaai c. 'id.', MDu. clei n. 'id.', Du. klei c. 'id.' ⇒ \*gloi-(i)o- (IE) - Formally identical to Gr. yA.oL6c; m. 'any glutinous substance, gum' < \*gloi-(i)o-. Aja- or a-stem derived from the root \*kli- < \*glei-, cf. the zero-grade present OHG klenan, MHG klenen s.v. 'to smear, stick' < \*klinan- < \*gli-n-e-. OE cliim m. 'mud', MDu. deem m. 'clay' < \*klaima- is a mo-stem that again gave rise to Far. kleima, Elfd. kliema, OE clceman, MDu. clemen, OHG kleimen w.v. 'to smear' < \*k/aimjan-. Also cf. OHG kliwa, klia, G Kleie f. 'bran' < \*k/fwo-, which is close to Latv.glive 'slime'.

\*klammjan- w.v. 'to clamp; to jam' - OE clemman w.v. 'to fetter, bind, enclose', Du. klemmen w.v. 'id.', OHG klemmen w.v. 'id.', G klemmen w.v. 'id.' (DRV). A causative derived from \*klimman- - \*klimpan-. Also cf. OHG k/amma f. 'narrow place; canyon' < \*klammo- and - with a root-final stop - MHG klampfe f. 'clamp' < \*klampo-.

\*klangjan- - \*klankjan- w.v. 'to make stick' - ON klengja-st w.v. 'to interfere ( < "to make oneself cling")', Elfd. klaindja w.v. 'to clamber', OE clengen w.v. 'to adhere, remain', be-clencan w.v. 'to make cling', E to clench, clinch, OHG klenken w.v. 'to tie, bind' (DRV). The causative to \*klingan- (q.v.). The root-final \*k of the variant \*klankjan- presupposes a Pre-Gm. form \*klankkjan-, which may have adopted its geminate from a further unattested iterative \*klunkkon-.
\*klankwjan- w.v. 'to make shrink' - ON k/;fkkva w.v. 'to make weak, weaken', Far. k/;fkka w.v. 'to frighten' (DRV). The causative to \*kling(w)an- - \*klinkwan-.
\*klaubjan- w.v. 'to split' - ON kleyfa w.v. 'id.', Far. kloyva w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*gloubh-eie- (EUR).

\*klawo- f. 'claw' - OE clawu, cla f. 'id.', E claw, Du. klauw c. 'id.', OHG k/awa, k/a, k/O(a) f. 'id.', G Klaue f. 'id.' (GM). An exclusively Germanic word. The Nordic forms ON k/6, Far. k/6gv, Elfd. kluo f. 'claw' can together with OHG k/6{a) be reconstructed as \*k/6w6-, which would be an ablaut variant of OHG klawa < \*k/aw6- (hardly \*k/ewo-). Indeed, there are traces of such ablaut in the pertaining verbal formations. OE clawian, OHG klawen w.v. 'to scratch' can simply be derived from a weak stative verb \*klawen-, but ON k/Q, pret kltioa vs. k/6, Far. k/Qa, pret klciaoi, kleroi, ptc. kligid and Elfd. klti, pret. kluo, ptc. k/aio (the latter with the meaning 'to toil') in combination with OSw. kleya, Da. kle, MDu. c/ooien, clouwen < \*k/aujan- strongly support the (parallel) occurrence of an originally strong verb \*klaujan- < \*glou-ie-.

\*kleuban- s.v. 'to cleave, split' - ON kljufa s.v. 'id.', Far. klugva s.v. 'id.', Elfd. kliuova s.v. 'id.', OE cleofan s.v. 'id.', E to cleave, OS klioban s.v. 'id.', Du. klieven w.v. 'id.', OHG klioban s.v. 'id.', G klieben s./w.v. 'id.' ==? \*g/eubh·e(EUR) - Identical to Lat. glii.b6, -ere 'to strip the bark from a tree, to peel' < \*gleubh-e-; also cf. Gr. yA.u<pw 'to carve, cut out, engrave' < \*glubh-e- and perhaps also OCS g/9bok-b, Ru. glub6kij adj. 'deep' < \*glu-m-bh-o-. A verb with a European distribution. A different formation is ON klyfia w.v. 'to split' < \*klubjan- < \*glubh-ie-. Also cf. \*klaubjan-.
\*klewan- m. 'lump, ball' - ON k/e, obl. klja m. 'stone to keep the warp straight in the upright loom', Far. klfggja-steinur m. 'id.', Nw. klja m. 'id.' ʬ

\*gleuh₂-on- (EUR) - OCS ib/y, iely f. 'ulcer, sore', Ru. ielvak m. 'tumor', Latv. dz՚/va f. 'swelling on the skin' < \*gelu-u(e)h₂-; Gr. yA.ouT6<; m. 'buttock' < \*glouh₂-to-. An n-stem potentially ablauting with OE clyne m. 'ball, lump' < \*kluni-. Perhaps, both formations can be unified into a single paradigm nom. \*klewo, gen. kluniz < PIE \*gMuH-on, gen. \*gluH-n-es. Further related to Skt. g/Qu- m. 'bump, tumor'? Within Germanic, cf. OHG kliuwi n. 'clew' < \*klewfn- and OHG kliuwa f. 'ball, clew', OE cleowe f. 'id.' < \*klewon-.

\*kliba- n. 'cliff - ON klif n. 'cliff, protruding isolated rock', Far. kliv f. 'steep place, cliff, rock', Elfd. kliv n. 'id.', MDu. clif n. 'cliff, steep, slope', OHG klep 'id.' (DRY).
