'to trudge through mud', however, seems more closely related to the zero-grade iterative \*supp/ban- 'to soak', q.v.). Also cf. Nw. dial. seven adj. 'wet, moist' < \*sajina- and Far. sevja, Nw. sevje f. 'sap' < \*safjon-.

\*sagja- 1 m. 'man, hero' - ON seggr m. 'id.', Far. poet. seggur m. 'id.', OE secg m. 'id.' ⇒ \*sokwH-i6- (IE) - Lat. socius m. 'companion, comrade' < \*sokwH-io-, Skt. sakhay- (nom. sakhii, dat. sakhye) m. 'friend, companion', Av. haxi- 'friend, companion' < \*sokwH-oi-. The word continues an original ablauting i-stem nom. \*sokwH-6i, gen. \*sokwH-i-os to the root \*sekw- 'to follow' (see \*sehwan- 'to see'). In Germanic, the labial element of the labiovelar was lost before the operation ofVerner's law.

\*sagja- 2 m. 'sedge' - OE secg m. 'id.', E sedge, EDu. segghe 'id.', Du. zegge c. 'id.' (IE?). Possibly related to \*sago- 'saw' (assuming that the original meaning was 'sharp grass', cf. OE secg f. 'sword'). For a different interpretation, see \*sahaza-.

\*sagjan- w.v. 'to say, recount' - ON segja w.v. 'id.', Far. siga w.v. 'id.', Elfd. saja w.v. 'id.', OE secgan w.v. 'id.', E to say, OFri. sedza, sidza w.v. 'id.', WFri. sizze w.v. 'id.', OS seggian w.v. 'id.', Du. zeggen w.v. 'id.', OHG sagen w.v. 'id.', G sagen w.v. 'id.' ( < \*sagen-) ⇒ \*sokw-eie- (EUR) - Identical to Lith. sakjti, Latv. sacit 'to say'; SCS soCiti 'to indicate', Ru. soCft' (socu) 'to look for, track down, summon', SCr. soCiti 'to establish the guilt of, reveal, find' < \*sokw-eie-; also cf. Gr. E:v(v)fow 'to say, recount, announce', Lat. lnsece 'to tell' (also cf. inquam < \*h₁en-skw-eh₂-m), Olr. seichid 'to say', OW, MW hebu 'to say', Lith. dial. sekti (sekit) 'to tell' < \*sekw-e-; ie-. A Germanic/Balto-Slavic intensive formation derived from the PIE root \*sekw- 'to recount'. The labialization of \*kw was lost in front of \*j when there was an o in the root (cf. \*hnaigjan- and \*sagja- 1). See also \*sagon-.

\*sago- f. 'saw' - ON spg f. 'id.', Far. sag f. 'id.', Elfd. saga f. 'id.' ( < \*sagon-), OE sagu f. 'id.', E saw, OS saga f. 'id.', MDu. sage f. 'id.', Du. zaag c. 'id.', OHG saga, sega f. 'id.', G Siige f. 'id.' ( < \*sego-) ⇒ \*sok-eh₂- (IE) - Mir. eiscid 'to cut off < \*h₁en-sek-; Lat. seco, -are 'to cut, sever' < \*sek-eh₂-ie-; OCS sesti 'to cut', Ru. sec' [seku) 'to cut to pieces, beat'; Alb. shat(e) m./f. 'hoe, mattock' < \*sek-teh₂-(?). Derived from the root PIE \*sek- 'to cut'. See also \*sahsa-.

\*sago(n)- f. 'recount' - ON saga f. 'story, account, history', Far. sfiJga f. 'id.', OE sagu f. 'statement, story', OFri. sege f. 'statement, testimony', OHG saga f. 'story, statement' (DRV). A formation related to \*sagjan- (q.v.). The resemblance to Lith. saka, Latv. saka f. 'tale, legend' is likely to be coincidental, as the underlying proto-form \*sokw-eh₂- would have given PGm. \*\*sawo-.

\*sahaza- m. 'sedge' - OS saher-ahi n. 'sedgy place', OHG sahar m. 'id.', MHG saher m. 'id.' ⇒ \*sakas-o-(?) (NEUR/NIE) - Ir. seisc f. 'sedge, rushes', W hesg pl. 'id.' < \*sek{-)s(k)-i-(?); BRu. osoka f. 'sedge' < \*asak-eh₂-(?). Also cf. OS in seon 'in algis' ( < \*seha-?) and \*sagja- 2 'sedge'. The word is often connected to \*sago- 'saw' through a meaning 'sharp grass'. The Slavic and Celtic *comparanda* imply that the oldest meaning was simply 'sedge', however. Technically, the variation of Pre-Gm. \*sakas- (\*s6kos-?), Pre-Celt. \*sesk- (metathesized from \*sek-s-) and Pre-SI. \*asak- is highly reminiscent of alternations exhibited by other potentially non-IE words, such as \*ams/on'blackbird' and \*sturja/on- 'sturgeon' ( q.v.), especially in view of the occurrence of an a-prefix in osoka.

\*sahsa- n. 'knife' - ON sax n. 'short sword; scissors', Elfd. saks m. 'scissors', OE seax n. 'knife', OFri. sax n. 'knife; short sword, dagger', OS sahs n. 'knife', OHG sahs n. 'id.' ⇒ \*sok-so- (IE). A thematicized s-stem to the PIE root \*sek- 'to cut'. It also occurs as the second element of the compound \*mati-sahsa- - \*mati-zahsa- 'knife', for which see \*mati- 'food'. Further cf. \*sago-, \*sagja- 2 and \*sahaza-.

\*saida- m. 'magic, charm' - ON seior m. 'charm, spell' ⇒ \*soit-o- (EUR) - Identical to Lith. saftas, seftas m. 'magic' and MW hud 'id.'; also cf. OCo. hudo/ 'magus', W hudol 'charming, illusory'. A European o-stem derived from the root found in ON sfoa s.v. 'to work charms' < \*sipan-. With a different ablaut, cf. OE -siden f. 'magic' < \*sidno-.
\*saikjan- w.v. 'to make drip' - MDu. seiken w.v. 'to piss; to filter', Du. zeiken w.v. 'to piss', OHG seihhen w.v. 'id.', G seichen w.v. 'id.' (IE).

\*saila/o- n./f. 'rope' - ON seil f. 'id.', Far. seil f. 'band, cow harness, scarf, OE siil m./f. 'rope, tether', OFri. wind-se/ n. 'rope', OS se/ n. 'id.', Du. zeel n. 'id.', G Seil n. 'rope, noose' ⇒ \*sh₂oi-lo- (IE) - Skt. syati 'to bind' < \*sh₂i-e-; Lith. sieti (sieju), Latv. sfeti 'to bind, link' < \*sh₂oi-. PGm. \*saila-, whence Go. in-sailjan w.v. 'to rope up', continues an /-derivation to the PIE root \*sh₂ei- 'to bind', cf. ON se/i, sili, Elfd. si/i, OHG silo, G Siele m. 'strap; harness' < \*silan-. This root developed from an archaic i-present, cf. Hitt. isbai - isbijanzi 'to bind, to wrap; to obligate with, to impose upon' < 3sg. \*sh₂-6i-ti, 3pl. \*sh₂-i-enti (Kloekhorst 2008: 391). For the semantic shift from 'tie' to 'obligation', cf. \*sidu- 'custom'.

\*saima- m. 'virgin honey' - ON seimr m. 'honeycomb', OS sem m. '(fresh) honey', ODu. seym m. 'id.', Du. zeem c. 'id.', OHG seim m. 'id.', G Honig-seim m. 'id.' ⇒ \*soikw-mo-(?) (IE?) - W hufen m. 'top of any liquid; cream' < \*soi(kw)-meno-. A word without a certain etymology. There are no objections against deriving it from the IE root \*seikw- 'to drip', however, for which see \*slhwan-. This link would imply a Pre-Gm. formation \*soikw-m6- that through \*saigma- may have given \*saima-.

\*saipwon- f. 'soap' - OE siipe f. 'id.', E soap, OFri. sepe f. 'id.', Du. zeep c. 'id.', OHG seiffa, seipfa f. 'id.', G Selfe, Swab., Alem. seipfe f. 'id.' (GM). An etymologically debated word with an exclusively West Germanic attestation (ON sapa is an Old English loanword). Two formations have been reconstructed for Proto-Germanic: 1) a form \*saipjiin- with aj-suffix so as to explain the gemination OHG seipfa, Swi. seipfe; and 2) a form \*saipiin- in order to explain the absence of umlaut in OE siipe. Superficially, the form \*saipjiin- is corroborated by Fi. saippio, but I would prefer to unify both OHG seipfa and OE siipe into a single proto-form \*saipwiin-. This form can explain both the lack of umlaut in Old English and the gemination in High German, and even has a reminiscence in Fi. saippua (note that the io-suffix of saippio can be of Finnish origin, Koivulehto 1995: 99). The derivation of the word is unknown, but the parallel occurrence of the semantically more primitive OE siip(e) 'amber, resin' (also cf. OE stiir-sæp n. 'resin' < \*saipja-) implies a Germanic origin. The suggested link with e.g. Sw. dial. sipa w.v. 'to flow slowly' (cf. Pokorny IEW: 894) is uncertain, however. Lat sapo m. 'soap' was borrowed from Germanic.

\*saiwalo- f. 'soul' - Go. saiwala f. 'id.', OE sawol, saw/ m. 'id.', E soul, OFri. sele f. 'id.', OS seola, siola f. 'id.', OHG seula, sela f. 'id.', G Seele f. 'id.' (GM). A West Germanic word considered to be without etymology. According to Schrijver 1997, it is of non-Indo-European origin. The word is formally and semantically close to Lith. siela f. 'soul, heart', OCS sila f. 'strength, force, miracle', Ru. sf/a f. 'strength, force', SCr. sl'/a f. 'id.' < \*seh₁i-l-eh₂-, but even if related, Germanic still has a different suffix. Note that ON sal and OSw. sire/ were borrowed from OE saw/ and OS seola respectively.

\*saiwi- m. 'sea' - Go. saiws m. 'lake, swamp', ON sær, gen. sj6ar m. 'sea', Ice!. sj6r m. 'id.', Far. sj6gvur m. 'id.', Elfd. sju m. 'id.', OE sci! m. 'id.', E sea, OFri. se m. 'id.', OS seu, seo m. 'id.', Du. zee c. 'id.', OHG se(o/u), gen. sewes m. 'id.', G See m. 'lake', f. 'sea' ⇒ \*soikw-f- (DRV). An i-stem (NWGm. a-stem) probably derived from the root of \*sfhwan(q.v.). Semantically parallel is the derivation of OHG gi-sig m./n. 'swamp, pool' < \*ga-siga- from \*sigan-, for which again see \*sihwan-.

\*sakan- s.v. 'to charge' - Go. sakan s.v. 'to dispute, reprimand', OS sakan s.v. 'to rebuke', MDu. mis-saken w./s.v. 'to deny, reject', OHG sahhan s.v. 'to argue, reprimand' ⇒ \*sh₂9-e- (IE) - Olr. saigid 'to try to reach; to claim' < \*sh₂9-ie-; MW haeddu 'to attempt' < \*sag-i-eh₂-ie-; Hitt. sagii.i- c. 'omen' < \*seh₂9-oi- (whence sakije/azi 'to give a sign, reveal'; Lat sagus adj. 'prophetic, prescient' < \*seh₂9-o-. A strong verb related to \*sako- and \*sokjan- (q.v.). Its original meaning was probably 'to charge', cf. Go. gasahts f. 'rebuke' < \*ga-sahti- and Go. un-sahtaba adv. 'without controversy' < \*sahta-). This meaning later changed into 'to make a case', cf. the semantically parallel Ru. soCit' (socu) 'to look for, track down, summon', SCr. soCiti 'to establish the guilt of, reveal, find' under \*sagjan-. On a deeper level, the etymological links with Lat. sagfre 'to perceive' and Hitt. sii.gai- 'omen' imply a primary sense 'to read a sign or track', which through 'to follow, pursue' may have given the Germanic meaning.

\*sakk/gon- w.v. 'to drop, sag' - ME saggen 'id.', E to sag, Du. zakken w.v. 'id.', G sacken w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*sok-neh₂- (GM). An o-grade iterative, 3sg. \*sakko/Ji, 3pl. \*sagunanþi < \*sok-neh₂-ti, \*sok-nh₂-enti, possibly related to \*sinkwan- (q.v.). A zero-grade variant \*sukkon- is supported by OHG suckon (probably not sucken < \*sukkjan-, pace Riecke 1996: 190). No further etymology. The potentially related Nw. sagga w.v. 'to walk slowly' together with the dialectal variants saga and sugga is formally close, but the semantics as well as the probable connection with Du. sukkelen 'to go slowly or clumsily' < \*sukkon- imply that this verb has a different origin.

\*sako- f. 'case, lawsuit' - ON spk f. 'lawsuit, accusation, trial', OE sacu f. 'strife, contention, dispute', E sake, OFri. seke f. 'case', OS saka f. 'affair, lawsuit', Du. zaak c. 'affair, lawsuit', OHG sahha f. 'case, guilt', G Sache f. 'affair, matter' (DRV). Also cf. ON saka w.v. 'to fight, do harm', Far. saka w.v. 'to hurt; to accuse' < \*sak6jan-. Derived from the strong verb \*sakan- ( q.v.).

\*salbo- f. 'ointment' - Go. salba f. 'id.', OE sea/f(e) f. 'id.', E salve, OS sa/ba f. 'id.', Du. zalf c. 'id.', OHG sa/ba f. 'id.', G Sa/be f. 'id.' ⇒ \*so/p-eh₂- (IE) - ToA $ti./yp, ToB $afype m. '(sesame) oil; salve, ointment', Gr. Hsch. £A.ro<; 'oil, fat', Alb. 9ja/pe n.jm. 'butter' < \*selp-o(s)-; also cf. Skt sarpf$- n. 'melted butter, melting-butter, lard' (with secondary f$·suffix); Skt S[pra- adj. 'greasy, smooth, sleek' < \*slp-ro-. Also cf. Go. salbon, OE sealfian, OS saloon, Du. za/ven, OHG salb6n, G salben w.v. 'to anoint' < \*sa/b6jan-.

\*salih6n- f. 'sallow, willow' - ON selja f. 'id.', Sw. sii/9 c. 'id.', OE sea/h f. 'id.', E sallow, OHG salaha f. 'id.', G Sal-weide 'id.' ⇒ \*salik- (EUR) - Lat. salix f. 'willow', Olr. sail, gen. sailech f. 'id.' < \*salik-; Myc. e-ri-ka, Gr. Arc. EALKTI 'id.' < \*selik-(?). A European word. If the Mycenaean word belongs here, it would imply a non-IE interchange of \*salik- vs. \*selik-.

\*saliz- n. 'hall, house' - ON salr m. 'id.', OE sࢰ/, salor n. 'hall', OS seli m. 'hall, building', Du. zaa/ c. 'hall', OHG sa/ n. 'hall, room', G Saal m. 'id.' ⇒ \*so/(HJ-es(NEUR) - Identical to OCS selo n. 'field, abode, tabernacle, village', Ru. se/6 n. 'village', SCr. se/o n. 'village, country'. ON salr, ace.pl. sa/ir and OS se/i imply an i-stem \*sali-, OE salor a formation \*sa/aza-, but both of these variants developed secondarily from the neuter s-stem \*salaz, gen. \*salizaz < \*so/(H)-os, \*so/(H)-es-os. Also cf. Go. salipwos f.pl. 'dwelling', OE selp, OS selitha, OHG salida, se/ida f. 'dwelling' < \*salipw6- and the conspicuously similar OCS se/itva f. 'dwelling'.

\*saljan- w.v. 'to offer, sell' - Go. saljan w.v. 'to lodge', ON selja w.v. 'to hand over; to sell', Far. selja w.v. 'to sell; to let go of; to surrender', Elfd. se/a w.v. 'to sell', OE sellan s.v. 'to give', E to sell, OFri. sella w.v. 'to hand over; to pay; to sell', OS 9i-se/lian w.v. 'give, sell', OHG sellan w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*so/h₁-eie- (EUR) - Gr. tAeTv aor. 'to take, seize', med. 'to take for oneself, pick out, choose' < \*selh₁- (also cf. Gr. EAwp n. 'booty' < \*selh₁-6r); OCS s'blati 'to send' < \*slh₁-eh₂-; Olr. selb f. 'possession', W helw, elw m. < \*selhrueh₂-; Olr. ad-roilli 'to earn', MW dyr/lid 'id.' < \*tu-ari-sliye- < \*-slhrie-. A causative to the PIE root \*selh₁- 'to take'. The primary meaning of the verb was 'to let take', whence 'to hand over'. Also note ON sala, OE sa/u f.

\*salta- n. 'salt' - Go. salt n. 'id.', ON salt n. 'id.', Far. salt n. 'id.', Elfd. salt n. 'id.', OE sea/t n. 'id.', E salt, OFri. salt n. 'id.', OS salt n. 'id.', Du. zout n. 'id.', OHG salz n. 'id.', G Salz n. 'id.' (DRY) - Lith. saldus adj. 'sweet', OCS slad'bko, Ru. dial. so/6dkij adj. 'id.' < \*sh₂e/-d-u-; ToA sale, ToB salyiye f. 'salt', Arm. af 'salt', Gr. &:As m. 'id.', Lat. sa/ m. 'id.', Olr. salann n. 'id.', W ha/en m. 'id.', Latv. sals f. 'id.', OPru. sa/ 'id.', OCS sol&, Ru. sol' f. 'id.'; Skt. sarira- n. 'sea, ocean'. A neuter collective noun derived from the strong verb \*saltan- (q.v.). The IE word for 'salt' was inflected as an ablauting /-stem nom. \*seh₂-/, gen. \*sh₂-/-6s. The dental suffix \*d, which is found in Germanic, Balto-Slavic and Italic, is parallel to that of PIE \*sueh₂d-u- (see \*swotu-).

\*saltan- s.v. 'to salt (down), pickle' - Go. sa/tan s.v. 'id.', ON salta w.v. 'id.', Elfd. so/ta w.v. 'id.', OE sea/ten ptc. 'id.', MDu. souten s./w.v. 'id.', Du. ge-zouten adj. 'pickled', OHG sa/zan s.v. 'to pickle', G salzen s./w.v. 'id.' (IE) - Lat. sallere (ptc. salsum < \*sh₂/d-to-) 'to salt' < \*sh₂/d-e-; Olr. sail/id 'id.' < \*sh₂/d-ie-; an extension to the stem \*sh₂e/-, cf. Skt. sarira- n. 'sea, ocean' < \*sh₂e/-; ToA sale, ToB salyiye f. 'salt' < \*sh₂e/-h₁en-; Arm. saf 'id.' < \*sh₂(e)l-i-; Gr. aA.s, W.6s m. 'id.', Lat. sii.l m. 'id.', OCS sol& f. 'id.' < \*sh₂e/-; Olr. salann 'id.' < \*sh₂(e)/-on-; OCS s/an'b 'salted' < \*sh₂el-no-. A strong verb directly akin to Lat. sal/ere. See also \*salta- and \*sultjo(q.v.).

\*salwa- adj. 'yellowish brown' - ON splr adj. 'yellow, pale', OE sa/o adj. 'dusky, dark', E sallow, MDu. sa/u, saluwe adj. 'pale yellow, dirty', OHG sa/o adj. 'dark, black, dirty' ⇒ \*so/H-uo- (NEUR) - Ru. so/6vyj adj. 'light bay' (also cf. CS s/avo-ocije n. 'state of having green eyes, glaucitas') < \*so/H-uo-. A Germanic-Slavic isogloss.

\*sama(n)- adj. 'the same' - Go. sama adj. 'id.', ON samr, sami adj. 'id.', Far. samur, sami adj. 'id.', OHG samo adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*somH-o- (IE) - Identical to Skt. sama- adj. 'equal, same', Av. hama- adj. 'same', OP hama- adj. 'one and the same', NP ham adj. 'same', Gr. 6μ6s adj. 'common, same'. See also \*suma-.

\*samda- m. 'sand' - ON sandr m. 'id.', Far. sandur m. 'id.', Elfd. sand m. 'id.', OE sand m. 'id.', E sand, OFri. san(d), saun(d) m. 'id.', OS sand m. 'id.', Du. zand n. 'id.', OHG sant m./n. 'id.', G Sand m. 'id.' ⇒ \*sam-ndh- (NIE) - Gr. l\laμμos f., lltaμa-Bos, &μa-Bos f. 'sand' < \*sam-ndh-o-; Lat. sabulum n. 'coarse sand, gravel' < \*sadh-/o-.

\*sampu- adj. 'soft' - OE sefte adj., softe adv. 'id.', E soft, OS safto adv. 'id.', Du. zacht adj. 'id.', OHG samfti, semfti adj., samfto adv. 'id.', G sanft adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*s6m-tu- (IE) - Skt. santya- adj. 'belonging together' < \*som-tio-. An adjective possibly derived from the PIE root sem- 'together'. In view of the umlaut alternation of OE sefte versus adverbial softe, the apparent ja-stem adjective must have developed from an older u-stem at a relatively late stage, i.e. somewhere in West Germanic. This disfavors the direct equasion of OHG samfti and OE sefte with Skt. santya-, as proposed by Hoffmann 1968). See also \*sama- < \*somH-o-.

\*sandjan- w.v. 'to cause to go, send' - Go. sandjan w.v. 'id.', ON senda w.v. 'id.', Far. senda w.v. 'id.', OE sendan w.v. 'id.', E to send, OFri. senda w.v. 'id.', OS sendian w.v. 'id.', Du. zenden w.v. 'id.', OHG sentan w.v. 'id.', G senden w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*sont-eie- (EUR). The causative to \*sinnan- 'to go, travel' (q.v.).

\*sangwa- m. 'singing; song' - Go. saggws m. 'id.', ON spngr m. 'id.', Far. songur m. 'id.', Elfd. saungg m. 'id.', OE sang m. 'id.', E song, OFri. song m. 'id.', OS sang m. 'id.', Du. zang c. 'id.', OHG sang m./n. 'id.', G Sang m. 'id.' ⇒ \*songwh_o- (ORV). An a-grade thematic noun derived from the strong verb \*singwan- ( q.v.).

\*sankwjan- w.v. 'to cause to sink' - Go. sagqjan w.v. 'id.', ON sekkva w.v. 'id.', Far. sekkja w.v. 'id.', Elfd. saintja w.v. 'id.', OE sencan w.v. 'to sink, plunge, immerse', E to sink, OS bi-senkian w.v. 'to cause to sink', OHG senkan w.v. 'id.', G senken w.v. 'id.' (IE). The causative to \*sinkwan- (q.v.).

\*sanpa- adj. 'true' - ON sat!Jr, sannr adj. 'true; meet and proper; guilty', Far. sannur adj. 'true; right', Elfd. sann adj. 'id.', OE sot!J adj. 'id.', OS soth adj. 'id.', OHG sand adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₁s-6nt-o- (IE) - Hitt. asant- 'true, real', Skt. sant- 'being, real, good', Lat. sons, sontis adj. 'guilty'. An adjective continuing the participle of the IE verb 'to be', viz. \*his-ant-. Jfwe accept Lat iens, gen. euntis 'going' < \*h₁i-{e)nt-s, \*h₁i-ont-es as a model, we may reconstruct the original paradigm of this participle as \*h₁s-(e)nt-s, gen. \*h₁s-ont-(e/o)s. Germanic generalized the a-grade at an early stage, however (cf. Go. -ands). The original meaning 'being' developed into 'true' in Germanic, probably through an intermediate sense 'actual'. The meaning 'guilty' as found in Old Norse sannr at spk is secondary, too, but in view of Lat sons must have developed at an early stage, possibly in a shared IE legal context. Cf. ON sanna w.v. 'to affirm, prove', OE sot!Jian w.v. 'to prove', OFri. sanna, senna w.v. 'to dispute' < \*sanpojan-. See also \*sunja-.

\*sarwa- m. 'armor, equipment' - Go. sarwa n. 'id.', OE searu, searo n. 'device, design, art', OHG saro n. 'armor' (GM?). No certain etymology. The word has been compared to Gr. e'lpw 'to join' (cf. opμoc; 'necklace') and Lat sero 'to arrange, join' < \*ser- (Pokorny JEW: 911), but it seems unsatisfactory to disconnect it from Lith. sarvas, OPru. sarwis m. 'armor'. The correspondence of PGm. \*s with Lith. s is irregular, however, and may point to independent borrowing from an unknown third language. Cf. also ON s;Jrvi, gi-sarawi, MHG ge-serwe n. 'equipment' < \*(ga-)sarwja- and OE sierwan, OFri. sera, sara, MHG serwen w.v. 'to arm, equip' < \*sarwjan-.

\*satjan- w.v. 'to put' - Go. satjan w.v. 'id.', ON setja w.v. 'id.', Far. seta w.v. 'id.', Elfd. settja s.v. 'id.', OE settan w.v. 'id.', E to set, OFri. setta w.v. 'id.', OS settian w.v. 'id.', Du. zetten w.v. 'id.', OHG sezzen w.v. 'id.', G setzen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*sod-eie- (IE) - YAv. ni-sii8aiiat 3sg.inj.caus. 'to remove', OP niya-siidayam lsg.impf.caus. 'to set down; to establish', OCS saditi, Ru. sadft' (saiu), SCr. saditi 'to plant, seat' < \*sod-eie-. The causative to \*set(j)an- (q.v.).

\*sauma- m. 'seam' - ON saumr m. 'id.', Far. seymur m. 'id.', Elfd. som m. 'id.', OE seam m. 'id.', E seam, OFri. siim m. 'id.', MDu. soom m. 'id.', Du. zoom c. 'id.', OHG soum m. 'id.', G Saum m. 'id.' ⇒ \*siouH-mo- (DRV) - Close to Skt syiiman- n. 'band, thong, bridle', Gr. uμiJv, -tvo<; m. 'film, membrane, sinew' < \*siuH-men-. A mo-stem created to the root of \*siujan- (q.v.): apparently, the Pre-Gm. form \*siouH-mo- lost its i. for which see \*seuda-. Also cf. ON seyma, Far. soyma, Elfd. soma, G siiumen 'to sow, seam' < \*saumjan-.

\*saupa- m. 'broth' - Nw. saud m. 'cooking', OE seao m. 'well', OFri. siith m. 'id.', MDu. soot m. 'well; boiling water', MHG sot m. 'boiling, seething; well; heartburn' ⇒ \*s6ut-o- (DRV). Derived from \*seupan-. Cf. with the same vowel Go. saups m. 'sacrifice' < \*saupi- and ON sauor, Far. seyour, Elfd. soo m. 'sheep' < \*saupa-.

\*sauza- adj. 'dry' - Nw. sflJyr adj. 'id.', OE sear adj. 'id.', E sear, MDu. soar adj. 'id.', Du. zoor adj. 'id.' ⇒ \* h₂sous-6- (IE) - Gr. al'.ioc; adj. 'dry', Lith. sailsas, Latv. sauss, OPru. sausan adj. (acc.) 'dry', OCS suxo, Ru. sux6j, SCr. silh adj. 'id.' < \*h₂sous-o- (Lubotsky 1985); Skt. su?yati 'to be dry' < \*h₂sus-ie-. Also cf. OHG soren w.v. 'to be dry' < \*sauzen-.

\*sawwa- n. 'juice' - Icel. soggur m. 'moisture', OE seaw m. 'juice, moisture', ME seu 'sap, juice, broth', OHG sou n. 'juice' ⇒ \*sou-6- (IE) - Skt. sava- m. 'juice'; Skt. sun6ti 'to press (Soma)', YAv. hunuta 3sg.pres.inj.med. 'to press, braise' < \*su-neu-; Lith. sula f. 'birch sap' < \*su-1-eh₂-; Mir. suth 'milk' < \*su-to-. An old a-grade thematicization to the PIE root \*seu-. The form is of importance to the debate on the conditioning of Holtzmann's law. It is often assumed that intervocalic \*j and \*y were lengthened by the assimilation of a following laryngeal (Lehmann 1965). PIE \*sou-6- never had a laryngeal, however, and thus implies that we must revert to the older formulation of Holtzmann's law as purely pretonic gemination. Note that lcel., Far. saggi m. 'moisture', apparently continuing \*saggan-, is a secondary formation created on the basis of ON \*spggr. Also cf. lcel. soggur, Nw. sflJgg adj. 'wet, damp' < \* , sou-a-.

\*sean- s.v. 'to sow' - Go. saian s.v. 'id.', ON sci pret. sera 'id.', Far. scia w.v. 'id.', OE siiwan s.v. 'id.', E to sow, OFri. e-sen ptc. 'id.', OS siiian w.v. 'id.', Du. zaaien w.v. 'id.', OHG siien w.v. 'id.', G siien w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*seh₁-e- (IE) - Lat. sero, -ere 'to plant, sow seed' < \*si-sh₁-e-; Lith. sJti (sJju), Latv. set 'to sow', OCS seti (sejp) 'id.' < \*seh₁(-ie-); Hitt. siii - sijanzi 'to prick, sting; to shoot, throw', med. 'to squirt, spurt, flow' < \*shr6i-ei, \*sh₁-i-enti (Kloekhorst 2008: 694-5). A verb with an originally reduplicated preterite (i.e. PIE perfect), cf. Go. saiso, ON sera, from PGm. 3sg. \*se-soe, 3pl. \*se-sunp < PIE \*se-sohre, \*se-sh rnt. Regarding the form of the verb, there is no trace whatsoever of a suffix \*-ie- in Germanic: the hiatus in \*sean- was simply filled up by either j (cf. G siien with umlaut) or w (E to sow) in the different daughter languages. Also note that the Go. digraph <ai> stands for [E], which arose from \*e by regular lowering before hiatus, and not for [ai], as is often assumed. See also \*seda-, \*sedi-, \*seman- and \*siled-.

\*sebjo- f. 'kinship' - Go. sibja f. 'id.', ON si[f. 'id.', OE sib f. 'id.', OFri. sibbe f. 'id.', OS sibbia f. 'id.', OHG sippa f. 'id.', G Sippe f. 'id.' ⇒ \*sebh-ieh₂- (IE). A formation likely to have arisen from a reflexive pronominal form, cf. OCS sebe, Lat. sibf dat. 'to oneself < \*se-bhe/oi. Less attractive are the comparisons with Ru. os6ba, SCr. osoba f. 'person' < \*-sobh-eh₂-(?) and the almost certainly unrelated Skt. sabh&- f. 'community' < \*sm-bheh₂- or SCS sebrb m. 'yeoman', Ru. sjabr m. 'neighbor' < \*sm-bhh₂-ro- (cf. Pokorny IEW: 882-4). Within Germanic, further cf. ON sift, OS sibbio, OFri. sibba m. 'relative' < \*sebjan-, Go. un-sibjis adj. 'lawless', OE sib, OFri. sib(be}, OHG sippi adj. 'related' < \*sebja- and Go. ga-sibjon, OE ge-sibbian w.v. 'to conciliate' < \*ga-sibj6jan-.

\*sebun- num. 'seven' - Go. sibun num. 'id.', ON sjau num. 'id.', Far. sjey num. 'id.', Elfd. sju num. 'id.', OE seofon, syfon num. 'id.', E seven, OFri. sigun num. 'id.', OS sibun, sibon num. 'id.', Du. zeven num. 'id.', OHG sibun num. 'id.', G sieben num. 'id.' ⇒ \*septm (IE) - Hitt. siptamija- n. 'liquid from seven substances' < \*s(e)ptm-io-; Skt. sapta 'seven', Av. hapta- 'id.', Gr. bmx 'id.'; Lat. septem 'id.', Olr. secht, W saith 'id.', Lith. septyni 'id.', OCS sedmb 'id.' < \*sept-m. As opposed to the other IE languages, which have \*sept-m, the Germanic form points to \*sep-m without a t. It has been argued that the word therefore must be a late loanword from a para-Semitic language (Vennemann 1995: 112ff), cf. Akk. sebu(m), f. sebettum '7', but it is more likely that the dental was lost in the ordinal \*sebunp/dan- < \*septm-tH6-, where it may have been dissimilated against the t of the suffix. This is even more likely in view of the fact that the ordinals in IE had a zero grade of the root, which would make the expected form \*sptm-tH6- especially liable to dissimilation. Also note that the final \*-n of \*sebun must be due to influence from the ordinal as well, as it would normally have been dropped in this position.

\*sebunfJ/dan- num. 'seventh' - Go. sibunda num. 'id.', ON sj{a)undi num. 'id.', Far. sjeyndi num. 'id.', Elfd. sjuund num. 'id.', OE seofooa num. 'id.', E seventh, OFri. sigunda num. 'id.' (with g after nigunda 'ninth'), OS sibondo, sibotho num. 'id.', Du. zevende num. 'id.', OHG sibunto, sibanto num. 'id.', G siebente num. 'id.' ⇒ \*septm-tH6- (IE) - Skt. saptatha-, Av. haptai9a-, Lith. septifl.tas 'seventh' < \*septm-tHo-. The ordinal numeral to \*sebun 'seven' (q.v.).

\*seda- n. 'seed' - ON sao n. 'id.', Far. sao n. 'id.', Elfd. saoer m.pl. 'bran', OE sci!d n. 'id.', E seed, OFri. sed n. 'id.', OS s<id n. 'id.', Du. zaad n. 'id.' ⇒ \*seh₁-t6- (DRV). A to-derivative from the root of\*sean- (q.v.).

\*sedi- f. 'seed' - Go. mana-seps f. 'mankind', OHG sat f. 'seed; field', G Saat f. 'seed; sowing' ⇒ \*seh₁-tf- (DRY) - Lat satus m. 'sowing, planting' < \*shi-tu-. A ti-stem derived from the root of\*sean- (q.v.).

\*sedla- n. 'sieve, riddle' - ON said n. 'id.', Elfd. said[ er) m. 'id.' ⇒ \*sehi-t/6- (IE) - Formally close to Olr. sfthal f. 'vase', MW hid/ f. 'filter, strainer, sieve' < \*sh₁-i-tleh₂-; also cf. Lith. sfetas, Latv. siets m. 'sieve', Ru. sfta, SCr. sl'ta n. 'id.' < \*seh₁-i-ta-; Lith. sij6ti (sij6ja), Latv. sijat 'to sift' < \*shi-i-eh₂-ie-; OCS seti, (sejp ); sejati, SCr. sljati 'id.' < \*seh₁-i-eh₂-; Alb. shash 'to sift, riddle' < \*seh₁+ / \*shi-ei-. An instrumental noun to the PIE root \*sehi- 'to sift'. This verbal base is otherwise exclusively found with an i-suffix, which points to the pre-existence of an PIE i-present 3sg. \*shi-ei-ti, 3pl. \*sh₁-i-enti.

\*sefan- m. 'mind' - ON sefi m. 'mind, affection', OE sefa m. 'understanding, mind, heart', OS mod-seba m. 'spirit, soul, heart' ⇒ \*sep-an- (DRY). An n-stem presumably derived from the strong verb \*sajjan- (q.v.).

\*segiz- n. 'victory' - Go. sigis n. 'id.', ON sigr m. 'id.', Far. sigur m. 'id.', OSw. sigher m. 'id.', Sw. seger c. 'id.', OE sigar m. 'id.', OFri. sige, sige m. 'id.', OS sigi-drohtin 'God', Du. zege c. 'id.', OHG sigi, sigu m. 'id.', G Sieg m. 'id.' ⇒ \*segh-es- (IE) - Identical to Skt. s<ihas-, Av. hazah- n. 'violence, power', Olr. sed, seg m. 'strength, vigor'. All attested forms directly or indirectly continue the old IE neuter s- stem \*segh-es-. This formation was derived from the verb \*segh-e- 'to overpower, possess', cf. Skt. s<ihate 'to overpower', Gr. £xw 'to have, hold' (also cf. the reduplicated Gr. \'.axw 'to hold' < \*si-sgh-e- and the intensive 6xE:w 'id.' < \*sagh-eie-). Within Germanic itself, the noun gave rise to ON sigra, OE sigarian, OHG ubar-sigiron w.v. 'to be victorious' < \*segizojan-.

\*segja- n. 'hard skin' - ON sigg n. 'hard skin', Far. sigg n. 'hard, cartilaginous whale blubber' ⇒ \*sek-i6- (WEUR) - Mir. seiche f. 'skin, hide' < \*sek-ieh₂-. A Germanic-Celtic isogloss possibly derived from the PIE root \*sek- 'to cut', for which see \*sago-.

\*segla- n. 'sail, canvas' - ON segl n. 'id.', Far. segl n. 'id.', Elfd. sigel n. 'id.', OE seg[e)l m. 'id.', E sail, OS segal n. 'id.' (also cf. segela f. 'curtain' < \*seglo-), Du. zeil n. 'id.', OHG segal, segil m. 'id.', G Segel n. 'id.' (WEUR) - Ir. seal m. 'sail, piece of cloth, (bed-)covering, tent', W hwyl f. 'sail' < \*se/ig(hl-/a-. If Olr. seal was not adopted from Germanic, as is often assumed, the etymon represents a Germanic-Celtic isogloss (cf. Schrijver 1995: 357; Thier 2011: 189-190). No certain etymology. By starting from a meaning 'cut

\*sehs num. 'six' - Go. saihs num. 'id.', ON sex num. 'id.', Far. seks num. 'id.', Elfd. sjiiks num. 'id.', OE siex, six num. 'id.', E six, OFri. sex num. 'id.', OS sehs num. 'id.', Du. zes num. 'id.', OHG sehs num. 'id.', G sechs num. 'id.' ⇒ \*sel{-s (IE) - Skt. $G$-, YAv. xsuuas, NP Sas, Oss. ll!XSæ!Z I æ!XSæ!Z num. 'six' < \*sue/(s; Arm. vec' num. 'id.' < \*ueJ<s; Gr. £?, Dor. fE?, MW chwech num. 'id.' < \*sue/(s, Lat. sex num. 'id.' < \*seKs. The PIE numeral 'six'. The oldest form must have been \*ue/(-s in view of the OPru. ordinal uschts < \*u/(-to- (see \*sehtan-). The initial s was probably adopted from PIE \*septm 'seven', for which see \*sebun.

\*sehtan- num. 'sixth' - Go. saihsta num. 'id.', ON setti num. 'id.', Far. Sa?tti num. 'id.', Elfd. sjiikst num. 'id.', OE siexta num. 'id.', E sixth, OFri. sexta num. 'id.', OS sehsto num. 'id.', Du. zesde num. 'id.', OHG sehto, sehsto num. 'id.', G sechste num. 'id.' ⇒ \*seK-tHo- (IE) - Skt. $U$thti- num. 'sixth', YAv. xstuuaadj. 'id.' < \*su(e}K-tH-uo-; OPru. uschts, wuschts num. 'id.' < \*u/(-tHo-; Lat. sextus num. 'id.' < \*seKs-tHo-. Most Germanic languages, including Gothic, have a form continuing a proto-form \*sehstan-. This form replaced the more archaic \*sehtan-, as evinced by ON setti and OHG sehto, under the influence of the cardinal numeral \*sehs. In PIE, the oldest form was \*u/(-to- (without both initial and root-final s), cf. OPru. uschts. It follows that \*s(u)eK-tHo- arose due to interference with \*sept-m 'seven' (see PGm. \*sebun).

\*sehwan- s.v. 'to see' - Go. saifvan s.v. 'id.', ON sja s.v. 'id.', Far. sfggja s.v. 'id.', Elfd. sjq s.v. 'id.', OE seon s.v. 'id.', E to see, OFri. sia, sian, sien s.v. 'id.', WFri. sjen s.v. 'id.', OS sehan s.v. 'id.', Du. zien s.v. 'id.', OHG sehan s.v. 'id.', G sehen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*sekW-e- (IE) - Skt. sacate 'to follow', Gr. £roμm 'id.', Lat. sequor, sequi'id.', Olr. sechid 'id.' < \*sekw-e-. The Germanic verb corresponds to the PIE root \*sekw- 'to follow'; apparently, the meaning shifted from 'to follow' to 'to keep an eye on'. As to the Nordic forms, note Elfd. sjq, which preserves the nasalisation of the infinitive suffix due to the late West Norse developement \*siii > \*sja (nasal vowels in unstressed syllables were otherwise lost). Far. sfggja developed from
\*sehwi- adj. 'visible' - ON -sær adj. 'id.' (DRV). The regular vṛddhi-gerundive to \*sehwan- 'to see' ( q.v.).

\*selba(n)- pron. 'self - Go. silba pron. 'id.', ON sjalfr pron. 'id.', Far. sjalvur pron. 'id.', Elfd. siuov pron. 'id.', OE self. seo/f pron. 'id.', E self. OFri. se/fpron. 'id.', OS self pron. 'id.', Du. ze/f pron. 'id.', OHG selb pron. 'id.', G se/ber, selbst pron. 'id.' (EUR). A pronoun apparently identical to the Venetic sse/boi-sselboi 'to oneself < \*se/bh-o- (for the reduplication, cf. Lat. ipsipse). Its further derivation is unclear. The often mentioned connection with PIE \*s(u)e- 'self rࣾmains conjectural.

\*selha- m. 'seal' - ON selr m. 'id.', Far. selur m. 'id.', OSw. siæl m. 'id.', Sw. sjiil c. 'id.', OE seo/h m. 'id.', E seal, OS selah m. 'id.', EDu. see/, see/-hont m. 'id.', Du. zee-hond c. 'id.', OHG selah m. 'id.', MHG se/e m. 'id.' (GM). A Germanic word with no certain IE etymology. The link with Lith. se!kti 'to crawl' (Torp 1909: 436) is erroneous, as this verb corresponds to PGm. \*stelan- (q.v.). The \*h may nevertheless correspond to the PIE animal suffix \*-J<o-, for which see \*elha{n)- 'elk' and \*baruga- 'boar'.

\*seman- m. 'seed' - OHG samo m. 'seed, offspring', G Samen m. 'seed' ⇒ \*sehrmen- (IE) - Lat. semen n. 'seed, semen, offspring', Lith. semuo m. 'flaxseed, seed, sowing', OPru. semen 'seed', OCS semǻ, Ru. semja, SCr. sjeme n. 'id.' < \*seh₁-m(e/o)n-. A PIE neuter mn-stem created to the root of \*sean- (q.v.). The masculine gender of the Baltic and Germanic forms is secondary.

\*semepa/o - n./f. 'rush' - OS semith n. 'id.', MLG sem(e)de f. 'id.', OHG semida f. 'id.', MHG semede, sende, semt, sebede n./f. 'id.' ⇒ \*sem- (WEUR/NIE?) - Olr. simin, sibin{n), sibhean(d) f. 'rush, reed; corn-stalk' < \*sem-ino-(?). A formation containing the \*ep-suffix for groups of trees and plants, cf. Sw. ide n. 'ivy' < \*iwepja- under \*lwa-. The underlying root \*sem- is likely to be connected with Olr. simin(n), sibin{n), sibhean (the forms with -b{h)arose by nasal dissimilation of [s&aCfJ.J} to [sivaCnl]). Its relation to ON sef, Far. sev, Sw. siiv n. 'reed' is unclear, however: if this Nordic word is not a loan from Old Irish, it implies a proto-form \*seba- that together with the element \*sem- would result in an irregular, un-Indo-European root alternation \*seb- - \*sem- as in e.g. \*samda- 'sand' (q.v.). Also cf. the secondary collective OS semithahi, OHG semidahi, MHG semedehe n. 'reed bank' < \*semep-ahja-.

\*seniga- adj. 'senior' - Go. sineigs, superl. sinista adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*sen-o- (IE) - Skt. sana-, YAv. hana-, Gr. £voe;, Arm. hin, Lat. senior comp., Olr. sen adj. 'old', Latv. s՚ns adj. 'earlier, previous'. The PIE adjective was \*sen-o-. Go. sineigs (var. seneigs) has a secondary suffix \*-fga- which is not entirely identical, but nevertheless close to that of Lat. senex m. 'old man, aged person'.

\*senuwo- f. 'sinew' - ON sin f. 'id.', Far. sina f. 'sinew, tendon; penis', Elfd. sina f. 'tendon', OE seonu, sionu, sinu f. 'id.', E sinew, OFri. sine, sini f. 'id.', OS senewa, sinewa f. 'id.', Du. zenuw c. 'nerve', OHG senawa, sena f. 'tendon', G Sehne f. 'id.' ⇒ \*senhrueh₂- (IE) - ToB ?fiaura pl. 'sinew', Skt. sn&van- n. 'id.', YAv. snauuara.bazura- adj. 'with straps(?) made of sinews', Oss. nwar / nawa:!r 'sinew', Arm. neard 'id.', Gr. vrnpa f. 'string, sinew', Lat. nervus m. 'sinew, muscle, nerve' < \*sneh₁-ur, \*snh₁-uen-s. Whereas the other IE languages point to a heteroclitic \*snehi-ur, PGm. \*senuwo- can only be derived from \*senhrueh₂- with the full-grade before the n. This is likely to be an innovation that arose after the loss of the laryngeal in the original zero-grade genitive \*s1Jhrueh₂-s.

\*serpan- - \*serdan- s.v. 'to hit; to violate sexually' - ON seroa, streoa s.v. 'id.', OE seoroan s.v. 'id.', MLG serden s.v. 'id.', OHG sertan s.v. 'id.', MHG serten s.v. 'to have sex; to bother, vex; to hit; to entice' ⇒ \*sert-e- (IE) - Hitt. fortai - sartanzi\* 'to wipe, rub' < \*s6rt-ei, \*srt-enti. A verb that referred to sexual violation, including rape (cf. MHG du kiimpst mir nit in meinen schlitz - lasz mich ungesorten), adultery (OE ne sero pu oores monnes wif 'non moechaberis'), male-to-male sex (ON ef maor kallar mann ragan eoa strooinn eoa soroinn ... ) and sex with animals. The Germanic verb was linked to the Hitt. bi-conjugated root sart- by Melchert 2002, who reconstructed a root \*serdh- with an original meaning 'to beat' or 'to rub'. This meaning is indeed close to the archaic MHG meaning 'to hit', cf. mit swerten von einander serten, but a problem with the reconstruction \*serdh- is that OE seoroan with its o pointing to PGm. \*p must be explained away as a Norse loanword. I therefore propose to reconstruct the root as \*sert- rather than \*serdh-, not least in view of the otherwise problematic connection of W serth adj. 'obscene' < \*sert-o-. Within Germanic, also cf. G Rhnl. surzen w.v. 'to beat up' < \*surton- < \*srt-nehi-.

\*sessa- m. 'seat, bench' - ON sess m. 'seat (in a row boat)', Far. sessur m. 'id.', OE sess m. 'seat, bench' ⇒ \*sed-to- (IE). A PIE derivation from the root \*sed- 'to sit', for which cf. \*set(j)an-.
\*seti- adj. 'possible to sit on' - Go. anda-sets adj. 'odious', ON Sa:!tr adj. 'suitable for sitting on', OE and-sa:!te adj. 'odious' ⇒ \*sed-i- (ORV).

\*setlj)an- s.v. 'to sit' - Go. sitan s.v. 'id.', ON sitja s.v. 'id.', Far. sita s.v. 'id.', Elfd. sittja s.v. 'id.', OE sittan s.v. 'id.', E to sit, OFri. sitta s.v. 'id.', OS sittian s.v. 'id.', Du. zitten s.v. 'id.', OHG sizzen s.v. 'id.', G sitzen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*sed-ie- (IE) - Formally identical to Gr. eЋoμm 'to sit (down)', Arm. nstim 'to sit' < \*(ni-)sed-ie-; further cf. Gr. '(Ћw, Skt. sfdati, OAv. hlfosat 3sg.des. 'to sit' < \*si-sd-e-; Lat. sedeo, -ere 'to sit, be seated', Olr. saidid 'to sit', MCo. hedhi, MBret. hezaff 'to stop' < \*sed-e-; Lith. sedhi, Latv. sedet, OCS sedeti < \*sed-eh₁-. The original IE verb meaning 'to sit', for which also cf. \*satjan-, \*sadula-, \*sessa-, \*set/a-, \*seti- and \*nista-. The dialectal difference between \*setan(Go. sitan) and \*setjan- (ON sitja, OE sittan, OHG sizzen) is debated, but may point to an original i-present 3sg. \*sed-ei-ti, 3pl. \*sed-i-enti.

\*set/a- m. 'seat' - Go. sitls m. 'id.', OE set/ m./n. 'id.', ME setel 'id.', Du. zetel c. 'id.', OHG sezzal m. 'id.', G Sessel m. 'id.' ⇒ \*sed-tlo- (IE) - Olr. seal m. 'den' < \*sed-lo-. A word derived from \*setOJan- 'to sit' with an /-suffix. A much older formation is continued by OE seal, OS setha/, OHG seda/ n. 'seat' < \*sepia-, which through Pre-Gm. \*setlo- is to be derived from PGm. \*sed-tlo- (an instrumental noun in \*-tlo-). Also cf. \*sadu/a-.

\*seuda- m. 'purse, money-bag' - ON sj6or m. 'id.', OE seod m. 'id.' ⇒ \*sieuH-t6- (ORV). A to-formation derived from the root of \*siujan- 'to sew' (q.v.). It is difficult to determine whether the word continues \*seuda- < \*seuH-to- or \*siuda< \*siuH-to- / \*siHu-to-, but in view of the related MHG siite f. 'seam' < \*siido< \*siuH-t-eh₂-, it is likely that Pre-Gm. \*sjiito- would have lost its j (cf. e.g. \*kewwan- < \*gieuH-e-). This makes \*sieuH-to- the preferred proto-form.

\*seukan- s.v. 'to be ill' - Go. siukan s.v. 'id.' (DRY?). A strong verb that given its final \*k ( < \*kk) is likely to have been created to the iterative \*suk{k)on- (q.v.). Cf. the derived Go. siuks, ON sjukr, OE seoc, OFri. siiik, OS siok, Du. ziek, OHG sioh, G siech adj. 'unwell, ill' < \*seuka-.

\*seuni- f. 'appearance, face' - Go. siuns f. 'sight, appearance, face', ON sj6n f. 'sight, eyesight; look', Far. sj6n f. 'id.', OE s/en f. 'sight, vision', OFri. siune f. 'face, sight, appearance', NFri. F sjiin 'apparition', OS siun f. 'sight', MDu. sune f. 'face, vision' ⇒ \*sekw-nf- (DRY). A ni-formation created to the root of \*sehwan- 'to see' (q.v.). The form plays an important role in the establishment of the relative chronology of the historical Germanic sound changes, as it proves that Verner's law pre-

\*seupan- s.v. 'to boil, seethe' - ON sj6oa s.v. 'id.', Far. sj6oa s./w.v. 'id.', Elfd. siuooa s.v. 'id.', OE seooan s.v. 'id.', E to seethe, OFri. siiitha s.v. 'id.', MDu. sieden s.v. 'id.', Du. zieden w.v.(?) 'id.', OHG siodan s.v. 'id.', G sieden s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*seut-e- (NEUR) - Lith. siaiisti (siauciu ) 'to play, rage', Ru. 5utft' 'to play, joke around' < \*seut-ie-; Lith. siusti (siuntu) 'to go crazy; to beat'. A Germanic/Balto-Slavic isogloss, possibly an extension of the PIE root \*(H)seu- 'to burn', for which see \*swfpan-. Related to \*saupa- 'broth' (q.v.) and MHG suddern, Du. sudderen w.v. 'to simmer', the frequentative to an otherwise lost formation \*supjan- < \*sut-ie-.

\*sida- adj. 'long' - ON sfor adj. 'long (e.g. of hair), hanging down', OE sfd adj. 'wide, broad, ample', OFri. side adj. 'low', MDu. side, sijt adj. 'broad, wide', Du. zijd adj. 'id.', OHG sfto adv. 'loose' ⇒ \*sh₁i-t6- (WEUR) - Olr. sith-, OW hit 'length', MW hyt m./f., OBret. hit 'length' < \*sh₁i-ti-. Possibly related to \*sfpu- 'late', which appears to be formed to an i-present to the root \*seh₁- 'long, lasting'; \*sfda- may then continue the original ptc. of this verb. The formation ON sfoa, OE, OFri. side, OS sfda, Du. zij(de), OHG sfta, G Seite f. 'side' < \*sfdon- was derived from the adjective through a meaning 'broad surface'.

\*sidu- m. 'custom' - Go. sidus m. 'id.', ON sior m. 'id.', Far. siour m. 'id.', Elfd. sio m. 'id.', OE sidu, seodu m. 'id.', OFri. side m.jf. 'id.', OS sidu m. 'id.', Du. zede c. 'id.', OHG situ m. 'id.', G Sitte m. 'id.' (IE). No existing etymology. The word has been compared to Gr. f:8oc; n. 'custom, usage' < \*suedh-es- and Skt. svadh₃- f. 'id.' < \*suedh-eh₂- (cf. Pokorny JEW: 882-4), but even ifthe first u was somehow lost (e.g. by dissimilation), the resulting \*sedu- would still have developed into ON \*\*sjpor with regular breaking of -e- to -jp- (Bjorvand/Lindeman 2000: 765-6). If Bjorvand and Lindeman are right, and the meaning 'custom' indeed developed from 'band, bond' (I.e.), it is possible to compare Hitt. isbai ` isbijanzi 'to bind, to wrap; to obligate with, to impose upon', for which see \*saila/o-. I thus tentatively reconstruct PGm. \*sidu- as \*sh₂i-tu- 'bond, rule, tradition'.

\*sihwan- s.v. 'to percolate' - ON sfa w.v. 'to filter, strain; to sip through the beard', OE seon s.v. 'to strain, filter; to ooze, trickle', MDu. sien s.v. 'to filter; to pour', OHG sfhan s.v. 'to filter, strain', G seihen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*seikw-e- (IE) - Skt. sificati 'to pour, pour out/upon, besprinkle', YAv. hir:icaiti 'to pour' < \*si-n-kw-e-; Gr. tKμac; f. 'wetness, moisture, secretion' < \*sikw-meh₂- (but cf. oμμa n. 'eye' < \*h₃ekw-mn); scs Sbcati, Ru. scat' (scu) 'to piss' < \*sikW-eh₂-.
\*sikkon- w.v. 'to trickle' - Nw. sikka w.v. 'to lower oneself, OE sicerian w.v. 'id.', G Pal. sickern w.v. 'id.', Rhnl. sicken w.v. 'to piss' ⇒ \*sikw-neh₂- (IE). The iterative to \*sihwan- (q.v.).

\*siled- f. 'herring' - ON sfld, pl. sildr f. 'id.', Far. sfld f. 'id.', Elfd. sild f. 'id.', Du. zee/t c. 'tench, doctor fish' ⇒ \*shi-{e)i-1-(?) (IE?) - Mir. silid 'to drop, flows, drip' < \*si/-e-. A fish name of unknown origin. The potential ablaut between ON sfld and Du. zeelt (if related!) as well as the occasional inflection of sfld as a consonant stem could theoretically point to an old t-stem, e.g. \*silo, gen. \*sildaz, Joe. \*silepi < \*seil-ot, \*sil-t-6s, \*sil-et-i. It is uncertain, however, whether such an archaic paradigm can really be substantiated by the extant material. Magnusson 1989: 815 rather starts from an original meaning 'fry, young fish' in view of ON sf/, lcel. sili n. 'fry', Far. sf/ n. 'trout, small sea trout' < \*silOJa-, and it seems likely, from this perspective, to connect also Far. sila w.v. 'to ejaculate the milt or soft roe' < \*silon-. This verb can technically be related to Mir. silid, whose root may be an extension of an i-present \*sh₁-ei-, cf. Hitt. foi - sijanzi 'to prick, sting; to shoot, throw; (med.) to squirt, spurt, flow' < \*shi-6i, \*sh₁-i-enti (see \*sean-). Ru. sel'd', seledka as well as Lith. siTke and Fi. silli are Gm. loanwords.

\*silubra- n. 'silver' - Go. silubr n. 'id.', ON silfr n. 'id.', Far. silvur n. 'id.', OE seolfor, siolufr, sylfor n. 'id.', E silver, OFri. sel(o)ver, silver, sulver n. 'id.', OS siluoar n. 'id.', Du. zilver n. 'id.', OHG silabar n. 'id.', G Silber n. 'id.' ⇒ \*silubhr(NlE) - Celtib. silabur 'id.' (cf. Basq. zilhar 'id.'?), OCS sbrebro n. 'id.', Lith. sidiibras m. 'id.'. A non-IE Wanderwort whose distribution appears to be "circum-Celtic".

\*sina- pron. 'own' - Go. seins pron. 'id.', ON sinn pron. 'id.', Far. sfn pron. 'id.', Elfd. sa; pron. 'id.', OE sin pron. 'id.', OFri. sin pron. 'id.', OS sin pron. 'id.', Du. zijn pron. 'id.', OHG sin pron. 'id.', G sein pron. 'id.' ⇒ \*seino- (DRV) - Ven. veina- 'one's own' < \*sueino-.

\*singwan- s.v. 'to sing' - Go. siggwan s.v. 'id.', ON syngva, syngja s.v. 'id.', Far. syngja s.v. 'id.', OSw. siunga s.v. 'id.', Elfd. singga s.v. 'id.', OE singan s.v. 'id.', E to sing, OFri. siunga, sionga s.v. 'id.', WFri. sjonge s.v. 'id.', OS singan s.v. 'id.', ODu. singon s.v. 'id.', Du. zingen s.v. 'id.', OHG singan s.v. 'id.', G singen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*sengwh_e- (IE) - Gr. 6μcpt; f. 'divine voice or revelation, oracle, emblem' < \*songwh-eh₂-. A Germanic-Greek isogloss pointing to a PIE root \*sengwh-. As to the NGm. forms, note ON syngva with West Norse w-mutation and OSw. siunga with East Norse w-breaking. See also \*sangwa-.

\*sinkwan- s.v. 'to sink' - Go. sigqan s.v. 'id.', ON sf1kkva s.v. 'id.', Far. sf1kka s.v. 'id.', OSw. siunka s.v. 'id.', Elfd. sikka s.v. 'id.', OE sincan s.v. 'id.', E to sink, OS sinkan s.v. 'id.', Du. zinken s.v. 'id.', OHG sincan s.v. 'id.', G sinken s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*si-n-k-nu- (GM). A strong verb of uncertain origin. In view of the iterative \*sakk/gon(q.v.), it is likely that the n of the root is infixal. Did the root-final labiovelar arise from \*-k-nu- under Kluge's law? See also \*sihwan-.

label_gloss: s.v. 'to head for; to long for'
gloss: to head for; to long for
opening: \*sinnan- s.v. 'to head for; to long for' - MDu. sinnen w.v. 'to think, contemplate', Du. zinnen s.v. 'to consider', OHG sinnan s.v. 'id.', G sinnen s.v. 'to contemplate' ⇒ \*sent-ne- (EUR) - Lat. sentio, -ire 'to sense, feel' < \*snt-ie-; Olr. set m. 'path', MW hint, hynt f. 'id.' < \*sent-u-; Lith. sintJti 'to think' < \*snt-ehrie-; CS Sf;St'b adj. 'sensible, wise' < \*snt-io-. A nasal present to the root \*sent- 'to feel'. Cf. Go. sinþs m./n. 'time', ON sinn n. 'id.', OS sith m. 'way, direction', OE sio m. 'journey, road, turn', OHG sint m. 'road, journey' < \*sinþa-. See also \*sandjan-.

\*siþu- adj. 'late' - Go. seiþus adj. 'id.', ON sior comp. 'id.', Far. siour comp. 'id.', MDu. side adj. 'id.', OHG sid comp. 'id.' ( < \*siþ-iz) ⇒ \*sh₁-ei-tu- (WEUR) - Lat. serus adj. 'belated, slow', Olr. sir adj. 'lasting, eternal', W hir adj. 'long' < \*sehrro-. Also cf. Go. /Jana seiþs, ON sfoan, OE siþþan 'from that time' < \*siþ{iz) þano. The word may be related to \*sida- 'long' ( q.v.).

\*siujan- w.v. 'to sew' - Go. siujan w.v. 'id.', ON syja w.v. 'id.', OE seowian, siwian w.v. 'id.', E to sew, OFri. sid w.v. 'id.', MLG siiwen w.v. 'id.', OHG siuwen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*siHu-ie- (IE) - Skt. sfvyati, ptc. syiitci- (cf. Oss. (D) xud 'sewn up') 'to sew', Lat. suere, ptc. siitum 'id.', Lith. sidti, Latv. silt, SCr. siti 'id.' < \*siHu-ie-, ptc. \*siuH-t6-.

\*skaban- s.v. 'to shave, scrape' - Go. skaban s.v. 'id.', ON skafa s.v. 'id.', Far. skava w.v. 'id.', Elfd. skcivci s.v. 'id.', OE scafan s.v. 'to shave', E to shave, MDu. schaven s./w.v. 'id.', Du. schaven w.v. 'to scrape', OHG scaban s.v. 'id.', G schaben w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*skh₂bh·e- (EUR) - Gr. aKarrw 'to dig (out), work the earth' < \*skh₂bh-ie-; Lat. scab6, -ere 'to scratch' < \*skh₂bh·e-; Lith. sk6bti 'to hollow out' < \*skeh₂bh-e-; OCS skoblb, Ru. sk6belb 'plane' < \*skh₂ebh-/-io-. A strong verb related to the iterative \*skapp/biin-. See also \*skepa'sheep'.

\*skadu- m. 'shadow' - Go. skadus m. 'id.', OE sceadu f. 'id.', E shadow, shade, OS skado m. 'id.', Du. schaduw c. 'id.', OHG scato m. 'id.', G Schatten m. 'id.' ⇒ \*skh₁-tu- (EUR) - Gr. aK6wc; m. 'darkness, dark' < \*skh₁-to-; Oir. se<ith n. 'shadow', MW ysgawd m. 'shade, darkness' < \*skoh₁-to-. Also cf. Far. skadda, skfJdda, Nw. skodde f. 'mist' < ON \*skadda, obi. \*skpddu (with an unclear geminate).

\*skaiwaro- f. 'magpie' - ON skj6r f. 'id.', Ice!. skj6r m. 'id.', Far. skj6ra f. 'id.', Nw. skjor, skjære f. 'id.', Elfd. stjier f. 'id.' (GM). An exclusively Nordic bird name (WGm. has \*ag/kkon-, q.v.). The word has been compared to OE higora m., higore f. 'magpie' < \*hig{V)ra/6n- and OHG hehara f. 'jay' < \*hih{V}r6n- under the assumption of an s mobile, but the underlying form \*hig(V)r6- is incompatible with the Nordic word: the alternation between West Nordic skj6r and East Nordic \*ska!ra points to a proto-form \*skaiwar6- rather than \*\*skehuro-. No generally accepted etymology. In view of the weak iridescence of the bird's darker feathers there could be a connection with Nw. skjoma w.v. 'to variegate', cf. Ice!. skj6mi, skj6r m. 'layer of mold'.

\*skakan- s.v. 'to shake; to leave' - ON skaka s.v. 'to shake', Nw. skaka s.v. 'id.', OE scacan s.v. 'to shake, shiver; to run, leave, flee', E to shake, OFri. skeka s.v. 'to run away from home', OS skakan s.v. 'to leave', MDu. schaken s./w.v. 'to leave, escape, run away with someone (a woman)', OHG unt-scahhon w.v. 'to be driven' (DRV). An o-grade intensive verb back-formed to the iterative \*skakk/gon- (q.v.), an o-grade variant of \*skukk/gon- (q.v.). The original meaning was 'to shake', whence 'to shake off and 'to leave, rob', as attested for OFri. skeka. In both Dutch and Frisian, \*skakan- became conflated with the originally weak \*skekojan-, which was derived from \*skeka-, cf. OHG scah, MHG schach m. 'robbery' (cf. OE sceacere, OHG scachari, G Schacher m. 'robber' < \*skekarja-). Or do we have to posit a second strong verb \*skekan- that was

\*skakk/gon- w.v. 'to shake' - Far. skaka w.v. 'to shake', Elfd. skakci w.v. 'to shake, shiver', ME shaggen w.v. 'to shake, cast off, tremble', E to shag w.v. 'to toss, shake, waggle', LG skaken w.v. 'to bump, shake', MHG schacken w.v. 'id.' (EUR) - Olr. scuichid 'to move, start, go', MW ysgogi 'to move, stir, tremble', OCS skoCiti, SCr. skoCiti 'to jump, leap' < \*skok-eie-. An o-grade iterative to \*skakan- (q.v.). Etymologically, the iterative seems to replace the PIE intensive \*skok-eie-, as attested in Baltic and Slavic. Within synchronic Germanic, it functions as the iterative to \*skehan- 'to move fast' (q.v.), however, which implies that both \*skakan- - with unetymological \*k - and the zero-grade iterative \*skukk/gon- (q.v.) - with unetymological \*u - were created secondarily.

\*skalda- n. 'skald, poet' - ON skald n. 'id.' (DRY?). An a-stem probably derived from \*ske/dan- (q.v.), cf. OHG ske/to, MHG schelte m. 'blamer, criticizer, satirist' < \*skaldjan-. The long a of Ice!. skdld is problematic and cannot be explained by lengthening before -Id- in view of the -ce- of e.g. skceldinn adj. 'skilled in poetry'. This is not enough reason, however, to reconstruct the word as \*skepla-, thus assuming a link with Olr. see/, MW chwedl 'story' < \*sJcwetlo- (pace Liden 1891: 507).

\*skalka- m. 'servant' - Go. ska/ks m. 'id.', OE scealc m. 'id.', OFri. skalk m. 'servant; villain', OS skalk m. 'servant; disciple', Du. schalk c. 'mischief, OHG scale m. 'servant, slave', G Schalk m. 'rogue, mischief (GM). Also cf. MLG scha/k, MDu. scha/c adj. 'evil' < \*skalka- and ON skelkr, Far. skelkur, skjelkur m. 'fear, shock' < \*ska/ki-. No certain etymology. The adjective \*ska/ka- is suggestive of a strong verb \*ske/kan-, for which cf. the possibly related iterative ON skulka w.v. 'to mock' < \*skulkon- < \*sklk/9Ch₃_neh₂-. Can all these formations be reconciled by assuming a primary meaning 'wry, wrong' (cf. \*ske/ha- - \*skelga-)? This would be preferable, at least, over the link with e.g. Far. sk<ilkur m. 'piece of wood' as claimed by Friesen 1897: 59-60 in view of the semantically parallel ON drengr m. 'stick; boy' (see \*dranga-); this skalkur was clearly borrowed from MLG scha/k meaning 'servant; villain', and metaphorically also 'wooden peg'. For other, even more unlikely proposals, see Lehmann 1989: 308-9.

\*skamo- f. 'shame' - OE scamu f. 'id.', E shame, OFri. skome f. 'shame; genitals', OS skama f. 'id.', MDu. schame f. 'id.', OHG scama f. 'id.', G Scham f. 'id.' ⇒ \*skoh₃-meh₂- (IE). No established etymology: the links with ON skammr adj. 'short' (De Vries 1962: 512), \*hamala- 'maimed' (Pokorny JEW: 929) and Gr. aKaμ,6<; adj.

\*skap(j)an- s.v. 'to form, create' - Go. ga-skapjan\* s.v. 'id.', ON skapa s.v., skepja w.v. 'to shape, form, mould', Far. skapa w./s.v. 'to shape, mould; to cause, bring about', OE scieppan, sceppan s.v. 'to form, create', OFri. skeppa s.v. 'to form, make', OS skeppian\* s.v. 'id.', Du. scheppen s.v. 'id.', OHG seep/en s./w.v. 'id.', G schopfen w.v. 'id.' (GM). A strong verb of uncertain origin. One possibility is to assume that the verb was derived from the iterative \*skapp/bon- 'to scrape': the required semantic shift from 'to hollow out' to 'to form' (cf. Lehmann 1986: 149) is not evident, but finds support in OS skap, OHG scaf n. 'container' and OS ska po m. 'pan'. Also cf. Go. ufar-skafts f. 'first fruits' < \*ska/ti-.

\*skapp/bon- w.v. 'to shave, scrape' - Nw. skabba w.v. 'id.', MDu. schabben w.v. 'to scratch', G Rhnl. schappen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*skh₂bh-neh₂- (DRY). The iterative to \*skaban- and the derivational the base of ON skabb, OE sceab m. 'scabies' < \*skabba-. MDu. schobben w.v. 'to scratch' points to an additional zero-grade iterative \*skubbon-. Related to \*skapjan- 'to form'?

\*skarda- adj. 'cut up' - ON skaror adj. 'damaged', OS ska rd adj. 'cut; hurt', OHG scart adj. 'id.', MHG schart adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*skordh·o- (EUR) - Olr. scerdaid 'to peel, scrape off < \*skerdh-eh₂-ie-; Lith. skefsti (skerdiiu) 'to butcher' < \* skerdh-ie-. An adjective to a European root \*skerdh-, for which see \*skertan-. Also cf. the factitive MHG scherten w.v. 'to cut, chop' < \*skardjan-.

\*skarna- n. 'dung, muck' - ON skarn n. 'id.', Far. skarn n. 'id.', OE scearn n. 'id.', E sharn, OFri. skern m./n. 'id.', WFri. skern c. 'id.', MDu. scharn n. 'id.' ⇒ \*sli-or-no- (IE) - Hitt. fokkar - fokn- n. 'excrement, dung, faeces' < \*so/i-r, gen. \*sli-n-6s; Gr. oxwp, gen. crKaT6<; n. 'muck, excrement' < \*s/i-or, \*-nt-6s; Latv. siir!J.i m.pl. 'excrement', Ru. sor m. 'id.' < \*sli-or-. A continuation of an old PIE heteroclitic noun nom. \*s6/i-r, gen. \*sli-n-6s. The Germanic formation combines both suffix variants.

\*skarpa- adj. 'sharp' - ON skarpr adj. 'sharp, bitter, desiccated', Far. skarpur adj. 'id.', Elfd. skarp adj. 'hard', OE scearp adj. 'sharp', E sharp, OFri. skerp adj. 'id.', OS skarp adj. 'id.', Du. scherp adj. 'id.', OHG scarp adj. 'id.', G
\*skatta- 441 scharf adj. 'id.' (DRV) - Derivationally comparable to Latv. skafbs adj. 'sharp' < \*skorbh-o-. An a-grade adjective derived from \*skerban- - \*skerpan- - \*skrepan( q.v.).

\*skatta- m. 'wealth, property, cattle' - Go. skatts m. 'coin', ON skattr m. 'tribute, tax; treasure', OE sceat m. 'property, goods, wealth', OFri. sket m./n. 'goods, property, cattle', OS skat m. 'treasure, money, possession', Du. schat c. 'treasure, wealth', OHG skaz m. 'treasure, money, wealth, tax', G Schatz m. 'treasure' (GM). A formation probably related to the strong verb WPhal. schden, pret schaid 'to yield' < \*skedan- (cf. WPhal. bnien, pret braid 'to roast' < \*bredan-) < \*skeh₁dh-e-(?). It follows that PGm. \*skatta- must continue \*skh₁dh-n6- (with Kluge's law). No further etymology. Hardly related to Lat scato, -ere 'to gush forth, swarm' (thus Pokorny IEW: 950), which in spite of WPhal. schcid 'frogspawn' may rather be related to \*skeutan-. OCS skot'b m. 'cattle' is a Germanic loanword.

\*skaþjan- s.v. 'to harm, do wrong' - Go. skaþjan s.v. 'id.', ON skeoja w.v. 'id.', OE sceþþan s./w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*skh₁t(H)-ie- (EUR) - Arm. c'aw 'pain, disease' < \*skh₁tH-o- (Olsen 1999: 180); Gr. acrKr]-8Ќc; adj. 'unhurt, unscathed' < \*n-skeh₁t(H)-es-; Olr. scfth adj. 'weary, tired' < \*skeh₁t(H)-o-. A strong verb to a European root \*skeh₁tH- (cf. LIV2: 551). Also cf. ON skaoi, OE sceaoa, OS skatho, Du. schade, OHG scado, G Schaden m. 'damage' < \*skaþan-, whence ON skaoa, OE sceaoian, OFri. skathia, OS skathon, Du. schaden, OHG scaden, scadon, G schaden w.v. 'to harm, damage' < \*skaþojan-. See also \*skoþi-.

\*skauni- adj. 'beautiful' - Go. skauns, skauneis adj. 'id.', OE sciene adj. 'id.', E sheen, OFri. skene adj. 'id.', OS skoni adj. 'id.', Du. schoon adj. 'beautiful; clean', OHG sconi adj. 'beautiful', G schon adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*skou-ni- (DRV). In view of the fully parallel derivation of Go. siuns, ON synn adj. 'visible' < \*seuni- from \*sehwan-, PGm. \*skauni- must be a semantically gerundival ni-stem to a lost strong verb \*skawan- < \*sk6u-e- (for which cf. \*skawa- 'obser.vant'). It follows that the original meaning was 'watchable', whence 'beautiful'.

\*skauta- m. 'seam' - Go. skaut(s) m./n. 'projection, edge, fringe', ON skaut n. 'corner; flap; sheet', Far. skeyt n. 'sheet; lap, bosom', OE sceat m. 'corner, angle; projection, promontory', OFri. skat m. 'lap; coat-tail', Du. schoot c. 'lap', OHG scoz m. 'tip, corner, shirt-tail', G Schoss m. 'lap; shirt-tail' (DRV). Derived from \*skeutan- in the sense of "to shoot out", i.e. 'to jut out'.

\*skawa- adj. 'observant(?)' - Go. us-skaws adj. 'sober' ⇒ \*skou-6- (IE) - Gr. -8UOCJKOO<; m./f. 'name of a sacrificial priest' < \*-skou-o-; NP sikoh, sukoh 'splendor' < \*skeu-etH-o-(?). An adjective to the PIE root \*skeu-. Also cf. OE sceawian, OFri. skowia, OS skawon, Du. schouwen, OHG scouwon, G schauen w.v. 'to see, inspect' < \*skawojan-. See also \*skauni- and \*skuwwan-.

\*skehan- s.v. 'to move quickly; to happen' - OE sceon w.v. 'to go quickly, fly', OFri. skiii.(n) s.v. 'to happen', MDu. scien, ge-scien s./w.v. 'to happen', Du. ge-schieden w.v. 'id.', OHG gi-scehan, scehan s.v. 'id.', MHG schehen s.v. 'to move quickly; to happen', G ge-schehen s.v. 'to happen' ⇒ \*skek-e- (EUR) - Olr. scuichid 'to move, start, go', MW ysgogi 'to move, stir, tremble', OCS skociti, SCr. skoCiti 'to jump, leap' < \*skok-eie-. A strong verb whose original meaning 'to move quickly' developed into 'to happen' at an early stage. Probably related to \*skakk/gon- and \*skukk/gon- ( q.v.).

\*skekkjan- w.v. 'to make move' - MDu. schicken w.v. 'to cause to happen, order', Du. schikken w.v. 'to order', MHG schicken w.v. 'to start, prepare, order, send', G schicken w.v. 'to send' (DRV?). The causative to \*skehan- ( q.v.). The origin of the geminate as well as the e-grade is unclear, the expected form being \*\*skahjan-. Perhaps we are dealing with a causative inchoative, quasi-PIE \*skek-nhreie- "to make start , move". See also \*skakk/gon- and \*skukk/gon-.

\*skeldan- s.v. 'to announce; to reproach' - ME sco/den w.v. 'id.', E to scold, OFri. skelda s.v. 'id.', ODu. sce/dan s.v. 'id.', Du. schelden s.v. 'id.', OHG see/tan s.v. 'id.', G schelten s.v. 'id.' (GM). A strong verb apparently created to a root \*ske/dh-. See also \*skalda-.

\*skeldu- m. 'shield' - Go. skildus m. 'id.', ON skjpld m. 'id.', Far. skjeldur m. 'id.', Elfd. stjold m. 'id.', OE scield m. 'id.', E shield, OFri. ske/d, skiold, skiuld m. 'id.', OS ske/d, skild m. 'id.', Du. schild n. 'id.', OHG sci/t m. 'id.', G Schild m. 'id.' ⇒ \*ske/H-tu- (GM) - Lith. ski/tis m. 'slice' < \*sk/H-ti-. A tu-stem probably created to the root \*ske/H- 'to split' (Pokorny JEW: 923-27), for which see \*ske/jan-. The Old Frisian word also denoted a coin, which allows for the explanation of Go. skilliggs m. 'solidus', ON skillingr, OE sci/ling, OHG sci/ling 'aureus (a gold coin)' as continuing \*skeld-linga(Schroder 1918: 254ff).

\*skelha- - \*skelga- adj. 'wry, oblique' - ON skjalgr adj. 'wry, oblique; squinting', Ice!. skjalgur adj. 'cross-eyed', Far. skjalgur adj. 'wry, askewed, warped', Elfd. stjal adj. 'wry, warped; exhausted', OE sceolh, sceol adj.

\*skeljan- w.v. 'to separate, distinguish' - ON skilja w.v. 'to divide, separate; to distinguish; to understand', Far. skilja w.v. 'to separate, remove; to distinguish; to grasp, understand', Elfd. stjila w.v. 'to separate' ⇒ \*ske/H-ie- (IE) - Hitt. iskal/ai - iskal/anzi 'to slit, split, tear' < \*sk/H-6i, \*sk/H-enti; Gr. oKilw 'to stir up, hoe' < \*sk/H-ie-; Arm. c'elum 'to split, tear', Lith. skelti (ske/iu), Latv. s[<eft 'to split' < \*ske/H-ie-; Lith. ski/ti (sky/u) 'to split, cleave', Latv. s[<iTt 'id.' < \*sk(-n-]/H-; Mir. scoilt, sco/t f. 'splitting' < \*sko/H-ti-. A European verb identical to Lith. skelti. Also cf. Go. skilja m. 'butcher' < \*skeljan-, whose meaning is reminiscent of Far. skilja 'to remove bones (from boiled fish)'. Also cf. ON ski/ n. 'difference' < \*skelja- and MDu. schele, sche/e m. 'id.' < \*skelan-, whence OE sce/ian w.v. 'to remove', MDu. schelen w.v. 'to deviate, differ' < \*ske/6jan-. Possibly related to \*skeldu- 'shield' (q.v.).

\*skelo- f. 'bowl' - ON ska/ f. 'bowl, cup', Far. ska/ f. 'id.', Elfd. ska/ f. 'id.', OS skii.la f. 'bowl; shell', Du. schaa/ c. 'id.', OHG scii./a f. 'bowl', M.HG schii.le f. 'id.' (DRV). An 6-stem probably derived from \*skeljan- (q.v.).

\*skepa- n. 'sheep' - OE seep, scfp n. 'id.', E sheep, OFri. skep n. 'id.', OS skii.p n. 'id.', Du. schaap n. 'id.', OHG scii.f n. 'id.', G Schafn. 'id.' (DRV). A Germanic word, probably derived from \*skaban- 'to shave, shear' (cf. Wood 1919: 207; Knobloch 1987: 476; and slightly differently Davis 1991). OHG scii.pii.ri, scii.ppii.ri, MHG schii.pcere, schceper m. 'sheepskin' points to a form with PGm. \*b < PIE \*bh (Krogmann 1937).

\*skera- n. 'pair of scissors' - ON skceri n.pl. 'id.', Far. poet. skcerur m. 'id.', OE scear(r)a f.pl. 'id.', OFri. skere f. 'id.', Du. schaar c. 'id.', OHG scii.ra, pl. scii.ri f. 'id.', G Schere f. 'id.' ⇒ \*skerH-ih₁ (DRV). A word derived from the strong verb \*skeran- 1 (q.v.). The i-endings of the Old Norse plura/e tantum and the Old High German plural scii.ri point to an old neuter dual form \*skii.r-f < PIE \*-ih₁•

\*skeran- 1 s.v. 'to cut' - ON skera s.v. 'to cut; to slaughter', Far. skera s.v. 'id.', Elfd. stjiira s.v. 'to cut', OE scieran s.v. 'to cut, shave', E to shear, OFri. skera s.v. 'id.', Du. scheren w.v. 'to shave; to skim', OHG sceran s.v. 'to shave, shear, cut', G scheren s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*skerH-e- (EUR) - Arm. k'erem 'to scratch' <

\*skeran- 2 m. 'shrew(?), vole(?)' - Nw. vand-skjer 'Norway lemming; shrew', Elfd. stjiiri m. 'shrew', OHG scero m. 'mouse, mole', G Scher-maus 'water vole', Swi. (Jaun) s;;ere m. 'mole' (GM). A word for an animal that is difficult to identify. Most languages point to a type of mouse, especially the shrew or the vole. OHG scero as well as some Swiss forms refer to the mole, but with the addition of Elfd. stjiiri, which like the latter continues an n-stem, the meaning 'shrew' is likely to have at least some antiquity (also cf. Far. skersa f. 'shrew; vixen'). No further etymology. The connection with \*skeran- 1 'to cut' is formally possible in view of the semantically parallel OE scyrfe-miis (derived from OE sceorfan s.v. 'to gnaw, bite'), but semantically uncompelling. There seems to be no link either with OE screawa, scr.:Ewa, E shrew < \*skrawan- or \*skrewan-.

\*skerban- - \*skerpan- - \*skrepan- s.v. 'to scrape, scratch' - OE sceorfan, sceorpan, screpan 'to scratch, bite, vex', MHG schre.fen, schreven s.v. 'to scratch' u \*skrebh·e- (EUR) - Ru. skrestf (skrebU) 'to scratch' < \*skrebh-e-; Latv. s[<irba f. 'fissure' < \*skrbh-eh₂-; Lith. skifbti 'to become sour' < \*skrbh-; Lat scrobis m./f. 'hole in the ground, pit' < \*skrobh-i-. A strong verb created to the root \*skrebh-: the primary form \*skreban- is not attested, but can be postulated on the basis of the clearly secondary variants \*skerban-, \*skerpan- and \*skrepan-, which were influenced by the iterative \*skrapp/bon- - \*skarbOn- (q.v.).

\*skertan- s.v. 'to cut off - Du. dial. scharten w.v. 'to scratch', MHG scherzen s.v. 'to cut off, shorten(?)' (EUR). A strong verb to the root \*skerdh-, for which see \*skarda-. The root-final t points to influence from an iterative \*skurton- < \*skrdh-neh₂-, cf. OHG, MHG schurz, OE scort, sceort adj. 'cut off; short' < \*skurta-. There is no compelling reason to assume that the latter adjective was borrowed from an unattested Lat. \*excurtus (thus Kluge 1897: 338).

\*skeuban- - \*skiiban- s.v. 'to shove' - Go. af skiuban s.v. 'to push away, reject', OE sceofan, sciifan s.v. 'to shove', OFri. skiiva s.v. 'to push, shove', Du. schuiven s.v. 'to shove, slide', OHG scioban s.v. 'id.', G schieben s.v. 'id.' u \*kseubh·e- (IE) - Skt. kڗ6bhate 'to shake, tremble', YAv. x5ufsqn 3pl.pres.subj. 'to tremble' < \*kseubh·e-; Pol. dial. chybac 'to sway, rock; to run, rush' < \*ksubh-eh₂- (with secondary \*y); Lith. skubus, Latv. skubrs adj.

label_gloss: 445 'hasty' < \*ksubh-(r)o-; unrelated to OCS skubati (skubp) 'to tear' < \*skoubh-eh₂-. A strong verb related to \*skupp/bon- 1. The PIE sequence \*ks- was regularly metathesized into \*sk- (cf. PGm. \*stelan- < \*tse/-e- and \*steuban-
opening: \*skeutan- 445 'hasty' < \*ksubh-(r)o-; unrelated to OCS skubati (skubp) 'to tear' < \*skoubh-eh₂-. A strong verb related to \*skupp/bon- 1. The PIE sequence \*ks- was regularly metathesized into \*sk- (cf. PGm. \*stelan- < \*tse/-e- and \*steuban- - \*stiiban- < \*tseubh-e-). Also cf. OS wind-skiijla f. 'wind vane', OHG sciijla, scub/a, G Schau/el f. 'shovel, vane' < \*skub/6-.

\*skeutan- s.v. 'to shoot, dash' - ON skj6ta s.v. 'id.', Far. skj6ta s.v. 'id.', Elfd. stjuota s.v. 'to shoot; to jump', OE sceotan s.v. 'id.', E to shoot, OFri. skiiita s.v. 'id.', OS sciotan s.v. 'id.', Du. schieten s.v. 'id.', OHG skiozan s.v. 'id.', G schieflen s.v. 'id.' (DRV). A strong verb usually connected to Skt. skundate 'to jump', which, however, is unattested. It is more likely that the verb was back-formed to the iterative \*skut(t)on-, which in turn may have been formed to a Pre-Gm. root \*sket-, cf. Lith. skasti 'to jump' under \*skut(t)on-. This would be parallel to the creation of e.g. \*dreupan- - \*drupan- to \*drupp/bon- 'to drip/droop'. The secondary nature of the zero-grade \*u also follows from MHG schatten w.v. 'to swing, shake' < \*skaddon- and Nw. skata w.v. 'to push, drive' < \*skaton-. As a result, all older etymological connections based on the Pre-Gm. reconstruction \*skeu-{d)-, e.g. Lith. siauti 'to shoot' < \*l<euH-, Skt. c6dati 'to push' < \*kfwleud- or Alb. hedh, hjedh 'to throw' < \*ske(u)d-, can be dropped. See also ON skj6tr, OE sceot adj. 'fast' < \*skeuta-, \*skauta- and \*skuti- (q.v.).

\*skewjan- w.v. 'to go, walk' - Go. skewjan w.v. 'to proceed, walk on', ON ska!va w.v. 'to stride, stride on' (GM). Probably related to MDu. schoyen, scooyen w.v. 'to go, rush, leave, roam', Du. schooien w.v. 'to hang around, go around begging' < \*skaujan-. Also cf. \*skehan- ( < \*skekw-e-?).
\*skiman- m. 'shine' - Go. skeima m. 'lantern, torch', OE scfma m. 'splendor, brightness', OS skfmo m. 'shine', OHG scfmo m. 'shine, beam' (DRV). An mn-stem derived from \*skfnan- ( q.v.).

\*skinan- s.v. 'to shine' - Go. skeinan s.v. 'id.', ON skfna s.v. 'id.', Far. skfna s.v. 'id.', Elfd. skaina s.v. 'id.', OE scfnan s.v. 'id.', E to shine, OFri. skfna s.v. 'to shine; to appear', OS skfnan s.v. 'to shine', Du. schijnen s.v. 'id.', OHG scfnan s.v. 'id.', G scheinen s.v. 'id.' (IE) - ToB skiyo n./f. 'shade, shadow', Skt. chiiyaf. 'shadow, reflection', Gr. CJKLa f. 'shade', Alb. hie f. 'shade, shadow' < \*skehrih₂, gen. \*skh₁-iehi-s; OCS sijati (sijajp), Ru. sijat' (sijaju), SCr. sjati 'to shine' < \*skh₁-i-ehrie-; OCS senb, SCr. sjen m. 'shadow' < \*skoh₁i-ni/o-.

\*skinkan- - \*skankan- - \*skunkan- A strong verb derived from an older nasal present \*skin6n-, cf. Nw. dial. skina, skjena w.v. 'to shimmer' < \*skh₁-i-neh₂-. Without the n, cf. e.g. ON skjar m., skja f. 'membrane' < \*skla/6- and \*skfman- 'shine' (q.v.).

\*skinkan- - \*skankan- - \*skunkan- m. 'shank, ham' - OE sceanca, sconca m. 'id.', E shank, OFri. skunka m. 'id.', WFri. skonk c. 'id.', OHG scinco m. 'id.', G G Schinken, Car., Swab. Schunke m. 'id.' ⇒ \*skeng-on- (IE) - Gr. CJKa0w 'to limp' < \*skng-ie- and Skt. khafijati 'id.' < \*skeng-e- (cf. Seebold 1970: 255). An ablauting n-stem derived from a PIE verbal root \*skeng·, in Germanic continued by the iterative Sw. dial. skunka w.v. 'to hop on one leg; to scamper ofr < \*skunk6n- < \*skr;J.g-neh₂-. The paradigm can be reconstructed as nom. \*skink6, gen. \*skunkaz < \*skeng-6n, \*skr;J.g-n-6s. The o-grade of the variant \*skankan- may just like in the case of \*be/kan- - \*ba/kan- - \*bulkan'beam' (q.v.) have spread from the accusative sg. \*skong-en-m, pl. \*skong-n-os (Kroonen 2011a: 187-9).

\*skipa- n. 'ship' - Go. skip n. 'id.', ON skip n. 'id.', OE scip n. 'id.', E ship, OFri. skip n. 'id.', OS skip n. 'id.', Du. schip n. 'id.', OHG self, see/ n. 'id.', G Schif n. 'id.' (LW). An early loanword from Lat. scyphus m. 'drinking cup' = Gr. crKUq>oc; m. 'cup, can'. Cf. OHG scif'vessel' and scifi n. 'id.' < \*skfp-fna-.

\*skitan· s.v. 'to shit' - ON skfta s.v. 'id.', OE scitan s.v. 'id.', E to shit, MDu. schiten s.v. 'id.', Du. schijten s.v. 'id.', OHG scfzan s.v. 'id.', G scheif,en s.v. 'id.' (GM). A strong verb with no certain etymology: possibly related to Gr. crxt0w 'to split' < \*skid-ie-, but at any rate associated with \*drftan- ( q.v.). See also \*skitt6n-.
\*skitton- w.v. 'to shit' - Far. skitta w.v. 'id.' (GM). The iterative to \*skitan- (q.v.).

\*sk6ha- m. 'shoe' - Go. skohs m. 'id.', ON sk6r m. 'id.', OE sc6h, sea n./m . 'id.', E shoe, OFri. ska m. 'id.', OS skah m. 'id.', Du. schoen c. 'id.', OHG scuoh m. 'id.', G Schuh m. 'id.' (GM). No certain etymology. Perhaps derived from \*skehan- 'to move quickly' (q.v.).
\*sk6/Ji- adj. 'harmful' - ON skreor adj. 'id.' (DRv). A V[ddhi-gerundive to \*skapjan- (q.v.). With the same root vowel, cf. ON sk6o n. 'something harmful' < \*skada-.

\*skrad/ttan- m. 'demon' - ON skratti, skrati m. 'wizard, troll', Ice!. skratti m. 'devil', OE scrcette f. 'adulteress', ME skratte 'hermaphrodite; goblin' ( < \*skratton-), OHG scrato, scratto, scraz m. 'demon, faun', MHG schrat(e) m. 'goblin', G Sch rat m. 'id.' (GM). An n-stem originally inflected as nom. \*skrado, gen. \*skrattaz < \*skrodh-on, \*skrodh-n-6s (Luhr 1988: 252-4; Kroonen 2011a: 344-6). Perhaps with a zero grade from the genitive: OHG scrota and Sw. dial. skrutt 'devil' < \*skrut(t)an-. OHG screzzo 'larva' and G Schretz m. 'demon' have analogical umlaut from the plural.

\*skrankwjan- w.v. 'to make fall(?)' - OE ge-screncan w.v. 'to cause to shrink, destroy, supplant', screncan w.v. 'to lay a stumbling-block in a person's way, trip up, ensnare', MDu. schrencken w.v. 'to ensnare, hinder, deceive', OHG screncken w.v. 'to lay crosswise, make fall, deceive', MHG schrenken w.v. 'to place crosswise' (GM). A verbal merger of the causative to \*skrinkwan- (q.v.) and a factitive to \*skranka-, cf. WFri. skrank adj. 'oblique, diagonal'. Also cf. OHG scranc, MHG schrank m. 'bar', G Schrank m. 'cupboard' < \*skranka-.

\*skrapp/biin- - \*skarbiin- w.v. 'to scrape, scratch' - ON skrapa w.v. 'to scrape, clatter', MDu. schraven, schrabben, schrappen, schrapen w.v. 'id.', Du. schrappen, schrapen w.v. 'to scratch', MHG schraffen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*skrobh-neh₂- (EUR). The iterative to \*skerban- - \*skerpan- - \*skrepan- (q.v.). The a-grade implies the pre-existence of an a-grade intensive strong verb \*skraban- < PIE \*skr6bh-, cf. Latv. skrabt 'to scratch'. Also cf. MDu. scarf, seer/ m., Du. scherf 'shard' < \*skarba-, whence OE scearfian, MDu. scharven, scherven, OHG scarbon, scarpon, MHG scharben w.v. 'to cut into small pieces' < \*skarbojan-. A zero grade iterative is attested as Nw. skrubba w.v. 'to rub' < \*skrubbon-.

\*skraudan- s.v. 'to cut, shred' - OE screadian w.v. 'to shred, cut off, prune', E to shred, Du. obs. schroden, schrooien w.v. 'to peel', OHG scrotan s.v. 'to cut, shred', G schroten w.v. 'to crack, crush' ⇒ \*skr6udh-e- (NEUR?) - To Lith. skriaudus adj. 'blunt, insulting; torrential; brittle' < \*skreudh-u-? Also cf. OE scread f. 'shred', OFri. skred n. 'cut, crack', OHG scrot m. 'cut; piece' < \*skrauda/6- and OE sknJ.d n. 'garment', ON skruo n. 'tackle, gear; ornament, apparel; costly stuff < \*skriida- (with secondary \*ii). Lat scriita n.pl. 'discarded goods' can perhaps be a Germanic loanword.
\*skrikkiin- w.v. 'to jump, skip' - MLG schrecken, schricken w.v. 'to dance', MDu. schricken w.v. 'to stride; to be startled', EDu. schricken w.v. 'to jump',

\*skrimman- - \*skrimpan- Du. schrikken s.v. 'to be startled', OHG screck6n w.v. 'to jump', MHG schricken w.v. 'id.' (GM). The oldest attestations all point to a weak verb \*skrikkon-, which in MHG and MDu. seems to have become strong in intransitive use, cf. MHG schrecken, pret.3sg. schriik, 3pl. schriiken, ptc. ge-schrocken and Du. schrikken, pret. schrok, ptc. ge-schrokken. It in turn gave rise to the transitive verb MHG schrecken w.v. 'to startle', Swi. (Visp.) frekxu 'to tear' (< 'to make burst', cf. G sprengen), apparently from PGm. \*skrakkjan-. The original meaning of the verb was 'to jump', cf. OHG hewi-skrecko, G Heu-schrecke m. 'grasshopper' < \*hauja-skrikkan-, lit. "hay-hopper". No certain etymology. The connection with Olr. screoin 'startling', which has been derived from \*skreg-ni-, must be abandoned because this meaning is secondary in Germanic.

\*skrimman- - \*skrimpan- s.v. 'to contract' - OE scrimman s.v. 'to shrink, contract', MDu. schrimpen s.v. 'id.', MHG schrimpfen s.v. 'to shrivel' ⇒ \*skrembh-e- (NEUR) - Close to Lith. skrebti (skrembU) 'to shrink' < \*skre-m-bh-e-. A strong verb based on a nasalized root \*skre-m-bh-, which gave \*skrimmthrough a form \*skrembh-ne-. The variant \*skrimpan- arose due to influence from the iterative \*skrumpon- (q.v.). See also \*skrinkwan-.

\*skrinkwan- s.v. 'to shrivel' - Nw. skrl'Jkka s.v. 'to shrivel', OE scrincan s.v. 'to shrink up, dry up, dwindle', E to shrink, EDu. schrincken 'to cringe, retreat' (GM). A strong verb (cf. OSw. skrunkin, Elfd. skrukken, Nw. skrokken ptc. 'shriveled'), not to be confused with the weak Far. skrykkja w.v. 'to wrinkle; to pull, tug', Elfd. skryttja w.v. 'to shrivel; to cringe' < \*skrunkjan- (derived from \*skrunkwa-, cf. Nw. skrukk m. 'wrinkle'). G schrincken is assumed to be an adaptation of E to shrink (DWB: 15, 1754) by the poet Georg Rudolf Weckherlin (1584-1653), who worked in England for a couple of years, but EDu. schrincken appears to be native. No clear etymology. Perhaps there is a link with ME shruggen 'to shiver, shudder; to move about', E to shrug, which is also attested with the meaning 'to cringe', and Sw. dial. skrukka, skruga w.v. 'to stoop' < \*skrukk/gon-, which is suggestive of a root \*skrek- or \*skregh-. See also \*skrankwjan-.

\*skriþan- - \*skridan- s.v. 'to stride' - ON skrfoa 'to creep, crawl', Far. skrfoa s.v. 'id.', Elfd. skraioa s.v. 'to pace', OE scrioan s.v. 'to go, take one's way', OFri. skrida s.v. 'to pass by', OS skrlthan, skridan s.v. 'to pace', Du. schrijden s.v. 'id.', OHG scritan s.v. 'id.', G schreiten s.v. 'id.' (NEUR) - Lith. skristi (skrindu, skridaii) 'to rush, run' < \*skridh-; Lith. skraidus adj. 'fast' < \*skroidh-u-; Latv. skriedinat 'to drive' < \*skridh-neh₂-.

\*skripi- m. 'pace' - ON skrior m. 'creeping', OE scrioe m. 'course', Du. schrede c. 'footstep', OHG scrit m. 'id.', G Schritt m. 'id.' (DRV). An i-stem created to the strong verb \*skr/pan- - \*skridan- ( q.v.).
\*skrumpon- w.v. 'to shrink, shrivel' - MDu. schrompen w.v. 'id.', Du. ver-schrompelen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*skr'!lbh-neh₂- (NEUR). The iterative to \*skrimman- - \*skrimpan- ( q.v.).

\*skudjan- w.v. 'to shiver, shake' - OS skuddian w.v. 'id.', Du. schudden w.v. 'id.', OHG scutten w.v. 'to shake, pour, break', G schiitten w.v. 'to pour', schiitteln w.v. 'to shake' (GM). Also cf. the r-frequentative ME shod(d)re, E to shudder, MLG schoderen, schaderen, MDu. schuderen < \*skudron-. OE scudende ptc. 'shaking' could point to a strong verb \*skiidan- (cf. G schaudern 'to shudder'?), but the length of the root vowel is uncertain .(Seebold 1970: 417). No clear etymology. The connection with Lith. kutJti 'to shake up' < \*kut-ehr and Lat quatio, -ere 'to shake, toss, hurry along' < \*kwt-ie- is formally problematic because of the alternation of \*kut- vs. \*kwt- and the required assumption of the poorly understood s-mobi/e.

\*skufta- 1 n. 'hair of the head' - Go. skuft n. 'id.', ON skopt n. 'id.', Nw. skoft n. 'tail; stump', OHG skuft f. 'id.' ⇒ \*skup-to- (GM). Related to e.g. ON skufr m. 'tassel' < \*skiiba-, G Pal. Schaupe f. 'forelock' < \*skiibbon- and G Schuppen m. 'tuft; shelter, barn', Swi. (Visp.) suppo m. 'bunch' < \*skubban-. The connection with Ru. cub'b, cup'b m. 'shock' is unclear.

label_gloss: 2 m. 'shoulder blade of cows or horses'
gloss: shoulder blade of cows or horses
opening: \*skufta- 2 m. 'shoulder blade of cows or horses' - MLG schuft m. 'id.', EDu. schoft, schocht 'id.', Du. schoft c. 'id.' ⇒ \*(s}Kupt-o- (IE) - Skt supti-, YAv. supti- f. 'shoulder', NP suft 'id.', Alb. cup, sup m. 'id.' < \*/{up-ti-. Usually connected to Skt supti- etc. The initial s, which only occurs in Germanic, may have been adopted from \*skuldra- 'shoulder'. I assume that the original form of the word, i.e. \*Kupt-i-, contains the element also found in Lith. petis, gen. peties f. 'shoulder' < \*pet+. In view of the Germanic meaning 'withers', it is further tempting to reconstruct the first element as \*h₁KU- 'horse', for which see \*ehwa-.

\*skukk/gon- w.v. 'to shake' - ME shoggen w.v. 'to shake, cast off, tremble', E to shog w.v. 'to shake, roll', obs. shock w.v. 'to go swiftly, dash', MDu. schocken w.v. 'to shake, clash', MHG schocken w.v. 'id.' (DRV). A zero-grade iterative to \*skakan-. The MHG word served as the source for Fr. choquer 'to shock', which was later borrowed back into German, Dutch and English. See also \*skakkjgon-.

\*skulan- pret.-pres. 'shall, to be obliged to' - Go. skulan pret.-pres. 'to owe, be indebted, be obliged to', ON skulu pret.-pres. 'to be obliged to, shall', Far. skula pret.-pres. 'id.', Elfd. ula pret.-pres. 'id.', OE sculon pret.-pres. 'id.', E to shall, OFri. skela pret.-pres. 'id.', OS sculan pret.-pres. 'id.', Du. zullen pret.-pres. 'id.', OHG scolan pret.-pres. 'id.', G sol/en pret.-pres. 'id.' ⇒ \*sk61-e(NEUR) - Lith. ske/Jti (skeliu) 'to owe' < \*skel-eh₁-ie-; OPru. skelliints adj. 'guilty' < \*skel-ont-. A preterite-present 3sg. \*skale, 3pl. \*skulunp < \*skol-e, \*skl-nt. Note that the ending -u of ON skulu is analogical after the 3pl. skulu, the infinitive usually being identical to this form. See also \*skuldi-.

\*skuldi- f. 'debt, guilt' - ON skuld f. 'debt', Far. skuld f. 'debt; guilt; cause', Elfd. skuld f. 'id.', OE scyld f. 'guilt, fault', OFri. skielde, skiolde f. 'guilt; tax', OS skuld f. 'id.', Du. schuld c. 'debt, guilt', OHG skuld f. 'id.', G Schuld f. 'id.' (DRV). A ti-stem to the root of \*skulan-.

\*skuldra- m. 'shoulder' - OE sculdor m. 'id.', E shoulder, OFri. skolder, skulder m. 'id.', Du. schouder c. 'id.', OHG scultirra, scultera f. 'id.', G Schulter f. 'id.' ( < \*skuldrjon-) (GM?). A West Germanic word: OSw., Sw. skuldra was adopted from Middle Low Germanic. No certain etymology. Perhaps to Gr. aK£A.o<; n. 'thigh, leg, hip, shank' < \*skel-es-, but the implied assumption of an instrumental suffix -dhro- is semantically problematic.

\*skupp/bon- 1 w.v. 'to shove' - lcel. skopa w.v. 'to swing, rock, roll', Far. skopa w.v. 'to shoot (of a star); to hurry up; to push, shove', Nw. skubba, skuppa, skopa w.v. 'to bump, shove', EDu. schobben, schoppen w.v. 'to stuff; to swing', Du. schoppen w.v. 'to kick', MHG schopfen, schoppen w.v. 'to stuff ⇒ \*ksubh-neh₂- (IE) - Formally parallel to Skt. k$ubhn6ti 'to shake, tremble' < \*ksubh-neu-, which may continue older \*k$ubhnilti (-neh₂-), and Lith. skubinti 'to rush' < \*ksubh-oh₂-. The iterative to \*skeuban- - \*skuban- ( q.v.).

\*skupp/bon- 2 w.v. 'to mock' - ON skopa w.v. 'id.', MLG schoven w.v. 'id.', EDu. schobben, schoppen w.v. 'id.', MHG schopfen w.v. 'to versify' ⇒ \*skp-nehr(?) (EUR) - Gr. aKwmw 'to mock, jest' < \*skop-ie.

\*skii.ra/o- m.jf. 'short shower (of rain/hail)' - Go. skura f. 'id.', ON skur f. 'id.', Far. skurur m. 'id.', Elfd. skaur f. 'id.', OE sciir m. 'id.', E shower, MDu. schuur m./f. 'id.', OHG skiir m. 'id.', G Schauer m. 'id.' (GM). Also cf. Nw. skl'!yre, skjere f. 'gust of wind' < \*skaurjon- and skyra w.v. 'to take off quickly' < \*skiirjan-. De Vaan 1999 compared the word to PGm. \*skur- 'to cut, tear', but in view of OS skion m. 'cloud', I propose to reconstruct a PGm. heteroclitic \*skewur, gen. skiinaz continuing a PIE ur/n-stem \*skehrur, gen. \*skhrun-6s (in view of the absence of Dybo's law, the accent must have shifted to the stem at an early stage).
